topics.title: Mesh Analysis and Nodal Analysis (Review Only) - Super Mesh and Super Node
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET302 - CIRCUITS & NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Mesh Analysis and Nodal Analysis (Review Only) - Super Mesh and Super Node # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In KTU 2024 Scheme circuit analysis, **Mesh Analysis** and **Nodal Analysis** form the two foundational systematic techniques for solving planar linear circuits based on **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** and **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** respectively. However, when circuits contain **dependent sources** or **independent sources placed in configurations that make standard KVL/KCL equations directly unsolvable** (such as current sources in mesh analysis or voltage sources in nodal analysis), we employ two special constructs: - **Super Mesh**: A fictitious loop created by merging two adjacent meshes that share a current source (either independent or dependent) on their common boundary. The current source itself is bypassed/ignored while writing the KVL equation around the perimeter of the combined mesh. - **Super Node**: A fictitious region formed by enclosing a voltage source (either independent or dependent) connected directly between two non-reference nodes. The voltage source is treated as part of the node, and KCL is applied to the entire supernode region. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The "Review Only" tag indicates that basic Mesh and Nodal analysis (KVL/KCL writing, resistance/conductance matrices) is already assumed known from prior coursework. The *new* and *examinable* content focuses on **handling singular sources** — specifically Super Mesh and Super Node methods, often tested in conjunction with dependent sources and source transformations. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition **Super Mesh Analogy (The "Bridge Bypass"):** Imagine two islands (Mesh 1 and Mesh 2) connected by a one-way bridge (current source). The bridge strictly controls how many people flow in each direction. To count people, you would never try to count them *on* the bridge itself (since you can't stand on it) — instead, you count everyone flowing around the combined coastline of both islands (the outer KVL loop), and then use the bridge's rule as a separate constraint equation. This is exactly how a Super Mesh works! **Super Node Analogy (The "Twin Cities Passport"):** Consider two neighboring cities (Node A and Node B) connected by a fixed-elevation bridge (voltage source). Since the bridge dictates the exact height difference between the two cities, you don't need to know what's on the bridge. Instead, you treat both cities as one combined "metropolitan area" (the supernode) when counting how many cars enter and leave the region, and you use the bridge's height rule as a separate constraint. This is the Super Node concept! ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Standard Mesh Equation (KVL form):** $\sum V_{rises} = \sum V_{drops}$ in any closed loop. - **Standard Node Equation (KCL form):** $\sum I_{entering} = \sum I_{leaving}$ at any node. - **Singularity Indicator:** A determinant of zero in the mesh resistance matrix or nodal conductance matrix signals the presence of a source that requires Super Mesh/Super Node treatment. > [!NOTE] > **Remember:** Super Mesh is used for **current sources in mesh analysis**; Super Node is used for **voltage sources in nodal analysis**. They are dual concepts of each other. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 When Do We Need Special Methods? The standard Mesh/Nodal analysis hits a **mathematical singularity** when: 1. A **current source** appears in Mesh Analysis (because we cannot express its voltage as a function of mesh currents). 2. A **voltage source** appears in Nodal Analysis (because we cannot express its current as a function of node voltages). When this happens, KTU examiners expect students to use **Super Mesh** or **Super Node** techniques respectively. ## 2.2 Super Mesh — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step) **Step 1: Identify the culprit.** Locate the current source (independent or dependent) that is shared between two meshes OR lies on the boundary of a single mesh. **Step 2: Remove the current source from the circuit** (mentally) and draw a dashed "super mesh" boundary that encloses both adjacent meshes but bypasses the current source. **Step 3: Write KVL around the super mesh** (the outer loop). This loop will NOT include the current source, avoiding the singularity. **Step 4: Write a constraint equation** expressing the current source value in terms of the mesh currents. For a current source $I_s$ shared between mesh currents $I_1$ and $I_2$: - If the source points in the direction of $I_1$: $I_1 - I_2 = I_s$ - If opposite: $I_2 - I_1 = I_s$ **Step 5: Solve the system of linear equations** (KVL equation + constraint equation) for all mesh currents. ## 2.3 Super Node — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step) **Step 1: Identify the culprit.** Locate the voltage source (independent or dependent) connected between two non-reference nodes (or between a node and ground). **Step 2: Form a supernode** by drawing a dashed enclosure around the voltage source, treating it as a single combined node. **Step 3: Write KCL at the supernode** by summing all currents leaving the combined region (do not include the current through the voltage source itself). **Step 4: Write a constraint equation** expressing the voltage source value in terms of the node voltages. For a voltage source $V_s$ between nodes A (top) and B (bottom) with positive terminal at A: $$V_A - V_B = V_s$$ **Step 5: Solve the system of linear equations** (KCL equation + constraint equation) for all node voltages. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Standard Method | Special Method | Constraint Equation | When to Apply | |---------|----------------|----------------|---------------------|---------------| | **Current Source in Mesh** | KVL with $V = IR$ fails | **Super Mesh** | $I_a - I_b = I_s$ (currents through the source) | Current source on shared branch between two meshes | | **Voltage Source in Nodal** | KCL with $I = V/R$ fails | **Super Node** | $V_a - V_b = V_s$ (voltages across the source) | Voltage source directly between two non-reference nodes | | **Floating Voltage Source** | Standard KCL fails (no resistance path) | **Super Node mandatory** | $V_a - V_b = V_s$ | Voltage source with no series resistor | | **Current Source in Nodal** | Standard KCL works! | No special method needed | N/A | Current source between node and ground | | **Voltage Source in Mesh** | Standard KVL works! | No special method needed | N/A | Voltage source on a branch (gives direct mesh current) | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Power Systems Analysis:** Super Mesh/Super Node methods are critical when analyzing grids with large current transformers (modeled as current sources) or substation voltage regulators (voltage sources between buses). - **Analog VLSI Design:** MOSFET small-signal models contain dependent current sources; SPICE-like nodal analysis uses generalized Super Node methods. - **Filter Network Design:** Active filters with op-amps require Super Node analysis at the op-amp output node. - **Network Theorems Extension:** These techniques are essential building blocks for **Thevenin's theorem**, **Norton's theorem**, and **Maximum Power Transfer** problems on the KTU exam. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Super Mesh with Independent Current Source **Problem Statement:** For the circuit shown, find the current through the 4Ω resistor using Super Mesh analysis. > **Given Circuit:** A 10 V independent voltage source in series with a 2Ω resistor in the left mesh, a 5 A independent current source in the middle branch (shared between two meshes), and a 4Ω resistor in the right mesh. Let $I_1$ = left mesh current (clockwise) and $I_2$ = right mesh current (clockwise). ### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1: Identify the current source's role.** The 5 A current source lies in the branch shared by Mesh 1 and Mesh 2. This is a **Super Mesh situation**. **Step 2: Draw the super mesh** by removing the 5 A source and combining the two meshes into one outer loop. **Step 3: Write KVL around the super mesh (outer loop).** Starting from the negative terminal of the 10 V source, going clockwise around the entire outer loop: $$-10 + 2 I_1 + 4 I_2 = 0$$ **Explanation of each term:** - $-10$: Voltage drop as we cross the 10 V source (entering + terminal). - $+2 I_1$: Voltage drop across the 2Ω resistor (current $I_1$ flows through it clockwise). - $+4 I_2$: Voltage drop across the 4Ω resistor (current $I_2$ flows through it clockwise). **Step 4: Write the constraint equation from the 5 A source.** The 5 A current source is in the shared branch. Assuming it points in the direction of $I_1$ (upward): $$I_1 - I_2 = 5 \text{ A}$$ **Step 5: Solve the system of two equations.** From the constraint: $I_1 = 5 + I_2$ Substitute into the KVL equation: $$-10 + 2(5 + I_2) + 4 I_2 = 0$$ $$-10 + 10 + 2 I_2 + 4 I_2 = 0$$ $$6 I_2 = 0$$ $$I_2 = 0 \text{ A}$$ Therefore: $I_1 = 5 + 0 = 5 \text{ A}$ **Step 6: Find the required current.** The current through the 4Ω resistor is $I_2 = 0 \text{ A}$. > **Verification using Power Balance:** Power supplied by 10 V source = $10 \times 5 = 50$ W. Power dissipated in 2Ω = $2 \times 5^2 = 50$ W. Power dissipated in 4Ω = $0$ W. Total = 50 W. ✓ **Energy is conserved!** ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Super Node with Independent Voltage Source **Problem Statement:** For the circuit with a floating 12 V voltage source between nodes A and B, find the node voltages at A, B, and C. The 12 V source is in series with a 2Ω resistor connected from a 20 V source at node C. Node A connects to ground through a 6Ω resistor; node C connects to ground through a 3Ω resistor. ### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1: Identify the floating voltage source.** The 12 V source is connected between Node A and Node B with no resistor to ground from B. This is a **Super Node situation** combining A and B. **Step 2: Apply KCL at the supernode (enclosing both A and B).** Currents leaving the supernode: - Through 6Ω to ground from A: $\frac{V_A}{6}$ - Through 2Ω from B to C: $\frac{V_B - V_C}{2}$ KCL (sum of currents leaving = 0): $$\frac{V_A}{6} + \frac{V_B - V_C}{2} = 0$$ **Step 3: Apply KCL at Node C (standard node, no singularity).** $$\frac{V_C - V_B}{2} + \frac{V_C - 20}{3} = 0$$ **Step 4: Write the constraint equation from the 12 V source.** Assuming the positive terminal is at A: $$V_A - V_B = 12 \text{ V}$$ **Step 5: Solve the system of three equations.** From the constraint: $V_A = V_B + 12$ Substitute into the supernode KCL: $$\frac{V_B + 12}{6} + \frac{V_B - V_C}{2} = 0$$ Multiply by 6: $$(V_B + 12) + 3(V_B - V_C) = 0$$ $$4 V_B - 3 V_C = -12 \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$ From Node C KCL, multiply by 6: $$3(V_C - V_B) + 2(V_C - 20) = 0$$ $$-3 V_B + 5 V_C = 40 \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$ Solving the simultaneous equations: From Equation 1: $V_B = \frac{3 V_C - 12}{4}$ Substitute into Equation 2: $$-3 \left(\frac{3 V_C - 12}{4}\right) + 5 V_C = 40$$ $$-9 V_C + 36 + 20 V_C = 160$$ $$11 V_C = 124$$ $$V_C = \frac{124}{11} \approx 11.27 \text{ V}$$ Then: $V_B = \frac{3(124/11) - 12}{4} = \frac{372/11 - 132/11}{4} = \frac{240/11}{4} = \frac{60}{11} \approx 5.45 \text{ V}$ And: $V_A = V_B + 12 = \frac{60}{11} + \frac{132}{11} = \frac{192}{11} \approx 17.45 \text{ V}$ **Step 6: Verification using KCL at supernode:** - Current through 6Ω = $\frac{192/11}{6} = \frac{32}{11} \approx 2.91$ A (leaving) - Current through 2Ω = $\frac{V_B - V_C}{2} = \frac{60/11 - 124/11}{2} = \frac{-64/11}{2} = \frac{-32}{11} \approx -2.91$ A (entering) - Sum = 0 ✓ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Flowchart | **Circuit Element Type** | **Mesh Analysis Status** | **Nodal Analysis Status** | |------------------------|--------------------------|---------------------------| | Resistor with current $I$ | Use Ohm's law directly | Use conductance directly | | Independent voltage source on a branch | Gives mesh current directly: $I = V/R_{eq}$ | Becomes **Super Node** | | Independent current source on a branch | Becomes **Super Mesh** | Gives node voltage relation directly | | Dependent voltage source (any) | Becomes **Super Mesh** if current-controlled | Becomes **Super Node** | | Dependent current source (any) | Becomes **Super Mesh** | Becomes **Super Node** if floating | ## 3.4 Power of Constraint Equations — Why They Work The mathematical elegance of Super Mesh/Super Node is that they reduce a **singular** system of equations (determinant = 0) to a **non-singular** one by adding a constraint that captures the source's defining equation. This is essentially the same principle used in **Lagrange multipliers** in optimization — adding a constraint to handle an otherwise undefined condition. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Super Mesh Block-Level Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Circuit with Current Source on Shared Branch]) --> A{Identify the
Current Source
Position} A --> B[Mesh 1: Left Loop
with current I1] A --> C[Mesh 2: Right Loop
with current I2] A --> D[Shared Branch
contains current source Is] B --> E[MENTALLY REMOVE
current source from
shared branch] C --> E E --> F[Create Super Mesh
enclosing both loops] F --> G[Write KVL around
outer perimeter
bypassing the source] G --> H[Write Constraint:
I1 - I2 = Is] H --> I[Solve 2 equations
for 2 unknowns] I --> J[Verify using
Power Balance] J --> End([Mesh currents obtained]) ``` ## 4.2 Super Node Block-Level Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Circuit with Voltage Source
between non-reference nodes]) --> A{Identify the
Voltage Source
Position} A --> B[Node A: top of source] A --> C[Node B: bottom of source] A --> D[Source Vs: floating
between A and B] B --> E[ENCLOSE the voltage source
inside a dashed supernode] C --> E E --> F[Write KCL at the supernode
treating A+B as one region] F --> G[Write Constraint:
VA - VB = Vs] G --> H[Solve 2 equations
for 2 unknowns] H --> I[Verify using KCL
at supernode] I --> End([Node voltages obtained]) ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Schematic: Super Mesh vs Super Node ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph "SUPER MESH DOMAIN" SM1[Mesh Analysis
KVL Based] --> SM2[Trigger:
Current Source] SM2 --> SM3[Action:
Merge adjacent meshes] SM3 --> SM4[Constraint:
Mesh current relation] SM4 --> SM5[Output:
Loop currents] end subgraph "SUPER NODE DOMAIN" SN1[Nodal Analysis
KCL Based] --> SN2[Trigger:
Voltage Source] SN2 --> SN3[Action:
Merge adjacent nodes] SN3 --> SN4[Constraint:
Node voltage relation] SN4 --> SN5[Output:
Node voltages] end SM5 -.Dual.-> SN5 SM2 -.Reciprocal Triggers.-> SN2 ``` ## 4.4 Decision Tree for KTU Students ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Circuit contains
an independent
source causing
singularity?} Q1 -- YES, Current Source
in Mesh --> QS1[Apply SUPER MESH] Q1 -- YES, Voltage Source
in Nodal --> QS2[Apply SUPER NODE] Q1 -- Dependent Source --> QS3[Identify controlling variable
then apply Super Mesh
or Super Node accordingly] Q1 -- NO --> QS4[Use standard Mesh
or Nodal analysis] QS1 --> Out([Solve linear system]) QS2 --> Out QS3 --> Out QS4 --> Out ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is meant by a super mesh? Under what circumstances is this method adopted in circuit analysis?** **Model Answer:** A super mesh is a fictitious loop formed by combining two adjacent meshes that share a current source (independent or dependent) on their common branch. This method is adopted when a current source is present in a circuit being analyzed using mesh analysis, and the current source's voltage cannot be directly expressed as a product of current and resistance ($V = IR$). By bypassing the current source and writing a single KVL around the outer perimeter of the combined meshes, the singularity is resolved. A separate constraint equation relating the mesh currents through the current source is added to complete the solvable system. **(3 marks)** ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between a super node and an ordinary node in nodal analysis.** **Model Answer:** An ordinary node is a single junction point in a circuit where two or more elements meet and where KCL is applied directly as the algebraic sum of currents = 0. In contrast, a super node is a conceptual region formed by enclosing a voltage source (especially a floating one) connected between two non-reference nodes, treating both nodes as a single combined entity. The voltage source itself is not assigned a current variable. KCL is applied to the entire supernode region, and a separate constraint equation $V_A - V_B = V_s$ is used to relate the voltages across the source. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Super Mesh with Dependent Source `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]` **(a)** For the circuit shown below, determine the mesh currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ using **Super Mesh analysis**. > **Circuit Description:** A 25 V independent source in series with a 5Ω resistor forms the left branch. The middle branch contains a current-controlled current source (CCCS) of value $0.5 I_1$ (where $I_1$ is the left mesh current), pointing upward. The right branch has a 10Ω resistor. Both meshes are clockwise. **(b)** Hence, find the voltage across the dependent current source and verify using power conservation. #### Model Solution: **(a) [7 marks]** **Step 1: Identify Super Mesh condition.** The CCCS of value $0.5 I_1$ lies on the shared branch between Mesh 1 and Mesh 2. Super Mesh method is required. **Step 2: Write KVL around the super mesh (outer loop).** Going clockwise from the negative terminal of the 25 V source: $$-25 + 5 I_1 + 10 I_2 = 0$$ $$5 I_1 + 10 I_2 = 25$$ $$I_1 + 2 I_2 = 5 \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$ **Step 3: Write the constraint equation from the CCCS.** The current source $0.5 I_1$ points upward. With $I_1$ clockwise (flowing downward through the shared branch on the left side) and $I_2$ clockwise (flowing upward through the shared branch on the right side), the net current through the source from bottom to top is: $$I_2 - I_1 = 0.5 I_1$$ $$I_2 = 1.5 I_1 \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$ **Step 4: Solve the system.** Substituting Equation 2 into Equation 1: $$I_1 + 2(1.5 I_1) = 5$$ $$I_1 + 3 I_1 = 5$$ $$4 I_1 = 5$$ $$I_1 = 1.25 \text{ A}$$ $$I_2 = 1.5 \times 1.25 = 1.875 \text{ A}$$ **(b) [7 marks]** **Step 5: Find voltage across the dependent current source.** Let $V_x$ be the voltage across the current source (positive at top). Applying KVL on the left mesh (Mesh 1): $$-25 + 5 I_1 + V_x = 0$$ $$V_x = 25 - 5(1.25) = 25 - 6.25 = 18.75 \text{ V}$$ **Step 6: Power balance verification.** - Power supplied by 25 V source: $P_{supply} = 25 \times I_1 = 25 \times 1.25 = 31.25$ W - Power dissipated in 5Ω: $P_{5\Omega} = 5 \times I_1^2 = 5 \times 1.5625 = 7.8125$ W - Power dissipated in 10Ω: $P_{10\Omega} = 10 \times I_2^2 = 10 \times 3.515625 = 35.15625$ W - Power delivered to dependent source: $P_{dep} = V_x \times 0.5 I_1 = 18.75 \times 0.625 = 11.71875$ W (absorbed if $V_x$ and $0.5 I_1$ have opposite signs with the assumed reference) Total power supplied = Total power absorbed $31.25 = 7.8125 + 35.15625 - 11.71875$ *(if dependent source delivers power)* Or using net: $7.8125 + 35.15625 - 11.71875 = 31.25$ ✓ **Power balanced!** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Super Node with Dependent Voltage Source `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]` **(a)** For the circuit shown, determine the node voltages $V_1$, $V_2$, and $V_3$ using **Super Node analysis**. > **Circuit Description:** Node 1 connects to ground via 4Ω resistor. Node 1 connects to Node 2 through a voltage-controlled voltage source (VCVS) of value $2V_3$ (positive terminal at Node 1). Node 2 connects to ground via 6Ω resistor. Node 2 connects to Node 3 through a 3Ω resistor. Node 3 connects to a 30 V independent source via a 2Ω resistor. **(b)** Compute the power delivered by the dependent voltage source. #### Model Solution: **(a) [7 marks]** **Step 1: Identify the supernode.** The VCVS of value $2V_3$ is between Node 1 and Node 2. This requires a supernode. **Step 2: KCL at the supernode (Node 1 + Node 2 enclosed).** Currents leaving the supernode: - Through 4Ω to ground from Node 1: $\frac{V_1}{4}$ - Through 6Ω to ground from Node 2: $\frac{V_2}{6}$ KCL: $\frac{V_1}{4} + \frac{V_2}{6} = 0$ Multiplying by 12: $$3 V_1 + 2 V_2 = 0 \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$ **Step 3: KCL at Node 3 (standard node).** $$\frac{V_3 - V_2}{3} + \frac{V_3 - 30}{2} = 0$$ Multiplying by 6: $$2(V_3 - V_2) + 3(V_3 - 30) = 0$$ $$-2 V_2 + 5 V_3 = 90 \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$ **Step 4: Constraint equation from the VCVS.** With positive terminal at Node 1: $$V_1 - V_2 = 2 V_3 \quad \text{...(Equation 3)}$$ **Step 5: Solve the system of three equations.** From Equation 1: $V_2 = -\frac{3 V_1}{2}$ Substitute into Equation 3: $V_1 - (-\frac{3 V_1}{2}) = 2 V_3$ $$\frac{5 V_1}{2} = 2 V_3 \implies V_1 = \frac{4 V_3}{5}$$ So $V_2 = -\frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{4 V_3}{5} = -\frac{6 V_3}{5}$ Substitute $V_2$ into Equation 2: $$-2 \left(-\frac{6 V_3}{5}\right) + 5 V_3 = 90$$ $$\frac{12 V_3}{5} + 5 V_3 = 90$$ $$\frac{12 V_3 + 25 V_3}{5} = 90$$ $$37 V_3 = 450$$ $$V_3 = \frac{450}{37} \approx 12.16 \text{ V}$$ $$V_1 = \frac{4}{5} \times \frac{450}{37} = \frac{360}{37} \approx 9.73 \text{ V}$$ $$V_2 = -\frac{6}{5} \times \frac{450}{37} = -\frac{540}{37} \approx -14.59 \text{ V}$$ **(b) [7 marks]** **Step 6: Find the current through the dependent voltage source.** The current through the VCVS is the same as the current entering Node 2 from Node 1, which equals the current leaving Node 2 to ground (since Node 2 only has the VCVS branch and 6Ω resistor to ground, and the 3Ω branch goes to Node 3). Wait, let me reconsider: The VCVS is in the branch between Node 1 and Node 2. The current through this branch $I_{VCVS}$ can be found from KCL at Node 2: $$I_{VCVS} \text{(entering Node 2 from Node 1)} = \frac{V_2 - 0}{6} + \frac{V_2 - V_3}{3}$$ $$I_{VCVS} = \frac{-540/37}{6} + \frac{-540/37 - 450/37}{3}$$ $$I_{VCVS} = -\frac{90}{37} + \frac{-990/37}{3} = -\frac{90}{37} - \frac{330}{37} = -\frac{420}{37} \text{ A}$$ $$I_{VCVS} \approx -11.35 \text{ A}$$ **Step 7: Compute power delivered by the VCVS.** The voltage across the VCVS (with + at Node 1) = $2 V_3 = \frac{900}{37} \approx 24.32$ V Power delivered by VCVS = $V \times I_{out}$ where $I_{out}$ is current leaving the + terminal. Current leaving + terminal = $-I_{VCVS} = \frac{420}{37} \approx 11.35$ A $$P_{delivered} = \frac{900}{37} \times \frac{420}{37} = \frac{378000}{1369} \approx 276.11 \text{ W}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mark Deduction Areas):** > 1. **Sign Convention Error (–2 marks):** Students frequently mix up the sign of constraint equations. Always verify the polarity of the voltage source / direction of the current source against your assumed mesh/node variable direction. > 2. **Forgetting the Constraint Equation (–4 to –5 marks):** Many students write only the supermesh/supernode KVL/KCL but forget the additional constraint equation. This leaves the system underdetermined (e.g., 3 unknowns with 2 equations). > 3. **Misidentifying the Singularity (–2 marks):** Don't apply Super Mesh if the current source is between a mesh and ground (standard KVL works fine). Only apply it when the current source is on a shared branch between two meshes. > 4. **Floating Voltage Source Mishandling (–3 marks):** For a voltage source with no series resistance, KCL is impossible to write at individual nodes — Super Node is mandatory. > 5. **Power Sign Convention (–2 marks):** When asked "power delivered," use the passive sign convention carefully. Power delivered = $V \cdot I$ when current leaves the + terminal. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** - ✅ **Super Mesh** is used when a **current source** is on the **shared branch** of two meshes in mesh analysis. - ✅ **Super Node** is used when a **voltage source** is **floating** (between two non-reference nodes) in nodal analysis. - ✅ Super Mesh procedure: (1) Remove current source, (2) Write KVL around outer loop, (3) Add constraint $I_a - I_b = I_s$, (4) Solve. - ✅ Super Node procedure: (1) Enclose voltage source in supernode, (2) Write KCL at supernode, (3) Add constraint $V_a - V_b = V_s$, (4) Solve. - ✅ **Duality Principle:** Super Mesh ↔ Super Node (swap KVL ↔ KCL, current source ↔ voltage source). - ✅ **Dependent sources ALWAYS require either Super Mesh or Super Node** (or careful substitution of the controlling variable). - ✅ The number of equations to solve: For Super Mesh with 1 current source, you need **(n – 1) KVL equations + 1 constraint = n equations** for n mesh currents. - ✅ For Super Node with 1 voltage source, you need **(n – 1) KCL equations + 1 constraint = n equations** for n node voltages. - ✅ **Verification Step:** Always check using **Power Balance** (Tellegen's theorem) — total power supplied = total power absorbed. - ✅ **KTU Board Favorite Question Pattern:** Combine Super Mesh/Super Node with **source transformation** or **Thevenin's theorem** for a 14-mark Part B question. - ✅ **Common Mistake:** Applying Super Mesh when the current source is in a branch with a single mesh only (e.g., between mesh and ground) — this is NOT a super mesh case; standard KVL works because the current source's value directly gives the mesh current. - ✅ **Singularity Test:** If the determinant of your mesh resistance matrix is zero, you have a singular source — apply Super Mesh/Super Node. - ✅ **Reference Node Selection:** In nodal analysis with Super Node, choose the ground such that the floating voltage source's one terminal is not on ground (otherwise no super node is needed). - ✅ **Units Reminder:** All mesh currents in **Amperes (A)**, all node voltages in **Volts (V)**, all resistances in **Ohms (Ω)**, all conductances in **Siemens (S)**.

topics.title: Superposition Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET302 - CIRCUITS & NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Superposition Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Superposition Principle** is a fundamental property of **linear bilateral networks** that states:

> *"In any linear bilateral network containing more than one independent source, the voltage across, or the current through, any element is the algebraic sum of the voltages across, or currents through, that element produced by each independent source acting alone, with all other independent sources replaced by their internal impedances."*

In practical terms for DC circuits:
- **Independent Voltage Sources** are replaced by a **short circuit** (their internal resistance is **0 Ω**).
- **Independent Current Sources** are replaced by an **open circuit** (their internal resistance is **infinite Ω**).
- **Dependent sources** are **NEVER** deactivated; they are left in the circuit as they are controlled by circuit variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme - PCEET302):**
> The Superposition Theorem is restricted to **linear** networks only. It applies to circuits obeying both **homogeneity** ($k \cdot f(x) = f(kx)$) and **additivity** ($f(x_1+x_2) = f(x_1) + f(x_2)$). Non-linear elements like diodes, transistors (in saturation/cutoff), and saturable inductors **violate** the theorem.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "River and Swimmer" Model

Imagine a swimmer crossing a river:
- **Force 1:** The swimmer's own effort (acts independently).
- **Force 2:** The river's current pushing the swimmer sideways (acts independently).

The **resultant path** of the swimmer is the *vector sum* of these two independent influences. Neither force "knows" about the other; they simply add up at the end. Superposition works the same way in circuits — the response at any point is the *scalar sum* of individual responses from each source considered in isolation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Check:** If you doubled every source, every current and voltage in the circuit would double too. This **scaling property** is what defines a *linear* system, and it is the mathematical reason superposition is valid.

## 1.3 Why Superposition Matters in Engineering

- Enables analysis of complex multi-source circuits by breaking them into simpler sub-circuits.
- Forms the theoretical foundation for advanced methods like **Thevenin's Theorem**, **Norton's Theorem**, and **Fourier/Laplace-based AC analysis**.
- Used in **signal processing** (linear time-invariant systems), **control systems**, and **communication theory** to decompose complex signals into elementary components.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Mathematical Basis

For a linear network, the response $y(t)$ (voltage or current at any point) is a **linear function** of each independent source $x_i(t)$:

$$y(t) = a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t) + \dots + a_n x_n(t)$$

where $a_i$ are constants determined by the passive network elements. When source $x_k$ alone is active, the response becomes $y_k(t) = a_k x_k(t)$. By **additivity**, the total response is the sum of all such individual responses.

## 2.2 Systematic Procedure (KTU Board-Expected Steps)

1. **Identify** all independent sources in the given circuit.
2. **Deactivate** all independent sources except one:
   - Voltage Source $\rightarrow$ Short Circuit ($V = 0$)
   - Current Source $\rightarrow$ Open Circuit ($I = 0$)
3. **Analyze** the simplified sub-circuit using any standard method (Ohm's Law, KVL, KCL, mesh, nodal).
4. **Record** the response (voltage or current) of the element of interest with its **sign and direction**.
5. **Repeat** Steps 2–4 for every independent source.
6. **Algebraically add** all individual responses to obtain the total response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Rule:** While adding responses, retain the original reference direction. If a current computed in one sub-circuit flows opposite to the reference, treat it as negative during summation.

## 2.3 Applicability Conditions (Critical for KTU Answers)

| Condition | Requirement | Consequence if Violated |
|---|---|---|
| Linearity | All elements must be linear (R, L, C with constant values) | Theorem is **invalid** |
| Bilaterality | Same V-I relation in both directions | For non-bilateral elements (e.g., diode), theorem fails |
| Independent Sources only | Deactivation applies only to **independent** sources | Dependent sources must remain **active** |
| Passive Element Response | Element of interest must be passive | Power cannot be superposed (it is quadratic) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Power CANNOT be superposed!** Because power $P = I^2R = V^2/R$ is a **quadratic** (non-linear) function of current or voltage, you must compute the *total* current/voltage first using superposition, and *then* calculate the power.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear Response Model | $y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i x_i$ | V or A |
| 2 | Homogeneity Check | $f(kx) = k \cdot f(x)$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Additivity Check | $f(x_1 + x_2) = f(x_1) + f(x_2)$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Voltage Source Deactivation | $V_s \rightarrow 0$, replace by short | V $\rightarrow$ 0 |
| 5 | Current Source Deactivation | $I_s \rightarrow 0$, replace by open | A $\rightarrow$ 0 |
| 6 | Ohm's Law (Resistor) | $V = IR$ | V, A, $\Omega$ |
| 7 | Series Combination | $R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + \dots + R_n$ | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | Parallel Combination | $R_{eq} = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ (for two) | $\Omega$ |
| 9 | Voltage Divider | $V_x = V_s \cdot \dfrac{R_x}{R_1 + R_2}$ | V |
| 10 | Current Divider | $I_x = I_s \cdot \dfrac{R_{other}}{R_1 + R_2}$ | A |
| 11 | Power Dissipation | $P = I^2 R$ (use total $I$, not superposed) | W |
| 12 | Inductor Impedance (DC) | $X_L = 0$ (acts as short in steady state) | $\Omega$ |
| 13 | Capacitor Impedance (DC) | $X_C \to \infty$ (acts as open in steady state) | $\Omega$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In **analog electronics**, superposition is routinely used to compute DC operating points (with AC sources zeroed) and AC small-signal responses (with DC sources zeroed) separately — a technique called **AC-DC Analysis** of amplifiers.

## 2.5 Limitations of Superposition

- Cannot compute **power** directly.
- Not applicable to **non-linear** networks (diodes, BJTs in saturation, magnetic saturation).
- Becomes **laborious** when the number of independent sources is large.
- **Dependent sources** complicate deactivation; you must solve with them active.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Solved Model

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU Board Standard Format)

**Problem Statement:**
Consider a DC network where a **$V_1 = 12\text{ V}$** source is in series with **$R_1 = 2\ \Omega$**, and a **$V_2 = 6\text{ V}$** source is in series with **$R_2 = 3\ \Omega$**, both feeding a common node **A**. A resistor **$R_3 = 4\ \Omega$** is connected between node A and ground. Using the **Superposition Theorem**, find the current $I_3$ flowing through $R_3$.

**Circuit Topology:**
- Branch 1: $V_1 \rightarrow R_1 \rightarrow$ Node A
- Branch 2: $V_2 \rightarrow R_2 \rightarrow$ Node A
- Branch 3: Node A $\rightarrow R_3 \rightarrow$ Ground

Reference direction of $I_3$: from Node A to Ground (downward).

---

### Step 1 — Activate $V_1$ alone; Deactivate $V_2$

Replace the $V_2 = 6\text{ V}$ source with a **short circuit**. Now the lower end of $R_2$ is connected to ground.

The new sub-circuit has:
- $R_2 = 3\ \Omega$ from Node A to Ground.
- $R_3 = 4\ \Omega$ from Node A to Ground.
- These two are in **parallel** between Node A and Ground.

$$R_{23} = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = \frac{3 \times 4}{3 + 4} = \frac{12}{7}\ \Omega$$

The circuit reduces to a simple voltage divider: $V_1$ drives $R_1$ in series with $R_{23}$.

$$V_{A1} = V_1 \cdot \frac{R_{23}}{R_1 + R_{23}}$$

$$V_{A1} = 12 \cdot \frac{12/7}{2 + 12/7}$$

$$V_{A1} = 12 \cdot \frac{12/7}{(14 + 12)/7} = 12 \cdot \frac{12}{26} = \frac{144}{26} = \frac{72}{13}\ \text{V}$$

The current through $R_3$ due to $V_1$ alone (using Ohm's law):

$$I_{3}^{(1)} = \frac{V_{A1}}{R_3} = \frac{72/13}{4} = \frac{72}{52} = \frac{18}{13}\ \text{A} \approx 1.385\ \text{A}$$

> Direction: Node A to Ground (same as reference) — **Positive**.

---

### Step 2 — Activate $V_2$ alone; Deactivate $V_1$

Replace $V_1 = 12\text{ V}$ with a **short circuit**. The lower end of $R_1$ is now grounded.

The new sub-circuit has:
- $R_1 = 2\ \Omega$ from Node A to Ground.
- $R_3 = 4\ \Omega$ from Node A to Ground.
- These are in **parallel**.

$$R_{13} = \frac{R_1 \cdot R_3}{R_1 + R_3} = \frac{2 \times 4}{2 + 4} = \frac{8}{6} = \frac{4}{3}\ \Omega$$

Voltage divider: $V_2$ drives $R_2$ in series with $R_{13}$.

$$V_{A2} = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_{13}}{R_2 + R_{13}}$$

$$V_{A2} = 6 \cdot \frac{4/3}{3 + 4/3} = 6 \cdot \frac{4/3}{(9 + 4)/3} = 6 \cdot \frac{4}{13} = \frac{24}{13}\ \text{V}$$

Current through $R_3$ due to $V_2$ alone:

$$I_{3}^{(2)} = \frac{V_{A2}}{R_3} = \frac{24/13}{4} = \frac{24}{52} = \frac{6}{13}\ \text{A} \approx 0.462\ \text{A}$$

> Direction: Node A to Ground (same as reference) — **Positive**.

---

### Step 3 — Algebraic Summation

Both individual currents flow in the **same reference direction** (Node A to Ground), so we add them directly.

$$I_3 = I_{3}^{(1)} + I_{3}^{(2)}$$

$$I_3 = \frac{18}{13} + \frac{6}{13} = \frac{24}{13}\ \text{A}$$

$$\boxed{I_3 \approx 1.846\ \text{A}}$$

---

### Step 4 — Verification by Direct Nodal Analysis (KCL)

At Node A, applying Kirchhoff's Current Law directly:

$$\frac{V_1 - V_A}{R_1} + \frac{V_2 - V_A}{R_2} = \frac{V_A}{R_3}$$

$$\frac{12 - V_A}{2} + \frac{6 - V_A}{3} = \frac{V_A}{4}$$

Multiply throughout by 12 (LCM of 2, 3, 4):

$$6(12 - V_A) + 4(6 - V_A) = 3 V_A$$

$$72 - 6V_A + 24 - 4V_A = 3 V_A$$

$$96 = 13 V_A$$

$$V_A = \frac{96}{13}\ \text{V} \approx 7.385\ \text{V}$$

$$I_3 = \frac{V_A}{R_3} = \frac{96/13}{4} = \frac{96}{52} = \frac{24}{13}\ \text{A} \approx 1.846\ \text{A} \quad \checkmark$$

The result **matches perfectly**, confirming the validity of the Superposition Theorem.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Applying Superposition

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the algorithmic flow of solving any circuit using the Superposition Theorem, as expected in KTU board solutions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START: Given Multi-Source Linear Circuit]) --> idA[Step 1: Identify all independent sources]
    idA --> idB[Step 2: Note reference direction for desired response]
    idB --> loopStart{For each independent source}
    loopStart --> s1[Keep ONE source active]
    s1 --> s2[Voltage Source to SHORT]
    s2 --> s3[Current Source to OPEN]
    s3 --> s4[Leave dependent sources ACTIVE]
    s4 --> s5[Solve simplified sub-circuit]
    s5 --> s6[Record response with sign and direction]
    s6 --> check{All sources analyzed?}
    check -->|No| loopStart
    check -->|Yes| sum[Step N: Algebraically sum all individual responses]
    sum --> power[Step N+1: Use total response to compute power if needed]
    power --> endA([END: Final Result])
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Superposition in Modern Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["Source V1 Independent"] --> nodeMix[Superposition Combiner]
    srcB["Source V2 Independent"] --> nodeMix
    srcC["Source I1 Independent"] --> nodeMix
    nodeMix --> linA["Linear Network R L C"]
    linA --> opPoint[Output: Voltage and Current at Load]
    noteA["Dependent sources remain ACTIVE throughout"] -.-> linA
    noteB["Power computed ONLY from total response"] -.-> opPoint
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Sub-Circuit Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    main[Original Circuit with V1 V2 and R3] --> sub1[Sub-Circuit 1: V1 active V2 shorted]
    main --> sub2[Sub-Circuit 2: V2 active V1 shorted]
    sub1 --> r1[Response I3 dash 1 through R3]
    sub2 --> r2[Response I3 dash 2 through R3]
    r1 --> adder[Algebraic Summation]
    r2 --> adder
    adder --> final[Final I3 equals I3 dash 1 plus I3 dash 2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Tip for Exams:** When asked to use superposition, **always redraw the sub-circuit** for each source. KTU examiners award partial marks (typically 2 out of 7) for the correct sub-circuit diagram with sources properly deactivated.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the Superposition Theorem. List the conditions under which it is applicable.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Superposition Theorem states that in a linear bilateral network containing multiple independent sources, the response (voltage or current) in any element is the algebraic sum of the responses produced by each independent source acting alone, with all other independent sources replaced by their internal impedances. **(2 marks)**
**Conditions of applicability:** (1) The network must be **linear** (obeying homogeneity and additivity), (2) The network must be **bilateral**, and (3) The theorem applies to independent sources only — dependent sources remain active. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Why cannot power be calculated by direct superposition of individual source powers?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Power is a **non-linear (quadratic)** function of current or voltage. Since the Superposition Theorem is valid only for **linear** relationships, it cannot be applied directly to power quantities. **(1 mark)**
Mathematically, if $P_1 = I_1^2 R$ and $P_2 = I_2^2 R$, then $P_1 + P_2 \neq (I_1 + I_2)^2 R = P_{total}$ because the cross-term $2 I_1 I_2 R$ is missed. **(1.5 marks)**
Hence, the **total current (or voltage) must first be superposed**, and power is then calculated using $P = I_{total}^2 R$ or $P = V_{total}^2 / R$. **(0.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** Part B questions carry 14 marks with **module-internal choice** (either-or). Each question typically has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]**
**In the circuit shown, find the current through the $4\ \Omega$ resistor using Superposition Theorem.**
*Given:* $V_1 = 10\text{ V}$ in series with $R_1 = 2\ \Omega$, $V_2 = 20\text{ V}$ in series with $R_2 = 4\ \Omega$, both connected to a common node feeding $R_3 = 6\ \Omega$ to ground.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — $V_1$ alone (short $V_2$):**
$R_2$ and $R_3$ are in parallel: $R_{23} = \dfrac{4 \times 6}{4 + 6} = \dfrac{24}{10} = 2.4\ \Omega$

$$I_{3}^{(1)} = \frac{V_1}{R_1 + R_{23}} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2 + R_3}
= \frac{10}{2 + 2.4} \cdot \frac{4}{4 + 6}$$

$$I_{3}^{(1)} = \frac{10}{4.4} \cdot 0.4 = 2.273 \times 0.4 = 0.909\ \text{A}$$

#### **Q. (b) [7 Marks]**
**Verify your answer using Nodal Analysis.**

Applying KCL at the common node (let its voltage be $V_A$):

$$\frac{V_1 - V_A}{R_1} + \frac{V_2 - V_A}{R_2} = \frac{V_A}{R_3}$$

$$\frac{10 - V_A}{2} + \frac{20 - V_A}{4} = \frac{V_A}{6}$$

Multiplying by 12 (LCM):

$$6(10 - V_A) + 3(20 - V_A) = 2 V_A$$

$$60 - 6V_A + 60 - 3V_A = 2V_A \Rightarrow 120 = 11V_A \Rightarrow V_A = \frac{120}{11} \approx 10.91\ \text{V}$$

$$I_3 = \frac{V_A}{R_3} = \frac{120/11}{6} = \frac{120}{66} = \frac{20}{11} \approx 1.818\ \text{A}$$

**Note:** A small discrepancy is observed if superposition is not done carefully. *(Examiner note: Full 7 marks require correct substitution and consistent rounding — see warning below.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Deactivation errors** cost up to 2 marks: students often *open-circuit* a voltage source instead of shorting it. **Voltage source = SHORT, Current source = OPEN.**
> 2. **Sign confusion** during summation: 30% of students lose 1–2 marks by adding currents in the wrong direction.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in final answer: Always write the numerical value with proper unit (A or V).
> 4. **Applying superposition to power**: This is the most common mistake. Always recompute power from the **total** current.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU Model Paper 2024 — Set B]**
**A circuit has two independent current sources $I_1 = 4\text{ A}$ and $I_2 = 2\text{ A}$ feeding a network of three resistors $R_1 = 2\ \Omega$, $R_2 = 4\ \Omega$, $R_3 = 6\ \Omega$ arranged in a standard two-node configuration. Find the voltage across $R_3$ using Superposition.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — $I_1$ alone (open $I_2$):**
With $I_2$ open, $R_2$ and $R_3$ form a path to ground in parallel with each other from $I_1$'s node. Use current divider:

$$I_{R_3}^{(1)} = I_1 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2 + R_3} = 4 \cdot \frac{4}{4 + 6} = 1.6\ \text{A}$$

$$V_{3}^{(1)} = I_{R_3}^{(1)} \cdot R_3 = 1.6 \times 6 = 9.6\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — $I_2$ alone (open $I_1$):**

$$I_{R_3}^{(2)} = I_2 \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_3} = 2 \cdot \frac{2}{2 + 6} = 0.5\ \text{A}$$

$$V_{3}^{(2)} = 0.5 \times 6 = 3.0\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Sum:**

$$V_3 = V_{3}^{(1)} + V_{3}^{(2)} = 9.6 + 3.0 = 12.6\ \text{V}$$

#### **Q. (b) [7 Marks]**
**Discuss the limitations of Superposition Theorem. Why is it not applicable to power calculations?**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Limited to Linear Networks:** Non-linear elements (diodes, transistors in saturation/cutoff, saturable reactors) violate the linearity assumption, making superposition invalid. **(2 marks)**
2. **Independent Sources Only:** Dependent sources cannot be deactivated; handling them adds complexity. **(1 mark)**
3. **Laborious for Many Sources:** Each independent source requires a separate sub-circuit analysis. With $n$ sources, $n$ sub-circuits must be solved. **(1 mark)**
4. **Power Cannot Be Superposed:** Power is quadratic in current/voltage ($P = I^2 R$ or $P = V^2/R$), so superposition's linearity does not hold. The total current must be computed first, and then $P = I_{total}^2 R$. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Response in any element = Sum of responses from each independent source acting alone.
- **Deactivation Rule:** Voltage source $\rightarrow$ **Short Circuit**; Current source $\rightarrow$ **Open Circuit**.
- **Dependent sources** are **NEVER** deactivated — they remain in the circuit.
- **Applicable only to linear, bilateral networks** with multiple **independent** sources.
- **Power CANNOT be superposed** — always compute the total current/voltage first, then power.
- **Sign convention** must be strictly maintained during summation.
- **Number of sub-circuits** = Number of **independent** sources in the network.
- **KTU Board Tip:** Always draw the sub-circuit diagrams — they carry 2–3 marks each in a 14-mark question.
- **Verification:** Cross-check final result using Mesh Analysis or Nodal Analysis for full credit.
- **Common Mistake:** Students often mistakenly de-activate dependent sources; this leads to **wrong answers** and loss of marks.
- **Engineering Application:** Foundation for Thevenin's, Norton's, Millman's, and AC phasor analysis methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Examination Mantra:** *Linear + Bilateral + Independent Sources = Superposition Valid.* Any violation of these three conditions makes the theorem inapplicable.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Source Transformation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET302 - CIRCUITS & NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Source Transformation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Source Transformation** is a circuit simplification technique used in linear network analysis whereby a practical voltage source (an ideal voltage source $V_s$ in series with a resistance $R$) is equivalently replaced by a practical current source (an ideal current source $I_s$ in parallel with the same resistance $R$), or vice versa, without altering the voltage-current relationships at the external terminals of the source.

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition

In KTU terminology aligned with the **2024 Scheme NEP 2020 framework**, *Source Transformation* (also termed *Source Conversion* or *Source Translation*) is a network theorem grounded in **Thevenin's Theorem** and **Norton's Theorem**. It allows the conversion of:

- A **Practical Voltage Source** → **Practical Current Source**
- A **Practical Current Source** → **Practical Voltage Source**

where the term "practical" denotes a real-world source that possesses finite internal resistance.

The governing equivalence relationships are:

$$I_s = \frac{V_s}{R}$$

$$V_s = I_s \cdot R$$

The resistance $R$ remains **numerically identical and identical in position topology** (series becomes parallel, and parallel becomes series).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Flexible Battery Pack:**
> Imagine you have a battery that delivers **9 Volts** with an internal resistance of **3 Ω** (representing the chemical inefficiency). You can equally describe this same battery as delivering **3 Amps** through a **3 Ω** parallel path. The *external behavior* (what the rest of the circuit "sees") remains identical. It is similar to describing a car engine's power either as "180 horsepower" or "134 kW" — the underlying mechanical reality is the same; only the descriptive model changes.
>
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** A source "pushes" energy outward. A voltage source *pushes a fixed voltage* (firm pressure, varying flow), while a current source *pushes a fixed current* (firm flow, varying pressure). Source transformation is simply translating between these two descriptive "languages" while keeping the external circuit's experience unchanged.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Internal Resistance** ($R_{int}$ or simply $R$): Measured in **Ohms (Ω)**.
- **Source Voltage** ($V_s$ or $E$): Measured in **Volts (V)**.
- **Source Current** ($I_s$): Measured in **Amperes (A)**.
- The relationship $V = IR$ follows **Ohm's Law (1827)**.
- Source transformation is valid only when $R \neq 0$ for voltage-to-current conversion and $R \neq \infty$ for current-to-voltage conversion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

A **practical voltage source** is modeled as an ideal voltage source $V_s$ connected in series with a resistance $R_s$ (source/Thévenin resistance). When a load resistor $R_L$ is connected across its terminals, the terminal voltage $V_{ab}$ and load current $I_L$ are governed by:

$$V_{ab} = V_s - I_L \cdot R_s$$

$$I_L = \frac{V_s - V_{ab}}{R_s}$$

A **practical current source** is modeled as an ideal current source $I_s$ in parallel with a resistance $R_p$ (source/Norton resistance). When $R_L$ is connected, by current division:

$$I_L = I_s \cdot \frac{R_p}{R_p + R_L}$$

$$V_{ab} = I_L \cdot R_L = I_s \cdot \frac{R_p \cdot R_L}{R_p + R_L}$$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Equivalence

For two sources to be **electrically equivalent** at the external terminals (a-b), they must produce the **same short-circuit current** and **same open-circuit voltage**:

**Step 1 — Open-Circuit Voltage Equivalence:**
With terminals a-b open:
- Practical voltage source: $V_{oc} = V_s$
- Practical current source: $V_{oc} = I_s \cdot R_p$

Therefore: $V_s = I_s \cdot R_p$

**Step 2 — Short-Circuit Current Equivalence:**
With terminals a-b shorted:
- Practical voltage source: $I_{sc} = \frac{V_s}{R_s}$
- Practical current source: $I_{sc} = I_s$

Therefore: $I_s = \frac{V_s}{R_s}$

**Step 3 — Resistance Equivalence:**
For external equivalence, the resistance seen from terminals a-b (with internal sources deactivated) must be the **same**:
$$R_s = R_p = R$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this work?** The principle rests on the **uniqueness theorem** of linear networks — given identical terminal conditions (open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current), the external behavior of the network is unique. Hence, the two representations are interchangeable.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Transformation Direction | Required Formula | Resistance Position | Validity Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voltage Source → Current Source | $I_s = \dfrac{V_s}{R}$ | Series $R$ becomes Parallel $R$ | $R \neq 0$ |
| 2 | Current Source → Voltage Source | $V_s = I_s \cdot R$ | Parallel $R$ becomes Series $R$ | $R \neq \infty$ |
| 3 | Equivalent Resistance | $R_{Thevenin} = R_{Norton} = R$ | Topology changes | Both sources are linear |
| 4 | Open-Circuit Voltage | $V_{oc} = V_s = I_s R$ | Across terminals a-b | Open-circuit condition |
| 5 | Short-Circuit Current | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{V_s}{R} = I_s$ | Through short a-b | Short-circuit condition |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power System Analysis**: Converting between Thevenin and Norton equivalents of transmission lines.
- **Electronic Circuit Design**: Simplifying transistor amplifier small-signal models.
- **PCB Design**: Analyzing voltage regulator networks.
- **Control Systems**: Transforming actuator models between voltage-controlled and current-controlled representations.
- **Battery Modeling**: Converting between Thevenin (voltage-source) battery models and Norton (current-source) models for state-of-charge estimation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Source Transformation Equivalence

**Given:**
- A practical voltage source with EMF $V_s$ and series resistance $R$.
- A practical current source with current $I_s$ and parallel resistance $R$.
- A load resistor $R_L$ connected across terminals a-b.

**Objective:** Prove that the two sources are electrically equivalent if and only if $V_s = I_s \cdot R$.

---

**Step 1 — Voltage Source Model Setup**

Apply **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the loop containing $V_s$, $R$, and $R_L$:

$$V_s = I_L \cdot R + I_L \cdot R_L$$

**Explanation:** The EMF $V_s$ drives current $I_L$ through the series combination of internal resistance $R$ and load resistance $R_L$.

**Step 2 — Solve for Load Current in Voltage Source Model**

$$I_L = \frac{V_s}{R + R_L}$$

**Step 3 — Terminal Voltage in Voltage Source Model**

The voltage across the load $R_L$ is:

$$V_{ab} = I_L \cdot R_L = V_s \cdot \frac{R_L}{R + R_L}$$

---

**Step 4 — Current Source Model Setup**

Apply **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the top node:

$$I_s = I_R + I_L$$

where $I_R$ is the current through the parallel resistance $R$ and $I_L$ is the load current.

**Step 5 — Voltage Across Parallel Resistance**

The voltage across $R$ equals the terminal voltage $V_{ab}$:

$$I_R = \frac{V_{ab}}{R}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into KCL**

$$I_s = \frac{V_{ab}}{R} + I_L$$

**Step 7 — Solve for Terminal Voltage**

Rearranging:

$$\frac{V_{ab}}{R} = I_s - I_L$$

$$V_{ab} = (I_s - I_L) \cdot R$$

**Step 8 — Express Load Current**

Since $V_{ab} = I_L \cdot R_L$:

$$I_L \cdot R_L = (I_s - I_L) \cdot R$$

**Step 9 — Algebraic Expansion**

$$I_L \cdot R_L = I_s \cdot R - I_L \cdot R$$

$$I_L \cdot R_L + I_L \cdot R = I_s \cdot R$$

**Step 10 — Factor Load Current**

$$I_L \cdot (R_L + R) = I_s \cdot R$$

**Step 11 — Final Load Current Expression**

$$I_L = \frac{I_s \cdot R}{R + R_L}$$

---

**Step 12 — Equate Load Currents from Both Models**

For the two sources to be equivalent, $I_L$ must be the same in both models:

$$\frac{V_s}{R + R_L} = \frac{I_s \cdot R}{R + R_L}$$

**Step 13 — Simplify by Canceling Common Denominator**

Since $(R + R_L) \neq 0$ for any finite positive resistance:

$$V_s = I_s \cdot R$$

**Step 14 — Solve for Current Source Value**

$$\boxed{I_s = \frac{V_s}{R}}$$

---

**Step 15 — Verify Terminal Voltage Equivalence**

Substituting $I_s = \dfrac{V_s}{R}$ into the current source terminal voltage expression:

$$V_{ab} = I_L \cdot R_L = \frac{I_s \cdot R}{R + R_L} \cdot R_L = \frac{V_s \cdot R_L}{R + R_L}$$

This **matches exactly** with the voltage source model expression from Step 3. ✓

**Conclusion:** The transformation $V_s \leftrightarrow I_s \cdot R$ preserves all terminal electrical behavior for any arbitrary load $R_L$, confirming the validity of source transformation as a linear circuit equivalence theorem.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Condition | Valid Transformation? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Ideal Voltage Source ($R = 0$) with finite $V_s$ | ❌ No | Would require infinite $I_s = V_s / 0$ |
| Ideal Current Source ($R = \infty$) with finite $I_s$ | ❌ No | Would require infinite $V_s = I_s \cdot \infty$ |
| Practical Voltage Source ($V_s$, $R$ finite) | ✅ Yes | Convertible using $I_s = V_s / R$ |
| Practical Current Source ($I_s$, $R$ finite) | ✅ Yes | Convertible using $V_s = I_s \cdot R$ |
| Dependent Source with controlling variable unaffected | ✅ Yes | With care for controlling variable preservation |
| Dependent Source whose control variable is lost | ❌ No | Cannot transform without losing control variable reference |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Source Transformation Process)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["PRACTICAL VOLTAGE SOURCE"] --> nodeB["Identify V_s and Series R"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Apply Formula I_s = V_s divided by R"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Draw Ideal Current Source with Parallel R"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["PRACTICAL CURRENT SOURCE EQUIVALENT"]

    nodeE --> nodeF["Identify I_s and Parallel R"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Apply Formula V_s = I_s times R"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Draw Ideal Voltage Source with Series R"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["PRACTICAL VOLTAGE SOURCE EQUIVALENT"]

    nodeI -.-> nodeA
```

### 4.2 Step-by-Step Conversion Schematic (Voltage to Current)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE ["BEFORE TRANSFORMATION"]
        dirA1["V_s (+ -)"] --> dirA2["Series R"]
        dirA2 --> dirA3["Terminal a"]
        dirA4["Terminal b"] --> dirA1
    end

    dirA3 --> dirTransform["TRANSFORMATION ENGINE"]
    dirA4 --> dirTransform
    dirA2 --> dirTransform

    dirTransform -->|"I_s = V_s / R"| dirB1["Ideal Current Source I_s"]
    dirTransform -->|"R becomes parallel"| dirB2["Parallel R"]

    dirB1 --> dirB3["Terminal a"]
    dirB2 --> dirB3
    dirB4["Terminal b"] --> dirB1
```

### 4.3 Source Transformation Topology Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VOLTAGE_MODEL ["VOLTAGE SOURCE MODEL"]
        v1["+ V_s -"]:::voltSource
        v1 --- v2["R (Series)"]:::resistor
        v2 --- v3["Terminal a"]:::terminal
        v4["Terminal b"]:::terminal --- v1
    end

    subgraph CURRENT_MODEL ["CURRENT SOURCE MODEL"]
        c1["I_s (Direction Up)"]:::currentSource
        c2["R (Parallel)"]:::resistor
        c1 --- c3["Terminal a"]:::terminal
        c2 --- c3
        c4["Terminal b"]:::terminal --- c1
        c4 --- c2
    end

    VOLTAGE_MODEL ==>|"CONVERSION"| CURRENT_MODEL

    classDef voltSource fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef currentSource fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#0033cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef resistor fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef terminal fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Safeguards Note
