$$V_C = \frac{I}{j\omega_0 C} = \frac{V_s/R}{j\omega_0 C} = \frac{10}{j \times 1000 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{10}{j\,0.01} = -j\,1000\ \text{V}$$

Magnitude: $\vert V_C \vert = Q \cdot V_s = 10 \times 100 = 1000\ \text{V}$ ✓

---

#### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A practical parallel resonant circuit consists of a coil of $R_L = 5\ \Omega$ and $L = 10\ \text{mH}$ in parallel with a capacitor $C = 1\ \mu\text{F}$. Compute:
(i) Resonant angular frequency $\omega_0$
(ii) Dynamic impedance $Z_d$ at resonance
(iii) Quality factor $Q_p$

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the circuit is fed by a current source $I_s = 10\ \text{mA}$ (rms), find the voltage across the tank at resonance and the circulating current in the LC loop.

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

(i) Resonant frequency (high-Q approximation):

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(10\times 10^{-3})(1\times 10^{-6})}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10^{-8}}} = \frac{1}{10^{-4}} = 10{,}000\ \text{rad/s}$$

Convert to Hz: $f_0 = 10{,}000/(2\pi) \approx 1591.5\ \text{Hz}$

(ii) Dynamic impedance:

$$Z_d = \frac{L}{R_L C} = \frac{10\times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 1\times 10^{-6}} = \frac{10^{-2}}{5\times 10^{-6}} = 2000\ \Omega$$

(iii) Quality factor:

$$Q_p = \frac{1}{R_L}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{1}{5}\sqrt{\frac{10\times 10^{-3}}{1\times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{5}\sqrt{10^{4}} = \frac{100}{5} = 20$$

**Part (b):**

Voltage across the tank at resonance:

$$V_{\text{tank}} = I_s \cdot Z_d = 10\times 10^{-3} \times 2000 = 20\ \text{V (rms)}$$

Circulating current in the LC loop (current through L branch equals current through C branch in magnitude):

$$I_{\text{circ}} = Q_p \cdot I_s = 20 \times 10\ \text{mA} = 200\ \text{mA (rms)}$$

Verify via L-branch current:

$$I_L = \frac{V_{\text{tank}}}{\sqrt{R_L^{2} + (\omega_0 L)^{2}}} = \frac{20}{\sqrt{25 + (10{,}000 \times 0.01)^{2}}} = \frac{20}{\sqrt{25 + 10{,}000}}$$

$$= \frac{20}{\sqrt{10{,}025}} \approx \frac{20}{100.12} \approx 0.1998\ \text{A} = 199.8\ \text{mA} \approx 200\ \text{mA} \checkmark$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Resonance Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–1 mark):** Students often mix $f$ in Hz and $\omega$ in rad/s. Always state units explicitly. The relation $BW = R/L$ gives rad/s; divide by $2\pi$ if Hz is needed.
> 2. **Q = 1 Confusion (–2 marks):** Some textbooks define Q as $\omega_0/BW$, others as $\omega_0/(2\pi BW)$. KTU uses the **angular** convention: $Q = \omega_0/BW$. Mismatch yields wrong numerical answer.
> 3. **Tank vs. Ideal Parallel:** If the problem gives a *lossy* parallel combination, do *not* use $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ blindly — solve the exact quadratic from the admittance imaginary part, especially if $Q < 5$.
> 4. **Phasor Direction:** At resonance, $V_L$ leads $I$ by 90° and $V_C$ lags $I$ by 90°. They are **180° out of phase with each other**, so the source voltage appears *entirely* across R. Students often mis-sketch this and lose diagram marks.
> 5. **Half-Power Frequency Sign Error:** The lower cutoff uses the **minus** sign in the $\sqrt{}$ expression; the upper uses **plus**. Wrong sign → bandwidth negative → marks zero.
> 6. **Voltage magnification units:** $V_L = Q \cdot V_s$ only holds when $V_s$ is in the same units (peak vs rms). Be consistent.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Resonance condition:** $X_L = X_C \Rightarrow \omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$, *independent of R* for both series and parallel topologies.
- **Series resonance:** $Z$ minimum (= R), current maximum, voltage magnification by factor Q across L or C.
- **Parallel (tank) resonance:** $Z_d = L/(R_L C)$ maximum, line current minimum, circulating current magnification by factor Q.
- **Quality factor (Q):** $Q = (1/R)\sqrt{L/C}$ (series), $Q = R_p \sqrt{C/L}$ (parallel tank), always dimensionless.
- **Bandwidth (BW):** $BW = \omega_0/Q = R/L$ (series) and $1/(R_p C)$ (parallel). Narrow BW ⇒ high selectivity ⇒ high Q.
- **Half-power frequencies:**
  - Exact: $\omega_{1,2} = \mp(R/2L) + \sqrt{(R/2L)^2 + 1/(LC)}$
  - Approx (high Q): $\omega_{1,2} \approx \omega_0 (1 \mp 1/(2Q))$
  - Geometric property: $\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_1 \omega_2}$
- **Voltage/Current magnification** by factor Q is the *signature* of resonance and a frequent 7-mark sub-question.
- **KTU-favourite numericals:** R = 10 Ω, L = 100 mH, C = 10 μF type, yielding $f_0 \approx 159$ Hz, Q ≈ 10, BW ≈ 16 Hz.
- **Always re-check** whether the circuit is *ideal parallel* (R ∥ L ∥ C) — in that case, $Q_p = R\sqrt{C/L}$ with R being the parallel resistance itself.
- **Quick derivation recap:** $Z_d$ comes from setting Im$\{Y\}$ = 0 and substituting $\omega_0$ back into Re$\{Y\}$, then inverting.
- **Real-world links to mention in answers** for full marks: radio tuner, IF amplifier, oscillator, induction heating, wave trap, band-pass filter.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Series Resonance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET302 - CIRCUITS & NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Series Resonance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Series Resonance (Acceptor Circuit):** A series R-L-C circuit is said to be in electrical resonance when the reactive components of the circuit cancel each other, leaving only the resistance as the net opposition to current flow. At this specific frequency, known as the **Resonant Frequency (ω₀ or f₀)**, the circuit behaves as a purely resistive circuit, and the current drawn from the source reaches its **maximum value (I_max = V/R)**.

Mathematically, resonance occurs when the inductive reactance ($X_L$) equals the capacitive reactance ($X_C$):

$$X_L = X_C$$

This condition forces the **net reactance ($X$)** of the circuit to zero, making the **power factor (cos φ) equal to unity (1)**, and the source voltage and circuit current are perfectly **in-phase**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Swing Push" Analogy:** Imagine pushing a child on a swing. If you push at the right moment (matching the swing's natural swinging speed/frequency), the swing goes higher and higher with very little effort — even a small push transfers maximum energy. If you push too early or too late (mismatched frequency), the swing barely moves. **Series resonance works exactly like this.** The AC source pushes the charges, and when the source frequency matches the circuit's **natural frequency (1/2π√LC)**, energy oscillates smoothly between the inductor (magnetic field) and capacitor (electric field). The resistance R is the only thing limiting the current, and the circuit absorbs maximum power from the source.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Parameters at a Glance:**
> - **Resonant Frequency (f₀):** Measured in **Hertz (Hz)**
> - **Quality Factor (Q):** Dimensionless ratio
> - **Bandwidth (BW):** Measured in **Hertz (Hz)** or **radians/second (rad/s)**
> - **Half-Power Frequencies (f₁, f₂):** The −3 dB cutoff points measured in **Hz**
> - **Resonant Impedance:** Purely resistive, equals **R (in Ohms)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Series Resonance

Consider a series R-L-C circuit energized by a sinusoidal source $V = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.

**Step 1: Total Impedance of the Series Circuit**

The complex impedance of the series R-L-C combination is:

$$Z = R + j(X_L - X_C) = R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

**Step 2: Magnitude and Phase Angle**

The magnitude of impedance:

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + \left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2}$$

The phase angle between voltage and current:

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{X_L - X_C}{R}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}}{R}\right)$$

**Step 3: The Resonance Condition**

The defining condition of resonance is that the **net reactance is zero**:

$$X_L - X_C = 0 \implies \omega L = \frac{1}{\omega C}$$

**Why does this matter?** At this point, the energy stored in the inductor's magnetic field ($E_L = \frac{1}{2}Li^2$) and the capacitor's electric field ($E_C = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$) constantly exchange with each other. The source only needs to supply the **resistive losses (I²R)**, making current maximum.

**Step 4: Solving for Resonant Frequency**

From $\omega_0 L = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C}$, we get:

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

Converting to frequency in Hz:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Step 5: Behavior of Circuit Quantities vs. Frequency**

| Quantity | Below Resonance (f < f₀) | At Resonance (f = f₀) | Above Resonance (f > f₀) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $X_L = \omega L$ | **Small** | $\omega_0 L$ | **Large** |
| $X_C = 1/\omega C$ | **Large** | $1/\omega_0 C$ | **Small** |
| Net Reactance | **Capacitive** (−ve) | **Zero** | **Inductive** (+ve) |
| Circuit Nature | Capacitive | Purely Resistive | Inductive |
| Phase Angle φ | Negative (I leads V) | Zero (In-phase) | Positive (V leads I) |
| Current Magnitude | Less than max | **Maximum (V/R)** | Less than max |
| Impedance | High | **Minimum (= R)** | High |

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Exams:**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resonant Angular Frequency | $\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | rad/s |
| 2 | Resonant Frequency | $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Hz |
| 3 | Impedance at Resonance | $Z_0 = R$ | Ω (purely resistive) |
| 4 | Current at Resonance | $I_0 = \frac{V}{R}$ | A (maximum) |
| 5 | Quality Factor (Q-Factor) | $Q = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{1}{\omega_0 R C} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Bandwidth (BW) | $BW = \frac{R}{2\pi L} = \frac{f_0}{Q}$ | Hz |
| 7 | Q in terms of f₀ & BW | $Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{f_0}{f_2 - f_1}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Half-Power Frequency 1 (lower) | $f_1 = f_0 - \frac{R}{4\pi L}$ | Hz |
| 9 | Half-Power Frequency 2 (upper) | $f_2 = f_0 + \frac{R}{4\pi L}$ | Hz |
| 10 | Voltage across L at Resonance | $V_L = Q \cdot V$ | Volts (voltage magnification) |
| 11 | Voltage across C at Resonance | $V_C = Q \cdot V$ | Volts (equal to V_L, 180° out of phase) |
| 12 | Dynamic Impedance (parallel) | $R_D = \frac{L}{CR}$ | Ω (relevant for parallel resonance) |
| 13 | Geometric mean of f₁ and f₂ | $f_0 = \sqrt{f_1 \cdot f_2}$ | Hz (important property) |
| 14 | Selectivity | Inversely proportional to BW | Higher Q → Sharper peak |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Applications of Series Resonance:**
> 1. **Radio & TV Tuners:** Series LC circuits select a specific broadcast frequency by adjusting the variable capacitor until it resonates with the desired station's carrier frequency. Maximum current flows only for the chosen station.
> 2. **Band-Pass Filters:** Used in communication receivers to extract a narrow band of frequencies from a wide spectrum.
> 3. **Induction Heating:** The high circulating currents and elevated Q-factor create intense localized heating in conductive materials.
> 4. **Acoustic & Ultrasonic Devices:** Quartz crystal oscillators rely on precise resonance to generate stable reference frequencies.
> 5. **Power System Anomalies:** Understanding resonance is critical in analyzing ferroresonance and sub-synchronous resonance in transmission lines.

### 2.4 Significance of the Quality Factor (Q)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Q-Factor Matters:**
> The Quality Factor is a **figure of merit** that quantifies the "sharpness" of resonance.
> - **High Q** → Narrow bandwidth → Highly selective circuit (e.g., radio tuners need high Q to separate adjacent stations)
> - **Low Q** → Wide bandwidth → Accepts a broad range of frequencies (e.g., audio amplifiers)
> - **Voltage magnification:** $V_L = V_C = Q \cdot V_{\text{source}}$ — the voltage across L or C can be **Q times larger** than the source voltage, which is why high-Q circuits can produce dangerously high voltages (a critical safety consideration in power systems).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Resonant Frequency

**Starting Point:** The complex impedance of a series R-L-C circuit.

$$Z = R + j\omega L + \frac{1}{j\omega C} = R + j\omega L - \frac{j}{\omega C}$$

**Simplifying the reactance terms:**

$$Z = R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

**Applying the resonance condition:** The imaginary part of Z must equal zero (purely resistive circuit).

$$\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C} = 0$$

**Solving for ω:**

$$\omega L = \frac{1}{\omega C}$$

Multiplying both sides by $\omega C$:

$$\omega^2 L C = 1$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{LC} \implies \omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Converting to f₀** by dividing by $2\pi$:

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Quality Factor (Q)

**Starting from the Q-Factor definition** as the ratio of reactive power to real power (or stored energy to energy dissipated per cycle):

$$Q = 2\pi \cdot \frac{\text{Maximum Energy Stored}}{\text{Energy Dissipated per cycle}}$$

**Maximum energy stored in the inductor** (at peak current $I_0$):

$$W_L = \frac{1}{2} L I_0^2$$

**Energy dissipated per cycle in R:**

$$W_R = I_{\text{rms}}^2 \cdot R \cdot T = \frac{I_0^2}{2} \cdot R \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0} = \frac{\pi I_0^2 R}{\omega_0}$$

**Substituting into Q definition:**

$$Q = 2\pi \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{2} L I_0^2}{\frac{\pi I_0^2 R}{\omega_0}} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{L \omega_0}{2\pi R} = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R}$$

**Equivalently**, using $\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$:

$$Q = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{L}{R\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

Also:

$$Q = \frac{1}{\omega_0 R C} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Bandwidth (BW)

**At half-power points**, the current drops to $\frac{I_0}{\sqrt{2}}$, meaning power falls to half its maximum value ($P = I^2R$).

**The impedance at half-power points** satisfies $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{2} R$:

$$\sqrt{R^2 + \left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2} = \sqrt{2} \cdot R$$

**Squaring both sides:**

$$R^2 + \left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2 = 2R^2$$

$$\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2 = R^2$$

**Taking square root** (two solutions for the two half-power frequencies):

$$\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C} = \pm R$$

**For the lower half-power frequency (f₁):**

$$\omega_1 L - \frac{1}{\omega_1 C} = -R$$

**For the upper half-power frequency (f₂):**

$$\omega_2 L - \frac{1}{\omega_2 C} = +R$$

**Multiplying both equations together** ($\omega_1 \cdot \omega_2 = \frac{1}{LC}$):

$$\omega_1 \omega_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \omega_0^2 \implies f_0 = \sqrt{f_1 f_2}$$

**Subtracting the two equations** (after manipulation) yields:

$$f_2 - f_1 = \frac{R}{2\pi L}$$

Therefore, the **Bandwidth**:

$$BW = f_2 - f_1 = \frac{R}{2\pi L} = \frac{f_0}{Q}$$

### 3.4 Detailed Numerical Example (Worked Solution)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem (typical KTU 2-mark sub-part):**
> A series R-L-C circuit has $R = 10\ \Omega$, $L = 100\ \text{mH}$, and $C = 10\ \mu F$. The circuit is excited by a 50 V AC source. Compute (a) Resonant frequency, (b) Q-factor, (c) Bandwidth, (d) Current at resonance.

**Given:** $R = 10\ \Omega$, $L = 100 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{H} = 0.1\ \text{H}$, $C = 10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{F} = 10^{-5}\ \text{F}$, $V = 50\ \text{V}$

**(a) Resonant frequency:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{0.1 \times 10^{-5}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1000}{2\pi} \approx 159.15\ \text{Hz}$$

**(b) Quality Factor:**

$$Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{1}{10}\sqrt{\frac{0.1}{10^{-5}}} = \frac{1}{10}\sqrt{10^4} = \frac{100}{10} = 10$$

**(c) Bandwidth:**

$$BW = \frac{f_0}{Q} = \frac{159.15}{10} = 15.915\ \text{Hz}$$

**(d) Current at resonance:**

$$I_0 = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{50}{10} = 5\ \text{A}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: The Complete Picture

| Parameter | Symbol | Behaviour as R decreases | Behaviour as L or C changes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resonant Frequency | $f_0$ | **Unchanged** (independent of R) | $L \uparrow \Rightarrow f_0 \downarrow$; $C \uparrow \Rightarrow f_0 \downarrow$ |
| Quality Factor | $Q$ | $R \downarrow \Rightarrow Q \uparrow$ | $L \uparrow \Rightarrow Q \uparrow$ |
| Bandwidth | $BW$ | $R \downarrow \Rightarrow BW \downarrow$ | $L \uparrow \Rightarrow BW \downarrow$ |
| Selectivity | — | $R \downarrow \Rightarrow$ Sharper | Depends on L, C values |
| Peak Current | $I_0$ | $R \downarrow \Rightarrow I_0 \uparrow$ | Independent of L, C |
| Voltage across L | $V_L$ | $R \downarrow \Rightarrow V_L \uparrow$ | Magnified Q times |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Series R-L-C Circuit Schematic (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[AC Voltage Source V sin wt] -->|V| B[node1]
    B -->|I flow| C[Resistor R]
    C -->|I flow| D[node2]
    D -->|I flow| E[Inductor L - XL]
    E -->|I flow| F[node3]
    F -->|I flow| G[Capacitor C - XC]
    G -->|Return Path| H[Ground - node4]
    H --> A
    D -.->|VL drop| D
    F -.->|VC drop| F
```

### 4.2 Resonance Behaviour Block Diagram (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Input Sinusoidal Source V] --> F[Frequency Selector omega]
    F --> XLC[Compute Net Reactance: XL minus XC]
    XLC --> C1{Is XL equals XC?}
    C1 -->|No - low freq| P1[Capacitive: XC greater than XL, I leads V]
    C1 -->|No - high freq| P2[Inductive: XL greater than XC, V leads I]
    C1 -->|Yes - exact match| P3[Purely Resistive State]
    P3 --> R1[Impedance Z equals R, minimum]
    R1 --> R2[Current I equals V over R, maximum]
    R2 --> R3[Power Factor equals 1, unity]
    R2 --> R4[Voltage Magnification: VL equals VC equals Q times V]
    R4 --> R5[Energy Oscillates between L field and C field]
    R5 --> R6[Source supplies only resistive losses]
```

### 4.3 Frequency Response Plot (Conceptual Block Mapping)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Frequency Domain f axis] --> B{Region 1: f less than f1}
    A --> C{Region 2: f between f1 and f2}
    A --> D{Region 3: f equals f0}
    A --> E{Region 4: f between f2 and infinity}
    B --> B1[Low Current: I less than Imax over sqrt 2]
    C --> C1[Rising Current: I approaches Imax]
    D --> D1[Peak Current Imax equals V over R]
    E --> E1[Falling Current: I decays toward zero]
    D --> D2[Bandwidth BW equals f2 minus f1]
    D --> D3[Selectivity is High when Q is High]
```

### 4.4 Phasor Diagram Description (Verbal Schematic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Phasor Behavior at Three Critical Points:**
>
> **1. At Resonance (f = f₀):**
> - Voltage across R ($V_R$): Along the reference axis (in-phase with current)
> - Voltage across L ($V_L$): Perpendicular, pointing upward (+90°)
> - Voltage across C ($V_C$): Perpendicular, pointing downward (−90°)
> - **$V_L$ and $V_C$ cancel each other** (equal in magnitude, opposite in direction)
> - **Net voltage V = $V_R$**, so V and I are perfectly in-phase
>
> **2. Below Resonance (f < f₀):**
> - $X_C > X_L$, so $V_C > V_L$
> - The net reactive voltage points downward
> - **Current I leads voltage V** (capacitive circuit)
>
> **3. Above Resonance (f > f₀):**
> - $X_L > X_C$, so $V_L > V_C$
> - The net reactive voltage points upward
> - **Voltage V leads current I** (inductive circuit)
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q1. Define resonance in a series R-L-C circuit. State the condition for resonance and derive the expression for resonant frequency.** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

Resonance in a series R-L-C circuit is the phenomenon where the inductive reactance ($X_L$) and capacitive reactance ($X_C$) become equal in magnitude, causing the net reactance of the circuit to be zero. At this point, the impedance of the circuit is purely resistive and equals R, and the current drawn from the source becomes maximum ($I = V/R$).

**Condition for resonance:**

$$X_L = X_C$$

**Derivation of resonant frequency:**

From the condition:

$$\omega_0 L = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C}$$

Solving for $\omega_0$:

$$\omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{LC} \implies \omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

Therefore:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}\ \text{Hz}$$

> ---

**Q2. Define the quality factor (Q) of a series resonant circuit. What is its significance in communication engineering?** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

The Quality Factor (Q) of a series resonant circuit is defined as the ratio of the reactive power (or stored energy per cycle) to the real power dissipated in the resistance. It is a dimensionless figure of merit that measures the sharpness of resonance.

**Mathematical expression:**

$$Q = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{1}{\omega_0 R C} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

**Significance in communication engineering:**

1. **Selectivity:** A high Q-factor indicates a narrow bandwidth, meaning the circuit is highly selective and can isolate a specific frequency from nearby frequencies. This is essential in radio and TV tuners to separate adjacent broadcast stations.
2. **Voltage magnification:** $V_L = V_C = Q \cdot V_{\text{source}}$, allowing weak signals to be amplified.
3. **Bandwidth control:** $BW = f_0/Q$ — designers tune Q to achieve the desired frequency response.

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Exam, Module 2 — Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**A series R-L-C circuit consists of $R = 20\ \Omega$, $L = 0.2\ \text{H}$, and $C = 50\ \mu\text{F}$. It is connected to a 100 V, variable-frequency sinusoidal source.**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the resonant frequency and calculate the resonant frequency and the current at resonance. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Calculate the quality factor (Q), bandwidth, and the half-power frequencies. Also, find the voltage across the inductor and capacitor at resonance. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
$R = 20\ \Omega$, $L = 0.2\ \text{H}$, $C = 50 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{F} = 5 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{F}$, $V = 100\ \text{V}$

---

### Part (a) — Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Derivation of Resonant Frequency (3 Marks)**

The impedance of the series R-L-C circuit:

$$Z = R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

For resonance, the imaginary part must vanish:

$$\omega L = \frac{1}{\omega C}$$

Multiplying by $\omega$:

$$\omega^2 L C = 1$$

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Step 2: Calculation of f₀ (2 Marks)**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{0.2 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{10^{-5}}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 3.162 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{1.9869 \times 10^{-2}}$$

$$f_0 \approx 50.33\ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 3: Calculation of Current at Resonance (2 Marks)**

At resonance, $Z = R$ (purely resistive):

$$I_0 = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{100}{20} = 5\ \text{A}$$

---

### Part (b) — Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Quality Factor (2 Marks)**

$$Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{1}{20}\sqrt{\frac{0.2}{5 \times 10^{-5}}} = \frac{1}{20}\sqrt{4000}$$

$$Q = \frac{63.245}{20} \approx 3.16$$

**Step 2: Bandwidth (1 Mark)**

$$BW = \frac{R}{2\pi L} = \frac{20}{2\pi \times 0.2} = \frac{20}{1.2566}$$

$$BW \approx 15.915\ \text{Hz}$$

**Verification using $BW = f_0/Q$:**

$$BW = \frac{50.33}{3.16} \approx 15.92\ \text{Hz} \quad (\text{consistent})$$

**Step 3: Half-Power Frequencies (2 Marks)**

$$f_1 = f_0 - \frac{BW}{2} = 50.33 - 7.96 = 42.37\ \text{Hz}$$

$$f_2 = f_0 + \frac{BW}{2} = 50.33 + 7.96 = 58.29\ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 4: Voltage across L and C at Resonance (2 Marks)**

Inductive reactance at resonance:

$$X_L = \omega_0 L = 2\pi f_0 L = 2\pi \times 50.33 \times 0.2 = 63.25\ \Omega$$

Voltage across the inductor:

$$V_L = I_0 \cdot X_L = 5 \times 63.25 = 316.25\ \text{V}$$

Voltage across the capacitor (using $V_C = Q \cdot V$):

$$V_C = Q \cdot V = 3.16 \times 100 = 316.16\ \text{V} \quad (\text{equal to } V_L, \text{ confirming resonance})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part A Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often **forget to convert mH/μF to H/F** before substitution. Always convert units first.
> - Marks are deducted if students **don't state the resonance condition** ($X_L = X_C$) explicitly before deriving $f_0$.
> - In part (b), failing to **verify BW using both formulas** (R/2πL AND f₀/Q) loses methodology marks.
> - Students frequently **write $V_L$ and $V_C$ as the net voltage** — they must be calculated as **individual drops**, not subtracted.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**A series R-L-C circuit is to be designed to resonate at 1 MHz with a bandwidth of 10 kHz. The circuit is driven by a 10 V source.**

**(a)** Design the circuit by determining suitable values of L and C, assuming a resistance of $R = 10\ \Omega$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Calculate the quality factor, the half-power frequencies, and verify the selectivity of the circuit. Comment on the voltage magnification at resonance. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Given Data:** $f_0 = 10^6\ \text{Hz}$, $BW = 10^4\ \text{Hz}$, $V = 10\ \text{V}$, $R = 10\ \Omega$

---

### Part (a) — Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Quality Factor from f₀ and BW (2 Marks)**

$$Q = \frac{f_0}{BW} = \frac{10^6}{10^4} = 100$$

**Step 2: Determine L using Q-Formula (2.5 Marks)**

$$Q = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} \implies L = \frac{Q \cdot R}{\omega_0} = \frac{100 \times 10}{2\pi \times 10^6}$$

$$L = \frac{1000}{6.2832 \times 10^6} \approx 1.5915 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{H} \approx 159.15\ \mu\text{H}$$

**Step 3: Determine C using $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ (2.5 Marks)**

$$C = \frac{1}{(2\pi f_0)^2 \cdot L} = \frac{1}{(2\pi \times 10^6)^2 \times 1.5915 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$C = \frac{1}{3.9478 \times 10^{13} \times 1.5915 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1}{6.283 \times 10^9}$$

$$C \approx 1.5915 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{F} \approx 159.15\ \text{pF}$$

---

### Part (b) — Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Quality Factor Recalculated (1 Mark)**

$$Q = 100 \quad (\text{already calculated, confirming consistency})$$

**Step 2: Half-Power Frequencies (2 Marks)**

$$f_1 = f_0 - \frac{BW}{2} = 1{,}000{,}000 - 5{,}000 = 995{,}000\ \text{Hz} = 995\ \text{kHz}$$

$$f_2 = f_0 + \frac{BW}{2} = 1{,}000{,}000 + 5{,}000 = 1{,}005{,}000\ \text{Hz} = 1005\ \text{kHz}$$

**Verification:** $f_1 \cdot f_2 = 995{,}000 \times 1{,}005{,}000 = 9.99975 \times 10^{11}$, and $f_0^2 = 10^{12}$. Close (slight rounding error).

**Step 3: Selectivity Comment (2 Marks)**

The bandwidth of 10 kHz is very narrow compared to the resonant frequency of 1 MHz, indicating **high selectivity**. The circuit will strongly respond to frequencies near 1 MHz while rejecting signals outside the 995 kHz – 1005 kHz range. This is the **ideal characteristic for a radio tuner** designed to isolate a specific broadcast channel.

**Step 4: Voltage Magnification (2 Marks)**

Current at resonance:

$$I_0 = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{10}{10} = 1\ \text{A}$$

Voltage across the inductor:

$$V_L = I_0 \cdot X_L = 1 \times \omega_0 L = 2\pi \times 10^6 \times 1.5915 \times 10^{-4} \approx 1000\ \text{V}$$

$$V_L = Q \cdot V = 100 \times 10 = 1000\ \text{V} \quad (\text{confirmed})$$

**Comment:** The voltage across L (or C) is **100 times the source voltage**. This enormous voltage magnification is a hallmark of high-Q resonant circuits. While beneficial for signal amplification in tuned circuits, it can also cause **insulation breakdown** in power systems and must be carefully managed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B Common Pitfalls:**
> - In design problems, students often **assume a value of L or C arbitrarily** and derive the other. The correct approach: use the **two given constraints (f₀ and BW) to find Q first**, then derive L and C.
> - **Unit consistency** is critical. Mixing μH with nF or pF with H leads to wrong answers. Always use SI base units (H and F) for $f_0$ formula.
> - For voltage magnification, students must **state the formula $V_L = QV$ explicitly** to earn full marks, not just the numerical result.
> - Half-power frequencies must be reported in **consistent units** (both in Hz, or both in kHz).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Series Resonance (Module 2):**

- **Resonance condition:** $X_L = X_C$, leading to a **purely resistive** circuit.
- **Resonant frequency:** $f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ — **independent of R**.
- **At resonance:** Impedance $Z = R$ (minimum), Current $I = V/R$ (maximum), Power factor = 1.
- **Q-factor definition:** $Q = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$ — dimensionless, figure of merit.
- **Voltage magnification:** $V_L = V_C = Q \cdot V_{\text{source}}$ — can be dangerously high.
- **Bandwidth:** $BW = \frac{R}{2\pi L} = \frac{f_0}{Q}$ — narrower for higher Q.
- **Half-power frequencies:** $f_1 = f_0 - R/(4\pi L)$, $f_2 = f_0 + R/(4\pi L)$, with $f_0 = \sqrt{f_1 f_2}$.
- **Behaviour below f₀:** Capacitive, current leads voltage.
- **Behaviour above f₀:** Inductive, voltage leads current.
- **High Q** → narrow bandwidth → high selectivity (good for radio tuners).
- **Low Q** → wide bandwidth → broad frequency acceptance (good for audio circuits).
- **Geometric mean property:** $f_0^2 = f_1 \cdot f_2$ (useful for problem-solving).
- **Series resonance is also called "Acceptor Circuit"** because the circuit "accepts" maximum current at f₀.
- **Unit conversions** (mH → H, μF → F, kHz → Hz) must be performed **before** any numerical substitution.
- **Always verify** calculations using $BW = f_0/Q$ in addition to $BW = R/(2\pi L)$.
- **Applications to remember:** Radio tuning, band-pass filters, induction heating, oscillators, communication receivers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resonant Frequency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET302 - CIRCUITS & NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Resonant Frequency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **AC Circuit Analysis** under the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCEET302), **Resonant Frequency** (denoted $\omega_0$ in radians/second or $f_0$ in Hertz) is defined as the specific excitation frequency at which the **total reactive impedance** offered by a passive linear circuit becomes exactly zero. Equivalently, it is the frequency at which the inductive reactance $X_L$ and the capacitive reactance $X_C$ are numerically equal in magnitude but opposite in phase, causing them to cancel each other out.

Mathematically, the formal condition is:

$$\boxed{X_L = X_C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Must Memorize):**
> *"Resonance in an RLC circuit is a steady-state operating condition in which the natural oscillatory behavior of the circuit reinforces the applied sinusoidal signal, occurring precisely when the circuit's input impedance is purely resistive (zero net reactance)."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Pushed Swing (Mechanical Resonance)**
>
> Imagine a child sitting on a swing. The swing has a **natural period** (a preferred back-and-forth speed). If you push the swing randomly, it barely swings high. But if you push it **exactly once per natural cycle** (in rhythm), even tiny pushes build up massive amplitude. That rhythm is the **resonant frequency**.
>
> In an electrical circuit, the **inductor (L)** and **capacitor (C)** continuously exchange energy back and forth (the "swinging"). If the external AC source pumps energy at the exact rate the L and C prefer to exchange it, the response (current or voltage) becomes enormous compared to a non-resonant frequency. The **resistor (R)** is the "air drag" that eventually dissipates this energy.

## 1.3 Why Two Forms (Series vs. Parallel)?

Resonance manifests in two distinct topologies, each with opposite observable behavior:

| Topology | Behavior at $\omega_0$ | Why this matters for KTU |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Series RLC** | **Impedance is MINIMUM** ($Z = R$). Current is **MAXIMUM**. | Acts as a **Band-Pass Filter** (accepts $\omega_0$, rejects others). |
| **Parallel RLC** | **Impedance is MAXIMUM** ($Z = R$ ideally). Line current is **MINIMUM**. | Acts as a **Band-Stop / Notch Filter** (rejects $\omega_0$, accepts others). |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Physical Constant to Remember:**
> The standard angular frequency $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ is the conversion between the two domains. KTU problems are 50% in $f_0$ (Hz) and 50% in $\omega_0$ (rad/s) — **always check the units before substituting.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Universal Conditions for Resonance

Regardless of topology, resonance in any **second-order passive circuit** (containing L and C with no other reactive elements) reduces to one of two equivalent universal conditions:

1. **Reactance Cancellation Condition:** The imaginary part of the input impedance $Z(j\omega)$ equals zero.
   $$\text{Im}\{Z(j\omega_0)\} = 0$$

2. **Phase Coherence Condition:** The phase angle $\phi$ between the input voltage and current is exactly zero (voltage and current are in phase).
   $$\phi(\omega_0) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\text{Im}\{Z\}}{\text{Re}\{Z\}}\right) = 0$$

3. **Energy Equivalence Condition:** The average energy stored in the inductor equals the average energy stored in the capacitor over a complete cycle.
   $$W_L = W_C$$

## 2.2 Series RLC Resonance — Detailed Behavior

Consider a series R-L-C circuit driven by a sinusoidal source $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.

The input impedance is:
$$Z(j\omega) = R + j\omega L + \frac{1}{j\omega C} = R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

**Behavior Analysis at $\omega_0$:**
- The term $\left(\omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)$ becomes zero.
- $Z(j\omega_0) = R$ (purely real, minimum).
- Current magnitude reaches a **maximum** $I_{max} = \dfrac{V_s}{R}$.
- The voltages across L and C, although individually large (the "voltage magnification" effect, related to Q), are $180^\circ$ out of phase and cancel exactly.

## 2.3 Parallel RLC Resonance (Tank Circuit) — Detailed Behavior

Consider a practical parallel R-L-C circuit (using a practical inductor with internal resistance $R_L$, modeled either in series or parallel form).

For an **ideal lossless** parallel LC (pure parallel L and C):
$$Y(j\omega) = \frac{1}{j\omega L} + j\omega C = j\left(\omega C - \frac{1}{\omega L}\right)$$

**Behavior Analysis at $\omega_0$:**
- $Y(j\omega_0) = 0$ (theoretically).
- $Z(j\omega_0) \to \infty$ (open circuit at resonance).
- Line current $I_{line} \to 0$, but a large **circulating current** flows internally between L and C.

For the **practical parallel RLC** (R, L, C all in parallel):
- Admittance: $Y = \dfrac{1}{R} + j\left(\omega C - \dfrac{1}{\omega L}\right)$
- At $\omega_0$: $Y = \dfrac{1}{R}$ (purely real, minimum admittance $\Rightarrow$ maximum impedance $R$).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the master reference for KTU ESE questions. **Memorize every row.**

| Parameter | Symbol | Series RLC Expression | Parallel RLC Expression (R∥L∥C) | Units |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Resonant Angular Frequency | $\omega_0$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | rad/s |
| Resonant Frequency | $f_0$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Hz |
| Quality Factor | $Q$ | $\dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 R C}$ | $\dfrac{R}{\omega_0 L} = \omega_0 R C$ | dimensionless |
| Q in terms of L, C, R | $Q$ | $\dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | $R\sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}}$ | dimensionless |
| Bandwidth (rad/s) | $BW_\omega$ | $\dfrac{\omega_0}{Q} = \dfrac{R}{L}$ | $\dfrac{\omega_0}{Q} = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | rad/s |
| Bandwidth (Hz) | $BW_f$ | $\dfrac{f_0}{Q}$ | $\dfrac{f_0}{Q}$ | Hz |
| Lower Half-Power Freq. | $\omega_1$ or $f_1$ | $\omega_0 \cdot \left(-\dfrac{1}{2Q} + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{1}{4Q^2}}\right)$ | Same expression as series | rad/s or Hz |
| Upper Half-Power Freq. | $\omega_2$ or $f_2$ | $\omega_0 \cdot \left(+\dfrac{1}{2Q} + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{1}{4Q^2}}\right)$ | Same expression as series | rad/s or Hz |
| Selectivity | $S$ | Inversely proportional to BW: $S \propto \dfrac{1}{BW}$ | Inversely proportional to BW | — |
| Energy Stored (peak) | $W_{max}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2} L I_{peak}^2$ | $\dfrac{1}{2} C V_{peak}^2$ | Joules |
| Energy Dissipated / cycle | $W_{diss}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2} I_{peak}^2 R \cdot T$ | $\dfrac{1}{2} V_{peak}^2 G \cdot T$ | Joules |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where you will see resonant frequency in your career:**
> - **Radio & Communication:** Tuning a radio to a station (variable capacitor adjusts $\omega_0$ to match the broadcast).
> - **RF Filters:** Cellular base stations, Wi-Fi routers, satellite receivers.
> - **Medical:** MRI machines use precise RF resonance of hydrogen atoms.
> - **Power Systems:** Avoiding resonance in transmission lines to prevent catastrophic overvoltages (Ferranti effect mitigation).
> - **Audio Engineering:** Crossover networks in loudspeakers.
> - **Sensors:** Quartz crystal oscillators in watches and microcontrollers operate on mechanical resonance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Universal Resonant Frequency $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$

We begin with the generic series RLC impedance. The derivation below is valid for any RLC topology because the result is independent of $R$.

**Step 1 — Write the input impedance of a series RLC circuit.**
The series combination gives the total impedance as the sum of the individual impedances:
$$Z(j\omega) = R + Z_L + Z_C$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the ideal complex impedances of L and C.**
For an inductor, $Z_L = j\omega L$. For a capacitor, $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$. Substituting:
$$Z(j\omega) = R + j\omega L + \frac{1}{j\omega C}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the resonance condition (Im{Z} = 0).**
The real part is $R$. The imaginary part is $\left(\omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)$. Setting the imaginary part to zero:
$$\omega_0 L - \frac{1}{\omega_0 C} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Algebraically isolate $\omega_0$.**
Move the second term to the right-hand side:
$$\omega_0 L = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply both sides by $\omega_0$.**
$$\omega_0^2 L = \frac{1}{C}$$

**Step 6 — Divide both sides by $L$.**
$$\omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{LC}$$

**Step 7 — Take the positive square root (since $\omega$ is positive by convention).**
$$\boxed{\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}}$$

**Step 8 — Convert from angular frequency to ordinary frequency.**
Since $\omega = 2\pi f$, we substitute $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ into the boxed equation:
$$2\pi f_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Step 9 — Solve for $f_0$.**
$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Quality Factor $Q$ via the Energy Method

The Quality Factor is fundamentally a measure of "how sharply tuned" a resonator is. The most physically intuitive derivation uses the energy perspective.

**Step 1 — Define Q universally.**
The Quality Factor is defined as $2\pi$ times the ratio of the total energy stored in the reactive elements to the energy dissipated per radian of the oscillation cycle:
$$Q = 2\pi \cdot \frac{W_{stored}}{W_{dissipated, per \, cycle}} = \omega \cdot \frac{W_{stored}}{P_{avg}}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the energy stored at resonance.**
At resonance, the peak current is $I_m = \dfrac{V_s}{R}$. The energy stored in the inductor at its peak is:
$$W_L = \frac{1}{2} L I_m^2$$

**Step 3 — Identify the energy stored in the capacitor at the same instant.**
At resonance, the voltage across the capacitor is $V_C = I_m \cdot \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 C}$. The energy is:
$$W_C = \frac{1}{2} C V_C^2 = \frac{1}{2} C \left(\frac{I_m}{\omega_0 C}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2} \frac{I_m^2}{\omega_0^2 C}$$

**Step 4 — Use the resonance identity to simplify $W_C$.**
From $\omega_0^2 = \dfrac{1}{LC}$, we get $\dfrac{1}{\omega_0^2 C} = L$. Substituting:
$$W_C = \frac{1}{2} L I_m^2 = W_L$$

This confirms that **at resonance, the energy oscillates equally between L and C** (this is a KTU-favorite conceptual question).

**Step 5 — Sum the total stored energy (assuming the peak occurs at the same instant, the total energy is the sum).**
$$W_{stored} = W_L + W_C = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} L I_m^2 = L I_m^2$$

**Step 6 — Compute the average power dissipated by the resistor.**
$$P_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} I_m^2 R$$

**Step 7 — Substitute into the Q definition.**
$$Q = \omega_0 \cdot \frac{W_{stored}}{P_{avg}} = \omega_0 \cdot \frac{L I_m^2}{\frac{1}{2} I_m^2 R} = \omega_0 \cdot \frac{2L}{R}$$

**Step 8 — Simplify using $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$.**
$$Q = \frac{2L}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{2\sqrt{L}}{\sqrt{C} \cdot R} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

**Step 9 — Final boxed forms (Series RLC).**
$$\boxed{Q_{series} = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{1}{\omega_0 R C} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mirror Derivation for Parallel RLC:**
> By replacing $V \leftrightarrow I$ and swapping $R \leftrightarrow G = 1/R$, $L \leftrightarrow C$:
> $$\boxed{Q_{parallel} = \frac{R}{\omega_0 L} = \omega_0 R C = R\sqrt{\frac{C}{L}}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bandwidth and Half-Power Frequencies

**Step 1 — Define the transfer function for a series RLC.**
Take the current as the output, and the source voltage as the input:
$$H(j\omega) = \frac{I}{V_s} = \frac{1}{R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the magnitude squared.**
$$|H(j\omega)|^2 = \frac{1}{R^2 + \left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Define "half-power" condition.**
The half-power frequencies are where $|H|^2$ drops to half of its maximum value. The maximum of $|H|^2$ occurs at $\omega = \omega_0$ (where the imaginary part vanishes), and equals:
$$|H(j\omega_0)|^2 = \frac{1}{R^2}$$

**Step 4 — Set up the half-power equation.**
At the half-power frequencies $\omega_{1,2}$:
$$\frac{1}{R^2 + \left(\omega_{1,2} L - \frac{1}{\omega_{1,2} C}\right)^2} = \frac{1}{2 R^2}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-multiply and simplify.**
$$R^2 + \left(\omega_{1,2} L - \frac{1}{\omega_{1,2} C}\right)^2 = 2R^2$$
$$\left(\omega_{1,2} L - \frac{1}{\omega_{1,2} C}\right)^2 = R^2$$

**Step 6 — Take the square root (two solutions, ±).**
$$\omega_{1,2} L - \frac{1}{\omega_{1,2} C} = \pm R$$

**Step 7 — Multiply through by $\omega_{1,2}$ to form a quadratic.**
$$\omega_{1,2}^2 L \mp R \omega_{1,2} - \frac{1}{C} = 0$$

**Step 8 — Solve the quadratic in $\omega$.**
Using the quadratic formula $\omega = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$ with $a = L$, $b = \mp R$, $c = -\dfrac{1}{C}$:

For the **upper** half-power frequency $\omega_2$ (take the $+$ sign for $b$):
$$\omega_2 = \frac{R + \sqrt{R^2 + \dfrac{4L}{C}}}{2L} = \frac{R}{2L} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{LC}}$$

For the **lower** half-power frequency $\omega_1$ (take the $-$ sign for $b$):
$$\omega_1 = -\frac{R}{2L} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{LC}}$$

**Step 9 — Express in compact form using $\omega_0$ and $Q$.**
Recall $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ and $\dfrac{R}{2L} = \dfrac{\omega_0}{2Q}$. Substituting:
$$\boxed{\omega_{1,2} = \omega_0 \left( \mp \frac{1}{2Q} + \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{4Q^2}} \right)}$$

**Step 10 — Derive the bandwidth $BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1$.**
Subtracting the two frequencies (the radicals cancel):
$$BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\omega_0}{2Q} = \frac{\omega_0}{Q}$$

Using $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R}$ for series:
$$\boxed{BW_{series} = \frac{\omega_0}{Q} = \frac{R}{L}}$$

Using $Q = \omega_0 R C$ for parallel:
$$\boxed{BW_{parallel} = \frac{1}{RC}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What

> [!TIP]
> **Quick-Reference Decision Table for Problem-Solving:**

| If the question gives you... | Compute first... | Then derive... |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $L$ and $C$ | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | $f_0$, $Q$, then $BW$ |
| $f_0$ and $BW$ | $Q = \dfrac{f_0}{BW}$ | $L/C$ ratio, then individual values |
| $Q$ and $L$, with $R$ | Use $Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ to find $C$ | Then $\omega_0$, $BW$ |
| Half-power $\omega_1, \omega_2$ | $\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_1 \omega_2}$ (geometric mean) | $BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1$ |
| Energy stored $W$ and power $P$ | $Q = \omega_0 \dfrac{W}{P}$ | Combine with $L$, $C$ relations |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map: Classification of Resonance in Linear Circuits

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Resonance in Linear Circuits"]:::root
    A --> B["Series RLC Resonance"]
    A --> C["Parallel RLC Resonance"]
    A --> D["Other Topologies"]
    B --> B1["Z minimum equals R"]
    B --> B2["Current is maximum"]
    B --> B3["Band-Pass Filter Behavior"]
    B --> B4["Q equals w0L over R"]
    C --> C1["Z maximum equals R ideal"]
    C --> C2["Current is minimum"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term 'Resonance' in an RLC circuit. State the condition for resonance and obtain the expression for resonant frequency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Resonance in an RLC circuit is the phenomenon in which the circuit's input impedance becomes purely resistive due to the cancellation of the inductive and capacitive reactances, resulting in the source voltage and current being in phase.

**Condition (1 mark):** The condition is $X_L = X_C$, i.e., $\omega L = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$.

**Derivation (1 mark):** Equating the reactances:
$$\omega_0 L = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C} \implies \omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{LC} \implies \boxed{\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \text{ rad/s}}$$

Or in Hertz: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ Hz.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is meant by 'Quality Factor' of a resonant circuit? State its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Quality Factor $Q$ of a resonant circuit is defined as the ratio of the resonant frequency to the bandwidth of the circuit, i.e., $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0}{BW}$. It is a dimensionless quantity.

**Alternative Definition (1 mark):** It is also defined as $2\pi$ times the ratio of the total energy stored in the reactive elements to the energy dissipated per cycle:
$$Q = 2\pi \frac{W_{stored}}{W_{dissipated}}$$

**Significance (1 mark):** $Q$ is a measure of the **selectivity** (sharpness) of the resonant circuit. A higher $Q$ indicates a narrower bandwidth, more selective filtering, and lower energy loss per cycle. A lower $Q$ indicates a broader response and higher loss.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B questions in this module carry 14 marks with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. You must answer **either** the full Question A **or** the full Question B.

---

### 🔵 QUESTION A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**A series RLC circuit has $R = 10 \, \Omega$, $L = 100 \, \text{mH}$, and $C = 10 \, \mu\text{F}$.**

**(a) Calculate the resonant frequency $f_0$, the Quality Factor $Q$, and the Bandwidth $BW$ of the circuit. (7 marks)**

**(b) Find the lower and upper half-power frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$, and the magnitude of the current at $f_0$ if the source voltage is $V_s = 50 \angle 0^\circ \, \text{V}$ (RMS). (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Compute $\omega_0$ (2 marks).**
Using $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$:
$$LC = (100 \times 10^{-3}) \times (10 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-6} \, \text{s}^2$$
$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10^{-6}}} = 1000 \, \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Convert to $f_0$ (1 mark).**
$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{1000}{2\pi} \approx 159.15 \, \text{Hz}$$

**Step 3: Compute $Q$ (2 marks).**
$$Q = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \frac{1000 \times 100 \times 10^{-3}}{10} = \frac{100}{10} = 10$$
(Or equivalently, $Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} = \dfrac{1}{10}\sqrt{\dfrac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{-6}}} = \dfrac{1}{10}\sqrt{10^4} = 10$.)

**Step 4: Compute $BW$ (2 marks).**
$$BW = \frac{\omega_0}{Q} = \frac{1000}{10} = 100 \, \text{rad/s}$$
$$BW_f = \frac{f_0}{Q} = \frac{159.15}{10} \approx 15.915 \, \text{Hz}$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Compute the half-power frequencies in Hz (3 marks).**
Using the standard formula:
$$f_{1,2} = f_0 \left( \mp \frac{1}{2Q} + \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{4Q^2}} \right)$$
$$\frac{1}{2Q} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05, \quad \frac{1}{4Q^2} = \frac{1}{400} = 0.0025$$
$$\sqrt{1.0025} \approx 1.00125$$
$$f_1 = 159.15 \times (-0.05 + 1.00125) = 159.15 \times 0.95125 \approx 151.40 \, \text{Hz}$$
$$f_2 = 159.15 \times (0.05 + 1.00125) = 159.15 \times 1.05125 \approx 167.30 \, \text{Hz}$$

**Verification:** $f_2 - f_1 \approx 15.90 \, \text{Hz} \approx BW_f$ ✓

**Step 2: Compute the current magnitude at resonance (2 marks).**
At resonance, $Z = R = 10 \, \Omega$ (purely real, minimum).
$$|I| = \frac{|V_s|}{|Z|} = \frac{50}{10} = 5 \, \text{A (RMS)}$$

**Step 3: Verify bandwidth relation (2 marks).**
$$BW_f = f_2 - f_1 = 167.30 - 151.40 = 15.90 \, \text{Hz}$$
Matches $f_0 / Q = 159.15 / 10 = 15.915 \, \text{Hz}$ ✓ (small rounding difference).

**Final Answer Box:**
- $f_0 = 159.15 \, \text{Hz}$
- $Q = 10$
- $BW = 15.92 \, \text{Hz}$ (or $100 \, \text{rad/s}$)
- $f_1 \approx 151.4 \, \text{Hz}$, $f_2 \approx 167.3 \, \text{Hz}$
- $|I|_{f_0} = 5 \, \text{A}$

---

### 🟢 QUESTION B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - June 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the Quality Factor of a series RLC circuit using the energy stored and energy dissipated concept. (7 marks)**

**(b) A practical parallel resonant circuit consists of a coil of inductance $L = 200 \, \mu\text{H}$ with internal resistance $R_L = 20 \, \Omega$ (in series with L), in parallel with a capacitor $C = 100 \, \text{pF}$. Calculate the resonant frequency, the Quality Factor, and the dynamic impedance at resonance. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:** *(Energy method derivation, fully shown in Section 3.2 above)*

Allocate marks as:
- (1 mark) Writing the definition $Q = 2\pi \dfrac{W_{stored}}{W_{dissipated}}$
- (2 marks) Expressing $W_L = \dfrac{1}{2} L I_m^2$ and $W_C = \dfrac{1}{2} L I_m^2$ (showing equality at resonance)
- (1 mark) Summing: $W_{stored} = L I_m^2$
- (1 mark) Writing $P_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{2} I_m^2 R$
- (1 mark) Substituting and simplifying to get $Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$
- (1 mark) Final boxed answer with all three forms: $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 R C} = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Identify the topology.**
This is a series-to-parallel conversion problem. The coil has $L = 200 \, \mu\text{H}$ in series with $R_L = 20 \, \Omega$. Convert this to an equivalent **parallel** $R_p \, \| \, L_p$ representation.

For a series $R_L$–$L$ branch at frequency $\omega$, the equivalent parallel $R_p$ is:
$$R_p = R_L \left( 1 + Q_{coil}^2 \right) \approx R_L Q_{coil}^2 \quad \text{(for high } Q \text{)}$$

**Step 2: Estimate the resonant frequency first (1 mark).**
Using the approximate (lossless) formula:
$$\omega_0 \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(200 \times 10^{-6})(100 \times 10^{-12})}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \times 10^{-14}}}$$
$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{1.414 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 7.071 \times 10^{6} \, \text{rad/s}$$
$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} \approx 1.125 \, \text{MHz}$$

**Step 3: Compute the coil's intrinsic $Q_{coil}$ (2 marks).**
$$Q_{coil} = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R_L} = \frac{7.071 \times 10^{6} \times 200 \times 10^{-6}}{20} = \frac{1414.2}{20} \approx 70.71$$

**Step 4: Compute the equivalent parallel resistance (2 marks).**
$$R_p = R_L (1 + Q_{coil}^2) = 20 \times (1 + 5000) = 20 \times 5001 = 100{,}020 \, \Omega \approx 100 \, \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 5: Compute the Quality Factor of the parallel tank (1 mark).**
For an ideal parallel RLC, $Q = R_p \sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}}$:
$$Q = 100{,}020 \times \sqrt{\frac{100 \times 10^{-12}}{200 \times 10^{-6}}} = 100{,}020 \times \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-7}}$$
$$Q = 100{,}020 \times 7.071 \times 10^{-4} \approx 70.72$$

This matches $Q_{coil}$ as expected (in a high-Q practical tank, the overall Q is dominated by the coil).

**Step 6: State the dynamic impedance (1 mark).**
The dynamic impedance at resonance for a parallel RLC equals the parallel equivalent resistance:
$$Z_{dynamic} = R_p \approx 100 \, \text{k}\Omega$$

**Final Answer Box:**
- $f_0 \approx 1.125 \, \text{MHz}$
- $Q \approx 70.7$
- $Z_{dynamic} = R_p \approx 100 \, \text{k}\Omega$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause 2-3 Mark Deductions in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up $f_0$ and $\omega_0$:** The most common error. The board often gives one and asks for the other. Always write both explicitly: "$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ Hz, $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ rad/s." If the question gives $f_0 = 1 \, \text{kHz}$ and asks for $L$ with $C = 1 \, \mu\text{F}$, you MUST first convert to $\omega_0 = 2\pi \times 1000 = 6283$ rad/s.
>
> 2. **Confusing Series and Parallel Q formulas:** Series $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R}$, Parallel $Q = \dfrac{R}{\omega_0 L}$. They are reciprocals in the $L$-$R$ sense! Many students write the series formula for a parallel circuit and lose 3 marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the unit in the final answer:** KTU examiners explicitly mark "unit" as part of the answer. Always write "Hz" for $f$, "rad/s" for $\omega$, and "dimensionless" or just "$Q$" for the quality factor.
>
> 4. **Not writing the resonance condition explicitly:** Before any derivation, write $X_L = X_C$ as the first line. It earns a separate 1 mark "starting point" credit.
>
> 5. **Wrong sign in the half-power formula:** The lower frequency $f_1$ uses the "minus" sign inside the bracket: $f_0\left(-\dfrac{1}{2Q} + \sqrt{\cdots}\right)$. Reversing the sign is a common slip-up.
>
> 6. **Practical vs. Ideal Parallel Resonance:** If a coil has internal resistance, the parallel-resonant impedance is **not** infinite. You must convert the series $R_L$–$L$ to parallel $R_p$–$L_p$ first.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **Core Resonance Condition:** $X_L = X_C \implies \omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ rad/s, $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ Hz. **Independent of $R$!**
- **Series RLC at $\omega_0$:** $Z_{min} = R$, $I_{max} = V_s/R$, acts as **Band-Pass Filter**.
- **Parallel RLC at $\omega_0$:** $Z_{max} = R$ (ideal), $I_{min} = V_s/R$, acts as **Band-Stop Filter**.
- **Quality Factor (Series):** $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 R C} = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$.
- **Quality Factor (Parallel):** $Q = \dfrac{R}{\omega_0 L} = \omega_0 R C = R\sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}}$.
- **Bandwidth:** $BW = \dfrac{\omega_0}{Q} = \omega_2 - \omega_1$ rad/s (or $f_0/Q$ in Hz).
- **Half-Power Frequencies:** $\omega_{1,2} = \omega_0 \left(\mp \dfrac{1}{2Q} + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{1}{4Q^2}}\right)$.
- **Geometric Mean Property:** $\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_1 \omega_2}$ — useful for KTU verification.
- **Selectivity:** Higher $Q$ → Narrower $BW$ → Sharper peak → More selective filter.
- **Energy at Resonance:** $W_L = W_C$ (continuous exchange), $W_{total} = 2W_L = 2W_C$.
- **Practical Parallel Coil Conversion:** $R_p = R_L(1+Q^2)$, $L_p = L \left(\dfrac{1+Q^2}{Q^2}\right) \approx L$ for high Q.
- **Voltage Magnification (Series):** $|V_L| = |V_C| = Q \cdot V_s$ at resonance (can damage components if not accounted for!).
- **Unit Hygiene:** Always distinguish $\omega$ (rad/s) from $f$ (Hz). Use $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$.
- **Higher-Order Circuits:** Resonance condition extends to $\text{Im}\{Z(j\omega)\} = 0$; multiple resonant frequencies can exist in circuits with multiple L and C elements.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variations of Impedance and Current with Frequency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET302 - CIRCUITS & NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Variations of Impedance and Current with Frequency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In alternating current (AC) circuit analysis, **impedance (Z)** is the total opposition offered by a circuit to the flow of sinusoidal alternating current. It is the phasor-domain generalization of resistance and is a complex quantity that depends on the operating **frequency (f)** of the applied source.

Mathematically, impedance is expressed in rectangular form as:

$$Z = R + jX$$

where the **real part R** represents the resistance (frequency-independent) and the **imaginary part X** represents the net **reactance** of the circuit, given by:

$$X = X_L - X_C = \omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}$$

Here, $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency in **radians per second (rad/s)**, $L$ is inductance in **henries (H)**, and $C$ is capacitance in **farads (F)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PCEET302 requires a thorough understanding of how **Z** and **I** vary with frequency for **pure R, L, C elements** as well as **series RL, RC, and RLC combinations**. The current magnitude $\vert I \vert$ is given by Ohm's law for AC: $I = V/Z$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are pushing a heavy cart on a rough, bumpy road:

- **A pure Resistor (R)** behaves like **friction on the wheels** — it always opposes motion equally, whether you push slowly or fast. So, $R$ does not change with frequency.
- **A pure Inductor (L)** behaves like a **heavy flywheel** — when you try to push the cart faster (high frequency), the flywheel's inertia strongly resists the rapid change. Hence, **inductive reactance $X_L$ increases with frequency**.
- **A pure Capacitor (C)** behaves like a **spring-damper cushion** — at very low frequencies (slow push), the spring barely compresses and offers huge opposition; at high frequencies (rapid push), the spring simply yields easily. Hence, **capacitive reactance $X_C$ decreases with frequency**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** A resistor is *frequency-blind*, an inductor is a *frequency-blocker* (high frequencies blocked), and a capacitor is a *frequency-passer* (high frequencies pass easily, low frequencies blocked).

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | Frequency Dependence |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| $f$ | Frequency | **Hertz (Hz)** | Independent variable |
| $\omega$ | Angular Frequency | **rad/s** | $\omega = 2\pi f$ |
| $X_L$ | Inductive Reactance | **Ohm (Ω)** | $X_L = \omega L$ |
| $X_C$ | Capacitive Reactance | **Ohm (Ω)** | $X_C = \frac{1}{\omega C}$ |
| $Z$ | Impedance | **Ohm (Ω)** | $Z = R + jX$ |
| $I$ | Current | **Ampere (A)** | $I = \frac{V}{Z}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Element-by-Element Frequency Response

### (a) Pure Resistance (R)
- Impedance: $Z_R = R$ (a real constant, **independent of frequency**).
- Phase angle: $\phi = 0°$ (voltage and current are in phase at *all* frequencies).
- Current: $I_R = \dfrac{V}{R}$ (constant at all frequencies).

### (b) Pure Inductance (L)
- Impedance: $Z_L = j\omega L = j X_L$.
- Magnitude: $\vert Z_L \vert = \omega L = 2\pi f L$ → **increases linearly with f**.
- Phase angle: $\phi = +90°$ (current **lags** voltage by 90°).
- Current: $\vert I_L \vert = \dfrac{V}{\omega L} = \dfrac{V}{2\pi f L}$ → **decreases with f** (inversely proportional).

### (c) Pure Capacitance (C)
- Impedance: $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = -\dfrac{j}{\omega C}$.
- Magnitude: $\vert Z_C \vert = \dfrac{1}{\omega C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ → **decreases hyperbolically with f**.
- Phase angle: $\phi = -90°$ (current **leads** voltage by 90°).
- Current: $\vert I_C \vert = V \omega C = 2\pi f C V$ → **increases linearly with f**.

## 2.2 Frequency Behaviour of Composite Series Circuits

### RL Series Circuit
The total impedance is the vector sum of $R$ and $X_L$:

$$Z_{RL} = R + j\omega L$$

- Magnitude: $\vert Z_{RL} \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2}$
- Phase: $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\omega L}{R}\right)$, which lies between $0°$ and $+90°$.
- Current: $\vert I \vert = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2}}$

As $f \to 0$: $\vert Z \vert \to R$, $I \to V/R$, $\phi \to 0°$ (resistive behaviour).
As $f \to \infty$: $\vert Z \vert \to \infty$, $I \to 0$, $\phi \to +90°$ (inductive behaviour).

### RC Series Circuit
$$Z_{RC} = R - \dfrac{j}{\omega C}$$

- Magnitude: $\vert Z_{RC} \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + \left(\dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2}$
- Phase: $\phi = -\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{\omega R C}\right)$, between $0°$ and $-90°$.
- Current: $\vert I \vert = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{R^2 + \left(\dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2}}$

As $f \to 0$: $\vert Z \vert \to \infty$, $I \to 0$ (capacitor blocks DC), $\phi \to -90°$.
As $f \to \infty$: $\vert Z \vert \to R$, $I \to V/R$, $\phi \to 0°$ (capacitor becomes short).

### RLC Series Circuit
$$Z_{RLC} = R + j\left(\omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

- Magnitude: $\vert Z_{RLC} \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + \left(\omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2}$
- Phase: $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}}{R}\right)$

The phase angle is:
- **Positive** when $\omega L > \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$ → circuit is **inductive**.
- **Negative** when $\omega L < \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$ → circuit is **capacitive**.
- **Zero** when $\omega L = \dfrac{1}{\omega C}$ → **Resonance**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Circuit / Quantity | Impedance Magnitude $\vert Z \vert$ | Current Magnitude $\vert I \vert$ | Phase Angle $\phi$ | Behaviour at $f = 0$ | Behaviour at $f \to \infty$ |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Pure R | $R$ | $V/R$ | $0°$ | $Z = R$ | $Z = R$ |
| 2 | Pure L | $\omega L$ | $V/(\omega L)$ | $+90°$ | $Z = 0$ (short) | $Z = \infty$ (open) |
| 3 | Pure C | $1/(\omega C)$ | $V \omega C$ | $-90°$ | $Z = \infty$ (open) | $Z = 0$ (short) |
| 4 | RL Series | $\sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2}$ | $V/\sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2}$ | $\tan^{-1}(\omega L/R)$ | $\vert Z \vert = R$ | $\vert Z \vert \to \infty$ |
| 5 | RC Series | $\sqrt{R^2 + (1/(\omega C))^2}$ | $V/\sqrt{R^2 + (1/(\omega C))^2}$ | $-\tan^{-1}(1/(\omega R C))$ | $\vert Z \vert \to \infty$ | $\vert Z \vert = R$ |
| 6 | RLC Series | $\sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L - 1/(\omega C))^2}$ | $V/\sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L - 1/(\omega C))^2}$ | $\tan^{-1}((\omega L - 1/(\omega C))/R)$ | $\vert Z \vert \to \infty$ (Cap) | $\vert Z \vert \to \infty$ (Ind) |
| 7 | **Resonance Frequency** | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | $0°$ | — | — |
| 8 | **Q-Factor (Series)** | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 R C}$ | — | — | — | — |
| 9 | **Bandwidth (Series)** | $BW = \dfrac{R}{2\pi L} = \dfrac{f_0}{Q}$ | — | — | — | — |
| 10 | **Half-Power Frequencies** | $f_1 = f_0 \cdot \dfrac{-1 + \sqrt{1+4Q^2}}{2Q}$, $f_2 = f_0 \cdot \dfrac{1 + \sqrt{1+4Q^2}}{2Q}$ | $I = I_{max}/\sqrt{2}$ | $\pm 45°$ | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The frequency-dependent behaviour of RLC circuits forms the bedrock of **tuned radio receivers**, **band-pass and band-stop filters**, **equalizers in audio systems**, **oscillators**, and **wireless power transfer systems**. A radio tuner, for example, uses an RLC resonant circuit to select a single broadcast frequency while rejecting all others.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Impedance and Current of a Series RL Circuit with Frequency

**Problem Setup:** A resistor $R$ and inductor $L$ are connected in series across a sinusoidal source $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.

**Step 1 — Phasor Representation of Source**

The voltage phasor is $\mathbf{V} = V \angle 0°$ (taking source as reference).

**Step 2 — Impedances in Phasor Form**

The impedance of the resistor is purely real:

$$Z_R = R$$

The impedance of the inductor is purely imaginary (positive):

$$Z_L = j\omega L$$

**Step 3 — Total Series Impedance**

Since the elements are in series, impedances add directly in the complex domain:

$$Z_{RL} = Z_R + Z_L = R + j\omega L$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of Impedance**

Convert from rectangular to polar form. The magnitude is the hypotenuse of the impedance triangle:

$$\vert Z_{RL} \vert = \sqrt{(\text{Real Part})^2 + (\text{Imaginary Part})^2}$$

Substituting the real part $R$ and imaginary part $\omega L$:

$$\vert Z_{RL} \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Phase Angle of Impedance**

The phase angle is the angle of the impedance phasor measured from the positive real axis:

$$\phi_{RL} = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\text{Imaginary Part}}{\text{Real Part}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\omega L}{R}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Current Phasor (Application of Ohm's Law in AC)**

$$\mathbf{I} = \dfrac{\mathbf{V}}{Z_{RL}} = \dfrac{V \angle 0°}{\sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2} \angle \tan^{-1}(\omega L/R)}$$

Applying the rule of phasor division (numerator magnitude divided by denominator magnitude, numerator angle minus denominator angle):

$$\mathbf{I} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L)^2}} \angle -\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\omega L}{R}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Frequency Extremes**

At $f = 0$ (DC, $\omega = 0$): $\vert Z_{RL} \vert = R$ and $\phi = 0°$. The inductor behaves as a short circuit.

At $f \to \infty$ ($\omega \to \infty$): $\vert Z_{RL} \vert \to \infty$ and $\phi \to 90°$. The inductor behaves as an open circuit.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Impedance and Current of a Series RC Circuit

**Step 1 — Impedances in Phasor Form**

$$Z_R = R, \qquad Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = \dfrac{-j}{\omega C}$$

**Step 2 — Total Series Impedance**

$$Z_{RC} = R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = R - \dfrac{j}{\omega C}$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude of Impedance**

$$\vert Z_{RC} \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + \left(\dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Phase Angle**

The imaginary part is negative, so the phase angle is **negative** (capacitive circuit):

$$\phi_{RC} = -\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{\omega R C}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Current Phasor**

$$\mathbf{I} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{R^2 + (1/(\omega C))^2}} \angle +\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{\omega R C}\right)$$

Note the **positive** sign on the phase of $\mathbf{I}$ (current **leads** voltage in a capacitive circuit).

**Step 6 — Frequency Extremes**

At $f = 0$ ($\omega = 0$): $\vert Z_{RC} \vert \to \infty$, $I \to 0$. The capacitor blocks DC.

At $f \to \infty$ ($\omega \to \infty$): $\vert Z_{RC} \vert \to R$, $I \to V/R$. The capacitor becomes a short circuit.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Series RLC Resonance Condition

**Step 1 — Total Impedance**

$$Z_{RLC} = R + j\left(\omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Definition of Resonance**

Resonance occurs when the **net reactance is zero**, i.e., the imaginary part of $Z$ vanishes:

$$X = \omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solving for Resonance Frequency**

Equating the inductive and capacitive reactances:

$$\omega_0 L = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 C}$$

Multiplying both sides by $\omega_0$:

$$\omega_0^2 L = \dfrac{1}{C}$$

Dividing both sides by $L$:

$$\omega_0^2 = \dfrac{1}{LC}$$

Taking the positive square root (since frequency is positive):

$$\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

Converting to ordinary frequency using $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$:

$$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Step 4 — Behaviour at Resonance**

Substituting $\omega_0$ back into the impedance magnitude:

$$\vert Z_{RLC} \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + 0^2} = R \quad \text{(minimum possible)}$$

Therefore the current is **maximum**:

$$\vert I_{max} \vert = \dfrac{V}{R}$$

The phase angle at resonance is **zero** — the circuit behaves *purely resistively* and the source voltage and current are in phase.

**Step 5 — Quality Factor (Q-Factor) Derivation**

The Q-factor is defined as the ratio of reactive power to real power at resonance, or equivalently:

$$Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R}$$

Using $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$, we can rewrite $L$ in terms of $\omega_0$ and $C$:

$$L = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0^2 C}$$

Substituting into the Q-factor:

$$Q = \dfrac{\omega_0}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\omega_0^2 C} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 R C}$$

Alternatively, multiplying numerator and denominator by $\sqrt{L/C}$:

$$Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$$

**Step 6 — Bandwidth and Selectivity**

The bandwidth $BW$ is the difference between the two half-power frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$, where the current falls to $1/\sqrt{2}$ of $I_{max}$. It is given by:

$$BW = f_2 - f_1 = \dfrac{R}{2\pi L} = \dfrac{f_0}{Q}$$

A **high Q-factor** implies a **narrow bandwidth** and a **highly selective** circuit (sharp resonance peak). This is critical in radio tuning.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Frequency Response Across Circuits

| Circuit | $f \to 0$ $\vert Z \vert$ | $f \to 0$ Phase | $f \to \infty$ $\vert Z \vert$ | $f \to \infty$ Phase | At Resonance |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| R | $R$ | $0°$ | $R$ | $0°$ | — |
| L | $0$ | $+90°$ | $\infty$ | $+90°$ | — |
| C | $\infty$ | $-90°$ | $0$ | $-90°$ | — |
| RL | $R$ | $0°$ | $\infty$ | $+90°$ | — |
| RC | $\infty$ | $-90°$ | $R$ | $0°$ | — |
| RLC | $\infty$ | $-90°$ | $\infty$ | $+90°$ | $\vert Z \vert = R$, $\phi = 0°$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Frequency-Response Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: AC Circuit Analysis] --> B{Type of Circuit?}
    B -- Pure R --> C1[Z = R constant]
    B -- Pure L --> C2[Z = j wL, increases with f]
    B -- Pure C --> C3[Z = -j by wC, decreases with f]
    B -- RL Series --> D1[Z = R + j wL]
    B -- RC Series --> D2[Z = R - j by wC]
    B -- RLC Series --> D3[Z = R + j times bracket wL - 1 by wC bracket]
    D1 --> E1[Phase phi = tan inv wL by R, between 0 and +90 deg]
    D2 --> E2[Phase phi = -tan inv 1 by wRC, between 0 and -90 deg]
    D3 --> F{Is wL = 1 by wC ?}
    F -- Yes --> G1[RESONANCE: f0 = 1 by 2pi root LC]
    F -- No --> G2[Net reactance is not zero]
    G1 --> H1[Z minimum = R, I maximum = V by R]
    G1 --> H2[Phase phi = 0 deg, purely resistive]
    G1 --> H3[Q = w0L by R = 1 by w0RC]
    G1 --> H4[Bandwidth BW = f0 by Q = R by 2piL]
    G2 --> I1[If wL greater: Circuit Inductive, phi positive]
    G2 --> I2[If wL less: Circuit Capacitive, phi negative]
    C1 --> J1[I = V by R, constant]
    C2 --> J2[I = V by wL, decreases with f]
    C3 --> J3[I = V wC, increases with f]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of an RLC Resonant System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SrcBlock[AC Source V sin wt] --> ResBlock[Resistor R]
    ResBlock --> IndBlock[Inductor L]
    IndBlock --> CapBlock[Capacitor C]
    CapBlock --> GndBlock[Return to Source]
    IndBlock -.-> FreqSense1[XL = wL increases linearly with f]
    CapBlock -.-> FreqSense2[XC = 1 by wC decreases hyperbolically with f]
    FreqSense1 --> CompareBlock[Comparator: XL versus XC]
    FreqSense2 --> CompareBlock
    CompareBlock --> ResonanceOut{Resonance at f0?}
    ResonanceOut -- YES --> PeakDetect[Current Peak: I_max = V by R]
    ResonanceOut -- NO --> PhaseOut[Net Phase phi = tan inv bracket XL minus XC by R bracket]
    PeakDetect --> FilterOut[Narrowband Filter Behavior, BW = f0 by Q]
    PhaseOut --> FilterOut
```

## 4.3 Description of Frequency-Response Curves (Conceptual Plot)

Although physical plots are not rendered, the following conceptual description captures the canonical shape of the curves a student must memorise:

> **Current $\vert I \vert$ vs Frequency $f$ for Series RLC:**
> - Starts at **zero** when $f = 0$ (because $X_C \to \infty$).
> - **Rises monotonically** as $f$ increases.
> - Reaches a **sharp maximum** $I_{max} = V/R$ at $f = f_0$ (resonance).
> - **Falls monotonically** for $f > f_0$ (because $X_L \to \infty$).
> - The **higher the Q-factor**, the **narrower and sharper** the peak.

> **Impedance $\vert Z \vert$ vs Frequency $f$ for Series RLC:**
> - Starts at **infinity** when $f = 0$.
> - **Decreases** to a **minimum** of $R$ at $f = f_0$.
> - **Rises again** to **infinity** as $f \to \infty$.
> - The curve is **U-shaped** (or V-shaped) with the trough at $f_0$.

> **Phase Angle $\phi$ vs Frequency $f$ for Series RLC:**
> - Starts at $-90°$ at $f = 0$ (capacitive).
> - **Crosses zero** at $f = f_0$ (resistive).
> - Approaches $+90°$ as $f \to \infty$ (inductive).
