The product of roots equals $c/a = -1/(LC)$... but the *physical* $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ are roots of *different* quadratics. Direct verification with computed values: $$\omega_1 \cdot \omega_2 = 987.58 \times 1012.58 \approx 1{,}000{,}000\ \text{(rad/s)}^2$$ $$\frac{1}{LC} = 10^6\ \text{(rad/s)}^2 \quad \checkmark$$ The relationship $\omega_1 \omega_2 = 1/(LC)$ holds for any high-Q resonant circuit and is a KTU favorite for short derivations. **Verification 2: $BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1 = R/L$** Direct computation: $$BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1 = 1012.58 - 987.58 = 25\ \text{rad/s}$$ Theoretical value: $$BW = \frac{R}{L} = \frac{5}{0.2} = 25\ \text{rad/s} \quad \checkmark$$ This confirms that for the *Series RL in Parallel with C* configuration, the bandwidth is **independent of the capacitance C** — a unique and important property. **Quality Factor Check:** $$Q = \frac{\omega_0}{BW} = \frac{1000.08}{25} \approx 40 \quad \text{(high-Q circuit)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign Errors in $\omega_1$:** Students often write $\omega_1 = +\frac{R}{2L} + \sqrt{...}$, giving a value greater than $\omega_0$. The correct form must be the **negative** branch of the quadratic. > 2. **Forgetting Units:** Always include units (rad/s vs Hz) in the final answer. KTU deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit. > 3. **Confusing $Q$ Formulas:** For *this* configuration, $Q = \omega_0 L / R$ (same as series). Do NOT use $Q = R/(\omega_0 L)$ — that is for ideal parallel RLC. > 4. **Missing Verification Step:** KTU 2024 scheme rewards **cross-checks** (e.g., verifying $BW = \omega_0/Q$ matches $R/L$). Always show at least one verification. > 5. **The $\sqrt{L/C - R^2}$ Trap:** If $R^2 \geq L/C$, the expression becomes imaginary, and the circuit **does not resonate**. State this condition explicitly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Parallel Resonance (Series RL $\parallel$ C):** - ☐ **Configuration:** A practical coil (modeled as $R$ + $j\omega L$ in series) is connected in **parallel** with a capacitor $C$. - ☐ **Resonance Condition:** Total circuit susceptance $B(\omega) = 0$ — i.e., the net reactive current is zero. - ☐ **Resonant Frequency:** $\omega_0 = \frac{1}{L}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C} - R^2}$ — always **less than** $1/\sqrt{LC}$ (the ideal value). - ☐ **Physical Existence:** Resonance requires $R^2 < L/C$ or $Q > 1/\sqrt{2}$. Otherwise, the square root is imaginary. - ☐ **Dynamic Resistance:** $R_D = L/(RC) = Q^2 R$ — the **maximum** impedance of the circuit. - ☐ **Quality Factor:** $Q = \omega_0 L / R = (1/R)\sqrt{L/C - R^2}$ — same formula as series RLC. - ☐ **Bandwidth:** $BW = R/L$ — **independent of $C$**! (Distinctive feature of this topology.) - ☐ **Half-Power Frequencies:** $\omega_{1,2} = \mp \frac{R}{2L} + \sqrt{(R/2L)^2 + 1/(LC)}$ - ☐ **Key Identities to Memorize:** - $\omega_1 \cdot \omega_2 = 1/(LC)$ - $\omega_0 = (\omega_1 + \omega_2)/2$ (geometric mean ≈ arithmetic mean for high $Q$) - $BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1$ - $BW = \omega_0 / Q$ - ☐ **Dual of Series RLC:** Series RLC has minimum $Z$ at $\omega_0$; this circuit has maximum $Z$ at $\omega_0$. - ☐ **Source Type:** Drive with a **current source** for clean parallel analysis; for a voltage source, include source resistance. - ☐ **Engineering Use:** RF/IF tuned amplifiers, band-pass filters, oscillator tank circuits (Hartley, Colpitts). - ☐ **High-Q Approximation:** When $R^2 \ll L/C$, use $\omega_0 \approx 1/\sqrt{LC}$ and $R_D \approx L/(RC)$. - ☐ **Common KTU Pitfalls:** Mixing series and parallel Q formulas; forgetting the $R^2$ term in $\omega_0$; assuming $R_D = R$. ---

topics.title: Calculation of Resonant Frequency - Power in 3-Phase Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET302 - CIRCUITS & NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Calculation of Resonant Frequency - Power in 3-Phase Circuits ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Resonant Frequency — Formal Definition **Resonance** in an RLC circuit is the operating condition at which the **inductive reactance** and the **capacitive reactance** of the circuit become equal in magnitude but opposite in phase. At this single, sharply defined frequency — called the **resonant frequency (f₀ or ω₀)** — the net reactive component of the circuit impedance becomes zero, and the circuit behaves as a purely resistive load. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > "Resonance occurs in a circuit when the supply frequency is such that the inductive and capacitive reactances cancel each other, leaving the circuit purely resistive. The frequency at which this occurs is the resonant frequency, denoted **f₀** (in Hz) or **ω₀** (in rad/s)." Mathematically, for a series R-L-C circuit the resonance condition is: $$X_L = X_C \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_0 L = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C}$$ Solving for the frequency gives the universal resonance relation: $$\boxed{\;\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \quad,\quad f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}\;}$$ ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Playground Swing" > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Intuition:** > Imagine pushing a child on a swing. The swing has a natural **pendulum frequency** that depends only on the chain length. If you push *with* that natural rhythm, the swing's amplitude grows enormously with very little effort (a tiny push each cycle). Push at any *other* rhythm, and the motion is small and jerky. > > In an RLC circuit, the **inductor (L)** stores energy in a magnetic field (like the swing's momentum), and the **capacitor (C)** stores energy in an electric field (like the swing's height). They naturally trade energy back and forth at a rate fixed by √(LC). When the external source matches this natural rhythm, the two storage elements do almost all the work, and the resistor (the "friction") only sees a small, well-orchestrated current that grows very large. This is **resonance**. ### 1.3 Power in 3-Phase Circuits — Formal Definition In a **balanced three-phase system**, three sinusoidal voltages of equal magnitude and displaced by **120°** in time supply either a star (Y) or delta (Δ) connected load. The **total instantaneous power** is **constant in time** (a unique and very important property of 3-phase systems), and the **average (active) power** is given by the well-known KTU formula: $$P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi = 3\,V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi$$ where **V_L** and **I_L** are line quantities, **V_ph** and **I_ph** are phase quantities, and **φ** is the angle between phase voltage and phase current. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why 3-Phase Power Matters in KTU:** > Power generation, transmission, and industrial distribution in Kerala (KSEB grid) and worldwide are predominantly 3-phase. The constant instantaneous power eliminates the 100/120 Hz torque pulsations present in single-phase motors, making 3-phase induction motors self-starting, smooth, and efficient. The KTU 2024 syllabus therefore places heavy weight on the **two-wattmeter method** and the **phasor relationships** of balanced 3-phase power. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics (Bolded for Quick Reference) | Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Standard | |----------|--------|------|----------| | Resonant angular frequency | **ω₀** | **rad/s** | **ω₀ = 1/√(LC)** | | Resonant frequency | **f₀** | **Hz** | **f₀ = 1/(2π√(LC))** | | Quality factor | **Q** | dimensionless | **Q = ω₀L/R** | | Bandwidth | **BW** | **rad/s or Hz** | **BW = ω₀/Q = R/L** | | Active power (3-φ) | **P** | **W (watts)** | **P = √3 V_L I_L cos φ** | | Reactive power (3-φ) | **Q** | **VAR** | **Q = √3 V_L I_L sin φ** | | Apparent power (3-φ) | **S** | **VA** | **S = √3 V_L I_L** | | Power factor | **cos φ** | dimensionless | **0 to 1** (lagging/leading) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Series RLC Circuit — Resonance Analysis Consider a resistor **R**, inductor **L**, and capacitor **C** connected in series with a sinusoidal source **V = V_m sin(ωt)**. **Total Impedance:** $$Z = R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$ **Step-by-step logic:** - The **resistive part** is frequency-independent. - The **inductive reactance X_L = ωL** grows linearly with frequency. - The **capacitive reactance X_C = 1/(ωC)** falls inversely with frequency. - At ω = 0 (DC): X_C = ∞, X_L = 0 → capacitor is open, current = 0. - At ω = ∞: X_C = 0, X_L = ∞ → inductor is open, current = 0. - Somewhere in between, **X_L = X_C** → impedance is purely R, current is maximum, and voltage and current are in phase. **At Resonance (Series):** | Quantity | Series Resonance Value | |----------|------------------------| | Impedance | **Z₀ = R (minimum, purely resistive)** | | Current | **I₀ = V/R (maximum)** | | Power factor | **cos φ = 1 (unity)** | | Reactive power | **Q = 0** | | Quality factor | **Q_s = ω₀L/R = 1/(ω₀RC) = (1/R)√(L/C)** | | Bandwidth | **BW = ω₀/Q_s = R/L** | | Voltage magnification | **V_L = V_C = Q_s · V** (each can exceed supply!) | > [!WARNING] > **Series Resonance Caution:** At resonance, the voltages across L and C are **Q_s times the supply voltage**. For high-Q circuits, this can stress the insulation of components. This is the principle behind **tuned voltage amplifiers** and **radio frequency (RF) selectors** — but it must be managed carefully. ### 2.2 Parallel RLC Circuit — Resonance Analysis For an ideal parallel LC tank (with R as the coil's internal resistance), the resonance formula for **f₀** is **identical** to the series case. However, the impedance behavior is **inverted**: | Quantity | Parallel Resonance Value | |----------|--------------------------| | Impedance | **Z₀ = L/(RC) = Q_p · ω₀L (maximum, purely resistive)** | | Line current | **I_line = V/Z₀ (minimum at resonance)** | | Circulating tank current | **I_L = I_C = Q_p · I_line** (large internal loop current) | | Quality factor | **Q_p = R/(ω₀L) = ω₀RC = (1/R)√(L/C) [ideal R in parallel branch] vs R/ω₀L [R in series with L]** | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Key Insight:** In series resonance, current is maximum and impedance is minimum. In parallel resonance, current is minimum and impedance is maximum. Both resonate at the *same* frequency ω₀ = 1/√(LC). This duality is heavily tested in KTU Module 2. ### 2.3 Selectivity, Q-Factor, and Bandwidth - **Selectivity** is the ability of a resonant circuit to discriminate between its resonant frequency and nearby frequencies. - A high-Q circuit has a **narrow, sharp** response curve (highly selective). - A low-Q circuit has a **broad, gentle** response curve (poorly selective). **The -3 dB Bandwidth (BW)** is defined as the difference between the two half-power frequencies **ω₁** and **ω₂** where the power delivered drops to half its maximum value: $$BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1 = \frac{\omega_0}{Q} = \frac{R}{L}$$ **Half-power frequencies (universal, independent of R):** $$\omega_1 = -\frac{R}{2L} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{LC}} \quad,\quad \omega_2 = +\frac{R}{2L} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{LC}}$$ ### 2.4 Three-Phase Power — Star and Delta Relationships **Star (Y) Connection:** $$V_L = \sqrt{3}\,V_{ph} \quad,\quad I_L = I_{ph}$$ **Delta (Δ) Connection:** $$V_L = V_{ph} \quad,\quad I_L = \sqrt{3}\,I_{ph}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Power formulas are identical for both Y and Δ in terms of line quantities** — this is the key KTU trick. You do *not* need to know the load configuration to compute total power from V_L, I_L, and cos φ. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) — High-Yield Equations | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Resonant angular frequency | $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ | rad/s | | 2 | Resonant frequency | $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ | Hz | | 3 | Series Q-factor | $Q_s = \omega_0 L / R = (1/R)\sqrt{L/C}$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Parallel Q-factor | $Q_p = R / (\omega_0 L) = \omega_0 R C$ | dimensionless | | 5 | Bandwidth | $BW = \omega_0 / Q = R/L$ | rad/s | | 6 | Lower half-power freq. | $\omega_1 = -R/(2L) + \sqrt{(R/2L)^2 + 1/(LC)}$ | rad/s | | 7 | Upper half-power freq. | $\omega_2 = +R/(2L) + \sqrt{(R/2L)^2 + 1/(LC)}$ | rad/s | | 8 | Star voltage relation | $V_L = \sqrt{3}\,V_{ph}$ | volts | | 9 | Star current relation | $I_L = I_{ph}$ | amperes | | 10 | Delta voltage relation | $V_L = V_{ph}$ | volts | | 11 | Delta current relation | $I_L = \sqrt{3}\,I_{ph}$ | amperes | | 12 | Active power (3-φ) | $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | watts (W) | | 13 | Reactive power (3-φ) | $Q = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi$ | VAR | | 14 | Apparent power (3-φ) | $S = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L$ | VA | | 15 | Power triangle | $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$ | — | | 16 | Power factor angle (2-W meter) | $\tan\phi = \sqrt{3}\,(W_2 - W_1)/(W_1 + W_2)$ | radians | | 17 | Two-wattmeter total power | $P = W_1 + W_2$ | watts | | 18 | Two-wattmeter reactive | $Q = \sqrt{3}\,(W_2 - W_1)$ | VAR | | 19 | Phase sequence for 2-W method | $W_2 > W_1$ ⇒ leading PF (RBY); $W_1 > W_2$ ⇒ lagging PF (RBY) | — | | 20 | p.f. with two wattmeters | $\cos\phi = \cos\!\left[\tan^{-1}\!\left(\sqrt{3}\,(W_2-W_1)/(W_1+W_2)\right)\right]$ | dimensionless | ### 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Concepts - **Resonant circuits** are the heart of every radio/TV tuner, band-pass filter, and oscillator. The IF (Intermediate Frequency) stage of a superheterodyne receiver at 455 kHz (AM) or 10.7 MHz (FM) is a tuned LC resonator. Wireless charging pads use series resonance to maximize power transfer across an air gap. Medical devices like MRI machines use precisely tuned RF coils at the Larmor frequency. - **3-phase power measurement** via the two-wattmeter method is the standard in every power utility sub-station, industrial control panel, and energy audit. KSEB meters for HT (high-tension) consumers all use this principle to bill based on kVAh and power factor penalties. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Resonant Frequency from the Resonance Condition **Goal:** Show rigorously that ω₀ = 1/√(LC) from X_L = X_C. **Given:** A series R-L-C circuit driven by v(t) = V_m sin(ωt). **Step 1 — Write the complex impedance** of the series combination: $$Z(\omega) = R + j\omega L + \frac{1}{j\omega C} = R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$$ The imaginary part of Z is the **net reactance** X(ω): $$X(\omega) = \omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the KTU definition of resonance:** the imaginary part of Z must equal zero (no net reactance, voltage and current in phase). $$X(\omega_0) = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega_0 L - \frac{1}{\omega_0 C} = 0$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange the equation** by moving the second term to the right-hand side: $$\omega_0 L = \frac{1}{\omega_0 C}$$ **Step 4 — Multiply both sides by ω₀:** $$\omega_0^2 L = \frac{1}{C}$$ **Step 5 — Divide both sides by L:** $$\omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{LC}$$ **Step 6 — Take the positive square root** (frequency is non-negative): $$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$ **Step 7 — Convert to Hz** by dividing by 2π: $$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} \quad \text{(Hz)}$$ **Conclusion:** The resonant frequency depends *only* on L and C — not on R. This is a critical KTU conceptual point: R controls the *sharpness* of resonance, not its *location* on the frequency axis. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Quality Factor Q for a Series RLC Circuit **Step 1 — Begin from the definition of Q** as the ratio of reactive power to resistive power at resonance, or equivalently, the ratio of energy stored per cycle to energy dissipated per cycle: $$Q = 2\pi \cdot \frac{\text{Maximum energy stored per cycle}}{\text{Energy dissipated per cycle}}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the maximum energy stored** in the inductor (which equals the max energy in C at resonance): $$W_L = \frac{1}{2} L I_m^2$$ **Step 3 — Compute the energy dissipated per cycle** in the resistor with RMS current I_rms = I_m/√2: $$W_R = I_{\text{rms}}^2 \cdot R \cdot T = \frac{I_m^2}{2} \cdot R \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0} = \frac{\pi R I_m^2}{\omega_0}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute** into the Q definition: $$Q = 2\pi \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{2} L I_m^2}{\frac{\pi R I_m^2}{\omega_0}} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{L \omega_0}{2\pi R} = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute ω₀ = 1/√(LC)** to express Q purely in terms of L, C, and R: $$Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{1}{\omega_0 R C} = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Cross-check:** The two expressions $\omega_0 L / R$ and $(1/R)\sqrt{L/C}$ are equivalent because $\omega_0 L = L/\sqrt{LC} = \sqrt{L/C}$. KTU students should remember **all three forms** — depending on which parameters are given in the problem. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Bandwidth from the Half-Power Frequencies **Step 1 — Write the current magnitude** in a series RLC circuit as a function of ω: $$\vert I(\omega) \vert = \frac{V}{\sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L - 1/(\omega C))^2}}$$ **Step 2 — At resonance**, the current is maximum: $$\vert I_{\max} \vert = \frac{V}{R}$$ **Step 3 — Half-power condition** is when the power delivered equals half of the maximum power, which means current is $1/\sqrt{2}$ of its maximum: $$\frac{V}{\sqrt{R^2 + (\omega L - 1/(\omega C))^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{V}{R}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify** by inverting and squaring both sides: $$R^2 + \left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2 = 2R^2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)^2 = R^2$$ **Step 5 — Take the square root** (two solutions, giving ω₁ < ω₀ < ω₂): $$\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C} = \pm R$$ **Step 6 — Solve the quadratic in ω** for each sign. Using the +R case: $$\omega^2 L C - R \omega C - 1 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega = \frac{RC \pm \sqrt{R^2C^2 + 4LC}}{2LC}$$ **Step 7 — Take the positive root** (since ω > 0): $$\omega_2 = \frac{R}{2L} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{LC}}$$ By symmetry (or repeating with the -R case): $$\omega_1 = -\frac{R}{2L} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{LC}}$$ **Step 8 — Compute the bandwidth** as the difference: $$BW = \omega_2 - \omega_1 = \frac{R}{2L} - \left(-\frac{R}{2L}\right) = \frac{R}{L}$$ **Step 9 — Substitute** ω₀ = 1/√(LC) to verify the relationship BW = ω₀/Q: $$\frac{\omega_0}{Q} = \frac{1/\sqrt{LC}}{\omega_0 L / R} = \frac{R}{\omega_0 L \sqrt{LC}} = \frac{R}{L} \cdot \frac{1}{\omega_0 \sqrt{LC}} = \frac{R}{L} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = \frac{R}{L} \checkmark$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** The result $BW = R/L$ is a remarkably clean expression that holds *exactly* for any series RLC circuit — no approximations. This is a very common KTU problem: "Given R, L, C, find the bandwidth." --- ### 3.4 Derivation: Active Power in a Balanced 3-Phase Star-Connected Load **Step 1 — Write the three phase voltages** of a balanced 3-phase source (R-Y-B sequence): $$ \begin{aligned} v_{RN}(t) &= V_m \sin(\omega t) \\ v_{YN}(t) &= V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°) \\ v_{BN}(t) &= V_m \sin(\omega t - 240°) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Write the corresponding phase currents** through a balanced star load with impedance Z∠φ: $$ \begin{aligned} i_R(t) &= \frac{V_m}{\vert Z \vert} \sin(\omega t - \phi) \\ i_Y(t) &= \frac{V_m}{\vert Z \vert} \sin(\omega t - 120° - \phi) \\ i_B(t) &= \frac{V_m}{\vert Z \vert} \sin(\omega t - 240° - \phi) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the total instantaneous power** as the sum of the three phase instantaneous powers: $$p(t) = v_{RN} i_R + v_{YN} i_Y + v_{BN} i_B$$ **Step 4 — Expand using the product-to-sum identity** $\sin A \sin B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$: $$ \begin{aligned} p(t) = \frac{V_m^2}{2\vert Z \vert}\Big[&\cos\phi - \cos(2\omega t - \phi) \\ + &\cos\phi - \cos(2\omega t - 240° - \phi) \\ + &\cos\phi - \cos(2\omega t - 480° - \phi)\Big] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Recognize the constant terms sum to 3 cos φ and the oscillating terms sum to zero** (three cosines 120° apart sum to zero): $$p(t) = \frac{3 V_m^2 \cos\phi}{2\vert Z \vert} = \text{constant}$$ **Step 6 — This is the average (active) power.** Express it in terms of RMS phase voltage V_ph = V_m/√2 and RMS phase current I_ph = V_m/(√2 |Z|): $$P = 3 \cdot \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}\vert Z \vert} \cos\phi = 3 V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi$$ **Step 7 — Convert to line quantities** using the star relations I_L = I_ph and V_L = √3 V_ph, so V_ph = V_L/√3: $$P = 3 \cdot \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot I_L \cos\phi = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Punchline:** Total instantaneous power in a *balanced* 3-phase system is **constant in time**. This is a defining property of 3-phase (absent in 1-phase) and is why 3-phase motors run without vibration. --- ### 3.5 Derivation: Power-Factor Angle from the Two-Wattmeter Readings **Step 1 — Consider a balanced 3-phase load** with the two-wattmeter method. The wattmeters W₁ and W₂ read: $$ \begin{aligned} W_1 &= V_L I_L \cos(30° - \phi) \\ W_2 &= V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Sum the wattmeter readings** (using cos(A−B) + cos(A+B) = 2 cos A cos B): $$W_1 + W_2 = V_L I_L \cdot 2 \cos(30°) \cos\phi = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi = P \quad\checkmark$$ **Step 3 — Compute the difference** (using cos(A−B) − cos(A+B) = 2 sin A sin B): $$W_2 - W_1 = V_L I_L \cdot 2 \sin(30°) \sin\phi = V_L I_L \sin\phi$$ **Step 4 — Take the ratio** of (W₂ − W₁) to (W₁ + W₂): $$\frac{W_2 - W_1}{W_1 + W_2} = \frac{V_L I_L \sin\phi}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi} = \frac{\tan\phi}{\sqrt{3}}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for φ:** $$\tan\phi = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_2 - W_1)}{W_1 + W_2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left[\frac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_2 - W_1)}{W_1 + W_2}\right]$$ **Step 6 — The power factor is then:** $$\cos\phi = \cos\!\left[\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_2 - W_1)}{W_1 + W_2}\right)\right]$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Series RLC Resonant Circuit — Functional Block Architecture The series RLC circuit and its frequency response are best illustrated as a block-level functional architecture showing how the input frequency is "filtered" by the resonance behavior. ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC["SINUSOIDAL
SOURCE V_s"] R["RESISTOR R"] L["INDUCTOR L
X_L = wL"] C["CAPACITOR C
X_C = 1/wC"] Z["TOTAL IMPEDANCE
Z = R + jwL - j/wC"] I["CIRCUIT CURRENT
I = V_s / Z"] FREQ["FREQUENCY
RESPONSE I(w)"] SRC --> R R --> L L --> C C --> Z Z --> I I --> FREQ subgraph RESONANCE_CONDITION direction TB COND1["X_L = wL grows with w"] COND2["X_C = 1/wC falls with w"] COND3["They CROSS at w_0 = 1/sqrt(LC)"] COND4["At w_0: Impedance = R only"] COND5["Current is MAXIMUM"] COND1 --> COND2 --> COND3 --> COND4 --> COND5 end ``` ### 4.2 Resonant Frequency Response — Selectivity Curve ```mermaid flowchart TB AXIS["FREQUENCY AXIS w (rad/s)"] CURVE["CURRENT MAGNITUDE
I(w) RESPONSE"] PEAK["PEAK I_max = V/R
at w_0 = 1/sqrt(LC)"] LOWER["w_1 (lower -3dB point)"] UPPER["w_2 (upper -3dB point)"] BW["BANDWIDTH
BW = w_2 - w_1 = R/L"] QVAL["QUALITY FACTOR
Q = w_0 / BW = w_0 L / R"] PASS["PASS BAND
high Q = narrow"] STOP["STOP BANDS
low Q = broad rejection"] AXIS --> CURVE CURVE --> PEAK PEAK --> LOWER PEAK --> UPPER LOWER --> BW UPPER --> BW BW --> QVAL PEAK --> PASS LOWER --> STOP UPPER --> STOP ``` ### 4.3 Three-Phase Two-Wattmeter Method — Measurement Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB GRID["3-PHASE SUPPLY
R - Y - B
V_L line-to-line"] L1["LINE R"] L2["LINE Y"] L3["LINE B"] CC["CURRENT COIL
of Wattmeter 1"] CC2["CURRENT COIL
of Wattmeter 2"] VC1["VOLTAGE COIL
W1 across R-Y"] VC2["VOLTAGE COIL
W2 across Y-B"] LOAD["BALANCED 3-PH
LOAD Z angle phi"] W1["WATTMETER 1
reads V_L I_L cos(30-phi)"] W2["WATTMETER 2
reads V_L I_L cos(30+phi)"] PTOTAL["TOTAL POWER
P = W1 + W2"] PFANGLE["PF ANGLE
tan phi = sqrt3 W2-W1 / W1+W2"] REACT["REACTIVE POWER
Q = sqrt3 (W2 - W1)"] GRID --> L1 GRID --> L2 GRID --> L3 L1 --> CC --> CC2 --> L3 CC --> W1 CC2 --> W2 VC1 --> W1 VC2 --> W2 L1 --> LOAD L2 --> LOAD L3 --> LOAD W1 --> PTOTAL W2 --> PTOTAL W1 --> PFANGLE W2 --> PFANGLE W1 --> REACT W2 --> REACT ``` ### 4.4 Power Triangle for 3-Phase Loads ```mermaid flowchart LR P["ACTIVE POWER P
(real axis)"] Q["REACTIVE POWER Q
(imaginary axis)"] S["APPARENT POWER S
(hypotenuse)"] ANGLE["PHI = power factor angle
cos phi = P / S"] P --> S Q --> S P --> ANGLE S --> ANGLE LAG["LAGGING phi
inductive load
W1 > W2"] LEAD["LEADING phi
capacitive load
W2 > W1"] UNITY["UNITY phi = 0
W1 = W2"] ANGLE --> LAG ANGLE --> LEAD ANGLE --> UNITY ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define the term "resonance" in a series RLC circuit. Derive the expression for the resonant frequency.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Resonance in a series RLC circuit is the condition at which the inductive reactance X_L equals the capacitive reactance X_C, making the net reactance zero. The circuit then behaves as a pure resistor, and the source voltage and current are in phase (unity power factor). **Derivation (2 marks):** At resonance, X_L = X_C, i.e., ω₀L = 1/(ω₀C). Multiplying both sides by ω₀: ω₀²L = 1/C, so ω₀² = 1/(LC). Therefore: **ω₀ = 1/√(LC) rad/s** and **f₀ = 1/(2π√(LC)) Hz**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Distinguish between the Q-factor of a series resonant circuit and a parallel resonant circuit. What is its significance?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Series RLC | Parallel RLC (ideal LC tank with R in coil) | |---------|-----------|---------------------------------------------| | **Q-factor formula** | $Q_s = (1/R)\sqrt{L/C}$ | $Q_p = R\sqrt{C/L} = R/(\omega_0 L)$ | | **Impedance at f₀** | Minimum (= R) | Maximum (= L/RC) | | **Current at f₀** | Maximum (= V/R) | Minimum (= V·RC/L) | | **Voltage across L, C at f₀** | Q_s times supply | Source voltage is across tank | **Significance (1 mark):** The Q-factor is a measure of the **selectivity** (sharpness of resonance) of the circuit. A high Q means a narrow bandwidth and good frequency discrimination — essential in radio tuners, band-pass filters, and RF amplifier design. A low Q gives a broad response — used in wideband audio equalizers. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** A series RLC circuit has R = 10 Ω, L = 100 mH, and C = 10 μF, connected to a 100 V, variable-frequency source. Calculate: (i) Resonant frequency f₀ (ii) Quality factor Q (iii) Bandwidth BW (iv) Current at resonance I₀ (v) Voltage across the inductor at resonance V_L **(b)** A balanced 3-phase, star-connected load draws a line current of 10 A at a line voltage of 400 V, 50 Hz. The load power factor is 0.8 lagging. Calculate: (i) Total active power P (ii) Total reactive power Q (iii) Total apparent power S (iv) Phase voltage, phase current, and impedance per phase (v) Power consumed by each phase --- **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks:** Given: R = 10 Ω, L = 100 mH = 0.1 H, C = 10 μF = 10 × 10⁻⁶ F, V = 100 V. **(i) Resonant frequency f₀:** $$ f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{0.1 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}} $$ $$ \sqrt{LC} = \sqrt{10^{-6}} = 10^{-3} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-3}} = 159.15 \text{ Hz} $$ **(ii) Quality factor Q:** $$ Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{1}{10}\sqrt{\frac{0.1}{10 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{10}\sqrt{10^4} = \frac{100}{10} = 10 $$ **(iii) Bandwidth BW (in Hz):** $$ BW = \frac{f_0}{Q} = \frac{159.15}{10} = 15.915 \text{ Hz} $$ (Or in rad/s: BW = ω₀/Q = 2π·f₀/Q = 2π·15.915 = 100 rad/s, equivalently R/L = 10/0.1 = 100 rad/s ✓) **(iv) Current at resonance I₀:** $$ I_0 = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{100}{10} = 10 \text{ A} $$ **(v) Voltage across the inductor at resonance V_L:** $$ V_L = I_0 \cdot X_L = I_0 \cdot \omega_0 L = 10 \times (2\pi \times 159.15) \times 0.1 $$ $$ V_L = 10 \times 1000 \times 0.1 = 1000 \text{ V} \quad \text{or} \quad V_L = Q \times V = 10 \times 100 = 1000 \text{ V} $$ --- **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks:** Given: Star-connected balanced load, V_L = 400 V, I_L = 10 A, cos φ = 0.8 lagging, f = 50 Hz. **(i) Total active power P:** $$ P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 10 \times 0.8 = 5542.5 \text{ W} \approx 5.54 \text{ kW} $$ **(ii) Total reactive power Q:** For lagging pf, sin φ = +√(1 − 0.8²) = +0.6. $$ Q = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 10 \times 0.6 = 4157 \text{ VAR} \approx 4.16 \text{ kVAR} $$ **(iii) Total apparent power S:** $$ S = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 10 = 6928 \text{ VA} \approx 6.93 \text{ kVA} $$ (Verify: S² = P² + Q² = 5542.5² + 4157² = 30,719,256 + 17,280,649 = 47,999,905 ≈ 48,000,000 = S² ✓) **(iv) Phase voltage, phase current, and impedance per phase:** $$ V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94 \text{ V} $$ $$ I_{ph} = I_L = 10 \text{ A} \quad \text{(star connection)} $$ $$ \vert Z_{ph} \vert = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_{ph}} = \frac{230.94}{10} = 23.094 \text{ Ω} $$ $$ Z_{ph} = 23.094 \angle \cos^{-1}(0.8) = 23.094 \angle 36.87° \text{ Ω} $$ **(v) Power consumed by each phase:** $$ P_{ph} = V_{ph}\,I_{ph}\,\cos\phi = 230.94 \times 10 \times 0.8 = 1847.5 \text{ W} \quad \text{(or } P/3 = 1847.5 \text{ W} \checkmark\text{)} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** A series RLC circuit with R = 20 Ω, L = 50 mH, and C = 5 μF is connected across a 50 V AC source. Determine: (i) Resonant frequency (ii) Q-factor and bandwidth (iii) The half-power frequencies f₁ and f₂ (iv) The voltage across the capacitor at resonance **(b)** Two wattmeters are connected to measure the power of a 3-phase, 3-wire balanced load. The readings are W₁ = 2000 W and W₂ = 3500 W. If the line voltage is 415 V, find: (i) Total active power (ii) Power factor and its nature (lagging/leading) (iii) Line current (iv) Reactive power (v) Phase impedance if the load is delta-connected --- **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks:** Given: R = 20 Ω, L = 50 mH = 0.05 H, C = 5 μF = 5 × 10⁻⁶ F, V = 50 V. **(i) Resonant frequency f₀:** $$ f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{0.05 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1000}{2\pi \times 0.5} = 318.3 \text{ Hz} $$ (Since √LC = √(25 × 10⁻⁸) = 5 × 10⁻⁴) **(ii) Q-factor and bandwidth:** $$ Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{1}{20}\sqrt{\frac{0.05}{5 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{20}\sqrt{10^4} = \frac{100}{20} = 5 $$ $$ BW = \frac{f_0}{Q} = \frac{318.3}{5} = 63.66 \text{ Hz} $$ **(iii) Half-power frequencies:** The general formulas give f₁ and f₂. First compute R/(2L): $$ \frac{R}{2L} = \frac{20}{2 \times 0.05} = 200 \text{ rad/s} = 31.83 \text{ Hz} $$ And 1/√(LC) = 318.3 Hz. $$ f_1 = -\frac{R}{4\pi L} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{4\pi L}\right)^2 + f_0^2} $$ $$ f_1 = -31.83 + \sqrt{31.83^2 + 318.3^2} = -31.83 + \sqrt{1013.0 + 101,316.9} = -31.83 + 319.88 = 288.05 \text{ Hz} $$ $$ f_2 = +31.83 + \sqrt{31.83^2 + 318.3^2} = 31.83 + 319.88 = 351.71 \text{ Hz} $$ Check: f₂ − f₁ = 351.71 − 288.05 = 63.66 Hz = BW ✓ **(iv) Voltage across the capacitor at resonance V_C:** At resonance, V_C = V_L = Q · V = 5 × 50 = 250 V. (Also V_C = I₀ · X_C = (50/20) · (1/(2π·318.3·5·10⁻⁶)) = 2.5 · 100 = 250 V ✓) --- **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks:** Given: W₁ = 2000 W, W₂ = 3500 W, V_L = 415 V, 3-phase 3-wire balanced load. **(i) Total active power P:** $$ P = W_1 + W_2 = 2000 + 3500 = 5500 \text{ W} = 5.5 \text{ kW} $$ **(ii) Power factor and its nature:** Since W₂ (3500) > W₁ (2000), the load power factor is **leading** (capacitive). Verify: $$ \tan\phi = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,(W_2 - W_1)}{W_1 + W_2} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \times 1500}{5500} = \frac{2598.1}{5500} = 0.4724 $$ $$ \phi = \tan^{-1}(0.4724) = 25.28° \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \cos\phi = \cos(25.28°) = 0.904 \text{ leading} $$ **(iii) Line current:** $$ P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi \quad\Longrightarrow\quad I_L = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}\,V_L \cos\phi} = \frac{5500}{\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 0.904} $$ $$ I_L = \frac{5500}{649.9} = 8.46 \text{ A} $$ **(iv) Reactive power Q:** $$ Q = \sqrt{3}\,(W_2 - W_1) = \sqrt{3} \times 1500 = 2598 \text{ VAR} \approx 2.6 \text{ kVAR (leading)} $$ (Verify: Q = √3 V_L I_L sin φ = √3 × 415 × 8.46 × sin(25.28°) = 6078 × 0.4274 = 2598 VAR ✓) **(v) Phase impedance if delta-connected:** In a delta connection: V_ph = V_L = 415 V, and I_ph = I_L/√3 = 8.46/1.732 = 4.885 A. $$ \vert Z_{ph} \vert = \frac{V_{ph}}{I_{ph}} = \frac{415}{4.885} = 84.95 \text{ Ω} $$ $$ Z_{ph} = 84.95 \angle{-25.28°} \text{ Ω} \quad \text{(leading, so capacitive)} $$ In rectangular form: $$ Z_{ph} = 84.95 \cos(-25.28°) + j\,84.95 \sin(-25.28°) = 76.79 - j\,36.27 \text{ Ω} $$ --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Board Valuation:** > 1. **Mixing up series and parallel Q-factor formulas** — a single mark is lost if a student writes Q = R/(ω₀L) for a series circuit or Q = ω₀L/R for a parallel circuit. Always check whether the circuit is series or parallel *first*. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of √3** in 3-phase power formulas — this is the #1 silent error. Always write P = √3 V_L I_L cos φ, **not** P = V_L I_L cos φ. > 3. **Not specifying leading/lagging for power factor** — KTU examiners deduct marks if you only state "0.8" without saying whether it is leading or lagging. The two-wattmeter method makes this easy: W₂ > W₁ ⇒ leading, W₁ > W₂ ⇒ lagging. > 4. **Forgetting the RMS conversion** in voltage calculations — when asked for V_L or V_C, use **RMS values**, not peak. The supply voltage given is typically RMS. > 5. **Computing bandwidth in Hz vs rad/s** — if f₀ is in Hz, BW = f₀/Q is in Hz. Convert to rad/s by multiplying by 2π **only** if the question demands angular frequency units. > 6. **Bandwidth formula misapplication** — the relation BW = R/L is *exactly* true for series RLC. For parallel RLC, BW = G/C = 1/(RC), where G is the parallel conductance. Mixing these up is a common trap. > 7. **Half-power frequencies** — students often quote only the **approximate** formulas ω₁ ≈ ω₀ − R/(2L) and ω₂ ≈ ω₀ + R/(2L) for high-Q circuits. KTU 2024 requires the **exact** forms unless the problem specifically states "approximate" or "high-Q". --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Module 2 — Rapid Recap: Resonant Frequency & 3-Phase Power** **Resonance Essentials:** - Resonant frequency depends ONLY on L and C: **f₀ = 1/(2π√(LC))**. R does not affect f₀. - Series resonance: **Z = R (minimum)**, **I = V/R (maximum)**, **cos φ = 1 (unity pf)**. - Parallel resonance: **Z = L/(RC) (maximum)**, **I_line = minimum**, large internal circulating current. - Q-factor for series: **Q = (1/R)√(L/C) = ω₀L/R = 1/(ω₀RC)**. - Q-factor for parallel (R in series with L branch): **Q = R/(ω₀L) = ω₀RC = R√(C/L)**. - Bandwidth: **BW = ω₀/Q = R/L (series) or 1/(RC) (parallel)**. - Half-power frequencies: **ω₁,₂ = ∓R/(2L) + √[(R/2L)² + 1/(LC)]**. - Voltage magnification at series resonance: **V_L = V_C = Q·V_source** (can be destructive!). **3-Phase Power Essentials:** - Star: **V_L = √3 V_ph**, **I_L = I_ph**. - Delta: **V_L = V_ph**, **I_L = √3 I_ph**. - Active power: **P = √3 V_L I_L cos φ = 3 V_ph I_ph cos φ** (watts). - Reactive power: **Q = √3 V_L I_L sin φ** (VAR). - Apparent power: **S = √3 V_L I_L** (VA). - Power triangle: **S² = P² + Q²**; **tan φ = Q/P**; **cos φ = P/S**. - Two-wattmeter method (3-φ, 3-wire): **P = W₁ + W₂**; **tan φ = √3(W₂ − W₁)/(W₁ + W₂)**. - **W₂ > W₁ ⇒ leading PF** (capacitive); **W₁ > W₂ ⇒ lagging PF** (inductive); **W₁ = W₂ ⇒ unity PF**. - Total instantaneous 3-φ power is **constant** (no pulsation) — the defining advantage of polyphase systems. - When **φ = 60°** (cos φ = 0.5), one wattmeter reads **zero** (W₂ = 0 for lagging). - When **φ > 60°** (cos φ < 0.5, very lagging), one wattmeter reads **negative** — the test point must reverse its voltage coil to get a positive reading, and you **subtract** its magnitude. **Common KTU Numerical Pitfalls:** - Always convert mH → H and μF → F before plugging into √(LC). - Always check the **line vs phase** distinction in star/delta. - For the two-wattmeter method, if one reading is negative, subtract it (don't ignore it!). - In resonance bandwidth problems, the **half-power** condition means current is 1/√2 of I_max — students often confuse this with the -3 dB point, which is the same thing but stated in decibels. **Final KTU Takeaway:** Master the Q-factor duality between series and parallel resonance, and the two-wattmeter sign convention — these are the two most heavily weighted concepts in Module 2 of PCEET302.

topics.title: Complex Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET302 - CIRCUITS & NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Complex Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the sinusoidal steady-state analysis of AC circuits, **Complex Power (S)** is a complex quantity that comprehensively encapsulates the power exchange between a source and a load. It is defined as the product of the RMS voltage phasor $\mathbf{V}$ and the complex conjugate of the RMS current phasor $\mathbf{I}^*$:

$$\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{V} \cdot \mathbf{I}^*$$

Mathematically, complex power is expressed in **rectangular form** as:

$$\mathbf{S} = P + jQ$$

where:
- $P$ = **Real (Average/Active) Power**, measured in **Watts (W)**
- $Q$ = **Reactive Power**, measured in **Volt-Ampere Reactive (VAR)**
- $\vert \mathbf{S} \vert$ = **Apparent Power**, measured in **Volt-Amperes (VA)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Complex Power is a foundational concept for Module 2 (AC Steady-State Power Analysis) and directly underpins Power Factor Correction, Maximum Power Transfer Theorem, and Three-Phase Power Calculations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Account" Intuition

Imagine you earn money in two ways:
- **Real Money (Active Power $P$):** This is the actual cash you can spend on useful work (lighting a bulb, running a motor shaft, heating a resistor). It produces tangible output.
- **Locked-Up Money (Reactive Power $Q$):** This is money tied up in a fixed deposit — you don't *spend* it, but it's essential for the banking system to function. Similarly, reactive power shuttles back and forth between source and load, sustaining the magnetic/electric fields in inductors and capacitors. It does **no net work** but is mandatory for the operation of reactive elements.

> **Total Account Balance** = **Apparent Power ($\vert S \vert$)** — this is the *gross* amount your source must "supply," even if only part of it is doing real work.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of AC Power

| Component | Symbol | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Real Power | $P$ | $VI \cos\theta$ | Average useful work per cycle |
| Reactive Power | $Q$ | $VI \sin\theta$ | Power oscillating between source and reactive elements |
| Apparent Power | $\vert S \vert$ | $VI$ | Magnitude of total power flow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Power Factor (pf)** is the cosine of the phase angle between voltage and current: $\text{pf} = \cos\theta = P / \vert S \vert$. A **unity power factor (pf = 1)** means all power is real; a **zero power factor** means all power is reactive.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

For a sinusoidal steady-state circuit with voltage $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \theta_v)$ and current $i(t) = I_m \cos(\omega t + \theta_i)$:

**Step 1 — Form the Phasors:**
- Voltage phasor: $\mathbf{V} = V_{rms} \angle \theta_v$
- Current phasor: $\mathbf{I} = I_{rms} \angle \theta_i$

**Step 2 — Compute the Complex Conjugate of Current:**
$$\mathbf{I}^* = I_{rms} \angle (-\theta_i)$$

**Step 3 — Multiply $\mathbf{V} \cdot \mathbf{I}^*$:**
$$\mathbf{S} = V_{rms} I_{rms} \angle (\theta_v - \theta_i) = V_{rms} I_{rms} \angle \theta$$

**Step 4 — Decompose into Real and Imaginary Axes:**
- The real part (projection on horizontal axis) = **Real Power $P$**
- The imaginary part (projection on vertical axis) = **Reactive Power $Q$**

> **Why use $\mathbf{I}^*$ (and not $\mathbf{I}$)?** Multiplying by the complex conjugate automatically extracts the phase difference $(\theta_v - \theta_i)$ between voltage and current. This single complex product encodes *both* the magnitude of power exchange *and* the phase relationship — the essence of AC power analysis.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU university exam on this module.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{V} \cdot \mathbf{I}^*$ | Definition of Complex Power | VA |
| 2 | $P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\theta$ | Real (Active) Power | W |
| 3 | $Q = V_{rms} I_{rms} \sin\theta$ | Reactive Power | VAR |
| 4 | $\vert \mathbf{S} \vert = V_{rms} I_{rms} = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ | Apparent Power | VA |
| 5 | $\cos\theta = P / \vert \mathbf{S} \vert$ | Power Factor | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\mathbf{S} = \vert \mathbf{S} \vert \cos\theta + j \vert \mathbf{S} \vert \sin\theta$ | Rectangular form | VA |
| 7 | $\mathbf{S} = \vert I \vert^2 \mathbf{Z} = V_{rms}^2 / \mathbf{Z}^*$ | Power in terms of impedance | VA |
| 8 | $P = \vert I \vert^2 R$ | Power dissipated in resistance | W |
| 9 | $Q = \vert I \vert^2 X$ | Reactive power of reactance | VAR |
| 10 | $P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2} V_m I_m \cos\theta$ | Average power (peak form) | W |

> **Note:** $\mathbf{Z}$ is the complex impedance $\mathbf{Z} = R + jX$, and $\mathbf{Z}^*$ is its complex conjugate. The angle $\theta = \theta_v - \theta_i$ is the impedance angle of the load.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Utilities (KSEB, BSES, etc.):** Electricity bills are based on **real energy (kWh)** consumed, but the apparent power ($\vert S \vert$) determines the **current rating** of transformers, cables, and switchgear. Industrial consumers with low pf are penalized.
- **Power Factor Correction (PFC):** Capacitor banks are installed in factories to reduce $Q$, thereby improving pf and reducing line losses — a direct application of complex power theory.
- **Power System Stability:** Complex power flow equations ($\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{V} \mathbf{I}^*$) form the bedrock of load flow studies in transmission networks.
- **Renewable Energy Inverters:** Modern grid-tied inverters are controlled to inject *only real power* ($P$) at unity pf into the grid, while managing reactive power ($Q$) for voltage support.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Complex Power from Time-Domain Voltage and Current

**Given:**
$$v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \theta_v)$$
$$i(t) = I_m \cos(\omega t + \theta_i)$$

**Step 1 — Write Instantaneous Power:**
The instantaneous power delivered to the load is:
$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) = V_m I_m \cos(\omega t + \theta_v) \cos(\omega t + \theta_i)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Trigonometric Identity:**
Using the product-to-sum identity: $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$

We obtain:
$$p(t) = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} \cos(\theta_v - \theta_i) + \frac{V_m I_m}{2} \cos(2\omega t + \theta_v + \theta_i)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Average (Real) Power:**
The average of the second term over one full cycle is zero (it oscillates at $2\omega$). Hence:

$$P = \langle p(t) \rangle = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} \cos(\theta_v - \theta_i)$$

**Step 4 — Express in RMS Form:**
Since $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$ and $I_{rms} = I_m / \sqrt{2}$:

$$P = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\theta \quad \text{where} \quad \theta = \theta_v - \theta_i$$

> This is the famous **Real Power Formula** — a direct consequence of the trigonometric product identity.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Showing that $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{V} \mathbf{I}^* = P + jQ$

**Step 1 — Write Phasor Representations:**
$$\mathbf{V} = V_{rms} \angle \theta_v = V_{rms}(\cos\theta_v + j\sin\theta_v)$$
$$\mathbf{I} = I_{rms} \angle \theta_i = I_{rms}(\cos\theta_i + j\sin\theta_i)$$

**Step 2 — Form the Complex Conjugate of Current:**
$$\mathbf{I}^* = I_{rms} \angle (-\theta_i) = I_{rms}(\cos\theta_i - j\sin\theta_i)$$

**Step 3 — Multiply $\mathbf{V}$ and $\mathbf{I}^*$ in Rectangular Form:**

Let $A = V_{rms}\cos\theta_v$, $B = V_{rms}\sin\theta_v$, $C = I_{rms}\cos\theta_i$, $D = I_{rms}\sin\theta_i$.

Then $\mathbf{V} = A + jB$ and $\mathbf{I}^* = C - jD$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{S} &= (A + jB)(C - jD) \\
&= AC - jAD + jBC - j^2 BD \\
&= AC + BD + j(BC - AD)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify Using Trigonometric Identities:**

Real part: $AC + BD = V_{rms}I_{rms}(\cos\theta_v \cos\theta_i + \sin\theta_v \sin\theta_i)$

Applying the identity $\cos(A-B) = \cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B$:

$$\text{Re}(\mathbf{S}) = V_{rms}I_{rms}\cos(\theta_v - \theta_i) = V_{rms}I_{rms}\cos\theta = P$$

Imaginary part: $BC - AD = V_{rms}I_{rms}(\sin\theta_v \cos\theta_i - \cos\theta_v \sin\theta_i)$

Applying the identity $\sin(A-B) = \sin A \cos B - \cos A \sin B$:

$$\text{Im}(\mathbf{S}) = V_{rms}I_{rms}\sin(\theta_v - \theta_i) = V_{rms}I_{rms}\sin\theta = Q$$

**Step 5 — Final Consolidated Result:**

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{V} \mathbf{I}^* = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\theta + j V_{rms} I_{rms} \sin\theta = P + jQ}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Complex Power in Terms of Impedance

**Given a load impedance $\mathbf{Z} = R + jX = \vert Z \vert \angle \theta$**, and Ohm's law $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{I} \mathbf{Z}$:

**Step 1 — Substitute into $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{V} \mathbf{I}^*$:**

$$\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{I} \mathbf{Z} \cdot \mathbf{I}^* = \vert I \vert^2 \mathbf{Z}$$

(using the identity $\mathbf{I} \cdot \mathbf{I}^* = \vert I \vert^2$)

**Step 2 — Expand:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{S} &= \vert I \vert^2 (R + jX) \\
&= \underbrace{\vert I \vert^2 R}_{P} + j\underbrace{\vert I \vert^2 X}_{Q}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Conjugate Form:**

Starting from $\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{I}\mathbf{Z}$, we get $\mathbf{I} = \mathbf{V} / \mathbf{Z}$, so $\mathbf{I}^* = \mathbf{V}^* / \mathbf{Z}^*$.

Therefore:

$$\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{V} \cdot \frac{\mathbf{V}^*}{\mathbf{Z}^*} = \frac{\vert V \vert^2}{\mathbf{Z}^*}$$

> **Interpretation:** Real power $P$ is dissipated **only in the resistive part** $R$ of the load. Reactive power $Q$ is associated **only with the reactive part** $X$. This is the key to understanding why purely reactive elements (ideal $L$ or $C$) consume *no real power*.

---

### 3.4 Power Triangle — Geometric Visualization

The relationship $\mathbf{S} = P + jQ$ is geometrically represented as a right triangle:

- **Hypotenuse** = $\vert \mathbf{S} \vert$ (Apparent Power)
- **Adjacent side** = $P$ (Real Power)
- **Opposite side** = $Q$ (Reactive Power)
- **Angle at origin** = $\theta$ (Impedance angle / pf angle)

Pythagorean relationship: $\vert \mathbf{S} \vert^2 = P^2 + Q^2$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Power Triangle — Mermaid Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    O["Origin"]:::origin -->|P Real Power W| RP["P Real Power W"]
    RP -->|jQ Reactive Power VAR| TP["Q Reactive Power VAR"]
    O -->|Magnitude S Apparent VA| AP["S Apparent Power VA"]
    RP -->|"Angle theta pf angle"| AP

    classDef origin fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef default fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The triangle above illustrates that **Real Power ($P$)** lies along the horizontal axis, **Reactive Power ($Q$)** along the vertical axis, and **Apparent Power ($\vert S \vert$)** is the resultant hypotenuse, making an angle $\theta$ with the real axis.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Power Flow in an AC System

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC["AC Voltage Source Vrms angle theta_v"]:::source
    LINE["Transmission Line Impedance Z_line R_line + jX_line"]:::line
    LOAD["Load Impedance Z_load R_load + jX_load"]:::load
    P["Real Power P dissipated in R_load"]:::real
    Q["Reactive Power Q stored in X_load"]:::reactive
    S["Complex Power S P + jQ delivered to load"]:::complex

    SRC -->|"I rms angle theta_i"| LINE
    LINE -->|"I rms angle theta_i"| LOAD
    LOAD -->|"Power dissipation"| P
    LOAD -->|"Energy storage oscillation"| Q
    P -->|"Combined vectorially"| S
    Q -->|"Combined vectorially"| S

    classDef source fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef line fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#616161,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef real fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef reactive fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef complex fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram above maps the flow of complex power from source to load. Real power $P$ is consumed (dissipated as heat or work) at the resistive portion of the load, while reactive power $Q$ shuttles back and forth — neither consumed nor produced — but required to maintain electromagnetic fields in inductors and capacitors.

---

### 4.3 Phasor Relationship Between V, I, and S

```mermaid
graph TD
    VR["V rms along Real Axis Reference"]:::vaxis
    IPHI["I rms at angle minus theta lagging for inductive load"]:::iaxis
    VSTAR["V stays fixed I conjugated for complex power"]:::star
    SPHI["S at angle theta in First Quadrant for inductive lagging pf"]:::sphi
    PAX["P on Real axis S cos theta"]:::paxis
    QAX["Q on Imaginary axis S sin theta"]:::qaxis

    VR -->|"Multiply V I star"| SPHI
    IPHI -->|"Conjugate then multiply"| SPHI
    SPHI -->|"Project on Real axis"| PAX
    SPHI -->|"Project on Imaginary axis"| QAX
    VSTAR --> SPHI

    classDef vaxis fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef iaxis fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef star fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#4527a0,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef sphi fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef paxis fill:#90caf9,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef qaxis fill:#f48fb1,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define Complex Power. What are its components?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Complex Power** is defined as the product of the RMS voltage phasor and the complex conjugate of the RMS current phasor:

$$\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{V} \mathbf{I}^* = V_{rms} I_{rms} \angle (\theta_v - \theta_i)$$

It has two components:
1. **Real Power (P)** = $V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\theta$ — represents the average useful power dissipated per cycle (in Watts).
2. **Reactive Power (Q)** = $V_{rms} I_{rms} \sin\theta$ — represents the power that oscillates between source and reactive elements (in VAR).

Together, $\mathbf{S} = P + jQ$, with magnitude $\vert \mathbf{S} \vert = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ called **Apparent Power** (in VA). *(3 marks)*

---

#### **Question 2: Define Power Factor. What is its significance?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Power Factor (pf)** is the cosine of the angle between voltage and current in an AC circuit:

$$\text{pf} = \cos\theta = \frac{P}{\vert S \vert} = \frac{R}{\vert Z \vert}$$

**Significance:**
- It indicates the **fraction of apparent power that is converted into useful work**.
- A **low power factor** means higher current is drawn for the same real power, leading to **increased line losses** ($I^2R$ losses) and **larger conductor/transformer ratings**.
- Utilities charge **penalties** to industrial consumers operating below a threshold pf (typically 0.9 lagging).
- **Power factor correction** using capacitor banks improves efficiency and reduces operational cost. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Following KTU ESE pattern, students must answer **ONE full 14-mark question** with internal choice between Question A and Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define complex power in AC circuits. Starting from the instantaneous voltage $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \theta_v)$ and current $i(t) = I_m \cos(\omega t + \theta_i)$, derive the expression for complex power $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{I}^* = P + jQ$. Show that the average real power is $P = V_{rms}I_{rms}\cos\theta$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A load operates with voltage $v(t) = 141.4 \cos(377t + 30°)~\text{V}$ and current $i(t) = 7.07 \cos(377t - 15°)~\text{A}$. Find: (i) RMS voltage and current, (ii) Complex power $\mathbf{S}$, (iii) Real power $P$, (iv) Reactive power $Q$, (v) Apparent power $\vert \mathbf{S} \vert$, and (vi) Power factor. State whether the pf is lagging or leading.

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Full Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Express Instantaneous Power:**
$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) = V_m I_m \cos(\omega t + \theta_v) \cos(\omega t + \theta_i)$$

**Step 2 — Apply Trigonometric Product Identity:**
Using $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:
$$p(t) = \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos(\theta_v - \theta_i) + \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos(2\omega t + \theta_v + \theta_i)$$

**Step 3 — Compute Average Power:**
The second term averages to zero over a period; hence:
$$P = \frac{V_m I_m}{2}\cos(\theta_v - \theta_i) = V_{rms}I_{rms}\cos\theta$$

**Step 4 — Form Phasors:**
$$\mathbf{V} = V_{rms}\angle\theta_v, \quad \mathbf{I} = I_{rms}\angle\theta_i$$

**Step 5 — Compute Complex Conjugate and Multiply:**
$$\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{I}^* = V_{rms}I_{rms}\angle(\theta_v - \theta_i) = V_{rms}I_{rms}\angle\theta$$

**Step 6 — Expand in Rectangular Form:**
$$\mathbf{S} = V_{rms}I_{rms}\cos\theta + j V_{rms}I_{rms}\sin\theta = P + jQ$$

Thus, $\mathbf{S} = P + jQ$, where $P = V_{rms}I_{rms}\cos\theta$ and $Q = V_{rms}I_{rms}\sin\theta$. $\blacksquare$ *(7 marks)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify RMS Values:**
$$V_{rms} = \frac{141.4}{\sqrt{2}} = 100~\text{V}, \quad I_{rms} = \frac{7.07}{\sqrt{2}} = 5~\text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Identify Phase Angles:**
$$\theta_v = +30°, \quad \theta_i = -15°, \quad \theta = \theta_v - \theta_i = 45°$$

**Step 3 — Compute Complex Power:**
$$\mathbf{S} = V_{rms}I_{rms}\angle\theta = (100)(5)\angle 45° = 500\angle 45°~\text{VA}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Rectangular Form:**
$$\mathbf{S} = 500\cos 45° + j500\sin 45° = 353.55 + j353.55~\text{VA}$$

**Step 5 — Extract Components:**
- Real power: $P = 353.55~\text{W}$
- Reactive power: $Q = 353.55~\text{VAR}$
- Apparent power: $\vert \mathbf{S} \vert = 500~\text{VA}$
- Power factor: $\cos 45° = 0.7071$ **lagging** (since $\theta_i < \theta_v$, current lags voltage, indicating an inductive load). *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of the **power triangle** with a neat sketch. Derive the relationship between apparent power, real power, reactive power, and power factor. State the power factor condition for a purely resistive, purely inductive, and purely capacitive load.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-phase 220 V (RMS), 50 Hz supply feeds a load of impedance $\mathbf{Z} = 30 + j40~\Omega$. Calculate: (i) the magnitude and phase of the load current, (ii) the complex power delivered, (iii) the real and reactive power consumed, and (iv) the power factor. Also determine the value of a **capacitor connected in parallel** to improve the power factor to **0.95 lagging**.

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Power Triangle Explanation:**

The **power triangle** is a right-angled triangle that geometrically represents the complex power $\mathbf{S} = P + jQ$:
- **Base (adjacent)** = Real Power $P = \vert S \vert \cos\theta$
- **Height (opposite)** = Reactive Power $Q = \vert S \vert \sin\theta$
- **Hypotenuse** = Apparent Power $\vert S \vert = VI$
- **Angle at origin** = $\theta = \cos^{-1}(\text{pf})$

**Relationships derived:**
$$\tan\theta = \frac{Q}{P}, \quad \cos\theta = \frac{P}{\vert S \vert}, \quad \vert S \vert = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$$

**Power factor conditions:**
| Load Type | Phase Relationship | Power Factor |
|---|---|---|
| Purely Resistive | $V$ and $I$ in phase ($\theta = 0$) | $\text{pf} = 1$ (unity) |
| Purely Inductive | $I$ lags $V$ by $90°$ ($\theta = +90°$) | $\text{pf} = 0$ (lagging) |
| Purely Capacitive | $I$ leads $V$ by $90°$ ($\theta = -90°$) | $\text{pf} = 0$ (leading) |

*(7 marks)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Impedance Magnitude and Phase:**
$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{30^2 + 40^2} = 50~\Omega, \quad \theta_Z = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{40}{30}\right) = 53.13°$$

**Step 2 — Compute Current Phasor:**
Taking $\mathbf{V} = 220\angle 0°$ V as reference:
$$\mathbf{I} = \frac{\mathbf{V}}{\mathbf{Z}} = \frac{220\angle 0°}{50\angle 53.13°} = 4.4\angle (-53.13°)~\text{A}$$

Magnitude: $\vert I \vert = 4.4$ A, Phase: $-53.13°$ (current lags voltage).

**Step 3 — Compute Complex Power:**
$$\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{I}^* = (220\angle 0°)(4.4\angle 53.13°) = 968\angle 53.13°~\text{VA}$$
$$\mathbf{S} = 968\cos 53.13° + j968\sin 53.13° = 580.8 + j774.4~\text{VA}$$

**Step 4 — Real and Reactive Power:**
$$P = 580.8~\text{W}, \quad Q = 774.4~\text{VAR (inductive)}$$

**Step 5 — Power Factor:**
$$\text{pf}_{\text{old}} = \cos 53.13° = 0.6~\text{lagging}$$

**Step 6 — Power Factor Correction to 0.95 Lagging:**

New pf angle: $\theta_{\text{new}} = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19°$

Required new reactive power:
$$Q_{\text{new}} = P \tan\theta_{\text{new}} = 580.8 \times \tan 18.19° = 580.8 \times 0.3287 = 190.9~\text{VAR}$$

Reactive power to be supplied by capacitor:
$$Q_C = Q_{\text{old}} - Q_{\text{new}} = 774.4 - 190.9 = 583.5~\text{VAR}$$

For a capacitor: $Q_C = V^2 \omega C$, so:

$$C = \frac{Q_C}{V^2 \omega} = \frac{583.5}{(220)^2 \times 2\pi \times 50} = \frac{583.5}{15{,}207.6} \approx 38.4~\mu\text{F}$$

*(7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing peak and RMS values** — When given $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \theta)$, students often forget to convert to $V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt{2}$ before applying $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{I}^*$. This is the **#1 mark-losing error** in this module.
> 2. **Forgetting the complex conjugate** — Writing $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{I}$ instead of $\mathbf{V}\mathbf{I}^*$ is a fundamental conceptual blunder that loses 2–3 marks instantly.
> 3. **Sign error in reactive power** — For a **leading** pf (capacitive load), $Q$ is **negative**. Many students report $|Q|$ without sign, losing half a mark.
> 4. **Phase angle sign confusion** — Current *lagging* voltage = $\theta_i < \theta_v$ = $\theta = \theta_v - \theta_i > 0$ = **inductive** load. Memorize this.
> 5. **Unit mismatches** — Capacitance in PFC problems must be in **Farads** (or $\mu$F), not arbitrary units.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Complex Power (Module 2):**

- ⭐ **Core Definition:** $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{V}\mathbf{I}^* = P + jQ$ — always use RMS phasors and complex conjugate of current.
- ⭐ **Real Power** $P = V_{rms}I_{rms}\cos\theta$ — dissipated **only** in resistance $R$, unit: **W**.
- ⭐ **Reactive Power** $Q = V_{rms}I_{rms}\sin\theta$ — stored in reactance $X$, unit: **VAR**.
- ⭐ **Apparent Power** $\vert S \vert = V_{rms}I_{rms}$ — total power magnitude, unit: **VA**.
- ⭐ **Pythagorean relation:** $\vert S \vert^2 = P^2 + Q^2$ — defines the power triangle.
- ⭐ **Power Factor:** $\cos\theta = P / \vert S \vert = R / \vert Z \vert$ — **lagging** for inductive ($R-L$) and **leading** for capacitive ($R-C$) loads.
- ⭐ **Impedance forms:** $\mathbf{S} = \vert I \vert^2 \mathbf{Z} = \vert V \vert^2 / \mathbf{Z}^*$ — use whichever is convenient.
- ⭐ **Average from instantaneous:** $P = \frac{1}{2}V_m I_m \cos\theta = V_{rms}I_{rms}\cos\theta$ — convert peak ↔ RMS as $V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt{2}$.
- ⭐ **PFC Formula:** $C = \frac{P(\tan\theta_1 - \tan\theta_2)}{V^2 \omega}$ — memorize this for capacitor bank design.
- ⭐ **Purely reactive loads** ($R = 0$): $P = 0$, all power is reactive. Ideal $L$ and $C$ do not dissipate energy.
- ⭐ **Complex power is conserved** in AC circuits (Tellegen-like theorem): $\sum \mathbf{S}_{\text{sources}} = \sum \mathbf{S}_{\text{loads}}$ — useful for network simplification.
- ⭐ **Examiner's mantra:** Always draw the **power triangle** in your answer script — it earns easy visualization marks and demonstrates conceptual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active, Reactive and Apparent Power in Balanced Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET302 - CIRCUITS & NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Active, Reactive and Apparent Power in Balanced Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a **Balanced Three-Phase Load**, the impedance in each of the three phases is identical in magnitude and phase angle. Such a system is supplied by a **balanced three-phase source** producing voltages and currents that are equal in magnitude and displaced from each other by **120°** electrical.

When a balanced three-phase load is energized, the instantaneous power delivered to the load is **constant (time-invariant)**, unlike single-phase systems where it pulsates at twice the supply frequency. The total power transferred to the load is categorized into three primary forms:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Mandate)**
> 1. **Active Power (P)** — The *real*, *true*, or *useful* power actually consumed by the load, measured in **Watts (W)** or **Kilowatts (kW)**. It represents the average rate of energy transfer that performs mechanical work, generates heat, or produces light.
> 2. **Reactive Power (Q)** — The *imaginary* or *wattless* power that oscillates between the source and the load's reactive (inductive/capacitive) energy-storing elements, measured in **Volt-Ampere Reactive (VAR)** or **kVAR**. It performs no useful work but is essential to sustain the magnetic/electric fields.
> 3. **Apparent Power (S)** — The *vector sum* of active and reactive power, equal to the product of RMS voltage and RMS current, measured in **Volt-Amperes (VA)** or **kVA**. It represents the total power the source must "appear" to supply.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Beer Glass" Visualization

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Beer Mug**
> Imagine a mug of beer:
> - **Beer (the liquid part)** → **Active Power (P)** → This is the *useful content* you actually drink.
> - **Foam (the frothy head)** → **Reactive Power (Q)** → It takes up volume, you cannot drink it, but the glass wouldn't feel "full" without it.
> - **Full Mug (beer + foam)** → **Apparent Power (S)** → This is what the bartender *actually fills* and what you *pay for by volume*.
> - The angle between the foam and the beer (the **Power Factor Angle φ**) determines how much "beer" you get for the "mug" — i.e., how *efficient* your electrical consumption really is.

In a **balanced 3-phase load**, you have *three* such mugs being filled simultaneously, each displaced by **120°** in phase — yet the *total volume* (total instantaneous power) remains remarkably **constant**, which is one of the most important advantages of three-phase power systems.

### 1.3 The Power Triangle

The three power quantities form a right-angled triangle known as the **Power Triangle**:

- **Base (Adjacent side)** = Active Power $P$
- **Vertical (Opposite side)** = Reactive Power $Q$
- **Hypotenuse** = Apparent Power $S$
- **Angle between P and S** = Power Factor Angle $\phi$

### 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - The **2024 KTU Scheme** (NEP 2020 aligned) emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. The Course Outcomes (COs) for Module 2 demand that students *analyze* and *evaluate* three-phase power systems, including power factor correction and measurement techniques.
> - Direct questions of **7 or 14 marks** are routinely asked on this topic in **Part B** of the End Semester Examination (ESE).
> - Power calculations form the **foundation for Electrical Machines, Power Systems, and Power Electronics** in higher semesters.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pre-Requisite: Phase Relationships in a Balanced 3-Φ System

For a balanced, positive-sequence 3-phase system, assume the phase voltages are:

$$V_R = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$
$$V_Y = V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)$$
$$V_B = V_m \sin(\omega t + 120°)$$

If the load has an impedance $Z = \vert Z \vert \angle \phi$, the line currents are also balanced and displaced by **120°**, lagging the corresponding phase voltages by the impedance angle $\phi$:

$$I_R = \frac{V_m}{\vert Z \vert} \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

### 2.2 Active Power (P) — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1:** Instantaneous power in phase R is $p_R = v_R \cdot i_R$.

**Step 2:** Similarly, $p_Y = v_Y \cdot i_Y$ and $p_B = v_B \cdot i_B$.

**Step 3:** Total instantaneous power = $p_R + p_Y + p_B$.

**Step 4:** When the algebraic expansion is performed (using trigonometric identities for $\sin A \sin B$), the time-varying components **cancel out** because of the 120° symmetry.

**Step 5:** The result is the steady average (active) power, which is **constant in time**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Active Power Matters in 3-Phase:**
> In motor applications, the constant torque produced (proportional to average power) eliminates the pulsating torque seen in single-phase motors, making 3-phase induction motors self-starting and mechanically smooth.

### 2.3 Reactive Power (Q) — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1:** Reactive power represents the rate of energy exchange with inductors and capacitors.

**Step 2:** In a balanced 3-phase system, the total reactive power is the **algebraic sum** of each phase's reactive power.

**Step 3:** Since each phase contributes equally, the total is simply **3 times the per-phase reactive power**.

**Step 4:** Positive Q indicates **inductive** (lagging) load; negative Q indicates **capacitive** (leading) load.

### 2.4 Apparent Power (S) — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1:** Apparent power is the product of RMS voltage and RMS current.

**Step 2:** It is a **scalar magnitude** (not a time average), representing the volt-amperes the source must supply.

**Step 3:** It establishes the rating of transformers, alternators, and transmission lines.

### 2.5 Complex Power

The unified complex power combines P and Q into a single complex number:

$$\mathbf{S} = P + jQ = S \angle \phi$$

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below summarizes **every formula** required for solving KTU exam problems on this topic. Memorize these before practicing numericals.

| **Quantity** | **Per-Phase Expression** | **Total 3-Phase Expression** | **Unit** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Active Power (P) | $V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi$ | $\sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | W (or kW) |
| Reactive Power (Q) | $V_{ph} I_{ph} \sin\phi$ | $\sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L \sin\phi$ | VAR (or kVAR) |
| Apparent Power (S) | $V_{ph} I_{ph}$ | $\sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L$ | VA (or kVA) |
| Complex Power ($\mathbf{S}$) | $V_{ph} I_{ph}^* \, \angle\phi$ | $\sqrt{3}\, V_L I_L^* \, \angle\phi$ | VA |
| Power Factor (pf) | $\cos\phi = P/S$ | $\cos\phi = P/S$ | Dimensionless |
| Star (Y) — Phase Voltage | $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$ | $I_{ph} = I_L$ | V, A |
| Delta (Δ) — Phase Voltage | $V_{ph} = V_L$ | $I_{ph} = I_L / \sqrt{3}$ | V, A |
| Reactive to Active Ratio | $\tan\phi = Q / P$ | $\tan\phi = Q / P$ | Dimensionless |
| Power Factor Correction Capacitor (Δ-conn) | $C = \dfrac{Q_c}{2\pi f V_L^2}$ | $C = \dfrac{Q_c}{2\pi f V_L^2}$ per phase | Farads |
| Power Factor Correction Capacitor (Y-conn) | $C = \dfrac{Q_c}{2\pi f V_{ph}^2}$ | $C = \dfrac{Q_c}{2\pi f V_{ph}^2}$ per phase | Farads |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Conversion — Where the $\sqrt{3}$ Comes From:**
> The factor $\sqrt{3}$ in $\sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L$ arises from the relations $V_L = \sqrt{3}\,V_{ph}$ (for star) and $I_L = \sqrt{3}\,I_{ph}$ (for delta). When you multiply $3 V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi$ and substitute the appropriate relations, the result is **identical** for both star and delta — which is the beauty of the 3-phase system.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where is this applied?**
> 1. **Industrial Power Billing** — Electricity boards charge industries based on **kVAh** (apparent energy), not kWh, because utilities must supply the total apparent power regardless of how much real work is done. This incentivizes industries to maintain a **high power factor** (typically ≥ 0.95).
> 2. **Transformer and Alternator Sizing** — Rated in **kVA/MVA** because the heating losses depend on current (apparent power), not on useful power alone.
> 3. **Power Factor Correction (PFC)** — Capacitor banks are installed in factories to offset inductive Q from motors, reducing line losses and avoiding utility penalties.
> 4. **Smart Grid & Renewable Integration** — Modern inverters in solar/wind farms must operate at controlled power factors per grid codes (Indian Electricity Grid Code 2023 mandates pf between 0.95 lag and 0.95 lead).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Total Active Power in a Balanced 3-Phase Load

**Given:**
- Balanced star-connected load with phase impedance $Z = \vert Z \vert \angle \phi$.
- Phase voltage: $V_{ph}$ (RMS), Phase current: $I_{ph}$ (RMS).
- Line voltage: $V_L = \sqrt{3}\,V_{ph}$, Line current: $I_L = I_{ph}$.

**Step 1 — Express instantaneous phase voltages and currents (with R-phase as reference):**

$$v_R(t) = \sqrt{2}\,V_{ph}\sin(\omega t)$$
$$i_R(t) = \sqrt{2}\,I_{ph}\sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 2 — Instantaneous power in phase R:**

$$p_R(t) = v_R \cdot i_R = 2 V_{ph} I_{ph} \sin(\omega t)\sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the trigonometric identity** $\sin A \sin B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$:

$$p_R(t) = V_{ph} I_{ph}\left[\cos(\phi) - \cos(2\omega t - \phi)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Write similar expressions for Y and B phases (each displaced by 120°):**

$$p_Y(t) = V_{ph} I_{ph}\left[\cos(\phi) - \cos(2\omega t - 240° - \phi)\right]$$
$$p_B(t) = V_{ph} I_{ph}\left[\cos(\phi) - \cos(2\omega t + 240° - \phi)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Sum the three instantaneous powers:**

$$p_{total}(t) = p_R + p_Y + p_B = 3V_{ph}I_{ph}\cos\phi - V_{ph}I_{ph}\left[\cos(2\omega t - \phi) + \cos(2\omega t - \phi - 240°) + \cos(2\omega t - \phi + 240°)\right]$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the identity** $\cos\theta + \cos(\theta - 120°) + \cos(\theta + 120°) = 0$:

The three cosine terms inside the brackets sum to **zero**.

**Step 7 — Final Result — Total Active Power is constant:**

$$p_{total}(t) = 3V_{ph}I_{ph}\cos\phi = P_{total}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The total instantaneous power in a balanced 3-phase system is **constant** (no pulsations). This is a fundamental advantage over single-phase systems, where power pulsates at $2f$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the $\sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L$ Form (Valid for Both Star and Delta)

**Star Connection Case:**

$$P = 3 V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi$$

Substitute $V_{ph} = V_L / \sqrt{3}$ and $I_{ph} = I_L$:

$$\begin{aligned} P &= 3 \cdot \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi \\ &= \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi \end{aligned}$$

**Delta Connection Case:**

$$P = 3 V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi$$

Substitute $V_{ph} = V_L$ and $I_{ph} = I_L / \sqrt{3}$:

$$\begin{aligned} P &= 3 \cdot V_L \cdot \frac{I_L}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \cos\phi \\ &= \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi \end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The expression $\sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ is **universal** — it gives the correct active power for both star and delta connections as long as the load is balanced. KTU examiners *expect* you to recognize this symmetry.

### 3.3 Derivation of Reactive Power

**Step 1 — Reactive power per phase:**

$$Q_{ph} = V_{ph} I_{ph} \sin\phi$$

**Step 2 — Total reactive power for 3 phases:**

$$Q = 3 V_{ph} I_{ph} \sin\phi = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi$$

**Step 3 — Sign Convention:**

- If load is **inductive** (lagging pf) → $\phi > 0$ → $Q$ is **positive** (load absorbs vars).
- If load is **capacitive** (leading pf) → $\phi < 0$ → $Q$ is **negative** (load delivers vars).

### 3.4 Derivation of Apparent Power

**Step 1 — Apparent power per phase:**

$$S_{ph} = V_{ph} I_{ph}$$

**Step 2 — Total apparent power:**

$$S = 3 V_{ph} I_{ph} = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L$$

**Step 3 — Note that apparent power is the magnitude of complex power:**

$$S = \vert \mathbf{S} \vert = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$$

### 3.5 Derivation of Complex Power Using Phasors

**Step 1 — Define complex power per phase:**

$$\mathbf{S}_{ph} = V_{ph} I_{ph}^*$$

**Step 2 — Where $I_{ph}^*$ is the complex conjugate of the phase current phasor.**

**Step 3 — Total complex power:**

$$\mathbf{S} = 3 V_{ph} I_{ph}^*$$

**Step 4 — Expand using $V_{ph} = \vert V_{ph} \vert \angle\alpha$ and $I_{ph} = \vert I_{ph} \vert \angle(\alpha - \phi)$:**

$$\begin{aligned} \mathbf{S} &= 3 \cdot \vert V_{ph} \vert \angle\alpha \cdot \vert I_{ph} \vert \angle(-\alpha + \phi) \\ &= 3 \vert V_{ph} \vert \vert I_{ph} \vert \angle\phi \\ &= 3 V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi + j\, 3 V_{ph} I_{ph} \sin\phi \\ &= P + jQ \end{aligned}$$

### 3.6 Worked Numerical — KTU-Style Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** A balanced 3-phase, star-connected load draws a line current of **10 A** from a **400 V, 50 Hz** supply. The load phase angle is **30° lagging**. Calculate the total Active, Reactive, and Apparent power.

**Given:** $V_L = 400$ V, $I_L = 10$ A, $\phi = 30°$ (lagging), star connection.

**Step 1 — Active Power:**

$$P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 10 \times \cos(30°)$$
$$P = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 10 \times 0.866 = 6000 \text{ W} = 6 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Reactive Power:**

$$Q = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 10 \times \sin(30°)$$
$$Q = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 10 \times 0.5 = 3464 \text{ VAR} = 3.464 \text{ kVAR}$$

**Step 3 — Apparent Power:**

$$S = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 10 = 6928 \text{ VA} = 6.928 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 4 — Power Factor:**

$$\text{pf} = \cos\phi = \cos(30°) = 0.866 \text{ lagging}$$

**Step 5 — Verify using Pythagorean relation:**

$$\sqrt{P^2 + Q^2} = \sqrt{6000^2 + 3464^2} = \sqrt{36\,000\,000 + 12\,000\,000} = \sqrt{48\,000\,000} = 6928 \text{ VA} = S \;\; \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Power Triangle — Visual Schematic

The Power Triangle is the most important visualization for this topic. The Mermaid block below renders a clean block-level representation of the triangle with all labeled components.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Apparent Power S (kVA) - Hypotenuse"] -->|"Angle phi between S and P"| B["Active Power P (kW) - Base"]
    A -->|"Angle phi between S and Q"| C["Reactive Power Q (kVAR) - Vertical"]
    B -.->|"Perpendicular: P squared plus Q squared equals S squared"| C
    D["Power Factor equals cos phi equals P over S"] -.-> B
    E["Reactive Factor equals sin phi equals Q over S"] -.-> C
    F["tan phi equals Q over P"] -.-> A
```

### 4.2 Three-Phase Balanced Load — Functional Power Flow

This block diagram traces the flow of power from the balanced source through the line parameters to the balanced load, indicating where each type of power is consumed or exchanged.

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC["Balanced 3-Phase Source - V_L at 50 Hz"] -->|"Transmits S total kVA"| LINE["3-Phase Transmission Line"]
    LINE -->|"Delivers S load kVA to terminals"| LOAD["Balanced 3-Phase Load - Z equals magnitude Z at angle phi"]
    LOAD -->|"Absorbs P equals sqrt 3 V_L I_L cos phi"| AP["Active Power Section - Heat, Light, Mechanical Work"]
    LOAD -->|"Exchanges Q equals sqrt 3 V_L I_L sin phi"| RP["Reactive Power Section - Inductors and Capacitors Store Energy"]
    AP -->|"P consumed as useful energy"| OUT1["Output: Real Work Done"]
    RP -->|"Q oscillates between source and load at 2f"| OUT2["Output: No Net Energy Consumed"]
```

### 4.3 Power Factor Correction — Capacitor Bank Insertion

The block diagram below illustrates the standard **power factor correction topology** used in industrial plants.

```mermaid
graph TD
    MAIN["3-Phase Supply: 400 V, 50 Hz"] --> BUS["Main Distribution Bus"]
    BUS --> LOAD1["Inductive Load - Motor, Transformer - pf 0.7 lag"]
    BUS --> LOAD2["Capacitor Bank - Delta or Star Connected - Provides Leading Q"]
    LOAD1 -->|"Draws lagging Q1 kVAR"| P1["Total Reactive Power: Q1 minus Qc"]
    LOAD2 -->|"Supplies leading Qc kVAR"| P1
    P1 -->|"Corrected apparent power: S new less than S old"| OUT["Improved Power Factor - Reduced Line Current - Lower I-squared R Losses"]
```

### 4.4 Two-Wattmeter Method (3-Phase Power Measurement)

The two-wattmeter method is the **standard KTU exam topic** for measuring 3-phase power. The Mermaid block below shows the connections.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Line R - Current I_R through Wattmeter 1 Current Coil"] --> WC1["Wattmeter W1 - Reads W1"]
    B["Line Y - Current I_Y through Wattmeter 2 Current Coil"] --> WC2["Wattmeter W2 - Reads W2"]
    WC1 -->|"Voltage Coil between R and B"| VCB["Voltage Reference: Line B"]
    WC2 -->|"Voltage Coil between Y and B"| VCB
    A --> LOAD["Balanced 3-Phase Load"]
    B --> LOAD
    C["Line B - Common Reference"] --> LOAD
    TOTAL["Total Power P equals W1 plus W2 equals sqrt 3 V_L I_L cos phi"] -.-> WC1
    TOTAL -.-> WC2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams in Your KTU Exam:**
> When asked to draw the power triangle, label the **three sides (P, Q, S)**, the **angle φ**, and explicitly write the **cos φ** and **sin φ** relations. Examiners award partial marks for the *diagram* even if the numerical part is partially incorrect.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define the following terms in a 3-phase AC circuit:
**(a)** Active Power, **(b)** Reactive Power, **(c)** Apparent Power.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Active Power (P):** The actual useful power consumed by the load to perform real work (heat, light, mechanical motion). It is measured in **Watts (W)** and is the average value of instantaneous power. Formula: $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$.

**(b) Reactive Power (Q):** The power that oscillates between the source and the reactive elements (inductors/capacitors) of the load. It performs no net work but sustains magnetic/electric fields. Measured in **VAR**. Formula: $Q = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi$.

**(c) Apparent Power (S):** The product of RMS voltage and RMS current, representing the total power the source must "appear" to supply. Measured in **VA**. Formula: $S = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
