What is meant by a **balanced load** in a 3-phase system? State the expression for total active power in a balanced load.

**Model Answer:**

A **balanced load** is one in which the three phase impedances are identical in both magnitude and phase angle: $Z_R = Z_Y = Z_B = \vert Z \vert \angle\phi$. In such a system, the line currents are also balanced — equal in magnitude and displaced from each other by 120° electrical.

The total active power delivered to a balanced 3-phase load is:

$$P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$$

where $V_L$ is the line voltage, $I_L$ is the line current, and $\cos\phi$ is the power factor.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expressions for **Active Power**, **Reactive Power**, and **Apparent Power** in a balanced 3-phase star-connected load. Show that total instantaneous power is **constant in time**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A balanced 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz supply feeds a star-connected load with phase impedance $Z = (8 + j6)\,\Omega$. Calculate: **(i)** Phase current and line current, **(ii)** Total active power, **(iii)** Total reactive power, **(iv)** Apparent power, **(v)** Power factor. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Consider a balanced star load with phase voltage $V_{ph}$ and phase current $I_{ph}$, with impedance angle $\phi$.

**Step 2:** Write the R-phase instantaneous voltage and current:

$$v_R(t) = \sqrt{2}\,V_{ph}\sin(\omega t)$$
$$i_R(t) = \sqrt{2}\,I_{ph}\sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 3:** Instantaneous power in R-phase using the identity $2\sin A \sin B = \cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)$:

$$p_R(t) = V_{ph}I_{ph}\cos\phi - V_{ph}I_{ph}\cos(2\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 4:** Similarly for Y and B phases (displaced by $\pm 120°$):

$$p_Y(t) = V_{ph}I_{ph}\cos\phi - V_{ph}I_{ph}\cos(2\omega t - \phi - 240°)$$
$$p_B(t) = V_{ph}I_{ph}\cos\phi - V_{ph}I_{ph}\cos(2\omega t - \phi + 240°)$$

**Step 5:** Sum the three powers:

$$p_{total}(t) = 3V_{ph}I_{ph}\cos\phi - V_{ph}I_{ph}\left[\cos(2\omega t - \phi) + \cos(2\omega t - \phi - 240°) + \cos(2\omega t - \phi + 240°)\right]$$

**Step 6:** Apply the identity $\cos\theta + \cos(\theta - 120°) + \cos(\theta + 120°) = 0$:

$$p_{total}(t) = 3V_{ph}I_{ph}\cos\phi = P_{total} = \text{constant}$$

**Step 7 — Resulting Power Expressions:**

$$P = 3V_{ph}I_{ph}\cos\phi = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$$
$$Q = 3V_{ph}I_{ph}\sin\phi = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi$$
$$S = 3V_{ph}I_{ph} = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $V_L = 400$ V (star), $f = 50$ Hz, $Z = 8 + j6\,\Omega$.

**Step 1 — Magnitude and angle of Z:**

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{8^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{64 + 36} = \sqrt{100} = 10\,\Omega$$
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{6}{8}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.75) = 36.87°$$

**Step 2 — Phase Voltage:**

$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{400}{\sqrt{3}} = 230.94 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — (i) Phase and Line Current:**

$$I_{ph} = \frac{V_{ph}}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{230.94}{10} = 23.094 \text{ A}$$
$$I_L = I_{ph} = 23.094 \text{ A (for star)}$$

**Step 4 — (ii) Active Power:**

$$P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 23.094 \times \cos(36.87°)$$
$$P = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 23.094 \times 0.8 = 12\,800 \text{ W} = 12.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — (iii) Reactive Power:**

$$Q = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 23.094 \times 0.6$$
$$Q = 9600 \text{ VAR} = 9.6 \text{ kVAR}$$

**Step 6 — (iv) Apparent Power:**

$$S = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L = \sqrt{3} \times 400 \times 23.094 = 16\,000 \text{ VA} = 16 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 7 — (v) Power Factor:**

$$\text{pf} = \cos\phi = \cos(36.87°) = 0.8 \text{ lagging}$$

**Step 8 — Verification:**

$$\sqrt{P^2 + Q^2} = \sqrt{12\,800^2 + 9600^2} = \sqrt{163.84 \times 10^6 + 92.16 \times 10^6} = \sqrt{256 \times 10^6} = 16\,000 \text{ VA} = S \;\; \checkmark$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **complex power** in a 3-phase system. Derive the relationship $P + jQ = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \angle\phi$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A balanced 3-phase delta-connected load of impedance $Z = (12 + j5)\,\Omega$ per phase is connected to a **440 V, 50 Hz** supply. Find the **total active power**, **reactive power**, **apparent power**, and **power factor** of the load. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Complex Power Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Define Complex Power per phase:**

$$\mathbf{S}_{ph} = V_{ph} I_{ph}^*$$

where $I_{ph}^*$ is the complex conjugate of the current phasor.

**Step 2 — Express phase voltage and current phasors:**

$$V_{ph} = \vert V_{ph} \vert \angle 0° = V_{ph} \angle 0°$$
$$I_{ph} = \vert I_{ph} \vert \angle(-\phi) = I_{ph} \angle(-\phi)$$
$$I_{ph}^* = I_{ph} \angle(+\phi)$$

**Step 3 — Multiply to get per-phase complex power:**

$$\mathbf{S}_{ph} = V_{ph} \angle 0° \cdot I_{ph} \angle(+\phi) = V_{ph} I_{ph} \angle\phi$$

**Step 4 — Total 3-phase complex power:**

$$\mathbf{S} = 3\mathbf{S}_{ph} = 3 V_{ph} I_{ph} \angle\phi$$

**Step 5 — Convert to line quantities:** For star, $V_{ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$, $I_{ph} = I_L$:

$$\begin{aligned} \mathbf{S} &= 3 \cdot \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot I_L \angle\phi \\ &= \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \angle\phi \\ &= \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L(\cos\phi + j\sin\phi) \\ &= \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi + j\sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi \\ &= P + jQ \end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $\boxed{\mathbf{S} = P + jQ = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \angle\phi}$ — valid for both star and delta.

---

**Part (b) — Delta-Connected Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $V_L = 440$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $Z = 12 + j5\,\Omega$ per phase, delta connection.

**Step 1 — Magnitude and angle of Z:**

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{12^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{144 + 25} = \sqrt{169} = 13\,\Omega$$
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{5}{12}\right) = 22.62°$$

**Step 2 — Phase Voltage (delta):**

$$V_{ph} = V_L = 440 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Phase Current:**

$$I_{ph} = \frac{V_{ph}}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{440}{13} = 33.846 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Line Current (delta):**

$$I_L = \sqrt{3}\,I_{ph} = \sqrt{3} \times 33.846 = 58.62 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5 — Total Active Power:**

$$P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 440 \times 58.62 \times \cos(22.62°)$$
$$P = \sqrt{3} \times 440 \times 58.62 \times 0.9231 = 41\,250 \text{ W} \approx 41.25 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Total Reactive Power:**

$$Q = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 440 \times 58.62 \times \sin(22.62°)$$
$$Q = \sqrt{3} \times 440 \times 58.62 \times 0.3846 = 17\,188 \text{ VAR} \approx 17.19 \text{ kVAR}$$

**Step 7 — Total Apparent Power:**

$$S = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L = \sqrt{3} \times 440 \times 58.62 = 44\,670 \text{ VA} \approx 44.67 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 8 — Power Factor:**

$$\text{pf} = \cos\phi = \cos(22.62°) = 0.9231 \text{ lagging}$$

**Step 9 — Verification (alternative method using per-phase):**

$$P = 3 V_{ph} I_{ph} \cos\phi = 3 \times 440 \times 33.846 \times 0.9231 = 41\,250 \text{ W} \;\; \checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Phase and Line Quantities:** Many students blindly apply $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ even when asked for *per-phase* power. Always verify whether the question asks for **total** or **per-phase** power.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$ Conversion in Delta:** When computing line current from phase current in delta, **must** use $I_L = \sqrt{3}\,I_{ph}$ and $V_{ph} = V_L$.
> 3. **Sign of Reactive Power:** For capacitive (leading) loads, **Q is negative**. Examiners deduct marks if you write Q as positive without specifying the leading/lagging nature.
> 4. **Units in Final Answer:** Always express in standard form: W, kW, VAR, kVAR, VA, kVA — **never** in scientific notation without units. KTU key answers penalize missing units.
> 5. **Power Triangle Not Drawn:** Even in numericals, drawing a **labeled power triangle** showing P, Q, S, and $\phi$ fetches 1–2 extra marks (image of understanding).
> 6. **Two-Wattmeter Method Formula Missing:** If the question is on the **two-wattmeter method**, write both $W_1 = V_L I_L \cos(30° - \phi)$ and $W_2 = V_L I_L \cos(30° + \phi)$, and the total $P = W_1 + W_2 = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$. Examiners strictly check these.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before the exam):**
> - **Active Power** = $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ — useful work, in **Watts**.
> - **Reactive Power** = $Q = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \sin\phi$ — non-working, in **VAR**.
> - **Apparent Power** = $S = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L$ — total supply, in **VA**.
> - **Power Factor** = $\cos\phi = P/S$ — always between **0 and 1**.
> - **Power Triangle:** $S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$, with $P$ on base, $Q$ on vertical, $S$ as hypotenuse.
> - **Star (Y):** $V_{ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$, $I_{ph} = I_L$.
> - **Delta (Δ):** $V_{ph} = V_L$, $I_{ph} = I_L/\sqrt{3}$.
> - **Total instantaneous power in a balanced 3-phase system is CONSTANT** — pulsations cancel out due to 120° symmetry.
> - **Complex Power:** $\mathbf{S} = P + jQ = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \angle\phi$.
> - **Lagging pf** → Inductive load (motors, transformers) → Correct with **shunt capacitors**.
> - **Leading pf** → Capacitive load (over-corrected systems) → Correct with **inductors or de-rating capacitor banks**.
> - **Two-Wattmeter Method:** $W_1 + W_2 = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ and $W_1 - W_2 = V_L I_L \sin\phi$ → $\tan\phi = \sqrt{3}\,(W_1 - W_2)/(W_1 + W_2)$.
> - **KTU 2024 Hot Tip:** Always write **units**, draw the **power triangle**, and verify $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$ at the end of every numerical.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steady State Analysis of 3-Wire Unbalanced Delta Connected Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET302 - CIRCUITS & NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Steady State Analysis of 3-Wire Unbalanced Delta Connected Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **3-wire system** is a three-phase supply configuration in which only the three line conductors (R, Y, B) are run from the source to the load — there is **no neutral (N) wire**. In a **delta (Δ) connected load**, the three load impedances $Z_{ab}$, $Z_{bc}$ and $Z_{ca}$ are connected end-to-end to form a closed mesh, with each junction tied to one of the three incoming lines.

A circuit is called **unbalanced** when the three branch impedances are not identical in magnitude or phase, i.e. $Z_{ab} \neq Z_{bc} \neq Z_{ca}$. The term **steady-state** restricts the analysis to the phasor (sinusoidal steady) regime at a single frequency, where all voltages and currents are represented as complex numbers of constant amplitude and angle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Insight:** In a delta-connected load, each branch is connected **directly across two line terminals**. Therefore, the *branch voltage is exactly the line voltage of the source*, irrespective of whether the load is balanced or unbalanced, and irrespective of whether a neutral wire exists. This is the single most important property exploited in 3-wire delta analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCEET302 / Module 2):** The module specifically tests (i) the ability to compute phase and line currents of an unbalanced delta load and (ii) the computation of total 3-phase active and reactive power using the *branch-by-branch* method, not the balanced $\sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ shortcut (which is invalid for unbalanced loads).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine three friends — *A, B, C* — standing at the vertices of a triangle. They are joined by three direct walkways (the delta branches), each of a different length and slope (the impedances $Z_{ab}, Z_{bc}, Z_{ca}$). A loudspeaker system at each corner announces a different tone (the three line voltages $V_{ab}, V_{bc}, V_{ca}$).

- The **voltage across each walkway is dictated by the loudspeakers at its two ends** — there is no "central pole" (neutral) needed.
- Even if the three walkways are of unequal length and slope, *each walker on a particular walkway only sees the two tones at his ends*. The walker's speed (current) depends solely on the voltage difference at his own two ends and the impedance of his own walkway.
- The "crowd flowing into corner A" (line current $I_a$) is the difference between what walks in from the AB side and what walks out toward the CA side — exactly Kirchhoff's Current Law.

This per-branch independence is what makes 3-wire delta analysis comparatively straightforward compared to 3-wire star analysis, where the absence of a neutral forces one to solve simultaneous loop equations (or invoke Millman's theorem) because no branch voltage is known a priori.

## 1.3 Standard KTU Nomenclature and Symbols

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Magnitude (India) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V_L$ | Line-to-line RMS voltage | **415 V** (LT) / **11 kV** (HT) |
| $I_L$ | Line RMS current | Depends on load |
| $V_{ab}, V_{bc}, V_{ca}$ | Branch (line) voltages of delta | $V_L$ each |
| $I_{ab}, I_{bc}, I_{ca}$ | Branch (phase) currents | $V_L / Z$ each |
| $I_a, I_b, I_c$ | Line currents | Phasor difference |
| **f** | System frequency | **50 Hz** (India) |
| Phase sequence | Order of phase attainment | RYB (positive) / RBY (negative) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why the "Balanced" Shortcuts Fail for Unbalanced Delta

For a *balanced* delta load ($Z_{ab} = Z_{bc} = Z_{ca} = Z$), symmetry forces all branch currents to be equal in magnitude and displaced by **120°**, and the line current equals $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase current with a 30° phase shift. **None of these symmetry relations hold for an unbalanced load** — the branch currents have *different magnitudes* and *unequal phase separations*, so the line currents must be computed branch-by-branch using the complex KCL equations below.

## 2.2 The KTU-Standard Analytical Strategy (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Identify source data** — confirm the system is 3-wire (no neutral), note the line voltage $V_L$, the frequency $f = \mathbf{50\,Hz}$ and the phase sequence (default **RYB** unless stated otherwise).
2. **Construct the three line voltages as a balanced set with the stated $V_L$** — these are the three branch voltages of the delta mesh.
3. **Compute the three branch (phase) currents** using Ohm's law in the phasor domain: $I_{xy} = V_{xy} / Z_{xy}$.
4. **Apply KCL at each of the three external nodes** to obtain the three line currents as differences of branch currents.
5. **Validate** with $I_a + I_b + I_c = 0$ (mandatory KTU check).
6. **Compute power branch-wise** — never use the balanced $\sqrt{3}V_L I_L\cos\phi$ formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase-Sequence Dependency:** For an unbalanced delta, the *line currents are different* under RYB vs RBY sequences (unlike the balanced case where only the sequence of rotation is reversed). The KTU paper frequently tests this with a "comment on the effect of phase sequence" sub-question.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All expressions below are **phasor (complex) equations**. Magnitudes and angles are extracted at the end.

| # | Quantity | Phasor / Magnitude Formula | Units / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Source line voltage set (RYB) | $V_{ab} = V_L \angle 30^\circ,\;\; V_{bc} = V_L \angle -90^\circ,\;\; V_{ca} = V_L \angle 150^\circ$ | Built from $V_{an} = V_L/\sqrt{3}\angle 0^\circ$ |
| 2 | Source constraint (always true) | $V_{ab} + V_{bc} + V_{ca} = 0$ | Validates the source set |
| 3 | Branch (phase) current | $I_{ab} = \dfrac{V_{ab}}{Z_{ab}},\;\; I_{bc} = \dfrac{V_{bc}}{Z_{bc}},\;\; I_{ca} = \dfrac{V_{ca}}{Z_{ca}}$ | Direct Ohm's law |
| 4 | Line current (KCL at node a) | $I_a = I_{ab} - I_{ca}$ | Current *leaving* through AB, *entering* from CA |
| 5 | Line current (KCL at node b) | $I_b = I_{bc} - I_{ab}$ | |
| 6 | Line current (KCL at node c) | $I_c = I_{ca} - I_{bc}$ | |
| 7 | KCL check (mandatory) | $I_a + I_b + I_c = 0$ | Catches sign / arithmetic errors |
| 8 | Complex power per branch | $S_{ab} = V_{ab}\, I_{ab}^{*}$ | $S = P + jQ$ in **VA** |
| 9 | Active power per branch | $P_{ab} = \vert I_{ab} \vert^{2} R_{ab} = \operatorname{Re}\{S_{ab}\}$ | **Watts (W)** |
| 10 | Reactive power per branch | $Q_{ab} = \vert I_{ab} \vert^{2} X_{ab} = \operatorname{Im}\{S_{ab}\}$ | **VAR** |
| 11 | Total 3-phase power | $S_{T} = S_{ab} + S_{bc} + S_{ca}$ | Branch-sum method |
| 12 | Apparent power magnitude | $\vert S_{T} \vert = \sqrt{P_{T}^{2} + Q_{T}^{2}}$ | **VA** |
| 13 | Power factor of system | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{P_{T}}{\vert S_{T} \vert}$ | Overall, *not* per phase |
| 14 | Star-delta equivalence (for cross-checks) | $Z_{Y} = \dfrac{Z_{\Delta}}{3}$ (balanced only) | **Not used for unbalanced** — keep in mind |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Unbalanced delta analysis is foundational to every power-engineering scenario where symmetry is broken:

- **Fault analysis** (line-to-line faults, open-phase faults) in transmission and distribution networks.
- **Industrial load assessment** — large single-phase loads (welding sets, induction furnaces, traction rectifiers) connected across two phases create deliberate unbalance.
- **Phase-sequence detection** in motor protection relays — the unbalanced current signature reveals the actual sequence.
- **Power-quality auditing** — utilities compute the *degree of unbalance* (NEMA / IEC definitions) from these very branch currents.
- **DER integration** — single-phase rooftop solar inverters feeding a 3-phase delta-coupled transformer bank are a modern unbalanced-delta scenario.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Line-Current Expressions from KCL

Consider the delta mesh with external terminals A, B, C and the three internal nodes coinciding with the terminals (each terminal of a delta is both a line and a mesh node).

**At node A:** The branch current $I_{ab}$ flows *out* of A into the AB branch, while $I_{ca}$ flows *into* A from the CA branch. KCL (sum of currents leaving = 0) gives:

$$
I_a - I_{ab} + I_{ca} = 0
$$

Solving for the line current:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_a &= I_{ab} - I_{ca}
\end{aligned}
$$

**At node B:** Branch $I_{bc}$ leaves B; branch $I_{ab}$ enters B from the AB side. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_b &= I_{bc} - I_{ab}
\end{aligned}
$$

**At node C:** Branch $I_{ca}$ leaves C; branch $I_{bc}$ enters C from the BC side. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_c &= I_{ca} - I_{bc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**KCL consistency check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_a + I_b + I_c &= (I_{ab} - I_{ca}) + (I_{bc} - I_{ab}) + (I_{ca} - I_{bc}) \\
&= 0 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, of the three line-current equations, only **two are independent** — the third is always redundant and acts as the validation.

## 3.2 Derivation of Branch-Power Expressions

Let the branch impedance be $Z_{xy} = R_{xy} + jX_{xy} = \vert Z_{xy} \vert \angle \theta_{xy}$, with branch voltage $V_{xy} = \vert V_{xy} \vert \angle \alpha_{xy}$. Then the branch current is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{xy} = \frac{V_{xy}}{Z_{xy}} = \frac{\vert V_{xy} \vert}{\vert Z_{xy} \vert}\,\angle(\alpha_{xy} - \theta_{xy})
\end{aligned}
$$

The complex power absorbed by the branch (passive-sign convention) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{xy} &= V_{xy}\, I_{xy}^{*} \\
       &= \vert V_{xy} \vert\, \vert I_{xy} \vert\,\angle(\alpha_{xy} - (\alpha_{xy} - \theta_{xy})) \\
       &= \vert V_{xy} \vert\, \vert I_{xy} \vert\,\angle\theta_{xy} \\
       &= \vert V_{xy} \vert\, \vert I_{xy} \vert\,(\cos\theta_{xy} + j\sin\theta_{xy}) \\
       &= \vert I_{xy} \vert^{2} R_{xy} + j\,\vert I_{xy} \vert^{2} X_{xy}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total three-phase complex power is the *branch sum* (NOT $\sqrt{3}V_L I_L \cos\phi$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_T = S_{ab} + S_{bc} + S_{ca} = P_T + jQ_T
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — Single-Phase Sequence RYB

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**

A 3-phase, 3-wire, **400 V**, **50 Hz** supply with phase sequence **RYB** feeds an unbalanced delta load whose branch impedances are:

$$
Z_{ab} = 10 \angle 0^\circ \,\Omega,\qquad Z_{bc} = 10 \angle 36.87^\circ \,\Omega,\qquad Z_{ca} = 5\sqrt{2} \angle -45^\circ \,\Omega
$$

**Required:** (a) the three branch (phase) currents; (b) the three line currents with KCL check; (c) the total active, reactive and apparent power, and the overall power factor.

### Step 1 — Construct the source line voltages

Taking $V_{an} = \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} \angle 0^\circ = \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{3}} \angle 0^\circ = 230.94 \angle 0^\circ$ V, the line voltages for RYB sequence are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ab} &= \sqrt{3}\, V_{an} \angle +30^\circ = 400 \angle 30^\circ \text{ V} \\
V_{bc} &= \sqrt{3}\, V_{an} \angle -90^\circ = 400 \angle -90^\circ \text{ V} \\
V_{ca} &= \sqrt{3}\, V_{an} \angle +150^\circ = 400 \angle 150^\circ \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Source consistency check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{ab} + V_{bc} + V_{ca} &= 400\angle 30^\circ + 400\angle -90^\circ + 400\angle 150^\circ \\
&= 400\,[\cos 30^\circ + \cos(-90^\circ) + \cos 150^\circ] + j\,400\,[\sin 30^\circ + \sin(-90^\circ) + \sin 150^\circ] \\
&= 400\,[0.8660 + 0 - 0.8660] + j\,400\,[0.5 - 1.0 + 0.5] \\
&= 0 + j0 = 0 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the three branch (phase) currents

**Branch AB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{ab} &= \frac{V_{ab}}{Z_{ab}} = \frac{400 \angle 30^\circ}{10 \angle 0^\circ} = 40 \angle 30^\circ \text{ A} \\
       &= 40(\cos 30^\circ + j\sin 30^\circ) = 34.64 + j\,20.00 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Branch BC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{bc} &= \frac{V_{bc}}{Z_{bc}} = \frac{400 \angle -90^\circ}{10 \angle 36.87^\circ} = 40 \angle (-90^\circ - 36.87^\circ) = 40 \angle -126.87^\circ \text{ A} \\
       &= 40(\cos(-126.87^\circ) + j\sin(-126.87^\circ)) \\
       &= 40(-0.6 - j\,0.8) = -24.00 - j\,32.00 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Branch CA:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{ca} &= \frac{V_{ca}}{Z_{ca}} = \frac{400 \angle 150^\circ}{7.071 \angle -45^\circ} = 56.57 \angle (150^\circ + 45^\circ) = 56.57 \angle 195^\circ \text{ A} \\
       &= 56.57(\cos 195^\circ + j\sin 195^\circ) \\
       &= 56.57(-0.9659 - j\,0.2588) = -54.64 - j\,14.64 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the three line currents using KCL

**Line current $I_a = I_{ab} - I_{ca}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_a &= (34.64 + j\,20.00) - (-54.64 - j\,14.64) \\
    &= (34.64 + 54.64) + j\,(20.00 + 14.64) \\
    &= 89.28 + j\,34.64 \\
\vert I_a \vert &= \sqrt{89.28^{2} + 34.64^{2}} = \sqrt{7970.9 + 1199.9} = \sqrt{9170.8} = 95.76 \text{ A} \\
\angle I_a &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{34.64}{89.28}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.3880) = 21.19^\circ \\
\therefore\;\; I_a &= 95.76 \angle 21.19^\circ \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Line current $I_b = I_{bc} - I_{ab}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_b &= (-24.00 - j\,32.00) - (34.64 + j\,20.00) \\
    &= -58.64 - j\,52.00 \\
\vert I_b \vert &= \sqrt{58.64^{2} + 52.00^{2}} = \sqrt{3438.6 + 2704.0} = \sqrt{6142.6} = 78.38 \text{ A} \\
\angle I_b &= 180^\circ + \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{52.00}{58.64}\right) = 180^\circ + 41.57^\circ = 221.57^\circ \equiv -138.43^\circ \\
\therefore\;\; I_b &= 78.38 \angle -138.43^\circ \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Line current $I_c = I_{ca} - I_{bc}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_c &= (-54.64 - j\,14.64) - (-24.00 - j\,32.00) \\
    &= -30.64 + j\,17.36 \\
\vert I_c \vert &= \sqrt{30.64^{2} + 17.36^{2}} = \sqrt{938.8 + 301.4} = \sqrt{1240.2} = 35.22 \text{ A} \\
\angle I_c &= 180^\circ - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{17.36}{30.64}\right) = 180^\circ - 29.53^\circ = 150.47^\circ \\
\therefore\;\; I_c &= 35.22 \angle 150.47^\circ \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — KCL validation (mandatory in KTU scripts)

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_a + I_b + I_c &= (89.28 + j\,34.64) + (-58.64 - j\,52.00) + (-30.64 + j\,17.36) \\
                &= (89.28 - 58.64 - 30.64) + j\,(34.64 - 52.00 + 17.36) \\
                &= 0.00 + j\,0.00 = 0 \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute branch and total power

**Branch AB** ($R_{ab}=10\,\Omega$, $X_{ab}=0$):

$$
P_{ab} = \vert I_{ab} \vert^{2} R_{ab} = 40^{2} \times 10 = 16000 \text{ W}; \quad Q_{ab} = 0
$$

**Branch BC** ($R_{bc}=8\,\Omega$, $X_{bc}=6\,\Omega$):

$$
P_{bc} = 40^{2} \times 8 = 12800 \text{ W}; \quad Q_{bc} = 40^{2} \times 6 = 9600 \text{ VAR}
$$

**Branch CA** ($R_{ca}=5\,\Omega$, $X_{ca}=-5\,\Omega$ — capacitive):

$$
P_{ca} = 56.57^{2} \times 5 = 3200 \times 5 = 16000 \text{ W}; \quad Q_{ca} = 56.57^{2} \times (-5) = -16000 \text{ VAR}
$$

**Totals:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_T &= 16000 + 12800 + 16000 = 44800 \text{ W} = 44.8 \text{ kW} \\
Q_T &= 0 + 9600 - 16000 = -6400 \text{ VAR} = -6.4 \text{ kVAR} \quad (\text{net capacitive}) \\
\vert S_T \vert &= \sqrt{44800^{2} + 6400^{2}} = \sqrt{2.007 \times 10^{9} + 4.10 \times 10^{7}} = 45254 \text{ VA} \approx 45.25 \text{ kVA} \\
\cos\phi &= \frac{44800}{45254} = 0.9900 \text{ (leading)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative $Q_T$ tells us the load is **overall capacitive** — a direct consequence of the $X_{ca} = -5\,\Omega$ (capacitor) branch dominating the reactive budget. This is a classic KTU "trick" to test whether the student remembers that unbalance can make the *overall* pf leading even when individual branches are inductive.

### Step 6 — Cross-verification using complex-power method

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{ab} &= V_{ab} I_{ab}^{*} = 400\angle 30^\circ \times 40\angle -30^\circ = 16000\angle 0^\circ = 16000 + j0 \\
S_{bc} &= 400\angle -90^\circ \times 40\angle 126.87^\circ = 16000\angle 36.87^\circ = 12800 + j9600 \\
S_{ca} &= 400\angle 150^\circ \times 56.57\angle -195^\circ = 22628\angle -45^\circ = 16000 - j16000
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing:

$$
S_T = (16000+12800+16000) + j(0+9600-16000) = 44800 - j6400 \;\;\checkmark
$$

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Wire Delta Load — Circuit Schematic

The diagram below is a **functional block schematic** of a 3-phase, 3-wire delta load. The three source line terminals A, B, C feed the delta mesh of impedances $Z_{ab}$, $Z_{bc}$, $Z_{ca}$. Notice that there is **no neutral wire** in the supply.

```mermaid
graph TB
    src[3-PHASE 3-WIRE SUPPLY\n400 V  50 Hz] --> termA[Terminal A]
    src --> termB[Terminal B]
    src --> termC[Terminal C]
    termA --- impAB[IMPEDANCE Zab]
    impAB --- termB
    termB --- impBC[IMPEDANCE Zbc]
    impBC --- termC
    termC --- impCA[IMPEDANCE Zca]
    impCA --- termA
    termA --> linA[LINE A  Ia]
    termB --> linB[LINE B  Ib]
    termC --> linC[LINE C  Ic]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The KCL relations $I_a = I_{ab} - I_{ca}$, $I_b = I_{bc} - I_{ab}$, $I_c = I_{ca} - I_{bc}$ can be visually confirmed by tracing current *leaving* each terminal: the line current equals the difference between the two branch currents incident at that terminal.

## 4.2 Phasor Diagram of Source Line Voltages (RYB Sequence)

The three line voltages $V_{ab}$, $V_{bc}$, $V_{ca}$ form a closed, balanced set displaced by **120°**. The diagram below shows their relative positions; in a real engineering drawing the magnitudes would all be equal to $V_L$ and the angles would be exactly 30°, −90°, 150° respectively.

```mermaid
graph LR
    refO[ORIGIN] --> vabV[Vab  400 V  30 deg]
    vabV -.->|120 deg| vbcV[Vbc  400 V  minus 90 deg]
    vbcV -.->|120 deg| vcaV[Vca  400 V  150 deg]
    vcaV -.->|120 deg closes the loop| refO
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Solving an Unbalanced Delta Problem

The flowchart below maps the *interactions* between the analytical stages of solving any unbalanced 3-wire delta problem, replacing a physical-flow diagram that mermaid cannot render cleanly.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    blockA[STAGE 1  SOURCE SPECIFICATION\nV_L, f, phase seq] --> blockB[STAGE 2  LINE VOLTAGE SET\nVab, Vbc, Vca]
    blockB --> blockC[STAGE 3  BRANCH CURRENTS\nIab, Ibc, Ica via Ohm law]
    blockC --> blockD[STAGE 4  KCL APPLICATION\nIa, Ib, Ic as phasor differences]
    blockD --> blockE[STAGE 5  KCL CHECK\nIa + Ib + Ic = 0]
    blockE --> blockF[STAGE 6  BRANCH POWERS\nPxy, Qxy, Sxy per branch]
    blockF --> blockG[STAGE 7  TOTALS\nP_T, Q_T, S_T, cos phi]
    blockE --> blockH[STAGE 8  PHASE SEQ STUDY\nReverse seq, recompute]
    blockG --> blockI[FINAL ANSWER SHEET]
    blockH --> blockI
```

## 4.4 Reference Phasor Diagram — Branch and Line Currents (RYB)

For the worked example of §3.3, the relative phasor positions are:

| Quantity | Magnitude (A) | Angle (°) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $I_{ab}$ | 40.00 | +30.0 |
| $I_{bc}$ | 40.00 | −126.87 |
| $I_{ca}$ | 56.57 | +195.0 |
| $I_a$ | 95.76 | +21.19 |
| $I_b$ | 78.38 | −138.43 |
| $I_c$ | 35.22 | +150.47 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The three line currents are *unequal in magnitude* (95.76, 78.38, 35.22) and *not separated by 120°* — these are the signatures of an unbalanced delta load. A balanced delta would have produced equal magnitudes and 120° separation.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Using $\sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ for power.** This formula is **strictly for balanced** loads. For unbalanced delta, the branch-sum method is mandatory; expect full mark loss if applied.
> 2. **Sign confusion in KCL.** Always take $I_a = I_{ab} - I_{ca}$, NOT $I_{ab} + I_{ca}$. Drawing the assumed current direction on the circuit and applying KCL systematically avoids this.
> 3. **Forgetting the phase sequence.** RYB and RBY give *different* line currents for an unbalanced delta. KTU 2024 papers frequently pose a "comment on the effect of phase sequence" follow-up.
> 4. **Ignoring the KCL check $I_a + I_b + I_c = 0$.** Examiners often award 1 mark specifically for this verification — *do not skip it*.
> 5. **Radian/degree mode errors in calculator.** The branch currents involve $\tan^{-1}$; ensure the calculator is in DEG mode before evaluation.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State any three reasons why a 3-wire system without a neutral can still feed an unbalanced delta-connected load without any neutral-point displacement problem.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. In a delta connection, each load branch is connected **directly between two line terminals**. Hence, the voltage across every branch is fixed by the source line voltage — it does not depend on the load or on a neutral point. **(1 mark)**
2. Even if the three branch impedances are unequal, **no branch carries a "return" current through a fourth wire**, because each branch forms a self-contained mesh with the source. The absence of a neutral has no effect on branch voltages. **(1 mark)**
3. Kirchhoff's Voltage Law guarantees $V_{ab} + V_{bc} + V_{ca} = 0$ for the source. The three branch currents automatically sum to zero around the closed delta mesh, satisfying KCL without any neutral conductor. **(1 mark)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Why is it invalid to use the formula $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ for an unbalanced 3-phase delta-connected load? What is the correct alternative?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The formula $P = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ is derived under the **assumption of perfect balance** — i.e., all three line currents have equal magnitude and identical 120° displacement, with a single power-factor angle $\phi$. For an unbalanced delta, **all three line currents have different magnitudes and unequal phase angles**, so a single $\phi$ does not exist for the whole system. **(1.5 marks)**
2. Moreover, even if a single $I_L$ value is used, the unbalanced $\cos\phi$ is not a true power factor; the apparent power $\sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L$ would be **physically meaningless** because the line currents are unequal. **(1 mark)**
3. The **correct approach** is the branch-sum method: compute the complex power of each branch as $S_{xy} = V_{xy} I_{xy}^{*}$, then sum: $S_T = S_{ab} + S_{bc} + S_{ca} = P_T + j Q_T$. The overall pf is $\cos\phi = P_T / \vert S_T \vert$. **(0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks, with choice)

### Question A (14 marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]*

A 3-phase, 3-wire, **415 V**, **50 Hz** supply with phase sequence **RYB** feeds an unbalanced delta-connected load. The branch impedances are:

$$
Z_{ab} = (12 + j\,5)\,\Omega,\quad Z_{bc} = (10 - j\,10)\,\Omega,\quad Z_{ca} = (8 + j\,6)\,\Omega
$$

#### Sub-part (a) — Compute the three phase (branch) currents and the three line currents. Verify with KCL. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Branch impedances in polar form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{ab} &= 12 + j5 = \sqrt{144+25}\;\angle\tan^{-1}(5/12) = 13.00\angle 22.62^\circ\,\Omega \\
Z_{bc} &= 10 - j10 = 10\sqrt{2}\;\angle -45^\circ = 14.14\angle -45.00^\circ\,\Omega \\
Z_{ca} &= 8 + j6 = 10\angle 36.87^\circ\,\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Line voltages (RYB sequence):**

$$
V_{ab} = 415\angle 30^\circ\,\text{V},\quad V_{bc} = 415\angle -90^\circ\,\text{V},\quad V_{ca} = 415\angle 150^\circ\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Branch currents:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{ab} &= \frac{415\angle 30^\circ}{13.00\angle 22.62^\circ} = 31.92\angle 7.38^\circ \text{ A} \\
I_{bc} &= \frac{415\angle -90^\circ}{14.14\angle -45^\circ} = 29.35\angle -45.00^\circ \text{ A} \\
I_{ca} &= \frac{415\angle 150^\circ}{10.00\angle 36.87^\circ} = 41.50\angle 113.13^\circ \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert branch currents to rectangular form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{ab} &= 31.92\cos 7.38^\circ + j\,31.92\sin 7.38^\circ = 31.66 + j\,4.10 \text{ A} \\
I_{bc} &= 29.35\cos(-45^\circ) + j\,29.35\sin(-45^\circ) = 20.75 - j\,20.75 \text{ A} \\
I_{ca} &= 41.50\cos 113.13^\circ + j\,41.50\sin 113.13^\circ = -16.30 + j\,38.10 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Line currents by KCL:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_a &= I_{ab} - I_{ca} = (31.66 + j\,4.10) - (-16.30 + j\,38.10) = 47.96 - j\,34.00 \\
    &= \sqrt{47.96^{2} + 34.00^{2}}\;\angle\tan^{-1}(-34.00/47.96) = 58.79\angle -35.34^\circ \text{ A} \\
I_b &= I_{bc} - I_{ab} = (20.75 - j\,20.75) - (31.66 + j\,4.10) = -10.91 - j\,24.85 \\
    &= 27.14\angle -113.73^\circ \text{ A} \\
I_c &= I_{ca} - I_{bc} = (-16.30 + j\,38.10) - (20.75 - j\,20.75) = -37.05 + j\,58.85 \\
    &= 69.55\angle 122.20^\circ \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — KCL verification:**

$$
I_a + I_b + I_c = (47.96 - 10.91 - 37.05) + j(-34.00 - 24.85 + 58.85) = 0 + j0 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**(7 marks: 1 for impedances, 1 for voltages, 1.5 for branch currents, 2 for line currents, 1.5 for verification)**

#### Sub-part (b) — Calculate the total active, reactive, apparent power and the overall power factor of the load. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Using the branch-sum method, $P_T = \sum \vert I_{xy} \vert^{2} R_{xy}$ and $Q_T = \sum \vert I_{xy} \vert^{2} X_{xy}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{ab} &= 31.92^{2} \times 12 = 12229 \text{ W} \\
P_{bc} &= 29.35^{2} \times 10 = 8614 \text{ W} \\
P_{ca} &= 41.50^{2} \times 8 = 13778 \text{ W} \\
\therefore\;\; P_T &= 12229 + 8614 + 13778 = 34621 \text{ W} \approx 34.62 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{ab} &= 31.92^{2} \times 5 = 5095 \text{ VAR (inductive)} \\
Q_{bc} &= 29.35^{2} \times (-10) = -8614 \text{ VAR (capacitive)} \\
Q_{ca} &= 41.50^{2} \times 6 = 10334 \text{ VAR (inductive)} \\
\therefore\;\; Q_T &= 5095 - 8614 + 10334 = 6815 \text{ VAR} \approx 6.82 \text{ kVAR}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert S_T \vert &= \sqrt{34621^{2} + 6815^{2}} = \sqrt{1.1986 \times 10^{9} + 4.644 \times 10^{7}} = 35285 \text{ VA} \\
\cos\phi &= \frac{P_T}{\vert S_T \vert} = \frac{34621}{35285} = 0.9811 \text{ (lagging, since } Q_T > 0\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $P_T \approx 34.62$ kW, $Q_T \approx 6.82$ kVAR (lagging), $\vert S_T \vert \approx 35.29$ kVA, pf = 0.981 lagging. **(7 marks: 2 for active power, 2 for reactive power, 1.5 for apparent power, 1.5 for power factor)**

---

### Question B (14 marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, alternative choice]*

A 3-phase, 3-wire, **400 V**, **50 Hz** system supplies an unbalanced delta load with:

$$
Z_{ab} = 20\angle 30^\circ\,\Omega,\quad Z_{bc} = 30\angle -60^\circ\,\Omega,\quad Z_{ca} = 40\angle 45^\circ\,\Omega
$$

#### Sub-part (a) — With phase sequence RYB, determine the three line currents. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Line voltages (RYB sequence):**

$$
V_{ab} = 400\angle 30^\circ\text{ V},\quad V_{bc} = 400\angle -90^\circ\text{ V},\quad V_{ca} = 400\angle 150^\circ\text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Branch currents:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{ab} &= \frac{400\angle 30^\circ}{20\angle 30^\circ} = 20.00\angle 0^\circ \text{ A} = 20.00 + j\,0.00 \text{ A} \\
I_{bc} &= \frac{400\angle -90^\circ}{30\angle -60^\circ} = 13.33\angle -30^\circ \text{ A} = 11.55 - j\,6.67 \text{ A} \\
I_{ca} &= \frac{400\angle 150^\circ}{40\angle 45^\circ} = 10.00\angle 105^\circ \text{ A} = -2.59 + j\,9.66 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Line currents by KCL:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_a &= I_{ab} - I_{ca} = (20.00 + j\,0.00) - (-2.59 + j\,9.66) = 22.59 - j\,9.66 \\
    &= \sqrt{22.59^{2} + 9.66^{2}}\;\angle\tan^{-1}(-9.66/22.59) = 24.57\angle -23.15^\circ \text{ A} \\
I_b &= I_{bc} - I_{ab} = (11.55 - j\,6.67) - (20.00 + j\,0.00) = -8.45 - j\,6.67 \\
    &= 10.76\angle -141.66^\circ \text{ A} \\
I_c &= I_{ca} - I_{bc} = (-2.59 + j\,9.66) - (11.55 - j\,6.67) = -14.14 + j\,16.33 \\
    &= 21.58\angle 130.87^\circ \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — KCL check:**

$$
I_a + I_b + I_c = (22.59 - 8.45 - 14.14) + j(-9.66 - 6.67 + 16.33) = 0 + j0 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**(7 marks: 2 for line voltages, 2 for branch currents, 2 for line currents, 1 for KCL check)**

#### Sub-part (b) — If the phase sequence is reversed to RBY, recalculate the line currents. Comment on the effect of phase sequence on an unbalanced delta load. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Line voltages for RBY sequence:**

$$
V_{ab} = 400\angle 30^\circ\text{ V},\quad V_{bc} = 400\angle +150^\circ\text{ V},\quad V_{ca} = 400\angle -90^\circ\text{ V}
$$

(Note: the convention fixes $V_{ab}$ as reference at 30°; under RBY, $V_{bc}$ and $V_{ca}$ swap their angle positions.)

**Step 2 — Branch currents:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{ab}' &= \frac{400\angle 30^\circ}{20\angle 30^\circ} = 20.00\angle 0^\circ \text{ A} \quad \text{(unchanged, since }V_{ab}\text{ is reference)} \\
I_{bc}' &= \frac{400\angle 150^\circ}{30\angle -60^\circ} = 13.33\angle 210^\circ \text{ A} = -11.55 - j\,6.67 \text{ A} \\
I_{ca}' &= \frac{400\angle -90^\circ}{40\angle 45^\circ} = 10.00\angle -135^\circ \text{ A} = -7.07 - j\,7.07 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Line currents by KCL:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_a' &= I_{ab}' - I_{ca}' = (20.00 + j\,0.00) - (-7.07 - j\,7.07) = 27.07 + j\,7.07 \\
     &= 27.98\angle 14.64^\circ \text{ A} \\
I_b' &= I_{bc}' - I_{ab}' = (-11.55 - j\,6.67) - (20.00 + j\,0.00) = -31.55 - j\,6.67 \\
     &= 32.25\angle -168.07^\circ \text{ A} \\
I_c' &= I_{ca}' - I_{bc}' = (-7.07 - j\,7.07) - (-11.55 - j\,6.67) = 4.48 - j\,0.40 \\
     &= 4.50\angle -5.10^\circ \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — KCL check:**

$$
I_a' + I_b' + I_c' = (27.07 - 31.55 + 4.48) + j(7.07 - 6.67 - 0.40) = 0 + j0 \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Comparative Comment (3 marks):**

| Quantity | RYB | RBY |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| $\vert I_a \vert$ (A) | 24.57 | 27.98 |
| $\vert I_b \vert$ (A) | 10.76 | 32.25 |
| $\vert I_c \vert$ (A) | 21.58 | 4.50 |

*Under RYB, the line currents were (24.57, 10.76, 21.58) A, while under RBY they become (27.98, 32.25, 4.50) A. **The line currents are completely different in both magnitude and phase angle** when the sequence is reversed. This is in sharp contrast to a balanced load, where reversing the phase sequence simply rotates all current phasors by 180° (i.e., reflects them about the origin) while preserving magnitudes. The unbalanced delta is therefore **highly sensitive to the supply phase sequence**, which is why phase-sequence relays are mandatory in protection schemes for unbalanced industrial loads.*

**(7 marks: 2 for new line voltages, 2 for branch currents, 2 for line currents + KCL, 1 for comparison comment)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Core principle:** In a delta load, *branch voltage = line voltage* — independent of balance and independent of the existence of a neutral. **(High yield)**
- **Branch currents first, line currents second.** Always compute $I_{ab}, I_{bc}, I_{ca}$ via Ohm's law, then derive $I_a, I_b, I_c$ using KCL.
- **KCL equations:**
  - $I_a = I_{ab} - I_{ca}$
  - $I_b = I_{bc} - I_{ab}$
  - $I_c = I_{ca} - I_{bc}$
- **Mandatory check:** $I_a + I_b + I_c = 0$ — worth a free mark in KTU scripts.
- **Power formula — never use** $\sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ **for unbalanced delta.** Use the branch-sum method: $S_T = S_{ab} + S_{bc} + S_{ca}$.
- **Two equivalent ways to compute branch power:**
  - $S_{xy} = V_{xy}\, I_{xy}^{*}$ (complex-power method)
  - $P_{xy} = \vert I_{xy} \vert^{2} R_{xy}$, $Q_{xy} = \vert I_{xy} \vert^{2} X_{xy}$ (resistance-reactance method)
- **Phase sequence matters:** RYB and RBY give different line currents for an unbalanced delta.
- **Source constraint:** $V_{ab} + V_{bc} + V_{ca} = 0$ for any 3-wire source.
- **Standard Indian supply:** $V_L = 415$ V (LT), $f = 50$ Hz, RYB sequence default.
- **Polar form of complex numbers** is mandatory for phasor operations — convert to rectangular only for addition/subtraction, then back to polar for final answers.
- **Sign of reactive power:** Inductive loads give $Q > 0$ (lagging); capacitive loads give $Q < 0$ (leading).
- **Common error to avoid:** The line-current expressions $I_a = I_{ab} + I_{bc}$ or $I_a = I_{ab} + I_{ca}$ are *always wrong* — only the differences are correct, as derived from KCL.
- **Difference from 3-wire star:** A 3-wire *star* load with unbalanced impedances *cannot* be analysed by direct Ohm's law — it requires loop equations, Millman's theorem, or neutral-displacement calculations. The delta topology avoids this complexity entirely.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steady State Analysis of 3-Phase 4-Wire and 3-Wire (Using Millman's Theorem Only) Unbalanced Star Connected Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEET302 - CIRCUITS & NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Steady State Analysis of 3-Phase 4-Wire and 3-Wire (Using Millman's Theorem Only) Unbalanced Star Connected Circuit # Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a 3-Phase Unbalanced Star-Connected Load? In a **3-phase star (Y) connection**, the three load impedances share a common node called the **load neutral point N'**. The system is said to be **unbalanced** when the three load impedances are *not identical* in magnitude or phase, i.e. $$Z_A \neq Z_B \neq Z_C \quad \text{(either in magnitude or in angle)}$$ The source side is always assumed to remain a balanced 3-phase source with phase sequence R-Y-B (or A-B-C), delivering the phasor voltages $$\underline{V}_{AN} = V_p \angle 0^\circ, \quad \underline{V}_{BN} = V_p \angle -120^\circ, \quad \underline{V}_{CN} = V_p \angle +120^\circ$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The **source** is balanced, but the **load** is unbalanced. This mismatch is the entire reason the analysis is non-trivial. ## 1.2 The 4-Wire vs 3-Wire Distinction | Configuration | Neutral Wire Present? | Load Neutral N' Status | |---------------|----------------------|------------------------| | **4-Wire** | Yes — direct wire between source neutral **N** and load neutral **N'** | N' is forced to be at the same potential as N → **No neutral displacement** | | **3-Wire** | No — only three line conductors A, B, C | N' is free to float → **Neutral displacement occurs** → voltages across individual loads become unequal | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Think of a 3-legged stool on a slightly tilted floor. > - **4-wire case:** A steel rod is welded from the floor to the stool's leg junction. The stool cannot tilt — every leg bears exactly the same fraction of the load (the phase voltages are identical to the source). > - **3-wire case:** The stool is free-standing. The leg-junction slides sideways until a new equilibrium is reached. The legs no longer carry equal loads; one leg is heavily compressed, another barely touches the ground. The displacement of the leg-junction is the **neutral displacement voltage** $V_{N'N}$. This physical shift of N' is what makes the 3-wire unbalanced case mathematically richer and is exactly what **Millman's Theorem** lets us compute in a single formula. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - Standard KTU exam values: **V_L = 415 V, 50 Hz** (Indian 3-phase LT supply) or **V_L = 400 V** (European/IEC default). - Phase voltage relationship: **$V_p = V_L / \sqrt{3}$**. - Source frequency: **f = 50 Hz**, $\omega = 2\pi f = 314.159$ **rad/s**. # Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Case A — 4-Wire Unbalanced Star The neutral wire of *zero* impedance ties $N \equiv N'$. Therefore: $$\underline{V}_{N'N} = 0 \quad \text{(by KVL, since no drop across ideal neutral wire)}$$ The phase voltages **across each load** equal the source phase voltages exactly: $$\underline{V}_{AN'} = \underline{V}_{AN}, \quad \underline{V}_{BN'} = \underline{V}_{BN}, \quad \underline{V}_{CN'} = \underline{V}_{CN}$$ The line currents are obtained independently (the three branches are decoupled): $$\underline{I}_A = \frac{\underline{V}_{AN}}{Z_A}, \quad \underline{I}_B = \frac{\underline{V}_{BN}}{Z_B}, \quad \underline{I}_C = \frac{\underline{V}_{CN}}{Z_C}$$ The neutral wire carries the **return** current (KCL at the load neutral): $$\underline{I}_N = \underline{I}_A + \underline{I}_B + \underline{I}_C \quad (\neq 0 \text{ in general})$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why is this the case?** The neutral wire absorbs the "unbalance" current. In a *balanced* star, $\underline{I}_N = 0$ trivially; in an *unbalanced* star, the imbalance circulates back through the neutral conductor. ## 2.2 Case B — 3-Wire Unbalanced Star — Why Millman's Theorem? In a 3-wire system, **no neutral conductor exists**. The load neutral N' is floating. KCL at N' demands $$\underline{I}_A + \underline{I}_B + \underline{I}_C = 0 \quad \text{(sum of currents leaving N' must be zero)}$$ If we treat $N'$ as a "super-node" with respect to the source neutral N, the voltage $V_{N'N}$ is precisely the voltage that the network itself decides to maintain to satisfy KCL. This is exactly the situation that **Millman's Theorem** is designed for. ### 2.2.1 Statement of Millman's Theorem > [!IMPORTANT] > **Millman's Theorem (general statement):** > Given any number of branches connected to a common node, where each branch $i$ has a voltage source $V_i$ in series with an impedance $Z_i$, the voltage of the common node with respect to a reference node is > $$\underline{V}_{node} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\underline{V}_i}{Z_i}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{Z_i}}$$ ### 2.2.2 Application to the 3-Phase 3-Wire Unbalanced Star Identifying the three branches as A–N', B–N', C–N' (each containing a source $\underline{V}_{AN}, \underline{V}_{BN}, \underline{V}_{CN}$ and a load impedance $Z_A, Z_B, Z_C$), the **neutral displacement voltage** is $$\boxed{\;\underline{V}_{N'N} = \frac{\dfrac{\underline{V}_{AN}}{Z_A} + \dfrac{\underline{V}_{BN}}{Z_B} + \dfrac{\underline{V}_{CN}}{Z_C}}{\dfrac{1}{Z_A} + \dfrac{1}{Z_B} + \dfrac{1}{Z_C}}\;}$$ This can equivalently be written using **admittances** $Y_i = 1/Z_i$: $$\underline{V}_{N'N} = \frac{Y_A \underline{V}_{AN} + Y_B \underline{V}_{BN} + Y_C \underline{V}_{CN}}{Y_A + Y_B + Y_C}$$ ## 2.3 The Five-Step KTU Board Procedure for 3-Wire Analysis The complete algorithm that examiners expect you to write: 1. **Express source phase voltages** as phasors with $V_p = V_L / \sqrt{3}$. 2. **Compute admittances** $Y_A, Y_B, Y_C$ in rectangular (a + jb) form. 3. **Apply Millman's formula** to get $\underline{V}_{N'N}$. 4. **Compute load voltages** using KVL around each loop: $$\underline{V}_{AN'} = \underline{V}_{AN} - \underline{V}_{N'N}, \quad \underline{V}_{BN'} = \underline{V}_{BN} - \underline{V}_{N'N}, \quad \underline{V}_{CN'} = \underline{V}_{CN} - \underline{V}_{N'N}$$ 5. **Compute line currents**: $$\underline{I}_A = \frac{\underline{V}_{AN'}}{Z_A}, \quad \underline{I}_B = \frac{\underline{V}_{BN'}}{Z_B}, \quad \underline{I}_C = \frac{\underline{V}_{CN'}}{Z_C}$$ 6. **Self-check:** verify $\underline{I}_A + \underline{I}_B + \underline{I}_C = 0$ (must be true for any 3-wire system). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Phase voltage from line voltage | $V_p = V_L / \sqrt{3}$ | V | Use for source phasors | | 2 | Source phasors (R-Y-B seq.) | $V_p\angle 0^\circ, \, V_p\angle -120^\circ, \, V_p\angle +120^\circ$ | V | $\underline{V}_{AN}$ is reference | | 3 | Millman numerator | $\sum (V_i / Z_i) = \sum Y_i V_i$ | A | Sum is **complex** | | 4 | Millman denominator | $\sum (1/Z_i) = \sum Y_i$ | S (Siemens) | Sum of admittances | | 5 | Neutral displacement voltage | $V_{N'N} = \dfrac{\sum Y_i V_i}{\sum Y_i}$ | V | **KTU high-yield formula** | | 6 | Load voltage (3-wire) | $V_{kN'} = V_{kN} - V_{N'N}$, $k \in \{A,B,C\}$ | V | KVL loop | | 7 | Line current | $I_k = V_{kN'} / Z_k$ | A | Ohm's law per branch | | 8 | Neutral current (4-wire) | $I_N = I_A + I_B + I_C$ | A | Non-zero for unbalanced | | 9 | KCL check (3-wire) | $I_A + I_B + I_C = 0$ | A | Must hold exactly | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical exam tip:** Never use the vertical bar `|` inside a Mermaid/markdown table to denote absolute value or phasor magnitude — use $\vert \cdot \vert$ LaTeX command instead. This is the most common markdown-rendering error in KTU answer sheets uploaded to the portal. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility This exact analysis is what power-system engineers perform when: - **Single-phase loads are unevenly distributed** across the three phases of a feeder (e.g., a residential colony). - **Fault analysis** is being done on a 3-phase system (single line-to-ground faults create exactly this "unbalanced star with floating neutral" topology). - **Designing neutral conductors** — overcurrent protection must be sized to handle $I_N$, not zero. # Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples ## 3.1 Derivation of the Millman Neutral-Displacement Formula We start with the KCL constraint at the floating load neutral N' for a 3-wire system. The three branch currents flowing **from** the source lines **into** N' must sum to zero (no path to escape): $$\underline{I}_A + \underline{I}_B + \underline{I}_C = 0$$ Now apply Ohm's law to each branch, taking the source neutral N as the reference (ground) node: $$\underline{I}_A = \frac{\underline{V}_{AN} - \underline{V}_{N'N}}{Z_A}$$ $$\underline{I}_B = \frac{\underline{V}_{BN} - \underline{V}_{N'N}}{Z_B}$$ $$\underline{I}_C = \frac{\underline{V}_{CN} - \underline{V}_{N'N}}{Z_C}$$ Substitute into the KCL equation: $$\frac{\underline{V}_{AN} - \underline{V}_{N'N}}{Z_A} + \frac{\underline{V}_{BN} - \underline{V}_{N'N}}{Z_B} + \frac{\underline{V}_{CN} - \underline{V}_{N'N}}{Z_C} = 0$$ Expand into two sums: $$\left[\frac{\underline{V}_{AN}}{Z_A} + \frac{\underline{V}_{BN}}{Z_B} + \frac{\underline{V}_{CN}}{Z_C}\right] - \underline{V}_{N'N}\left[\frac{1}{Z_A} + \frac{1}{Z_B} + \frac{1}{Z_C}\right] = 0$$ Isolate $\underline{V}_{N'N}$ on the left side: $$\underline{V}_{N'N}\left[\frac{1}{Z_A} + \frac{1}{Z_B} + \frac{1}{Z_C}\right] = \frac{\underline{V}_{AN}}{Z_A} + \frac{\underline{V}_{BN}}{Z_B} + \frac{\underline{V}_{CN}}{Z_C}$$ $$\boxed{\;\underline{V}_{N'N} = \frac{\dfrac{\underline{V}_{AN}}{Z_A} + \dfrac{\underline{V}_{BN}}{Z_B} + \dfrac{\underline{V}_{CN}}{Z_C}}{\dfrac{1}{Z_A} + \dfrac{1}{Z_B} + \dfrac{1}{Z_C}}\;} \qquad \text{Q.E.D.}$$ > [!NOTE] > This single boxed result is worth **7 marks** on a typical KTU Part-B question on its own. Memorise it precisely. ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — 3-Phase 4-Wire Unbalanced Star (Quick Analysis) **Problem.** A 3-phase, 4-wire star-connected unbalanced load has impedances $Z_A = 10\angle 30^\circ\ \Omega$, $Z_B = 15\angle -45^\circ\ \Omega$, $Z_C = 20\angle 60^\circ\ \Omega$. The line voltage is **V_L = 415 V, 50 Hz**, R-Y-B phase sequence. Determine the three line currents and the neutral current. **Step 1 — Phase voltage magnitude.** $$V_p = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{415}{\sqrt{3}} = 239.60\ \text{V}$$ **Step 2 — Source phasors** (take $\underline{V}_{AN}$ as reference): $$\underline{V}_{AN} = 239.60\angle 0^\circ = 239.60 + j0\ \text{V}$$ $$\underline{V}_{BN} = 239.60\angle -120^\circ = 239.60(-0.5 - j0.866) = -119.80 - j207.50\ \text{V}$$ $$\underline{V}_{CN} = 239.60\angle +120^\circ = 239.60(-0.5 + j0.866) = -119.80 + j207.50\ \text{V}$$ **Step 3 — Line currents** (4-wire: N' is tied to N, so $V_{kN'} = V_{kN}$): $$\underline{I}_A = \frac{239.60\angle 0^\circ}{10\angle 30^\circ} = 23.96\angle -30^\circ\ \text{A}$$ Convert to rectangular for the addition step: $$\underline{I}_A = 23.96(\cos(-30^\circ) + j\sin(-30^\circ)) = 20.75 - j11.98\ \text{A}$$ $$\underline{I}_B = \frac{239.60\angle -120^\circ}{15\angle -45^\circ} = 15.97\angle -75^\circ\ \text{A}$$ $$\underline{I}_B = 15.97(0.2588 - j0.9659) = 4.13 - j15.42\ \text{A}$$ $$\underline{I}_C = \frac{239.60\angle +120^\circ}{20\angle 60^\circ} = 11.98\angle 60^\circ\ \text{A}$$ $$\underline{I}_C = 11.98(0.5 + j0.866) = 5.99 + j10.37\ \text{A}$$ **Step 4 — Neutral current (KCL at the load neutral N'):** $$\underline{I}_N = \underline{I}_A + \underline{I}_B + \underline{I}_C$$ $$\underline{I}_N = (20.75 + 4.13 + 5.99) + j(-11.98 - 15.42 + 10.37)$$ $$\underline{I}_N = 30.87 - j17.03\ \text{A}$$ $$\vert \underline{I}_N \vert = \sqrt{30.87^2 + 17.03^2} = \sqrt{952.96 + 290.02} = \sqrt{1242.98} = 35.26\ \text{A}$$ $$\angle \underline{I}_N = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-17.03}{30.87}\right) = -28.87^\circ$$ $$\boxed{\underline{I}_N = 35.26\angle -28.87^\circ\ \text{A}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** If this had been a *balanced* load, $I_N$ would have been exactly zero. The 35.26 A is the price the system pays for imbalance, and the neutral conductor must be sized to carry it continuously. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — 3-Phase 3-Wire Unbalanced Star (Millman's Theorem) **Problem.** A 3-phase, 3-wire star-connected load has $Z_A = (5 + j0)\ \Omega$, $Z_B = (5 + j5)\ \Omega$, $Z_C = (5 - j5)\ \Omega$, supplied from a balanced 415 V, 50 Hz source with R-Y-B sequence. Use **Millman's theorem** to determine the neutral displacement voltage and the line currents. **Step 1 — Phase voltage.** $$V_p = \frac{415}{\sqrt{3}} = 239.60\ \text{V}$$ $$\underline{V}_{AN} = 239.60 + j0 = 239.60\angle 0^\circ\ \text{V}$$ $$\underline{V}_{BN} = 239.60\angle -120^\circ = -119.80 - j207.50\ \text{V}$$ $$\underline{V}_{CN} = 239.60\angle +120^\circ = -119.80 + j207.50\ \text{V}$$ **Step 2 — Branch admittances** (in rectangular form): $$Y_A = \frac{1}{5 + j0} = \frac{5 - j0}{25} = 0.2000 + j0\ \text{S}$$ $$Y_B = \frac{1}{5 + j5} = \frac{5 - j5}{5^2 + 5^2} = \frac{5 - j5}{50} = 0.1000 - j0.1000\ \text{S}$$ $$Y_C = \frac{1}{5 - j5} = \frac{5 + j5}{50} = 0.1000 + j0.1000\ \text{S}$$ **Step 3 — Sum of admittances (Millman denominator):** $$Y_A + Y_B + Y_C = (0.2 + 0.1 + 0.1) + j(0 - 0.1 + 0.1) = 0.4000 + j0\ \text{S}$$ **Step 4 — Numerator** $\sum Y_k \underline{V}_{kN}$: $$Y_A \underline{V}_{AN} = (0.2)(239.60 + j0) = 47.92 + j0\ \text{A}$$ $$Y_B \underline{V}_{BN} = (0.1 - j0.1)(-119.80 - j207.50)$$ Expand the product: $$= (0.1)(-119.80) + (0.1)(-j207.50) + (-j0.1)(-119.80) + (-j0.1)(-j207.50)$$ $$= -11.98 - j20.75 + j11.98 + j^2(20.75)$$ $$= -11.98 - j20.75 + j11.98 - 20.75$$ $$= -32.73 - j8.77\ \text{A}$$ $$Y_C \underline{V}_{CN} = (0.1 + j0.1)(-119.80 + j207.50)$$ $$= (0.1)(-119.80) + (0.1)(j207.50) + (j0.1)(-119.80) + (j0.1)(j207.50)$$ $$= -11.98 + j20.75 - j11.98 + j^2(20.75)$$ $$= -11.98 + j20.75 - j11.98 - 20.75$$ $$= -32.73 + j8.77\ \text{A}$$ Sum: $$\sum Y_k \underline{V}_{kN} = (47.92 - 32.73 - 32.73) + j(0 - 8.77 + 8.77)$$ $$= -17.54 + j0\ \text{A}$$ **Step 5 — Apply Millman's theorem:** $$\underline{V}_{N'N} = \frac{-17.54 + j0}{0.4 + j0} = -43.85 + j0\ \text{V}$$ $$\boxed{\underline{V}_{N'N} = 43.85\angle 180^\circ\ \text{V}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The neutral has **displaced by 43.85 V in the negative-real direction**. The negative sign (or equivalently, $\angle 180^\circ$) tells us N' lies on the opposite side of N from $\underline{V}_{AN}$ along the real axis — i.e., the floating neutral has migrated away from the source. **Step 6 — Compute load phase voltages** (KVL: $V_{kN'} = V_{kN} - V_{N'N}$): $$\underline{V}_{AN'} = (239.60 + j0) - (-43.85 + j0) = 283.45 + j0\ \text{V} = 283.45\angle 0^\circ\ \text{V}$$ $$\underline{V}_{BN'} = (-119.80 - j207.50) - (-43.85) = -75.95 - j207.50\ \text{V}$$ $$\vert \underline{V}_{BN'} \vert = \sqrt{75.95^2 + 207.50^2} = \sqrt{5768.4 + 43056.3} = \sqrt{48824.7} = 220.96\ \text{V}$$ $$\angle \underline{V}_{BN'} = 180^\circ + \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{207.50}{75.95}\right) = 180^\circ + 70.0^\circ = -110.0^\circ\ (\text{or }+250^\circ)$$ $$\underline{V}_{CN'} = (-119.80 + j207.50) - (-43.85) = -75.95 + j207.50\ \text{V}$$ $$\vert \underline{V}_{CN'} \vert = 220.96\ \text{V}, \quad \angle \underline{V}_{CN'} = 180^\circ - 70.0^\circ = +110.0^\circ$$ **Step 7 — Line currents:** $$\underline{I}_A = \frac{283.45\angle 0^\circ}{5\angle 0^\circ} = 56.69\angle 0^\circ\ \text{A}$$ $$\underline{I}_B = \frac{220.96\angle -110^\circ}{7.071\angle +45^\circ} = 31.25\angle -155^\circ\ \text{A}$$ $$\underline{I}_C = \frac{220.96\angle +110^\circ}{7.071\angle -45^\circ} = 31.25\angle +155^\circ\ \text{A}$$ **Step 8 — KCL verification:** $$\underline{I}_A + \underline{I}_B + \underline{I}_C$$ $$= 56.69 + 31.25(\cos(-155^\circ) + j\sin(-155^\circ)) + 31.25(\cos 155^\circ + j\sin 155^\circ)$$ $$= 56.69 + 31.25(-0.9063 - j0.4226) + 31.25(-0.9063 + j0.4226)$$ $$= 56.69 - 28.32 - j13.21 - 28.32 + j13.21$$ $$= 0.05 + j0\ \approx 0\ \text{A} \quad \checkmark$$ > [!NOTE] > The non-zero residual (0.05 A) is purely **rounding error** from using only 4 significant figures. In a KTU exam, a residual under 1% of the largest line current is treated as zero. Always perform this KCL check — it is the examiner's first verification step during valuation. ### 3.3.1 Summary Table of Results | Quantity | Magnitude | Angle | |----------|-----------|-------| | $V_{N'N}$ (neutral displacement) | **43.85 V** | 180° | | $V_{AN'}$ | 283.45 V | 0° | | $V_{BN'}$ | 220.96 V | −110° | | $V_{CN'}$ | 220.96 V | +110° | | $I_A$ | 56.69 A | 0° | | $I_B$ | 31.25 A | −155° | | $I_C$ | 31.25 A | +155° | | $I_A + I_B + I_C$ | ≈ 0 | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice how phase A carries **almost double** the current of phases B and C. This is because the unloaded voltage $V_{AN'}$ is the highest (283.45 V) and the impedance $Z_A$ is the lowest. The "stiffest" branch hogs current in a 3-wire unbalanced star. # Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 3-Phase 4-Wire Unbalanced Star — Topology Map ```mermaid graph TB SrcA[Source Phase A

Note the neutral wire tying `SrcN` to `LoadN` — this single link is what forces $V_{N'N} = 0$. --- ## 4.2 3-Phase 3-Wire Unbalanced Star — Topology with Floating Neutral ```mermaid graph TB SrcA2[Source Phase A
