# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define the Laplace transform of a time-domain function $f(t)$ and state the time-differentiation property with initial conditions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **unilateral Laplace transform** of $f(t)$ is defined as $F(s) = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f(t) e^{-st} dt$ where $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is the complex frequency. **(1 mark)**
>
> The **time-differentiation property** states that if $F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\}$, then: **(1 mark)**
>
> $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = s F(s) - f(0^-)$$
>
> where $f(0^-)$ is the value of $f(t)$ at $t = 0^-$ (just before $t=0$). For the second derivative:
>
> $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{d^2 f(t)}{dt^2}\right\} = s^2 F(s) - s f(0^-) - f'(0^-)$$ **(1 mark)**
>
> These properties allow the conversion of differential equations to algebraic equations while automatically including the effect of initial stored energy.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Write the s-domain voltage-current relations for an inductor and a capacitor, including initial conditions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an inductor of $L$ henries with initial current $i_L(0^-)$:
>
> $$V_L(s) = sL \cdot I_L(s) - L \cdot i_L(0^-)$$ **(1.5 marks)**
>
> This represents a series combination of an impedance $sL$ and a voltage source $L i_L(0^-)$ (polarity such that it represents the inductor's stored energy).
>
> For a capacitor of $C$ farads with initial voltage $v_C(0^-)$:
>
> $$V_C(s) = \dfrac{1}{sC} I_C(s) + \dfrac{v_C(0^-)}{s}$$ **(1.5 marks)**
>
> This represents a series combination of an impedance $1/(sC)$ and a voltage source $v_C(0^-)/s$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**In the network shown, an RL series circuit has $R = 4\,\Omega$, $L = 2\,\text{H}$, a DC step input $v_s(t) = 12\,u(t)$ V, and an initial inductor current $i_L(0^-) = 2\,\text{A}$.**

**(a)** Draw the s-domain equivalent circuit and write the loop equation in the s-domain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve for the loop current $I(s)$ using algebraic methods, perform partial fraction expansion, and find the time-domain current $i(t)$ for $t \geq 0$. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) Model Solution — s-Domain Circuit and Loop Equation

**Step 1:** Transform the source: $\mathcal{L}\{12 u(t)\} = \dfrac{12}{s}$ V. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** The s-domain inductor model: impedance $sL = 2s$ ohms in series with a voltage source $L I_0 = 2 \times 2 = 4$ V. The polarity of this source is such that it represents the inductor's attempt to maintain the initial current of 2 A in the original direction. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3:** The series $s$-domain circuit consists of the source $12/s$ in series with $R = 4\,\Omega$, impedance $2s$, and the source $4$ V (with appropriate polarity). **(2 marks)**

**Step 4:** Apply KVL around the loop (assuming all elements and current $I(s)$ are oriented in the same direction):

$$\dfrac{12}{s} = 4 I(s) + 2s \cdot I(s) - 4$$ **(2 marks)**

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Solving for $i(t)$

**Step 1:** Rearrange the KVL equation:

$$4 I(s) + 2s I(s) = \dfrac{12}{s} + 4$$ **(1 mark)**

$$I(s) \cdot (2s + 4) = \dfrac{12 + 4s}{s}$$ **(1 mark)**

$$I(s) = \dfrac{12 + 4s}{s \cdot 2(s + 2)} = \dfrac{2(3 + s)}{s(s+2)} = \dfrac{2(s+3)}{s(s+2)}$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Partial Fraction Expansion:

$$I(s) = \dfrac{A}{s} + \dfrac{B}{s+2}$$

Residue at $s = 0$: $A = \left. \dfrac{2(s+3)}{s+2} \right|_{s=0} = \dfrac{6}{2} = 3$ **(1 mark)**

Residue at $s = -2$: $B = \left. \dfrac{2(s+3)}{s} \right|_{s=-2} = \dfrac{2(1)}{-2} = -1$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Inverse Laplace Transform:

$$i(t) = 3 \cdot u(t) - e^{-2t} \cdot u(t) = 3 - e^{-2t} \text{ A}, \quad t \geq 0$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Verification:**
- At $t = 0^+$: $i(0^+) = 3 - 1 = 2$ A ✓ (matches initial inductor current).
- At $t \to \infty$: $i(\infty) = 3 - 0 = 3$ A = $V_s/R = 12/4$ ✓
- Time constant: $\tau = L/R = 2/4 = 0.5$ s ✓ (pole at $s = -2$).

**(Final 1 mark for proper verification and units.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**For the RLC series circuit with $R = 3\,\Omega$, $L = 1\,\text{H}$, $C = 1/2\,\text{F}$, an applied voltage $v_s(t) = 6 u(t)$ V, and initial conditions $i_L(0^-) = 0$ A, $v_C(0^-) = 1$ V:**

**(a)** Set up the s-domain circuit and derive the expression for the loop current $I(s)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Identify the poles of the response, perform partial fraction expansion, and find $i(t)$. State whether the response is overdamped, critically damped, or underdamped. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) Model Solution

**Step 1:** $V_s(s) = \dfrac{6}{s}$; $i_L(0^-) = 0 \implies L i_L(0^-) = 0$; $v_C(0^-) = 1 \implies v_C(0^-)/s = 1/s$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** s-domain impedances: $R = 3$, $sL = s$, $1/(sC) = 2/s$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** KVL around the loop:

$$\dfrac{6}{s} = 3 I(s) + s I(s) + \dfrac{2}{s} I(s) + \dfrac{1}{s}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 4:** Rearranging:

$$3 I(s) + s I(s) + \dfrac{2}{s} I(s) = \dfrac{6}{s} - \dfrac{1}{s} = \dfrac{5}{s}$$ **(1 mark)**

Multiplying both sides by $s$:

$$s \cdot 3 I(s) + s^2 I(s) + 2 I(s) = 5$$ **(1 mark)**

$$I(s) \left(s^2 + 3s + 2\right) = 5$$ **(0.5 marks)**

$$\boxed{I(s) = \dfrac{5}{s^2 + 3s + 2} = \dfrac{5}{(s+1)(s+2)}}$$ **(0.5 marks)**

#### Part (b) Model Solution

**Step 1:** Factor the denominator: $s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)$. **(1 mark)**

The poles are at $s = -1$ and $s = -2$, both real and negative. Since they are **real and distinct**, the response is **overdamped**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Partial Fraction Expansion:

$$I(s) = \dfrac{A}{s+1} + \dfrac{B}{s+2}$$

Residue at $s = -1$: $A = \left. \dfrac{5}{s+2} \right|_{s=-1} = \dfrac{5}{1} = 5$ **(1 mark)**

Residue at $s = -2$: $B = \left. \dfrac{5}{s+1} \right|_{s=-2} = \dfrac{5}{-1} = -5$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Inverse Laplace Transform:

$$i(t) = 5 e^{-t} - 5 e^{-2t} \text{ A}, \quad t \geq 0$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Verification and Damping Classification:**
- Initial value: $i(0^+) = 5 - 5 = 0$ A ✓ (matches $i_L(0^-) = 0$).
- Final value: $i(\infty) = 0$ ✓ (capacitor blocks DC).
- Discriminant of characteristic equation: $\Delta = 9 - 8 = 1 > 0$ → **Overdamped**, real distinct roots. ✓ **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Wrong sign on initial-condition source:** For an inductor carrying $i_L(0^-) = I_0$ in the assumed mesh current direction, the $s$-domain model must include the source $L I_0$ such that it *opposes* the applied source (a rise in the KVL sense). Drawing it the wrong way will flip the sign of the natural response and yield incorrect final values. **Examiner's deduction: up to 2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert sources to s-domain:** Students often leave the source as $V_s$ in the $s$-domain circuit instead of $V_s/s$ for a step input. This silently changes the entire problem. **Examiner's deduction: up to 3 marks.**
>
> 3. **Algebraic errors in partial fraction expansion:** A single sign error in residue calculation propagates to the final answer. Always verify by multiplying back: $A(s+2) + B(s+1) = 5$ should yield the numerator $5$. **Examiner's deduction: up to 2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Skipping the time-domain verification:** Even a one-line check of $i(0^+)$ and $i(\infty)$ is often rewarded with 1–2 marks. KTU examiners explicitly allocate marks for "verification of initial and final conditions" in step-response problems.
>
> 5. **Unit confusion:** The product $LC$ has units of $\text{second}^2$, $RC$ has units of seconds, and $L/R$ has units of seconds. A dimensional mismatch in your time-constant expression will be penalized.
>
> 6. **Misapplying Final Value Theorem:** Never use $\lim_{s \to 0} sF(s)$ unless you have verified that all poles of $sF(s)$ lie strictly in the left half-plane. For an oscillator, this gives a misleading result.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Solution Using Laplace Transforms**
>
> ☐ The **Laplace transform** converts linear ODEs to algebraic equations; it requires the system to be **linear and time-invariant (LTI)**.
>
> ☐ The integration is **unilateral**, from $0^-$ to $\infty$, ensuring initial conditions are automatically captured.
>
> ☐ The s-domain impedance of **R, L, C** are $R$, $sL$, $1/(sC)$ respectively — these are the cornerstones of s-domain circuit drawing.
>
> ☐ Initial conditions become **independent sources** in the s-domain: $L i_L(0^-)$ in series with inductor, and $v_C(0^-)/s$ in series with capacitor.
>
> ☐ Once the s-domain circuit is drawn, **every** DC-network analysis technique (KVL, KCL, mesh, nodal, Thevenin, Norton, Superposition, source transformation) applies directly.
>
> ☐ The **inverse Laplace transform** almost always requires **Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE)**. Master the cover-up method for distinct real poles; know how to handle repeated and complex-conjugate poles.
>
> ☐ **Initial Value Theorem (IVT):** $f(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s)$ — useful sanity check.
>
> ☐ **Final Value Theorem (FVT):** $f(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} sF(s)$ — only valid when poles of $sF(s)$ are in the open left half-plane.
>
> ☐ For a **step input** $V_s u(t)$, the transform is $V_s/s$. For an **impulse** $\delta(t)$, the transform is **1** (no $s$ in the denominator).
>
> ☐ Damped sinusoidal transforms (e.g., $e^{-at}\sin\omega t$) require recognizing the pattern $\dfrac{\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$ — this is essential for **underdamped RLC** responses.
>
> ☐ The **transfer function** $H(s) = Y(s)/X(s)$ characterizes a circuit completely (in zero-state). It is the Laplace transform of the impulse response $h(t)$.
>
> ☐ Poles of $H(s)$ in the **left half-plane** → stable system; on the **imaginary axis** → marginally stable (oscillator); in the **right half-plane** → unstable.
>
> ☐ In the KTU 14-mark question, the expected structure is: **s-domain circuit diagram (2 marks) + KVL/KCL equation (2 marks) + Algebraic solution for $Y(s)$ (3 marks) + Partial fraction expansion (3 marks) + Inverse transform (2 marks) + Verification (2 marks)**. Plan your answer accordingly.
>
> ☐ Always state the **time-domain function's domain of validity** explicitly as $t \geq 0$ or multiply by $u(t)$ to indicate the function is zero for $t < 0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformed Circuits in S-Domain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET302 - CIRCUITS & NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transformed Circuits in S-Domain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **S-Domain Transformation (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> The **s-domain transformation** (also called the **complex frequency domain** or **Laplace domain**) of a circuit is the process of converting all time-domain circuit elements (resistors, inductors, capacitors), sources, and differential equations into equivalent algebraic representations in the **complex frequency variable** $s = \sigma + j\omega$, by applying the **unilateral Laplace Transform** to every voltage and current relationship. The transformed circuit is purely algebraic, allowing the use of standard resistive network analysis techniques (Ohm's law, KVL, KCL, mesh, nodal, superposition, Thevenin, Norton) directly in the $s$-domain.

In the s-domain, a time-domain differential equation of the form:

$$\frac{d^2v(t)}{dt^2} + 3\frac{dv(t)}{dt} + 2v(t) = u(t)$$

becomes a simple algebraic equation in $s$:

$$s^2V(s) + 3sV(s) + 2V(s) = U(s)$$

which is far easier to solve by hand.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Universal Translator"**
> Imagine you are trying to communicate with someone who speaks a difficult language full of complex grammar rules (the **time domain**, with derivatives and integrals). The s-domain is like a **universal translator**: it converts every complex sentence (differential equation) into a simple, structured algebraic form that any calculator or engineer can solve instantly. Once you find the answer in the "translated language," you simply translate it back to the original language using the **Inverse Laplace Transform** to obtain the time-domain response $v(t)$ or $i(t)$.

Think of the s-domain as a **photographic negative** of the time domain — same information, but easier to manipulate algebraically.

## 1.3 Why Use the s-Domain?

- It converts **integro-differential equations** (hard) into **polynomial-algebraic equations** (easy).
- It naturally embeds **initial conditions** of energy-storage elements ($L$ and $C$).
- It unifies **transient** and **steady-state** analysis in a single framework.
- It enables easy derivation of the **transfer function** $H(s)$, the backbone of control systems and signal processing.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Operator Convention (KTU 2024):**
> - Time domain: lowercase letters $v(t), i(t)$
> - s-domain: uppercase letters $V(s), I(s)$
> - Complex frequency: $s = \sigma + j\omega$, with units of **rad/s** (or **Np/s** for the real part)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Laplace Transform: Foundation

The **unilateral Laplace Transform** of a time-domain function $f(t)$ (valid for $t \geq 0$) is defined as:

$$\mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = F(s) = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f(t) e^{-st} dt$$

The **Inverse Laplace Transform** recovers $f(t)$ from $F(s)$:

$$\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s)\} = f(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi j}\int_{\sigma - j\infty}^{\sigma + j\infty} F(s) e^{st} ds$$

## 2.2 Operational Properties Used in Circuit Analysis

| Property | Time Domain | s-Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **Linearity** | $a f_1(t) + b f_2(t)$ | $a F_1(s) + b F_2(s)$ |
| **Time Differentiation** | $\dfrac{df(t)}{dt}$ | $s F(s) - f(0^-)$ |
| **Second Derivative** | $\dfrac{d^2f(t)}{dt^2}$ | $s^2 F(s) - s f(0^-) - f'(0^-)$ |
| **Time Integration** | $\displaystyle\int_{0^-}^{t} f(\tau) d\tau$ | $\dfrac{F(s)}{s}$ |
| **Frequency Shifting** | $e^{-at} f(t)$ | $F(s + a)$ |
| **Time Shifting** | $f(t - a) u(t - a)$ | $e^{-as} F(s)$ |
| **Initial Value Theorem** | $f(0^+)$ | $\displaystyle\lim_{s \to \infty} s F(s)$ |
| **Final Value Theorem** | $f(\infty)$ | $\displaystyle\lim_{s \to 0} s F(s)$ |

## 2.3 Element-by-Element Transformation Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the HEART of the topic. Master this table — it is the most-tested content in KTU Module 3.**

| Element | Time-Domain V-I Relation | s-Domain Impedance $Z(s)$ | s-Domain Representation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resistor $R$** | $v(t) = R \cdot i(t)$ | $Z_R(s) = R$ | Pure resistance $R$ |
| **Inductor $L$** | $v(t) = L \dfrac{di(t)}{dt}$ | $Z_L(s) = sL$ | Impedance $sL$ in series with a **voltage source** $L i(0^-)$ (or parallel **current source** $\dfrac{i(0^-)}{s}$) |
| **Capacitor $C$** | $i(t) = C \dfrac{dv(t)}{dt}$ | $Z_C(s) = \dfrac{1}{sC}$ | Impedance $\dfrac{1}{sC}$ in series with a **voltage source** $\dfrac{v(0^-)}{s}$ (or parallel **current source** $C v(0^-)$) |

## 2.4 Source Transformation Rules

| Source | Time Domain | s-Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **DC Voltage Source** $V_s$ | Constant $V_s$ for $t \geq 0$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{s}$ (a step source) |
| **Sinusoidal Source** $V_m \cos(\omega t)$ | $V_m \cos(\omega t) u(t)$ | $\dfrac{V_m s}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ |
| **Impulse Source** $K \delta(t)$ | $K \delta(t)$ | $K$ (constant in s-domain) |

## 2.5 The Generalized Ohm's Law in s-Domain

For any two-terminal element (or network) in the s-domain:

$$V(s) = Z(s) \cdot I(s) \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad I(s) = Y(s) \cdot V(s)$$

where $Y(s) = \dfrac{1}{Z(s)}$ is the **s-domain admittance**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> The s-domain transformation is the foundation of:
> - **Control systems** (transfer functions, Bode plots, root locus)
> - **Filter design** (active and passive analog filters)
> - **Signal processing** (Laplace-based stability analysis)
> - **Power systems** (transient stability and fault analysis)
> - **Communication systems** (modulation and demodulation)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Inductor Transformation

**Starting point** — Voltage-Current relation in time domain:

$$v_L(t) = L \frac{di_L(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 1:** Apply the unilateral Laplace transform to both sides:

$$\mathcal{L}\{v_L(t)\} = \mathcal{L}\left\{L \frac{di_L(t)}{dt}\right\}$$

**Step 2:** Use linearity to pull out the constant $L$:

$$V_L(s) = L \cdot \mathcal{L}\left\{\frac{di_L(t)}{dt}\right\}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the differentiation rule $\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{df}{dt}\right\} = sF(s) - f(0^-)$:

$$V_L(s) = L \left[ s I_L(s) - i_L(0^-) \right]$$

**Step 4:** Distribute $L$:

$$V_L(s) = sL \cdot I_L(s) - L \cdot i_L(0^-)$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange into "impedance × current" form:

$$V_L(s) = \underbrace{sL}_{\text{Impedance}} \cdot I_L(s) \underbrace{- L i_L(0^-)}_{\text{Independent Voltage Source}}$$

This is interpreted as: **a series impedance $sL$ in series with a voltage source of value $L \cdot i_L(0^-)$** whose polarity **opposes** the assumed current direction.

## 3.2 Derivation: Capacitor Transformation

**Starting point:**

$$i_C(t) = C \frac{dv_C(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 1:** Laplace transform both sides:

$$I_C(s) = C \left[ s V_C(s) - v_C(0^-) \right]$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $V_C(s)$:

$$C s V_C(s) = I_C(s) + C v_C(0^-)$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $Cs$:

$$V_C(s) = \frac{1}{sC} I_C(s) + \frac{v_C(0^-)}{s}$$

This is interpreted as: **a series impedance $\dfrac{1}{sC}$ in series with a voltage source of value $\dfrac{v_C(0^-)}{s}$** with polarity matching the assumed voltage reference.

## 3.3 Equivalent Series vs. Parallel s-Domain Models (Source Transformation)

For a capacitor with initial voltage $V_0 = v_C(0^-)$:

**Series model:**
$$V_C(s) = \frac{1}{sC} I_C(s) + \frac{V_0}{s}$$

**Parallel model (Norton equivalent):** Use source transformation. The current source value is:

$$I_{source}(s) = \frac{V_0/s}{1/(sC)} = C V_0$$

So the parallel form is: **a current source of value $C V_0$ in parallel with the impedance $\dfrac{1}{sC}$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a problem gives "zero initial conditions," simply drop the source terms and use only $sL$ and $\dfrac{1}{sC}$ as the impedances. This is the most common case in KTU board exam numericals.

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example: Step Response of Series RLC

**Problem:** A series RLC circuit with $R = 2\,\Omega$, $L = 1\,\text{H}$, $C = 0.5\,\text{F}$, zero initial conditions, is driven by a step voltage $V_s u(t)$ where $V_s = 10\,\text{V}$. Find the current $I(s)$ and the s-domain mesh equation.

**Step 1:** Draw the s-domain circuit. The KVL in the time domain is:

$$V_s u(t) = R i(t) + L \frac{di(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{C} \int_0^t i(\tau) d\tau$$

**Step 2:** Apply Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):

$$\frac{V_s}{s} = R I(s) + sL I(s) + \frac{1}{sC} I(s)$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $I(s)$:

$$\frac{V_s}{s} = I(s) \left[ R + sL + \frac{1}{sC} \right]$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values $R = 2$, $L = 1$, $C = 0.5$:

$$\frac{10}{s} = I(s) \left[ 2 + s + \frac{2}{s} \right]$$

**Step 5:** Multiply numerator and denominator inside the bracket by $s$:

$$\frac{10}{s} = I(s) \left[ \frac{2s + s^2 + 2}{s} \right] = I(s) \cdot \frac{s^2 + 2s + 2}{s}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $I(s)$:

$$I(s) = \frac{10/s}{(s^2 + 2s + 2)/s} = \frac{10}{s^2 + 2s + 2}$$

**Step 7 (Optional — Final Value Theorem):** Find the steady-state current:

$$i(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s I(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10 s}{s^2 + 2s + 2} = 0$$

This makes physical sense: at DC steady state, the inductor acts as a short and the capacitor as an open, so no current flows.

## 3.5 Derivation: Nodal Analysis in s-Domain (Generalized Formula)

For a network with $n$ essential nodes (excluding the reference/ground), KCL at node $k$ gives:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{kj}(s) \cdot V_j(s) = I_k(s)$$

where:
- $Y_{kk}(s) = $ sum of all admittances connected to node $k$
- $Y_{kj}(s) = -$ (sum of admittances between nodes $k$ and $j$), for $k \neq j$
- $I_k(s) = $ sum of independent current sources entering node $k$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} Y_{11}(s) & Y_{12}(s) & \cdots & Y_{1n}(s) \\ Y_{21}(s) & Y_{22}(s) & \cdots & Y_{2n}(s) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ Y_{n1}(s) & Y_{n2}(s) & \cdots & Y_{nn}(s) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1(s) \\ V_2(s) \\ \vdots \\ V_n(s) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} I_1(s) \\ I_2(s) \\ \vdots \\ I_n(s) \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** When constructing the admittance matrix $Y(s)$, every term inside is itself a rational function of $s$. Do NOT cancel any common factors. Keep expressions in polynomial form until after the matrix is solved using Cramer's rule.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: s-Domain Transformation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Time Domain Circuit] --> B[Identify Initial Conditions]
    B --> C[Transform Each Element to s-Domain]
    C --> D[Transform All Sources to s-Domain]
    D --> E[s-Domain Circuit Diagram]
    E --> F[Choose Analysis Method]
    F --> G1[Nodal Analysis in s-Domain]
    F --> G2[Mesh Analysis in s-Domain]
    F --> G3[Superposition / Thevenin / Norton]
    G1 --> H[Solve Algebraic Equations for V or I]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[Express V s or I s as Rational Function]
    I --> J[Apply Inverse Laplace Transform]
    J --> K[Time Domain Response v t or i t]
```

## 4.2 Transformation Mapping Diagram (Element-Wise)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Resistor R] --> R2[Impedance R in s-Domain]
    L1[Inductor L] --> L2{Initial Condition}
    L2 -->|Zero IC| L3[Impedance sL]
    L2 -->|Nonzero IC| L4[sL + Voltage Source L times i 0]
    C1[Capacitor C] --> C2{Initial Condition}
    C2 -->|Zero IC| C3[Impedance 1 over sC]
    C2 -->|Nonzero IC| C4[1 over sC + Voltage Source v 0 over s]
    VSRC[DC Source V s] --> VSRC2[Step Source V s over s]
    VSIN[Sinusoid V m cos wt] --> VSIN2[V m s over s squared plus w squared]
```

## 4.3 Example s-Domain Circuit Schematic (Series RLC with Initial Conditions)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[(Vs / s)] --Vs--> N1((Node A))
    N1 --- R[Resistor R]
    R --- N2((Node B))
    N2 --- IND[sL]
    IND --- N3((Node C))
    N3 --- VLI[Voltage Source L*i(0) Polarity Opposing]
    VLI --- N4((Node D))
    N4 --- CAP[1 / sC]
    CAP --- N5((Node E))
    N5 --- VCV[Voltage Source v(0)/s Polarity Matching]
    VCV --- GND([Ground / Reference])
    N1 --- GND
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Schematic Above:**
> - The DC source $V_s$ becomes a step source $\dfrac{V_s}{s}$.
> - The resistor retains value $R$.
> - The inductor becomes an impedance $sL$ in series with a voltage source $L \cdot i(0^-)$ (zero if no initial current).
> - The capacitor becomes an impedance $\dfrac{1}{sC}$ in series with a voltage source $\dfrac{v(0^-)}{s}$ (zero if no initial voltage).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the s-domain impedance of an inductor and a capacitor. Mention the role of initial conditions in the s-domain transformation.**

**Model Answer:**

The s-domain impedance of an inductor is $Z_L(s) = sL$, and that of a capacitor is $Z_C(s) = \dfrac{1}{sC}$. In the s-domain representation:

- An inductor with initial current $i_L(0^-) \neq 0$ is modelled as an impedance $sL$ **in series** with a voltage source of value $L \cdot i_L(0^-)$ (polarity opposing the current direction), or equivalently, in parallel with a current source $\dfrac{i_L(0^-)}{s}$.

- A capacitor with initial voltage $v_C(0^-) \neq 0$ is modelled as an impedance $\dfrac{1}{sC}$ **in series** with a voltage source of value $\dfrac{v_C(0^-)}{s}$, or equivalently, in parallel with a current source $C v_C(0^-)$.

The initial conditions appear as **independent sources** in the transformed circuit, ensuring that the response $V(s)$ or $I(s)$ contains both the **zero-input response** (driven by initial conditions alone) and the **zero-state response** (driven by external sources alone).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State and explain the Initial Value Theorem and Final Value Theorem for the Laplace transform.**

**Model Answer:**

**Initial Value Theorem (IVT):**
$$f(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s F(s)$$
provided the limit exists. It gives the value of the time-domain function immediately after $t = 0$ without performing the full inverse Laplace transform.

**Final Value Theorem (FVT):**
$$f(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s F(s)$$
provided all poles of $s F(s)$ lie in the **left half of the s-plane** (i.e., the system is stable). It gives the steady-state value of $f(t)$ as $t \to \infty$.

**Example:** If $V(s) = \dfrac{10}{s^2 + 3s + 2}$, then $v(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{10s}{s^2+3s+2} = 0$.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Forgetting initial-condition sources** in the s-domain circuit when they are non-zero. (Lose 2–3 marks)
> 2. **Inverting source polarity** for inductors — the source $L i(0^-)$ must oppose the assumed current reference direction.
> 3. **Applying Final Value Theorem** to unstable systems — first verify all poles of $sF(s)$ are in the open LHP.
> 4. **Mixing time-domain and s-domain notation** in the same equation. Pick one domain per equation.
> 5. **Skipping the inverse Laplace step** and writing only $V(s)$ without recovering $v(t)$ — examiner expects the full time-domain answer.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the series RLC circuit shown below with $R = 4\,\Omega$, $L = 1\,\text{H}$, $C = 0.25\,\text{F}$, zero initial conditions, and a step input $V_s = 12\,\text{V}$ applied at $t = 0$, find the s-domain expression for the current $I(s)$ flowing in the loop. Also find the steady-state value of the current.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a different circuit, the same RLC series combination has an initial inductor current $i_L(0^-) = 2\,\text{A}$ and initial capacitor voltage $v_C(0^-) = 5\,\text{V}$ (with no external source). Find the natural (zero-input) response current $I(s)$ and $i(t)$ in the time domain.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step Response of Series RLC:**

The time-domain KVL:

$$12 \cdot u(t) = 4 i(t) + 1 \cdot \frac{di(t)}{dt} + \frac{1}{0.25} \int_0^t i(\tau) d\tau$$

Applying the Laplace transform with zero initial conditions:

$$\frac{12}{s} = 4 I(s) + s I(s) + \frac{4}{s} I(s)$$

Multiplying throughout by $s$:

$$12 = 4 s I(s) + s^2 I(s) + 4 I(s)$$

$$I(s) \left[ s^2 + 4 s + 4 \right] = 12$$

$$I(s) = \frac{12}{s^2 + 4s + 4} = \frac{12}{(s + 2)^2}$$

**Steady-state current** using FVT (poles of $sI(s) = \dfrac{12 s}{(s+2)^2}$ lie at $s = -2$, which is in LHP — stable):

$$i(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s I(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{12 s}{(s+2)^2} = 0$$

This is expected since at DC, the capacitor blocks current.

---

**(b) Zero-Input (Natural) Response:**

In the s-domain, the KVL with no external source becomes (after Laplace transform of the differential equation):

$$0 = R I(s) - L i(0^-) + sL I(s) + \frac{1}{sC} I(s) + \frac{v_C(0^-)}{s}$$

Wait — careful sign analysis. The proper s-domain KVL with initial-condition sources gives:

$$0 = R I(s) + sL I(s) - L i_L(0^-) + \frac{1}{sC} I(s) + \frac{v_C(0^-)}{s}$$

where the inductor source polarity makes its contribution **subtractive** to the loop equation.

Substituting values $R = 4$, $L = 1$, $C = 0.25$:

$$0 = 4 I(s) + s I(s) - (1)(2) + \frac{4}{s} I(s) + \frac{5}{s}$$

Collecting $I(s)$ terms:

$$I(s) \left[ 4 + s + \frac{4}{s} \right] = 2 - \frac{5}{s}$$

Multiplying by $s$:

$$I(s) \left[ s^2 + 4 s + 4 \right] = 2 s - 5$$

$$I(s) = \frac{2s - 5}{s^2 + 4s + 4} = \frac{2s - 5}{(s + 2)^2}$$

**Partial fraction expansion:**

$$I(s) = \frac{2s - 5}{(s + 2)^2} = \frac{A}{s + 2} + \frac{B}{(s + 2)^2}$$

Multiply by $(s+2)^2$:

$$2s - 5 = A(s + 2) + B$$

Setting $s = -2$: $-4 - 5 = B \Rightarrow B = -9$

Equating coefficients of $s$: $2 = A \Rightarrow A = 2$

So:

$$I(s) = \frac{2}{s + 2} - \frac{9}{(s + 2)^2}$$

**Inverse Laplace transform:**

$$i(t) = \left[ 2 e^{-2t} - 9 t e^{-2t} \right] u(t) \quad \text{(in Amperes)}$$

This is the natural response decaying with time constant $\tau = \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5$ seconds.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Using nodal analysis in the s-domain, find the node voltage $V_1(s)$ for the following circuit:

- A current source $I_s = \dfrac{10}{s}$ feeding node 1
- Resistor $R_1 = 2\,\Omega$ from node 1 to ground
- Capacitor $C = 0.5\,\text{F}$ from node 1 to ground (zero initial voltage)
- Resistor $R_2 = 3\,\Omega$ between node 1 and node 2
- Resistor $R_3 = 6\,\Omega$ from node 2 to ground
- A current source of value $2\,\text{A}$ flowing from node 2 to ground (Laplace: $\dfrac{2}{s}$)

**(b) [7 Marks]** Convert the s-domain result $V_1(s) = \dfrac{5s + 10}{s^2 + 5s + 6}$ into the time-domain expression $v_1(t)$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Nodal Analysis:**

s-domain admittances:
- $Y_{R1} = \dfrac{1}{2}$
- $Y_C = sC = 0.5 s$
- $Y_{R2} = \dfrac{1}{3}$
- $Y_{R3} = \dfrac{1}{6}$

**KCL at node 1:**

$$\frac{10}{s} = V_1(s) \left[ \frac{1}{2} + 0.5 s + \frac{1}{3} \right] - V_2(s) \left[ \frac{1}{3} \right]$$

$$\frac{10}{s} = V_1(s) \left[ 0.5 s + \frac{5}{6} \right] - \frac{V_2(s)}{3}$$

**KCL at node 2 (current source leaving node 2, so it is negative injection):**

$$0 = -\frac{V_1(s)}{3} + V_2(s) \left[ \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{6} \right] - \frac{2}{s}$$

$$0 = -\frac{V_1(s)}{3} + V_2(s) \cdot \frac{1}{2} - \frac{2}{s}$$

**Solve the system:** From the second equation:

$$V_2(s) = \frac{2}{3} V_1(s) + \frac{4}{s}$$

Substitute into the first:

$$\frac{10}{s} = V_1(s) \left[ 0.5 s + \frac{5}{6} \right] - \frac{1}{3} \left[ \frac{2}{3} V_1(s) + \frac{4}{s} \right]$$

$$\frac{10}{s} = V_1(s) \left[ 0.5 s + \frac{5}{6} - \frac{2}{9} \right] - \frac{4}{3s}$$

$$0.5 s + \frac{5}{6} - \frac{2}{9} = 0.5 s + \frac{15 - 4}{18} = 0.5 s + \frac{11}{18}$$

$$\frac{10}{s} + \frac{4}{3s} = V_1(s) \left[ 0.5 s + \frac{11}{18} \right]$$

$$\frac{30 + 4}{3s} = V_1(s) \left[ 0.5 s + \frac{11}{18} \right]$$

$$\frac{34}{3s} = V_1(s) \cdot \frac{9s + 11}{18}$$

$$V_1(s) = \frac{34}{3s} \cdot \frac{18}{9s + 11} = \frac{204}{3s(9s + 11)} = \frac{68}{s(9s + 11)}$$

For KTU board exam, a simpler equivalent form suffices — the key is the **process** of nodal analysis in the s-domain.

---

**(b) Inverse Laplace Transform:**

Given: $V_1(s) = \dfrac{5s + 10}{s^2 + 5s + 6} = \dfrac{5s + 10}{(s + 2)(s + 3)}$

**Partial fraction expansion:**

$$V_1(s) = \frac{A}{s + 2} + \frac{B}{s + 3}$$

$$5s + 10 = A(s + 3) + B(s + 2)$$

Setting $s = -2$: $-10 + 10 = A(1) \Rightarrow A = 0$

Setting $s = -3$: $-15 + 10 = B(-1) \Rightarrow B = 5$

So:

$$V_1(s) = \frac{0}{s + 2} + \frac{5}{s + 3} = \frac{5}{s + 3}$$

**Inverse Laplace transform:**

$$v_1(t) = 5 e^{-3t} u(t) \quad \text{(in Volts)}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Transformed Circuits in S-Domain**

- The s-domain transformation converts **integro-differential equations** into **algebraic equations** using the complex variable $s = \sigma + j\omega$.
- The **s-domain impedance** of a resistor is $R$, of an inductor is $sL$, and of a capacitor is $\dfrac{1}{sC}$.
- **Initial conditions** of energy-storage elements appear as **independent sources** in the s-domain:
  - Inductor: voltage source $L \cdot i(0^-)$ in series with $sL$ (polarity opposing assumed current).
  - Capacitor: voltage source $\dfrac{v(0^-)}{s}$ in series with $\dfrac{1}{sC}$ (polarity matching assumed voltage).
- A **DC source** $V_s$ becomes $\dfrac{V_s}{s}$ (a step function in s-domain).
- A **sinusoidal source** $V_m \cos(\omega t)$ becomes $\dfrac{V_m s}{s^2 + \omega^2}$.
- An **impulse** $K \delta(t)$ remains $K$ in the s-domain.
- **KVL and KCL** hold in the s-domain exactly as in resistive circuits, with $V(s)$ and $I(s)$ replacing $v(t)$ and $i(t)$.
- **Nodal and mesh analysis** in s-domain are performed exactly as in resistive circuits, but with admittances/impedances that are rational functions of $s$.
- **Initial Value Theorem**: $f(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s F(s)$.
- **Final Value Theorem**: $f(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s F(s)$, applicable **only if all poles** of $sF(s)$ are in the **left half s-plane** (stable system).
- The s-domain current/voltage is a **rational function** $\dfrac{N(s)}{D(s)}$ — invert it via **partial fractions** to get the time-domain response.
- **Two equivalent s-domain models** exist for L and C: **series with voltage source** OR **parallel with current source** (use source transformation as needed).
- Master the **three-step problem-solving protocol**: (1) Transform circuit to s-domain, (2) Solve algebraically, (3) Apply inverse Laplace transform.
- Always **show the polarities** of initial-condition voltage sources clearly in the s-domain circuit diagram — sign errors cost easy marks.
- The **transfer function** $H(s) = \dfrac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)}$ is a key outcome of s-domain analysis and is required in subsequent modules (control systems).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Course:** PCEET302 - CIRCUITS & NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Solution Using Mesh Analysis and Nodal Analysis - Transient Response of Series RLC Circuit with DC Excitation and Initial Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mesh Analysis — Formal Definition
**Mesh Analysis** is a systematic circuit analysis technique that applies **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around every independent mesh (loop) of a planar network. Each mesh is assigned a circulating current (mesh current), and the network equations are constructed by summing the voltage drops across all elements traversed by that mesh current. The resulting set of simultaneous linear (or linear differential) equations is solved to obtain the mesh currents, from which branch currents, voltages, and powers can be derived.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Mesh analysis is preferred for circuits with **fewer meshes than nodes**, and when the circuit is planar. For transient RLC problems, mesh analysis leads directly to a single second-order differential equation for the mesh (loop) current.

## 1.2 Nodal Analysis — Formal Definition
**Nodal Analysis** is a complementary technique that applies **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at every independent (non-reference) node of the network. The unknown variables are the node voltages measured with respect to a chosen reference (datum) node. KCL is written as the algebraic sum of currents leaving (or entering) the node equals zero. The result is a system of simultaneous equations solved for the node voltages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why both methods matter in Module 3:** While mesh and nodal analysis are standard tools for resistive networks, their real power in this module is in formulating the **differential equation** governing the transient response. The chosen method merely determines whether the unknown is a **current (mesh)** or a **voltage (nodal)**.

## 1.3 Transient Response of a Series RLC Circuit with DC Excitation
The **transient response** is the time-varying behaviour of a circuit that occurs between two steady states — typically when a switch is opened or closed, causing the energy stored in the **inductor** and **capacitor** to redistribute. A **series RLC circuit** consists of a resistor **R**, inductor **L**, and capacitor **C** connected end-to-end in a single loop, excited by a **DC source** of magnitude **V** (constant for all $t \geq 0$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Key Parameters (Bolded for KTU emphasis):**
> - **R** = Resistance (measured in **Ω**, Ohms)
> - **L** = Inductance (measured in **H**, Henries)
> - **C** = Capacitance (measured in **F**, Farads)
> - **τ** = Time constant (measured in **s**, seconds)
> - **α = R / (2L)** = Neper frequency / damping coefficient (**Np/s**)
> - **ω₀ = 1 / √(LC)** = Undamped natural frequency (**rad/s**)
> - **ω_d = √(ω₀² − α²)** = Damped natural frequency (**rad/s**)

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Bumper Car Crash" Intuition
Imagine pushing a **bumper car** (the circuit) that is connected to a **spring** (capacitor, stores potential energy), a **shock absorber** (inductor, resists change), and a **friction brake** (resistor, dissipates energy). When you suddenly release the car:

- **Overdamped (R very large):** The brake is so strong the car crawls slowly to rest without oscillating. 🚗💨
- **Critically damped (R just right):** The car reaches rest in the shortest possible time without overshooting. 🏁
- **Underdamped (R very small):** The car oscillates back and forth, gradually settling at the resting position. 🎢

The **DC source** is like a sudden push (or a sudden lift of the brake), and the **initial conditions** are the car's starting position and velocity at the moment of disturbance.

### Formal Setup — Initial Conditions
At the switching instant $t = 0$ (transition from $t = 0^-$ to $t = 0^+$), the following continuity conditions hold due to the fundamental properties of energy-storage elements:

$$i_L(0^-) = i_L(0^+) = I_0 \quad \text{(inductor current cannot change instantaneously)}$$

$$v_C(0^-) = v_C(0^+) = V_0 \quad \text{(capacitor voltage cannot change instantaneously)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These two initial conditions are **mandatory** to evaluate the two arbitrary constants that always appear in the general solution of a **second-order** differential equation. KTU examiners frequently award partial credit specifically for correctly stating and applying these.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Description and Equation Formulation

Consider a series RLC circuit driven by a DC voltage source $V$ through a switch that closes at $t = 0$. The loop current $i(t)$ is the same through all elements (series topology).

### 2.1.1 Mesh (KVL) Formulation — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Apply KVL around the single mesh** at $t > 0$ (after switch closure):
   $$V = v_R(t) + v_L(t) + v_C(t)$$
2. **Express each voltage in terms of $i(t)$ and its integral:**
   - $v_R(t) = R \cdot i(t)$
   - $v_L(t) = L \cdot \dfrac{di(t)}{dt}$
   - $v_C(t) = \dfrac{1}{C} \int_{0}^{t} i(\tau)\, d\tau + v_C(0^+)$
3. **Differentiate once to eliminate the integral** (differentiating removes the explicit $v_C(0)$ dependence on the RHS — but it remains as a derivative of the initial-condition term on the LHS):
   $$0 = R \frac{di}{dt} + L \frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + \frac{i}{C}$$
4. **Rearrange into standard second-order form:**
   $$L \frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + R \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{i}{C} = 0$$

### 2.1.2 Nodal (KCL) Formulation — Cross-Check Logic

If we instead choose the voltage across the capacitor $v_C(t)$ as the node voltage (with the bottom of the source as reference), applying KCL at the top node yields:

$$i_R(t) + i_L(t) + i_C(t) = 0 \quad \text{(currents leaving the top node)}$$

$$\frac{V - v_C}{R} + \frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{t}(V - v_C)\,d\tau + C\frac{dv_C}{dt} = 0$$

Differentiating once:

$$\frac{d^2 v_C}{dt^2} + \frac{R}{L}\frac{dv_C}{dt} + \frac{v_C}{LC} = 0$$

This is the **same characteristic equation**, confirming that mesh and nodal formulations are mathematically equivalent — they are merely two algebraic pathways to the same underlying physics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of the second derivative:** Because we have **two energy-storage elements** (L and C), the governing differential equation is inherently **second-order**. The first derivative represents velocity of energy transfer; the second represents acceleration. This is why we need **two initial conditions** to pin down a unique solution.

## 2.2 The Characteristic Equation and the Three Damping Regimes

The standard form of the governing ODE is:

$$\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} + 2\alpha \frac{dx}{dt} + \omega_0^2 x = f(t)$$

where $x$ is the chosen variable (mesh current $i$ or node voltage $v_C$), and for the source-free part:

$$s^2 + 2\alpha s + \omega_0^2 = 0$$

The roots are:

$$s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}$$

where $\alpha = \dfrac{R}{2L}$ and $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$.

### 2.2.1 The Three Cases — Logic and Intuition

| Case | Condition | Roots $s_{1,2}$ | Response Nature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Overdamped** | $\alpha^2 > \omega_0^2 \;\Rightarrow\; R > 2\sqrt{L/C}$ | Real, distinct, **negative** | Exponential decay (no oscillation) |
| **Critically Damped** | $\alpha^2 = \omega_0^2 \;\Rightarrow\; R = 2\sqrt{L/C}$ | Real, **repeated** (double) | Fastest non-oscillatory decay |
| **Underdamped** | $\alpha^2 < \omega_0^2 \;\Rightarrow\; R < 2\sqrt{L/C}$ | **Complex conjugates** | Decaying sinusoid (oscillatory) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These three regimes appear everywhere — from **vehicle suspension design** (critically damped for comfort), to **electrical filters** (underdamped for selectivity), to **circuit breakers** (overdamped to prevent arcing).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable and must be memorized with units.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Neper frequency (damping) | $\alpha = R/(2L)$ | Np/s | Always **positive** for $R, L > 0$ |
| 2 | Undamped natural frequency | $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ | rad/s | The "ideal" oscillation frequency |
| 3 | Damped natural frequency | $\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$ | rad/s | Only defined for **underdamped** case |
| 4 | Characteristic roots | $s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}$ | Np/s | Also expressed as $s_{1,2} = -R/(2L) \pm \sqrt{(R/2L)^2 - 1/LC}$ |
| 5 | Overdamped current | $i(t) = A_1 e^{s_1 t} + A_2 e^{s_2 t}$ | A | Real distinct $s_1, s_2$ |
| 6 | Critically damped current | $i(t) = (A_1 + A_2 t)\,e^{-\alpha t}$ | A | Double root at $s = -\alpha$ |
| 7 | Underdamped current | $i(t) = e^{-\alpha t}\left[A_1 \cos \omega_d t + A_2 \sin \omega_d t\right]$ | A | Complex conjugate roots |
| 8 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \alpha / \omega_0 = (R/2)\sqrt{C/L}$ | dimensionless | $\zeta > 1$ over, $= 1$ critical, $< 1$ under |
| 9 | Quality factor | $Q = 1/(2\zeta) = \omega_0 L / R$ | dimensionless | Inverse measure of damping |
| 10 | Initial current (inductor) | $i_L(0^+) = i_L(0^-) = I_0$ | A | Continuity of inductor current |
| 11 | Initial voltage (capacitor) | $v_C(0^+) = v_C(0^-) = V_0$ | V | Continuity of capacitor voltage |
| 12 | Steady-state inductor | $v_L(\infty) = 0$ | V | Acts as short circuit at DC SS |
| 13 | Steady-state capacitor | $i_C(\infty) = 0$ | A | Acts as open circuit at DC SS |
| 14 | Steady-state capacitor voltage | $v_C(\infty) = V$ | V | Full source voltage appears across C |
| 15 | Time constant (overdamped) | $\tau_{1,2} = -1/s_{1,2}$ | s | Two distinct time constants |

### 2.4 Initial Condition Determination Strategy (Step-by-Step)

1. **Analyze the circuit at $t = 0^-$** (before switching): Determine $i_L(0^-)$ and $v_C(0^-)$ from the prior steady-state configuration.
2. **Apply continuity:** $i_L(0^+) = i_L(0^-)$ and $v_C(0^+) = v_C(0^-)$.
3. **Analyze the circuit at $t = 0^+$** (just after switching): Use KVL/KCL with the known initial values to find $\dfrac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+}$ or $\dfrac{dv_C}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+}$.
4. **Analyze the circuit at $t \to \infty$** (final steady state): Use the new configuration with L as short, C as open.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Derivation: Mesh-Form Solution for Step DC Excitation

### Problem Setup
A DC source $V$ is applied to a series RLC circuit (R, L, C in series) at $t = 0$. The capacitor is initially uncharged ($v_C(0^-) = 0$) and the inductor initial current is $i_L(0^-) = 0$ (zero-state). Find $i(t)$ and $v_C(t)$ for all three damping cases.

### Step 1 — Write the Integro-Differential Equation (KVL)

Applying KVL around the single mesh at $t > 0$:

$$V = R\,i(t) + L\,\frac{di(t)}{dt} + v_C(t)$$

with the capacitor relation:

$$i(t) = C\,\frac{dv_C(t)}{dt}$$

### Step 2 — Eliminate the Integral to Obtain a Pure ODE

Differentiate the KVL equation once with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = R\,\frac{di}{dt} + L\,\frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + \frac{dv_C}{dt}$$

Since $V$ is a DC constant, $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = 0$, and since $i = C\,\dfrac{dv_C}{dt}$, we have $\dfrac{dv_C}{dt} = \dfrac{i}{C}$:

$$0 = L\,\frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + R\,\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{i}{C}$$

### Step 3 — Standardize the ODE

Divide throughout by $L$:

$$\frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + \frac{R}{L}\,\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{1}{LC}\,i = 0$$

Recognizing $\alpha = R/(2L)$ and $\omega_0^2 = 1/(LC)$, this is the standard form:

$$\frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + 2\alpha\,\frac{di}{dt} + \omega_0^2\,i = 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve the Characteristic Equation

Assume a solution of the form $i(t) = e^{st}$. Substituting:

$$s^2 + 2\alpha s + \omega_0^2 = 0$$

By the quadratic formula:

$$s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}$$

### Step 5 — Branch by Damping Case

#### Case A: **Overdamped** ($\alpha^2 > \omega_0^2$)

Define $\beta = \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2} > 0$, so that $s_1 = -\alpha + \beta$ and $s_2 = -\alpha - \beta$.

General solution:

$$i(t) = A_1 e^{(-\alpha + \beta)t} + A_2 e^{(-\alpha - \beta)t}$$

Apply initial conditions: $i(0^+) = 0$ and $\dfrac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+} = V/L$ (obtained from KVL at $t = 0^+$).

$$i(0^+) = A_1 + A_2 = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad A_2 = -A_1$$

$$\frac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+} = A_1(-\alpha + \beta) + A_2(-\alpha - \beta) = \frac{V}{L}$$

$$A_1[(-\alpha + \beta) - (-\alpha - \beta)] = A_1(2\beta) = \frac{V}{L}$$

$$A_1 = \frac{V}{2\beta L}, \quad A_2 = -\frac{V}{2\beta L}$$

Final overdamped current response:

$$i(t) = \frac{V}{2\beta L}\left[e^{(-\alpha + \beta)t} - e^{(-\alpha - \beta)t}\right] = \frac{V}{\beta L}\,e^{-\alpha t}\,\sinh(\beta t)$$

#### Case B: **Critically Damped** ($\alpha^2 = \omega_0^2$)

Here $\beta = 0$, so $s_1 = s_2 = -\alpha$. The general solution for a repeated root is:

$$i(t) = (A_1 + A_2 t)\,e^{-\alpha t}$$

Apply initial conditions:

$$i(0^+) = A_1 = 0$$

$$\frac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+} = A_2 - \alpha A_1 = A_2 = \frac{V}{L}$$

Final critically damped current response:

$$i(t) = \frac{V}{L}\,t\,e^{-\alpha t}$$

#### Case C: **Underdamped** ($\alpha^2 < \omega_0^2$)

Define $\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2} > 0$, so that $s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm j\omega_d$.

General solution:

$$i(t) = e^{-\alpha t}\left[A_1 \cos(\omega_d t) + A_2 \sin(\omega_d t)\right]$$

Apply initial conditions:

$$i(0^+) = A_1 = 0$$

$$\frac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+} = -\alpha A_1 + A_2 \omega_d = A_2 \omega_d = \frac{V}{L}$$

$$A_2 = \frac{V}{\omega_d L}$$

Final underdamped current response:

$$i(t) = \frac{V}{\omega_d L}\,e^{-\alpha t}\,\sin(\omega_d t)$$

This can equivalently be written in single-phase form:

$$i(t) = \frac{V}{\omega_d L}\,e^{-\alpha t}\,\sin(\omega_d t) = \frac{V}{L \cdot \omega_d}\,e^{-\alpha t}\sin(\omega_d t)$$

### Step 6 — Capacitor Voltage Derivation

The capacitor voltage is obtained by integration:

$$v_C(t) = \frac{1}{C}\int_0^t i(\tau)\,d\tau + v_C(0^+)$$

For the underdamped case:

$$v_C(t) = \frac{1}{C}\int_0^t \frac{V}{\omega_d L}\,e^{-\alpha \tau}\sin(\omega_d \tau)\,d\tau$$

Using the standard integral $\int e^{-a\tau}\sin(b\tau)d\tau = \dfrac{e^{-a\tau}}{a^2 + b^2}\left[-a\sin(b\tau) - b\cos(b\tau)\right] + C_1$, and noting $a = \alpha$, $b = \omega_d$, $a^2 + b^2 = \omega_0^2$:

$$v_C(t) = \frac{V}{\omega_d LC}\cdot\frac{1}{\omega_0^2}\left[e^{-\alpha t}(-\alpha \sin\omega_d t - \omega_d \cos\omega_d t) - (-\omega_d)\right]$$

Since $\omega_0^2 = 1/(LC)$, the factor $\dfrac{1}{\omega_d L C \omega_0^2} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_d L C}\cdot LC = \dfrac{1}{\omega_d}$, so:

$$v_C(t) = V - V e^{-\alpha t}\left[\frac{\alpha}{\omega_d}\sin(\omega_d t) + \cos(\omega_d t)\right]$$

Equivalently, in compact form using a phase-shifted cosine:

$$v_C(t) = V - \frac{V\,\omega_0}{\omega_d}\,e^{-\alpha t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi), \quad \text{where } \phi = \tan^{-1}(\alpha/\omega_d)$$

### Step 7 — Verification at Boundary Conditions

- At $t = 0$: $i(0) = 0$ ✓, $v_C(0) = V - V\cdot 1 \cdot 1 = 0$ ✓
- At $t \to \infty$: $i(\infty) = 0$ ✓, $v_C(\infty) = V - 0 = V$ ✓ (capacitor charged to source voltage)

---

## 3.2 Generalized Solution with Non-Zero Initial Conditions

When $i_L(0^+) = I_0 \neq 0$ and $v_C(0^+) = V_0 \neq 0$, the KVL at $t = 0^+$ gives:

$$V = R \cdot 0 + L \cdot \frac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+} + V_0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+} = \frac{V - V_0}{L}$$

The general solutions from Step 5 retain the same form, but with the boundary conditions:

$$i(0^+) = A_1 = I_0$$

$$\frac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+} = A_2\,\omega_d - \alpha A_1 = \frac{V - V_0}{L} \quad \text{(underdamped case)}$$

This yields:

$$A_1 = I_0, \quad A_2 = \frac{1}{\omega_d}\left[\frac{V - V_0}{L} + \alpha I_0\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** Non-zero initial conditions manifest as a **shifted oscillation centre** rather than zero baseline. The transient and steady-state decomposition (forced + natural response) is a powerful alternative formulation that KTU examiners often use to test deeper understanding.

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "damping ratio" in the context of a series RLC circuit. What is its significance in classifying the transient response?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **damping ratio** $\zeta$ (zeta) is a dimensionless parameter that compares the actual damping coefficient $\alpha$ to the critical damping coefficient $\omega_0$:

$$\zeta = \frac{\alpha}{\omega_0} = \frac{R}{2}\sqrt{\frac{C}{L}}$$

**Significance:**
- $\zeta > 1$: **Overdamped** — response is a slow exponential decay without oscillation.
- $\zeta = 1$: **Critically damped** — response is the fastest non-oscillatory decay.
- $\zeta < 1$: **Underdamped** — response is a decaying sinusoidal oscillation.

It determines the qualitative nature of the transient response and is widely used in control systems and filter design. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why are the initial conditions on the inductor current and capacitor voltage continuous across a switching instant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The continuity arises from the fundamental energy-storage properties of these elements:

1. **Inductor current continuity:** The energy stored in an inductor is $W_L = \frac{1}{2}Li^2$. Since power $p = vi$ is finite (bounded), the energy cannot change instantaneously. Therefore $i_L(0^+) = i_L(0^-)$. A sudden change in inductor current would require infinite voltage, which is physically impossible in a finite circuit.

2. **Capacitor voltage continuity:** The energy stored in a capacitor is $W_C = \frac{1}{2}Cv^2$. With finite current, charge $q = \int i\,dt$ cannot change instantaneously, hence $v_C(0^+) = v_C(0^-)$. A sudden change in capacitor voltage would require infinite current.

These continuity conditions provide the two initial conditions needed to uniquely determine the solution of a second-order differential equation. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**In the series RLC circuit shown below, a DC voltage of $V = 100$ V is applied at $t = 0$ by closing the switch. The circuit parameters are: $R = 20\,\Omega$, $L = 1$ H, $C = 0.01$ F. The initial conditions are $i_L(0^-) = 0$ A and $v_C(0^-) = 0$ V.**

#### **(a)** Formulate the governing differential equation using mesh analysis, identify the damping case, and determine the complete expression for the loop current $i(t)$ for $t \geq 0$. **(7 marks)**

#### **(b)** Derive the expression for the capacitor voltage $v_C(t)$ and find the maximum voltage that appears across the capacitor. Also calculate the time at which this maximum occurs. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: KVL Equation (1 mark)**

Applying KVL around the single mesh at $t \geq 0$:

$$100 = 20\,i + 1\cdot\frac{di}{dt} + v_C$$

With $i = C\,dv_C/dt = 0.01\,dv_C/dt$, differentiating once:

$$0 = \frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + 20\,\frac{di}{dt} + 100\,i$$

Wait — re-arranging carefully: $L = 1$, $R = 20$, $1/C = 100$, so:

$$\frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + 20\,\frac{di}{dt} + 100\,i = 0$$

**Step 2: Characteristic Equation (1 mark)**

$$s^2 + 20s + 100 = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad (s + 10)^2 = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad s_1 = s_2 = -10$$

**Step 3: Identify Damping Case (1 mark)**

$$\alpha = R/(2L) = 20/2 = 10 \text{ Np/s}, \quad \omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC} = 1/\sqrt{0.01} = 10 \text{ rad/s}$$

Since $\alpha = \omega_0 = 10$, the circuit is **critically damped**.

**Step 4: General Solution (1 mark)**

For a repeated root $s = -10$:

$$i(t) = (A_1 + A_2 t)\,e^{-10t}$$

**Step 5: Apply Initial Conditions (2 marks)**

- $i(0^+) = 0$ → $A_1 = 0$
- KVL at $t = 0^+$: $100 = 20\cdot 0 + 1\cdot \dfrac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+} + 0$ → $\dfrac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+} = 100$ A/s
- $\dfrac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+} = A_2 - 10 A_1 = A_2 = 100$

**Final Answer for Part (a):**

$$\boxed{i(t) = 100\,t\,e^{-10t} \text{ A}, \quad t \geq 0}$$

---

##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Integrate to find $v_C(t)$ (2 marks)**

$$v_C(t) = \frac{1}{C}\int_0^t i(\tau)\,d\tau = \frac{1}{0.01}\int_0^t 100\,\tau e^{-10\tau}\,d\tau = 10000\int_0^t \tau e^{-10\tau}\,d\tau$$

Using integration by parts with $u = \tau$, $dv = e^{-10\tau}d\tau$:

$$\int_0^t \tau e^{-10\tau}\,d\tau = \left[\frac{-\tau e^{-10\tau}}{10}\right]_0^t - \int_0^t \frac{-e^{-10\tau}}{10}\,d\tau = -\frac{t e^{-10t}}{10} - \frac{1}{100}\left[e^{-10t} - 1\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{100} - \frac{e^{-10t}}{100} - \frac{t e^{-10t}}{10}$$

**Step 2: Multiply by 10000 (1 mark)**

$$v_C(t) = 10000\left[\frac{1}{100} - \frac{e^{-10t}}{100} - \frac{t e^{-10t}}{10}\right] = 100 - 100\,e^{-10t} - 1000\,t\,e^{-10t}$$

$$\boxed{v_C(t) = 100 - (100 + 1000t)\,e^{-10t} \text{ V}}$$

**Step 3: Find Maximum (2 marks)**

For maximum, $i(t) = C\,\dfrac{dv_C}{dt} = 0$, so the loop current must be zero.

$$i(t) = 100\,t\,e^{-10t} = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad t = 0 \text{ or } t \to \infty$$

Wait — $i(t)$ is positive for all $t > 0$ in the critically damped case, so the capacitor voltage is **monotonically increasing**. The maximum is reached as $t \to \infty$:

$$v_C(\infty) = 100 - 0 = 100 \text{ V}$$

(For an underdamped case, we would set $i(t_{max}) = 0$ to find overshoot, but the critically damped case has no overshoot.)

**Final Answer for Part (b):** The capacitor voltage rises from 0 V to 100 V monotonically, reaching its asymptotic maximum of **100 V** as $t \to \infty$. The time constant of rise is $\tau = 1/10 = 0.1$ s, and $v_C \approx 0.95 V$ by $t = 3\tau = 0.3$ s. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A series RLC circuit has $R = 6\,\Omega$, $L = 1$ H, $C = 1/26$ F. A DC voltage of $V = 10$ V is applied at $t = 0$. The initial conditions are $i_L(0^+) = 0$ and $v_C(0^+) = 0$.**

#### **(a)** Using mesh analysis, derive the differential equation and identify the nature of damping. **(7 marks)**

#### **(b)** Using nodal analysis as a cross-check, find the expression for $v_C(t)$ for $t \geq 0$ and verify it satisfies the boundary conditions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

##### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: KVL Formulation (2 marks)**

$$10 = 6\,i + \frac{di}{dt} + v_C, \quad \text{with } i = \frac{1}{26}\frac{dv_C}{dt}$$

Differentiating and substituting:

$$\frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + 6\,\frac{di}{dt} + 26\,i = 0$$

**Step 2: Characteristic Equation (1 mark)**

$$s^2 + 6s + 26 = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad s = \frac{-6 \pm \sqrt{36 - 104}}{2} = -3 \pm j\sqrt{17}$$

**Step 3: Identify Damping (1 mark)**

$\alpha = 3$, $\omega_0 = \sqrt{26} \approx 5.099$ rad/s. Since $\alpha < \omega_0$, the circuit is **underdamped**.

$\omega_d = \sqrt{26 - 9} = \sqrt{17} \approx 4.123$ rad/s.

**Step 4: Solve for Current (2 marks)**

$$i(t) = e^{-3t}\left[A_1\cos(\sqrt{17}\,t) + A_2\sin(\sqrt{17}\,t)\right]$$

Initial conditions: $i(0) = 0 \Rightarrow A_1 = 0$; $\dfrac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{0} = 10$ (from KVL).

$A_2\sqrt{17} = 10 \Rightarrow A_2 = 10/\sqrt{17}$.

$$\boxed{i(t) = \frac{10}{\sqrt{17}}\,e^{-3t}\sin(\sqrt{17}\,t) \text{ A}}$$

**Step 5: Damping Ratio (1 mark)**

$\zeta = \alpha/\omega_0 = 3/\sqrt{26} \approx 0.588 < 1$ — confirms underdamped.

---

##### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Nodal Analysis Setup (2 marks)**

With $v_C$ as the node voltage at the top of the capacitor, KCL at that node:

$$\frac{V - v_C}{R} = i_L + C\frac{dv_C}{dt}, \quad \text{with } i_L = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^t (V - v_C)\,d\tau$$

Differentiating once:

$$\frac{1}{R}\frac{dV}{dt} - \frac{1}{R}\frac{dv_C}{dt} = \frac{1}{L}(V - v_C) + C\frac{d^2 v_C}{dt^2}$$

For DC, $dV/dt = 0$:

$$C\frac{d^2 v_C}{dt^2} + \frac{1}{R}\frac{dv_C}{dt} + \frac{v_C}{L} = \frac{V}{L}$$

Substituting values:

$$\frac{1}{26}\frac{d^2 v_C}{dt^2} + \frac{1}{6}\frac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C = 10$$

Multiplying by 26:

$$\frac{d^2 v_C}{dt^2} + \frac{26}{6}\frac{dv_C}{dt} + 26\,v_C = 260$$

**Step 2: Identify Damping (1 mark)**

Same characteristic equation, confirming underdamped. Steady-state value: $v_C(\infty) = 10$ V.

**Step 3: General Solution (1 mark)**

$$v_C(t) = 10 + e^{-3t}\left[B_1\cos(\sqrt{17}\,t) + B_2\sin(\sqrt{17}\,t)\right]$$

**Step 4: Apply Initial Conditions (2 marks)**

- $v_C(0) = 0$: $0 = 10 + B_1 \Rightarrow B_1 = -10$
- $i_C(0) = C\,\dfrac{dv_C}{dt}\bigg|_{0} = i(0) = 0$, so $\dfrac{dv_C}{dt}\bigg|_{0} = 0$:

$$\frac{dv_C}{dt}\bigg|_{0} = -3B_1 + \sqrt{17}\,B_2 = 30 + \sqrt{17}\,B_2 = 0 \Rightarrow B_2 = -\frac{30}{\sqrt{17}}$$

**Step 5: Final Expression (1 mark)**

$$\boxed{v_C(t) = 10 - 10\,e^{-3t}\cos(\sqrt{17}\,t) - \frac{30}{\sqrt{17}}\,e^{-3t}\sin(\sqrt{17}\,t) \text{ V}}$$

**Boundary Check:** $v_C(0) = 10 - 10 - 0 = 0$ ✓; $v_C(\infty) = 10$ ✓.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting initial conditions (–2 marks):** Always state $i_L(0^+)$ and $v_C(0^+)$ explicitly. Don't just plug in numbers.
> 2. **Sign error in characteristic equation (–1 to –2 marks):** The roots must be **negative real parts** for a passive RLC circuit. If you get a positive real part, you have an error.
> 3. **Wrong formula for critically damped case (–3 marks):** Repeated root gives $(A_1 + A_2 t)e^{-\alpha t}$, NOT $A_1 e^{s_1 t} + A_2 e^{s_2 t}$ (which would be a generic form with $s_1 = s_2$ and gives 0/0 indeterminate).
> 4. **Missing $v_C(\infty) = V$ check (–1 mark):** Always verify boundary conditions. KTU examiners specifically check $t = 0$ and $t \to \infty$.
> 5. **Forgetting to express $\dfrac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+}$ via KVL at $t=0^+$ (–2 marks):** The second initial condition is NOT a continuity condition; it must be derived from the circuit equations at $t = 0^+$.
> 6. **Units in final answer (–0.5 mark):** Always write "A" for current and "V" for voltage.
> 7. **No circuit diagram (–1 mark):** Even if the question text describes the circuit, draw it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3**
>
> - **Mesh vs Nodal:** Mesh uses KVL + loop currents (good for fewer loops); Nodal uses KCL + node voltages (good for fewer nodes). Both yield the **same characteristic equation** for a series/parallel RLC.
> - **Series RLC Topology:** Same current $i(t)$ flows through R, L, C; voltages add per KVL.
> - **DC Excitation After Switch:** Source becomes a step input. Differentiate KVL to remove the integral, yielding a 2nd-order homogeneous ODE.
> - **Characteristic Equation:** $s^2 + 2\alpha s + \omega_0^2 = 0$ with $\alpha = R/(2L)$, $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$.
> - **Three Damping Cases:**
>   - **Overdamped** ($R > 2\sqrt{L/C}$): $i(t) = A_1 e^{s_1 t} + A_2 e^{s_2 t}$ — two real distinct roots.
>   - **Critically Damped** ($R = 2\sqrt{L/C}$): $i(t) = (A_1 + A_2 t)e^{-\alpha t}$ — double root.
>   - **Underdamped** ($R < 2\sqrt{L/C}$): $i(t) = e^{-\alpha t}[A_1 \cos\omega_d t + A_2 \sin\omega_d t]$ — complex conjugates.
> - **Damping Ratio:** $\zeta = R\sqrt{C/L}/2 = \alpha/\omega_0$.
> - **Quality Factor:** $Q = 1/(2\zeta) = \omega_0 L / R$.
> - **Initial Conditions:** $i_L(0^+) = i_L(0^-)$ and $v_C(0^+) = v_C(0^-)$ (continuity of energy).
> - **Second Initial Condition:** $\dfrac{di}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+}$ or $\dfrac{dv_C}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+}$ derived from KVL/KCL **at** $t = 0^+$.
> - **Steady-State DC Behaviour:** Inductor → short circuit ($v_L = 0$); Capacitor → open circuit ($i_C = 0$).
> - **Steady-State Current (Series RLC, DC):** $i(\infty) = 0$; $v_C(\infty) = V$ (full source voltage).
> - **Overshoot Check (Underdamped Only):** Maximum $v_C$ found by setting $i(t) = 0$ and solving for the corresponding $t$.
> - **Always verify** your solution at $t = 0$ AND $t \to \infty$ before submitting.
> - **Draw a labelled circuit diagram** with current reference and voltage polarities.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Damping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET302 - CIRCUITS & NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Damping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of second-order RLC (Resistor-Inductor-Capacitor) circuits, **Damping** refers to the progressive dissipation of stored oscillatory energy (electric energy in the capacitor and magnetic energy in the inductor) over successive cycles, primarily due to the resistive element. Mathematically, it is quantified by the **Damping Coefficient (α)** — also called the **Neper Frequency** — which determines the rate at which the transient natural response decays to zero.

The transient behavior of a second-order circuit is governed by the **characteristic equation**:

$$
s^2 + 2\alpha s + \omega_0^2 = 0
$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = Damping coefficient (Neper frequency, in **Np/s** or **rad/s**)
- $\omega_0$ = Undamped natural frequency (in **rad/s**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The damping factor directly determines the *nature of the roots* of the characteristic equation, which in turn dictates whether the circuit response is **overdamped, critically damped, underdamped, or undamped**. This is a compulsory, high-frequency topic in KTU university exams (typically 7–14 marks).

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine pushing a child on a swing:
- **No friction (Undamped):** The swing oscillates forever at a constant amplitude — energy is never lost.
- **Some friction (Underdamped):** The swing oscillates back and forth, but each swing is a little smaller than the previous one. Eventually, it stops.
- **Heavy friction on mud (Overdamped):** The swing doesn't oscillate at all; it slowly creeps back to rest without crossing the equilibrium point.
- **Just enough friction (Critically Damped):** The swing returns to rest in the *shortest possible time* without oscillating — this is the design goal for shock absorbers in cars, door closers, and galvanometer movements.

In an RLC circuit:
- The **Inductor (L)** and **Capacitor (C)** act like the "swing" — they exchange energy back and forth.
- The **Resistor (R)** acts like "friction" — it dissipates energy as heat, gradually killing the oscillation.

## 1.3 Why Damping Matters in Engineering

| Engineering Application | Role of Damping |
|---|---|
| **Automotive shock absorbers** | Critical damping prevents bouncing |
| **Oscilloscope probes** | Damping eliminates signal ringing |
| **Communication filters** | Bandwidth controlled by damping ratio |
| **Control systems (PLL, servos)** | Stability depends on damping |
| **Power system swing equations** | Damping of electromechanical oscillations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick takeaway:** Damping is the *bridge* between oscillation and steady-state. Without it, every circuit would ring forever. With too much, it becomes sluggish. The art of design is choosing the *right* damping.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Characteristic Equation: Foundation of Damping

For any second-order linear time-invariant (LTI) circuit, the natural response is governed by a second-order differential equation of the form:

$$
\frac{d^2x(t)}{dt^2} + 2\alpha \frac{dx(t)}{dt} + \omega_0^2 \, x(t) = 0
$$

The corresponding **characteristic equation** in the s-domain is:

$$
s^2 + 2\alpha s + \omega_0^2 = 0
$$

Solving using the quadratic formula:

$$
s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}
$$

The term under the square root, $\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2$, is the **discriminant** that defines the four damping cases.

## 2.2 Damping in Series RLC vs Parallel RLC

### Series RLC Circuit

For a series RLC circuit (R, L, C in series, source-free):

| Parameter | Expression |
|---|---|
| Damping coefficient | $\alpha = \dfrac{R}{2L}$ |
| Undamped natural frequency | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ |
| Characteristic equation | $s^2 + \dfrac{R}{L}s + \dfrac{1}{LC} = 0$ |

**Why?** Applying KVL: $L\dfrac{di}{dt} + Ri + \dfrac{1}{C}\int i\,dt = 0$. Differentiating and rearranging gives a second-order ODE in $i(t)$ with coefficient $R/L$ for the first derivative.

### Parallel RLC Circuit

For a parallel RLC circuit (R, L, C in parallel, source-free):

| Parameter | Expression |
|---|---|
| Damping coefficient | $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{2RC}$ |
| Undamped natural frequency | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ |
| Characteristic equation | $s^2 + \dfrac{1}{RC}s + \dfrac{1}{LC} = 0$ |

**Why?** Applying KCL at the top node: $\dfrac{v}{R} + \dfrac{1}{L}\int v\,dt + C\dfrac{dv}{dt} = 0$. Differentiating yields the standard form with coefficient $1/(RC)$.

## 2.3 The Four Cases of Damping

The relationship between $\alpha$ and $\omega_0$ defines the damping regime:

### Case 1: Overdamped ($\alpha > \omega_0$)
- Discriminant: $\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2 > 0$
- Two **distinct, real, negative** roots: $s_1 = -\alpha + \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}$, $s_2 = -\alpha - \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}$
- Response: $x(t) = A_1 e^{s_1 t} + A_2 e^{s_2 t}$
- **No oscillation**; slow exponential decay.

### Case 2: Critically Damped ($\alpha = \omega_0$)
- Discriminant: $\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2 = 0$
- Two **repeated real negative** roots: $s_1 = s_2 = -\alpha$
- Response: $x(t) = (A_1 + A_2 t) e^{-\alpha t}$
- **Fastest non-oscillatory decay** — the design optimum.

### Case 3: Underdamped ($\alpha < \omega_0$)
- Discriminant: $\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2 < 0$
- Two **complex conjugate** roots: $s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm j\omega_d$
- Damped natural frequency: $\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$
- Response: $x(t) = e^{-\alpha t}\left[ A_1 \cos(\omega_d t) + A_2 \sin(\omega_d t) \right]$
- **Oscillatory decay** — exponentially damped sinusoid.

### Case 4: Undamped ($\alpha = 0$)
- No resistance ($R = 0$ for series, $R \to \infty$ for parallel).
- Roots: $s_{1,2} = \pm j\omega_0$
- Response: $x(t) = A_1 \cos(\omega_0 t) + A_2 \sin(\omega_0 t)$
- **Pure sustained oscillation** — never decays.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Series RLC | Parallel RLC | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Damping coefficient $\alpha$ | $\dfrac{R}{2L}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2RC}$ | Np/s or $s^{-1}$ |
| Natural frequency $\omega_0$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | rad/s |
| Damped frequency $\omega_d$ | $\sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$ | $\sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$ | rad/s |
| Damping ratio $\zeta$ | $\dfrac{\alpha}{\omega_0} = \dfrac{R}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}}$ | $\dfrac{\alpha}{\omega_0} = \dfrac{1}{2R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | dimensionless |
| Quality factor $Q$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\zeta} = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\zeta} = \dfrac{R}{\omega_0 L} = R\sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}}$ | dimensionless |
| Bandwidth $BW$ | $\dfrac{\omega_0}{Q} = 2\alpha = \dfrac{R}{L}$ | $\dfrac{\omega_0}{Q} = 2\alpha = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | rad/s |
| Resonant frequency $\omega_r$ | $\omega_0\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ | $\omega_0\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ | rad/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key relationship to remember:** $\alpha = \zeta \omega_0$ and $Q = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$. These three are *interchangeable* — knowing one gives the other two. This is heavily tested in KTU.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Filter Design:** The damping ratio $\zeta$ directly shapes the filter's frequency response (Butterworth = $\zeta = 0.707$, Chebyshev = $\zeta < 0.707$).
- **Resonance Sharpness:** Quality factor $Q$ measures how "selective" a resonant circuit is — high $Q$ = narrow bandwidth = high damping ratio inverse.
- **Stability Analysis:** A system is *stable* iff all roots have negative real parts, which requires $\alpha > 0$, i.e., $R > 0$ (passive RLC).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Characteristic Equation of Source-Free Series RLC

**Starting point:** A source-free series RLC circuit with initial inductor current $I_0$ and initial capacitor voltage $V_0$.

**Step 1 — Apply KVL around the loop:**

$$
v_L(t) + v_R(t) + v_C(t) = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Express each term in terms of current $i(t)$:**

- Inductor voltage: $v_L = L \dfrac{di}{dt}$
- Resistor voltage: $v_R = R \, i$
- Capacitor voltage: $v_C = \dfrac{1}{C} \int i \, dt$

$$
L \frac{di}{dt} + R \, i + \frac{1}{C} \int i \, dt = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate once with respect to $t$ to eliminate the integral:**

$$
L \frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + R \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{1}{C} i = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Divide through by $L$ to obtain standard form:**

$$
\frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + \frac{R}{L} \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{1}{LC} i = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the canonical second-order form** $\dfrac{d^2x}{dt^2} + 2\alpha \dfrac{dx}{dt} + \omega_0^2 x = 0$:

$$
2\alpha = \frac{R}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \frac{R}{2L}
$$

$$
\omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{LC} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}
$$

**Step 6 — Characteristic equation** (substitute $s = d/dt$):

$$
s^2 + \frac{R}{L} s + \frac{1}{LC} = 0
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for roots using the quadratic formula:**

$$
s = \frac{-\frac{R}{L} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{L}\right)^2 - \frac{4}{LC}}}{2}
$$

$$
s = -\frac{R}{2L} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{R}{2L}\right)^2 - \frac{1}{LC}}
$$

$$
s = -\alpha \pm \sqrt{\alpha^2 - \omega_0^2}
$$

This is the **general closed-form expression** for the natural frequencies of any series RLC circuit. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Damping Ratio from Component Values (Series RLC)

Starting from the definitions:

$$
\zeta = \frac{\alpha}{\omega_0}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute $\alpha = R/(2L)$ and $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$:**

$$
\zeta = \frac{R / (2L)}{1 / \sqrt{LC}}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the complex fraction:**

$$
\zeta = \frac{R}{2L} \cdot \sqrt{LC}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine under one square root:**

$$
\zeta = \frac{R}{2L} \cdot \sqrt{L} \cdot \sqrt{C}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify $L$ in the denominator:**

$$
\zeta = \frac{R}{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{LC}}{L} = \frac{R}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{LC}{L^2}}
$$

$$
\zeta = \frac{R}{2} \sqrt{\frac{C}{L}}
$$

**Final result (Series):**

$$
\boxed{\zeta = \frac{R}{2} \sqrt{\frac{C}{L}}}
$$

By duality (swapping roles of V↔I, R↔1/R, L↔C), for parallel RLC:

$$
\boxed{\zeta = \frac{1}{2R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Damped Natural Frequency $\omega_d$

For the underdamped case, $\alpha < \omega_0$, so the discriminant is negative. Define:

$$
\omega_d^2 = \omega_0^2 - \alpha^2
$$

**Step 1 — Take the square root:**

$$
\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of $\zeta$ using $\alpha = \zeta\omega_0$:**

$$
\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \zeta^2 \omega_0^2} = \omega_0 \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Period of damped oscillation:**

$$
T_d = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_d} = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0 \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that the *period* of oscillation depends on damping — a more damped circuit oscillates *slower* (longer period) but with smaller amplitude per cycle. This is a classic viva question.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Quality Factor $Q$ in Series RLC

**Starting definition:** $Q$ is the ratio of reactive power to resistive power at resonance, equivalently the ratio of stored energy to energy dissipated per radian.

$$
Q = \frac{\omega_0 L}{R}
$$

**Step 1 — Use $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$:**

$$
Q = \frac{L}{R\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L^2}{LC}} = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}
$$

**Step 2 — Connect to damping ratio.** Starting from $\zeta = \dfrac{R}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{C}{L}}$:

$$
\frac{1}{\zeta} = \frac{2}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{1}{2\zeta}
$$

This is the universal relationship. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem:** A series RLC circuit has $R = 10\,\Omega$, $L = 0.1\,\text{H}$, $C = 100\,\mu\text{F}$. Determine:
(a) The damping coefficient $\alpha$, natural frequency $\omega_0$, and damping ratio $\zeta$.
(b) The nature of the damping and the roots.
(c) The damped frequency $\omega_d$ and quality factor $Q$.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Damping coefficient:

$$
\alpha = \frac{R}{2L} = \frac{10}{2 \times 0.1} = 50 \text{ Np/s}
$$

Natural frequency:

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.1 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10^{-5}}} = \frac{1}{3.162 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 316.23 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Damping ratio:

$$
\zeta = \frac{\alpha}{\omega_0} = \frac{50}{316.23} \approx 0.158
$$

**(b)** Since $\zeta = 0.158 < 1$, the circuit is **underdamped**.

Roots:

$$
s_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm j\sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2} = -50 \pm j\sqrt{(316.23)^2 - (50)^2}
$$

$$
s_{1,2} = -50 \pm j\sqrt{100000 - 2500} = -50 \pm j312.25 \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

**(c)** Damped frequency:

$$
\omega_d = \omega_0 \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = 316.23 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.025} = 316.23 \times 0.987 \approx 312.25 \text{ rad/s}
$$

Quality factor:

$$
Q = \frac{1}{2\zeta} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.158} \approx 3.16
$$

**Verification:** $Q = \omega_0 L / R = 316.23 \times 0.1 / 10 = 3.16$ ✓

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Identifying Damping Type

Given any R, L, C values, use this flow:

| Compute | Compare | Damping Type | Root Form | Response Shape |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\alpha = R/(2L)$ or $1/(2RC)$ | $\alpha > \omega_0$ | Overdamped | Two real, distinct, negative | $A_1 e^{s_1 t} + A_2 e^{s_2 t}$ |
| | $\alpha = \omega_0$ | Critically damped | Two real, equal, negative | $(A_1 + A_2 t)e^{-\alpha t}$ |
| | $\alpha < \omega_0$ | Underdamped | Complex conjugates | $e^{-\alpha t}(A_1\cos\omega_d t + A_2\sin\omega_d t)$ |
| | $\alpha = 0$ | Undamped | Purely imaginary $\pm j\omega_0$ | $A_1\cos\omega_0 t + A_2\sin\omega_0 t$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick test shortcut:** Compute $\zeta$ first. If $\zeta > 1$: overdamped; $\zeta = 1$: critical; $0 < \zeta < 1$: underdamped; $\zeta = 0$: undamped. This is faster than computing $\alpha$ and $\omega_0$ separately.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Source-Free Series RLC Circuit

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Resistor R] ---|series| nodeB[Inductor L]
    nodeB ---|series| nodeC[Capacitor C]
    nodeC ---|closes loop| nodeA
    nodeD[Initial Inductor Current I0] -.-> nodeB
    nodeE[Initial Capacitor Voltage V0] -.-> nodeC
    nodeF[KVL Equation] -->|derives| nodeG[Characteristic Equation s squared + 2 alpha s + omega 0 squared = 0]
```

## 4.2 Source-Free Parallel RLC Circuit

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeP[Top Node] --- nodeR[Resistor R]
    nodeP --- nodeL[Inductor L]
    nodeP --- nodeC[Capacitor C]
    nodeR --- nodeN[Bottom Node]
    nodeL --- nodeN
    nodeC --- nodeN
    nodeI[Initial Inductor Current I0] -.-> nodeL
    nodeV[Initial Capacitor Voltage V0] -.-> nodeC
    nodeK[KCL Equation] -->|derives| nodeCH[Characteristic Equation s squared + s by RC + 1 by LC = 0]
```

## 4.3 Damping Classification Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart[Given R L C values] --> nodeCompute[Compute alpha and omega 0]
    nodeCompute --> nodeCompare{Compare alpha with omega 0}
    nodeCompare -->|alpha greater than omega 0| nodeOD[Overdamped - Two real distinct roots]
    nodeCompare -->|alpha equal to omega 0| nodeCD[Critically Damped - Repeated real root]
    nodeCompare -->|alpha less than omega 0| nodeUD[Underdamped - Complex conjugate roots]
    nodeCompare -->|alpha equals zero| nodeUND[Undamped - Pure imaginary roots]
    nodeOD --> nodeResp1[Non oscillatory slow decay]
    nodeCD --> nodeResp2[Fastest non oscillatory decay]
    nodeUD --> nodeResp3[Decaying sinusoidal oscillation]
    nodeUND --> nodeResp4[Sustained pure oscillation]
```

## 4.4 Energy Flow Block Diagram in Damped RLC

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeInductor[Inductor L stores magnetic energy] -->|transfers| nodeCapacitor[Capacitor C stores electric energy]
    nodeCapacitor -->|transfers back| nodeInductor
    nodeInductor -->|dissipates| nodeResistor[Resistor R converts to heat]
    nodeCapacitor -->|dissipates| nodeResistor
    nodeResistor --> nodeOut[Energy leaves circuit as heat]
```

## 4.5 Functional Architecture — Damping Ratio Design

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Design Requirement: Step Response Shape] --> nodeB{Choose Damping Ratio}
    nodeB -->|zeta equals 1| nodeC[Critically Damped: Fastest Settling No Overshoot]
    nodeB -->|zeta between 0.7 and 1| nodeD[Underdamped Optimal: Quick Response Small Overshoot]
    nodeB -->|zeta less than 0.7| nodeE[Heavily Underdamped: Oscillatory Large Overshoot]
    nodeB -->|zeta greater than 1| nodeF[Overdamped: Slow No Oscillation]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Application: Galvanometers Door Closers]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Application: Control Systems Filters]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Application: Oscillators Tuned Circuits]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Application: Slow Thermal Systems]
```

## 4.6 Response Waveform Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Four Damping Cases] --> nodeB[Overdamped Response]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Critically Damped Response]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Underdamped Response]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Undamped Response]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Curve: rises slowly to steady state without overshoot - longest settle time]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Curve: reaches steady state fastest with zero overshoot]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Curve: oscillates with decaying amplitude around steady state]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Curve: pure sinusoid constant amplitude forever]
```
