## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define the *time constant* of a first-order R-L circuit. A series R-L circuit has $R = 5\ \Omega$ and $L = 20\ \text{mH}$. Compute the time constant and the time required for the transient to decay to **0.1%** of its initial value.

**Model Answer:**

The **time constant** $\tau$ of a first-order R-L circuit is the time required for the transient response to decay to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its initial value. It is defined as:

$$\tau = \frac{L}{R} \text{ seconds}$$

For $R = 5\ \Omega$ and $L = 20\ \text{mH} = 0.02\ \text{H}$:

$$\tau = \frac{0.02}{5} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4 \text{ ms}$$

For decay to 0.1%: $e^{-t/\tau} = 0.001 \Rightarrow t = \tau \ln(1000) = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 6.9078 \approx 27.6\ \text{ms}$. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** Write the steady-state expression for the current in a series R-C circuit driven by $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ and explain why the current leads the voltage.

**Model Answer:**

The steady-state current in a series R-C circuit is:

$$i_{ss}(t) = \frac{V_m}{\vert Z \vert}\sin(\omega t + \phi), \quad \text{where} \quad \vert Z \vert = \sqrt{R^2 + \frac{1}{(\omega C)^2}}, \quad \phi = \arctan\!\left(\frac{1}{\omega R C}\right)$$

The current **leads** the voltage because the capacitor stores energy in its electric field. During the first quarter-cycle, charge accumulates on the capacitor; this accumulated charge *anticipates* the voltage, causing the current to peak before the voltage does. In phasor terms, the capacitor's impedance $-j/(\omega C)$ contributes a negative imaginary component, rotating the current phasor counter-clockwise relative to the voltage phasor. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 3, Past Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the aid of the Laplace transform, derive the complete current response $i(t)$ of a series R-L circuit energized by a sinusoidal voltage source $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ under zero initial conditions. Clearly identify the transient and steady-state components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A series R-L circuit has $R = 6\ \Omega$, $L = 8\ \text{mH}$, and is connected to $v(t) = 50 \sin(500t)$ V at $t = 0$ with zero initial current. Compute (i) the time constant, (ii) the impedance magnitude and phase angle, and (iii) the complete expression for $i(t)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation** (7 marks):

- **Step 1 — KVL equation:** $L\dfrac{di}{dt} + R i = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ ... (1 mark)
- **Step 2 — Laplace with $i(0) = 0$:** $I(s)(sL + R) = \dfrac{V_m \omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ ... (1 mark)
- **Step 3 — Solve for $I(s)$:** $I(s) = \dfrac{V_m \omega}{L(s + R/L)(s^2 + \omega^2)}$ ... (1 mark)
- **Step 4 — PFE with** $A = \dfrac{V_m \sin\phi}{\vert Z \vert}$, $B = -A$, $C = \dfrac{V_m \cos\phi}{\vert Z \vert}$ ... (2 marks)
- **Step 5 — Inverse Laplace and identify** $i_{tr}$ and $i_{ss}$ ... (1 mark)
- **Step 6 — Final answer** with $\phi = \arctan(\omega L / R)$ ... (1 mark)

Final result:

$$i(t) = \frac{V_m \sin\phi}{\vert Z \vert} e^{-Rt/L} + \frac{V_m}{\vert Z \vert} \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

**(b) Numerical solution** (7 marks):

- $\omega = 500\ \text{rad/s}$, $L = 8 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{H}$, $R = 6\ \Omega$
- $\tau = L/R = 8 \times 10^{-3}/6 = 1.333\ \text{ms}$ ... (1 mark)
- $\omega L = 500 \times 8 \times 10^{-3} = 4\ \Omega$, so $\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{6^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.211\ \Omega$ ... (2 marks)
- $\phi = \arctan(4/6) = 33.69° \approx 0.588\ \text{rad}$ ... (1 mark)
- $\sin\phi = 4/\sqrt{52} = 0.5547$, $V_m/\vert Z \vert = 50/\sqrt{52} \approx 6.934\ \text{A}$ ... (1 mark)

$$i(t) = (6.934)(0.5547) e^{-750t} + 6.934 \sin(500t - 0.588)$$

$$\boxed{i(t) = 3.846\, e^{-750t} + 6.934 \sin(500t - 0.588) \text{ A}}$$

**Verification:** $i(0) = 3.846 + 6.934\sin(-0.588) = 3.846 - 3.846 = 0$ ✓ ... (1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Using the Laplace transform method, derive the complete voltage response $v(t)$ across a parallel R-C circuit excited by a sinusoidal current source $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ with zero initial capacitor voltage. Clearly state the time constant and the phase relationship. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parallel R-C circuit has $R = 20\ \Omega$ and $C = 50\ \mu F$, and is fed by a current source $i(t) = 4 \sin(2000t)$ A. Find: (i) the admittance magnitude and phase angle, (ii) the time constant, and (iii) the complete expression for $v(t)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Derivation** (7 marks):

- **Step 1 — KCL:** $C\dfrac{dv}{dt} + \dfrac{v}{R} = I_m \sin(\omega t)$ ... (1 mark)
- **Step 2 — Laplace with $v(0) = 0$:** $V(s)\left(sC + \dfrac{1}{R}\right) = \dfrac{I_m \omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ ... (1 mark)
- **Step 3 — Solve:** $V(s) = \dfrac{I_m \omega R}{(s^2 + \omega^2)(sRC + 1)} = \dfrac{I_m \omega}{C(s + 1/RC)(s^2 + \omega^2)}$ ... (1 mark)
- **Step 4 — PFE and inverse transform** yielding: ... (2 marks)

$$v(t) = -\frac{I_m \sin\phi}{\vert Y \vert} e^{-t/RC} + \frac{I_m}{\vert Y \vert}\sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

- **Step 5 — Identify $\tau = RC$, $\phi = \arctan(\omega R C)$, voltage lags current (capacitive loading)** ... (1 mark)
- **Step 6 — Physical interpretation** (current leads voltage because of capacitor's energy storage) ... (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical solution** (7 marks):

- $\omega = 2000\ \text{rad/s}$, $R = 20\ \Omega$, $C = 50 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{F}$
- $\omega C = 2000 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 0.1\ \text{S}$, $1/R = 0.05\ \text{S}$
- $\vert Y \vert = \sqrt{0.05^2 + 0.1^2} = \sqrt{0.0125} \approx 0.1118\ \text{S}$ ... (2 marks)
- $\phi = \arctan(0.1/0.05) = \arctan(2) \approx 63.43° \approx 1.107\ \text{rad}$ ... (1 mark)
- $\tau = RC = 20 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 1\ \text{ms}$ ... (1 mark)
- $I_m/\vert Y \vert = 4/0.1118 \approx 35.78\ \text{V}$, $\sin\phi = 0.8944$

$$v(t) = -(35.78)(0.8944) e^{-1000t} + 35.78 \sin(2000t + 1.107)$$

$$\boxed{v(t) = -32.0\, e^{-1000t} + 35.78 \sin(2000t + 1.107) \text{ V}}$$

**Verification:** $v(0) = -32.0 + 35.78 \sin(1.107) = -32.0 + 32.0 = 0$ ✓ ... (1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in PFE coefficient** — Students often lose 2 marks by mixing up $+B$ and $-B$ in the expansion. Always re-derive by setting $s = -\text{real pole}$ to extract $A$ independently as a sanity check.
> 2. **Missing the $\omega$ factor in the inverse Laplace of $\dfrac{1}{s^2+\omega^2}$** — it is $\dfrac{1}{\omega}\sin(\omega t)$, *not* $\sin(\omega t)$. This single slip costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to verify $i(0) = 0$** — always substitute $t=0$ in the final answer to confirm the initial condition is satisfied. Examiners award partial credit for this verification.
> 4. **Confusing series/parallel phase angles** — In series R-L, $\phi = \arctan(\omega L/R)$; in parallel R-L, $\phi = \arctan(R/(\omega L))$ — they are **complementary** to each other. The same is true for R-C.
> 5. **Mixing time constants** — Series R-L and parallel R-L both have $\tau = L/R$, but the impedance angle differs; same for R-C with $\tau = RC$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Pipeline:** Time-domain equation → Laplace transform (with $i(0)=0$, $v_C(0)=0$) → algebraic $I(s)$ or $V(s)$ → **PFE** → inverse transform → $i(t)$ or $v(t)$.
- **Universal Result Form:** $x(t) = (\text{transient amplitude}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau} + (\text{steady-state amplitude}) \cdot \sin(\omega t \pm \phi)$.
- **Time Constants:** $\tau_{RL} = L/R$ (both series and parallel); $\tau_{RC} = RC$ (both series and parallel).
- **Impedance vs. Admittance:** Use $\vert Z \vert$ for series circuits and $\vert Y \vert = 1/\vert Z \vert$ for parallel circuits. Both have the same numerical structure, just inverted.
- **Phase Angle Convention:** Series R-L → $\phi = \arctan(\omega L / R)$, current *lags*; Series R-C → $\phi = \arctan(1/(\omega R C))$, current *leads*; Parallel R-L → $\phi = \arctan(R/(\omega L))$; Parallel R-C → $\phi = \arctan(\omega R C)$.
- **Initial Conditions:** Zero ICs guarantee $i(0^+) = 0$ in inductors and $v_C(0^+) = 0$ in capacitors; the transient amplitude is *exactly* the value needed to cancel the steady-state at $t=0$.
- **Steady-State** dominates after $t \geq 5\tau$ (since $e^{-5} \approx 0.0067 < 1\%$).
- **Pole Locations:** Simple real pole at $s = -1/\tau$ (transient), imaginary conjugate pair at $s = \pm j\omega$ (steady-state).
- **KTU 2024 NEP emphasis:** The CO mapping typically targets *Apply* and *Analyze* Bloom's levels — be prepared to derive the result and apply it to a numerical problem in the same question.
- **Common board trap:** When asked for "transient response," do **not** include the steady-state term. When asked for "complete response" or "total current," include both.
- **Dimensional check:** Every term in the final answer must have the same units (V or A). Use this as a quick verification before submitting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solution Using Laplace Transforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET302 - CIRCUITS & NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Solution Using Laplace Transforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Laplace Transform Method in Network Analysis** is a systematic technique that converts time-domain integro-differential equations governing a linear time-invariant (LTI) circuit into algebraic equations in the complex frequency domain (the **s-domain**, where $s = \sigma + j\omega$). The transformed circuit is solved using algebraic methods, and the resulting $s$-domain response is converted back to the time domain via the **Inverse Laplace Transform**, thereby obtaining the complete response (transient + steady-state) of the network in a single unified framework.

Mathematically, the **Unilateral (One-Sided) Laplace Transform** of a time-domain function $f(t)$ is defined as:

$$F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$

where:
- $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is the **complex frequency** with units of **rad/s** (or Np/s for the real part).
- The lower limit $0^-$ explicitly captures the effect of **initial conditions** at the instant just before $t = 0$, a critical feature for transient analysis.
- $F(s)$ exists (converges) only when $\text{Re}(s) > \sigma_c$, where $\sigma_c$ is the **abscissa of convergence**.

The **Inverse Laplace Transform** recovers the time-domain signal:

$$f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s)\} = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \int_{\sigma - j\infty}^{\sigma + j\infty} F(s)\, e^{st}\, ds, \quad t \geq 0$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Language Translator"**
> Imagine you are reading a complex legal contract written in Latin (the time-domain differential equation). The Laplace Transform acts like a **universal translator** that instantly converts the entire document into plain English (the $s$-domain algebraic equation) — much easier to read, analyze, and solve. Once you have the answer in English, another translator (Inverse Laplace) brings it back to the original language for practical use. The remarkable benefit is that *all* the difficult calculus operations (differentiation and integration) reduce to simple multiplication and division by $s$, and critically, the **initial conditions are automatically baked into the translated document** — so you do not need to separately find the arbitrary constants as in the classical method.

In circuit terms:
- **Resistors** stay as $R$ (no change).
- **Inductors** become impedances $sL$ with a *series voltage source* $L\,i(0^-)$ (capturing stored magnetic energy).
- **Capacitors** become impedances $1/(sC)$ with a *parallel voltage source* $v(0^-)/s$ (capturing stored electric energy).

After the network is drawn in the $s$-domain, you can apply **Kirchhoff's Laws (KVL/KCL)**, **Mesh Analysis**, **Nodal Analysis**, **Superposition**, **Thevenin's Theorem**, or **Norton's Theorem** using ordinary algebra — exactly as you would for a resistive network.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants and Conventions Used Throughout KTU Examinations:**
> - Complex frequency: $s = \sigma + j\omega$, with **$\sigma$ in Np/s** and **$\omega$ in rad/s**.
> - The unit of $F(s)$ for a voltage transform is **V·s** (or V/Hz equivalent), and for a current transform is **A·s**.
> - The **natural response** of a circuit decays with time constants $\tau_i = -1/\text{Re}(p_i)$ where $p_i$ are the poles of the $s$-domain network function.
> - **Zero-state response** assumes all initial conditions are zero; **Zero-input response** assumes all inputs are zero.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pillars of the Laplace Method

The complete solution procedure follows a strict five-stage pipeline. Each stage has a specific mathematical and physical purpose:

### Stage 1 — Transform the Excitation
Replace every time-domain source $v(t)$ or $i(t)$ with its Laplace equivalent $V(s)$ or $I(s)$. Use standard transform pairs (see table below).

### Stage 2 — Transform the Passive Elements
Apply the **s-domain element models** that include initial-condition generators:

- **Resistor:** $V_R(s) = R \cdot I_R(s)$
- **Inductor:** $V_L(s) = sL \cdot I_L(s) - L \cdot i_L(0^-)$
- **Capacitor:** $V_C(s) = \dfrac{1}{sC} I_C(s) + \dfrac{v_C(0^-)}{s}$

These three element relations are the **foundation of s-domain circuit analysis** and must be memorized.

### Stage 3 — Draw the s-Domain Equivalent Circuit
Replace every circuit element with its s-domain model. The network now consists of:
- **Impedances** $Z(s)$ in series with voltage sources (for inductors), or
- **Admittances** $Y(s)$ in parallel with current sources (for capacitors).

Series and parallel combinations obey the same algebraic rules as resistors.

### Stage 4 — Solve the Algebraic Network
Apply any standard network theorem (KVL, KCL, mesh, nodal, Thevenin, Norton, Superposition) to solve for the desired $s$-domain response $Y(s)$.

### Stage 5 — Inverse Transform
Decompose $Y(s)$ using **Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE)** into a sum of recognizable terms, then apply the inverse transform table to obtain $y(t)$ for $t \geq 0$.

## 2.2 Why the Laplace Method Works (The Core Insight)

> [!NOTE]
> The Laplace operator $\mathcal{L}\{\cdot\}$ is **linear** and converts the time-differentiation operator $\dfrac{d}{dt}$ into the algebraic multiplication by $s$ (with a correction term for initial conditions). Therefore, the linear integro-differential circuit equation in $t$ collapses to a linear algebraic equation in $s$. This is the **fundamental reason** why the Laplace method is preferred for transient analysis of higher-order networks (second order, third order, and beyond), where the classical method becomes increasingly cumbersome due to the need to evaluate arbitrary constants from initial conditions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table thoroughly — these are the formulas the KTU examiner expects you to write without hesitation.**

| # | Quantity / Operation | Time Domain | s-Domain (Laplace) | Units / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Definition of $F(s)$ | $f(t)$ | $F(s) = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f(t)e^{-st}dt$ | $s$ in rad/s |
| 2 | Linearity | $a f_1(t) + b f_2(t)$ | $a F_1(s) + b F_2(s)$ | Superposition holds |
| 3 | First Derivative | $\dfrac{df(t)}{dt}$ | $s F(s) - f(0^-)$ | Captures $f(0^-)$ |
| 4 | Second Derivative | $\dfrac{d^2f(t)}{dt^2}$ | $s^2 F(s) - s f(0^-) - f'(0^-)$ | Useful for 2nd order |
| 5 | nth Derivative | $\dfrac{d^n f(t)}{dt^n}$ | $s^n F(s) - \sum_{k=1}^{n} s^{n-k} f^{(k-1)}(0^-)$ | Generalized form |
| 6 | Integration | $\displaystyle\int_{0^-}^{t} f(\tau)d\tau$ | $\dfrac{F(s)}{s}$ | Initial integral zero |
| 7 | Time Shift (Delay) | $f(t-a)u(t-a)$ | $e^{-as} F(s)$ | $a > 0$ |
| 8 | s-Shift (Frequency Shift) | $e^{-at} f(t)$ | $F(s+a)$ | Modulates decay |
| 9 | Multiplication by $t$ | $t f(t)$ | $-\dfrac{dF(s)}{ds}$ | Frequency differentiation |
| 10 | Division by $t$ | $\dfrac{f(t)}{t}$ | $\displaystyle\int_{s}^{\infty} F(\sigma)d\sigma$ | Requires limit check |
| 11 | Initial Value Theorem | $\displaystyle\lim_{t \to 0^+} f(t)$ | $\displaystyle\lim_{s \to \infty} s F(s)$ | Valid if limit exists |
| 12 | Final Value Theorem | $\displaystyle\lim_{t \to \infty} f(t)$ | $\displaystyle\lim_{s \to 0} s F(s)$ | Poles of $sF(s)$ in LHP only |
| 13 | Unit Step | $u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s}$ | Energy in step |
| 14 | Unit Impulse | $\delta(t)$ | $1$ | Pure input |
| 15 | Exponential Decay | $e^{-at}u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s+a}$ | First-order pole |
| 16 | Ramp | $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s^2}$ | Pole at origin (double) |
| 17 | Sine | $\sin(\omega t) u(t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega}{s^2+\omega^2}$ | Purely imaginary poles |
| 18 | Cosine | $\cos(\omega t) u(t)$ | $\dfrac{s}{s^2+\omega^2}$ | Purely imaginary poles |
| 19 | Damped Sine | $e^{-at}\sin(\omega t)u(t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega}{(s+a)^2+\omega^2}$ | Complex conjugate poles |
| 20 | Damped Cosine | $e^{-at}\cos(\omega t)u(t)$ | $\dfrac{s+a}{(s+a)^2+\omega^2}$ | Complex conjugate poles |
| 21 | Resistor Impedance | $v_R(t) = R i_R(t)$ | $V_R(s) = R \cdot I_R(s)$ | $R$ in $\Omega$ |
| 22 | Inductor Impedance | $v_L(t) = L\dfrac{di_L}{dt}$ | $V_L(s) = sL \cdot I_L(s) - L i_L(0^-)$ | $sL$ in $\Omega$ |
| 23 | Capacitor Impedance | $i_C(t) = C\dfrac{dv_C}{dt}$ | $V_C(s) = \dfrac{1}{sC} I_C(s) + \dfrac{v_C(0^-)}{s}$ | $1/(sC)$ in $\Omega$ |
| 24 | General Impedance | $Z(t)$ in time | $Z(s) = \dfrac{V(s)}{I(s)}$ | Frequency-domain Ohm's Law |
| 25 | Transfer Function | $h(t)$ impulse response | $H(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{X(s)}$ | Zero initial conditions |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The Laplace transform method is the **backbone of modern control systems, signal processing, and analog filter design**. Every transfer function of a linear system — be it a cruise controller in a car, a phase-locked loop in a smartphone, a PID compensator in a chemical plant, or an active filter in audio equipment — is fundamentally an $s$-domain rational function. Therefore, the ability to set up and solve network equations in the $s$-domain, as taught in this module, is the gateway skill to **Control Theory (PCECT303)**, **Signals & Systems (PCECT304)**, and **Linear Integrated Circuits** that you will encounter in higher semesters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the s-Domain Element Models

### 3.1.1 Resistor

By Ohm's Law: $v_R(t) = R \cdot i_R(t)$.

Taking the unilateral Laplace transform of both sides:

$$\mathcal{L}\{v_R(t)\} = \mathcal{L}\{R \cdot i_R(t)\} \implies V_R(s) = R \cdot I_R(s)$$

The resistance $R$ is **independent of frequency**, so it remains unchanged in the $s$-domain.

### 3.1.2 Inductor

By the voltage-current relation: $v_L(t) = L \dfrac{di_L(t)}{dt}$.

Apply the differentiation property of the Laplace transform:

$$V_L(s) = \mathcal{L}\left\{L \dfrac{di_L}{dt}\right\} = L \cdot \left[s I_L(s) - i_L(0^-)\right]$$

Rearranging the terms:

$$V_L(s) = sL \cdot I_L(s) - L \cdot i_L(0^-)$$

**Physical interpretation:**
- $sL$ is the **frequency-dependent impedance** of the inductor, often called **complex impedance** or **operational impedance**.
- $L \cdot i_L(0^-)$ is a **series voltage source** whose polarity must be such that it represents the voltage across the inductor due to its initial current. The positive terminal is at the **top** of the inductor symbol if $i_L(0^-)$ was defined flowing **downward** through it (and vice versa, per passive sign convention).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget to include the initial-condition source for inductors when the circuit has stored magnetic energy. Always check whether the problem statement says "initially at rest" (then $i_L(0^-) = 0$) or specifies a numerical value.

### 3.1.3 Capacitor

By the current-voltage relation: $i_C(t) = C \dfrac{dv_C(t)}{dt}$.

Apply the integration property (divide by $s$):

$$V_C(s) = \dfrac{1}{sC} I_C(s) + \dfrac{v_C(0^-)}{s}$$

**Physical interpretation:**
- $1/(sC)$ is the complex impedance of the capacitor.
- $v_C(0^-)/s$ is a **series voltage source** in series with the capacitor's $s$-domain impedance, representing the initial voltage stored in the capacitor at $t = 0^-$.

An equivalent model uses a **parallel current source** of value $C v_C(0^-)$ in parallel with the impedance $1/(sC)$ — both models are mathematically equivalent and are used depending on the analysis method (mesh vs nodal).

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: RL Circuit with DC Step Input

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** In the series $RL$ circuit shown, the switch is closed at $t = 0$, connecting a DC voltage $V_s$ across an inductor with initial current $i_L(0^-) = I_0$ in series with a resistor $R$. Derive $i_L(t)$ for $t \geq 0$ using the Laplace transform method.

**Step 1 — Identify circuit elements and initial conditions.**

- Voltage source: $v_s(t) = V_s \cdot u(t) \implies V_s(s) = \dfrac{V_s}{s}$
- Resistor $R$ in series with inductor $L$ with $i_L(0^-) = I_0$.

**Step 2 — Draw the s-domain circuit.**

The transformed circuit has $V_s/s$ in series with $R$ and the inductor model $sL$ in series with a voltage source $L I_0$ (polarity opposing the source if $I_0$ flows in the same direction as the applied current).

**Step 3 — Apply KVL around the loop in the s-domain.**

$$V_s(s) = R I_L(s) + sL I_L(s) - L I_0$$

Wait — we must carefully track the sign of the initial-condition source. By passive sign convention, if the actual initial current $I_0$ flows through the inductor in the same direction as the defined mesh current $I_L(s)$, then the source $L I_0$ has its **positive terminal at the entry point** of the inductor in the mesh, and thus appears as a **rise** in the KVL equation (i.e., subtracted on the right side). The correct KVL becomes:

$$V_s(s) = R I_L(s) + sL I_L(s) - L I_0$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $I_L(s)$.**

Rearrange to isolate $I_L(s)$:

$$R I_L(s) + sL I_L(s) = \dfrac{V_s}{s} + L I_0$$

$$I_L(s) \cdot (R + sL) = \dfrac{V_s}{s} + L I_0$$

$$I_L(s) = \dfrac{V_s / s + L I_0}{R + sL}$$

To combine into a single fraction, multiply the numerator terms by appropriate denominators:

$$I_L(s) = \dfrac{V_s + sL I_0}{s(R + sL)}$$

**Step 5 — Partial Fraction Expansion.**

Factor and rewrite as:

$$I_L(s) = \dfrac{V_s + sL I_0}{s \cdot L \cdot (s + R/L)}$$

Split into partial fractions of the form:

$$I_L(s) = \dfrac{A}{s} + \dfrac{B}{s + R/L}$$

where $A$ and $B$ are the residues at the poles $s = 0$ and $s = -R/L$ respectively.

**Residue at $s = 0$:**

$$A = \left. s \cdot I_L(s) \right|_{s=0} = \left. \dfrac{V_s + sL I_0}{L(s + R/L)} \right|_{s=0} = \dfrac{V_s}{L \cdot (R/L)} = \dfrac{V_s}{R}$$

**Residue at $s = -R/L$:**

$$B = \left. (s + R/L) \cdot I_L(s) \right|_{s = -R/L} = \left. \dfrac{V_s + sL I_0}{sL} \right|_{s = -R/L}$$

Substituting $s = -R/L$:

$$B = \dfrac{V_s + (-R/L) L I_0}{(-R/L) L} = \dfrac{V_s - R I_0}{-R} = \dfrac{R I_0 - V_s}{R} = I_0 - \dfrac{V_s}{R}$$

**Step 6 — Final $s$-domain expression:**

$$I_L(s) = \dfrac{V_s/R}{s} + \dfrac{I_0 - V_s/R}{s + R/L}$$

**Step 7 — Inverse Laplace Transform using standard pairs:**

Applying the pair $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{1/(s+a)\} = e^{-at} u(t)$:

$$i_L(t) = \underbrace{\dfrac{V_s}{R}}_{\text{forced/ steady-state}} + \underbrace{\left(I_0 - \dfrac{V_s}{R}\right) e^{-(R/L)t}}_{\text{natural/transient}}, \quad t \geq 0$$

**Step 8 — Physical Verification.**

- At $t = 0^+$: $i_L(0^+) = V_s/R + (I_0 - V_s/R)(1) = I_0$. ✓ Inductor current cannot change instantaneously.
- At $t \to \infty$: $i_L(\infty) = V_s/R + 0 = V_s/R$. ✓ The inductor acts as a short circuit in DC steady state.
- The time constant is $\tau = L/R$. ✓

## 3.3 General Analytical Matrix — Common s-Domain Network Topologies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table summarizes the standard results for canonical first-order and second-order circuits, all derived from Laplace analysis. KTU questions frequently test these topologies with modified initial conditions or sources.**

| # | Topology | Excitation | Initial Condition | $s$-Domain Response | Time-Domain Response $y(t)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RC series, source $V_s/s$ step | $v_s = V_s u(t)$ | $v_C(0^-) = V_0$ | $V_C(s) = \dfrac{V_0 s + V_s/(RC)}{s(s + 1/RC)}$ | $V_s + (V_0 - V_s) e^{-t/RC}$ |
| 2 | RL series, source $V_s/s$ step | $v_s = V_s u(t)$ | $i_L(0^-) = I_0$ | $I_L(s) = \dfrac{I_0 s + V_s/L}{s(s + R/L)}$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{R} + \left(I_0 - \dfrac{V_s}{R}\right)e^{-(R/L)t}$ |
| 3 | RLC series, impulse input | $v_s = \delta(t)$ | All zero | $I(s) = \dfrac{1}{Ls^2 + Rs + 1/C}$ | Depends on damping $\zeta$ |
| 4 | RLC series, step input | $v_s = V_s u(t)$ | $i_L(0^-) = I_0$, $v_C(0^-) = V_0$ | $V_C(s) = \dfrac{s V_0 + s \cdot \dfrac{I_0}{C} + \dfrac{V_s}{LC}}{s(s^2 + (R/L)s + 1/(LC))}$ | Overdamped / Critically / Underdamped |
| 5 | Parallel RLC with current source | $i_s = I_s u(t)$ | $i_L(0^-) = I_0$, $v_C(0^-) = V_0$ | $V(s) = \dfrac{s V_0 + s^2 L I_0 + s L I_s/C}{s(s^2L C + sL/R + 1)}$ | Second-order response |

## 3.4 Worked Example: RLC Series Circuit with Initial Conditions

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU University Exam Pattern):** For the series RLC circuit with $R = 6\,\Omega$, $L = 1\,\text{H}$, $C = 1/5\,\text{F}$, an applied step voltage $v_s(t) = 10 u(t)$ V, and initial conditions $i_L(0^-) = 1\,\text{A}$, $v_C(0^-) = 2\,\text{V}$. Find the complete current $i(t)$ for $t \geq 0$ using the Laplace transform method.

**Step 1 — Write KVL in s-domain.**

For a series loop with the s-domain inductor model $sL \cdot I(s) - L I_0$ and the s-domain capacitor model $I(s)/(sC) + V_0/s$:

$$V_s(s) = R I(s) + sL I(s) - L I_0 + \dfrac{I(s)}{sC} + \dfrac{V_0}{s}$$

**Step 2 — Collect $I(s)$ terms.**

$$\dfrac{V_s}{s} + L I_0 - \dfrac{V_0}{s} = I(s) \left[R + sL + \dfrac{1}{sC}\right]$$

$$\dfrac{V_s + sL I_0 - V_0}{s} = I(s) \cdot \dfrac{s^2 L C + s R C + 1}{sC}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $I(s)$.**

$$I(s) = \dfrac{C(V_s - V_0) + s L C I_0}{s^2 L C + s R C + 1}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

With $R = 6$, $L = 1$, $C = 1/5$, $V_s = 10$, $I_0 = 1$, $V_0 = 2$:

- $LC = 1 \cdot 1/5 = 1/5$
- $RC = 6 \cdot 1/5 = 6/5$
- $C(V_s - V_0) = (1/5)(10 - 2) = 8/5$
- $s L C I_0 = s \cdot (1/5) \cdot 1 = s/5$

Therefore:

$$I(s) = \dfrac{8/5 + s/5}{s^2/5 + 6s/5 + 1} = \dfrac{s + 8}{s^2 + 6s + 5}$$

**Step 5 — Factor the denominator.**

$$s^2 + 6s + 5 = (s+1)(s+5)$$

So:

$$I(s) = \dfrac{s + 8}{(s+1)(s+5)}$$

**Step 6 — Partial Fraction Expansion.**

$$I(s) = \dfrac{A}{s+1} + \dfrac{B}{s+5}$$

Cover-up method for $A$:

$$A = \left. \dfrac{s+8}{s+5} \right|_{s=-1} = \dfrac{-1+8}{-1+5} = \dfrac{7}{4}$$

Cover-up method for $B$:

$$B = \left. \dfrac{s+8}{s+1} \right|_{s=-5} = \dfrac{-5+8}{-5+1} = \dfrac{3}{-4} = -\dfrac{3}{4}$$

**Step 7 — Inverse Laplace Transform.**

$$i(t) = \dfrac{7}{4} e^{-t} - \dfrac{3}{4} e^{-5t} \quad \text{A}, \quad t \geq 0$$

**Step 8 — Verification.**

- Initial value: $i(0^+) = 7/4 - 3/4 = 1$ A. ✓ Matches $i_L(0^-) = 1$ A (inductor continuity).
- Final value: $i(\infty) = 0$. ✓ DC steady state with capacitor blocking current.
- The two negative real poles confirm an **overdamped** response.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Time-Domain to s-Domain Element Transformation Map

The following block diagram summarizes the conversion of each circuit element from the time domain into its $s$-domain equivalent, with initial-condition generators explicitly shown.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["TIME DOMAIN ELEMENT"]:::startNode --> B{"Element Type?"}:::decision

    B -->|Resistor| C1["R in Ohms"]:::resistorNode
    C1 --> C2["s-Domain: V_R(s) = R I_R(s)\nNo initial condition source"]:::resultNode
    C2 --> C3["Series Impedance Z_R(s) = R"]:::impedanceNode

    B -->|Inductor| D1["L in Henrys\nwith i_L(0-) = I_0"]:::inductorNode
    D1 --> D2["s-Domain Model: \nImpedance sL in series\nwith voltage source L*I_0"]:::resultNode
    D2 --> D3["V_L(s) = sL I_L(s) - L I_0"]:::formulaNode

    B -->|Capacitor| E1["C in Farads\nwith v_C(0-) = V_0"]:::capacitorNode
    E1 --> E2["s-Domain Model:\nImpedance 1/(sC) in series\nwith voltage source V_0/s"]:::resultNode
    E2 --> E3["V_C(s) = I_C(s)/(sC) + V_0/s"]:::formulaNode

    C3 --> F["Draw Full s-Domain Circuit"]:::outputNode
    D3 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G["Apply KVL/KCL/Network Theorems\nSolve Algebraically for Y(s)"]:::outputNode
    G --> H["Inverse Laplace Transform\nto obtain y(t)"]:::outputNode

    classDef startNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decision fill:#facc15,stroke:#ca8a04,color:#1f2937,stroke-width:2px
    classDef resistorNode fill:#ef4444,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef inductorNode fill:#8b5cf6,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    classDef impedanceNode fill:#ec4899,stroke:#be185d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef outputNode fill:#374151,stroke:#1f2937,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define the Laplace transform of a time-domain function $f(t)$ and state the time-differentiation property with initial conditions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **unilateral Laplace transform** of $f(t)$ is defined as $F(s) = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f(t) e^{-st} dt$ where $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is the complex frequency. **(1 mark)**
>
> The **time-differentiation property** states that if $F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\}$, then: **(1 mark)**
>
> $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = s F(s) - f(0^-)$$
>
> where $f(0^-)$ is the value of $f(t)$ at $t = 0^-$ (just before $t=0$). For the second derivative:
>
> $$\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{d^2 f(t)}{dt^2}\right\} = s^2 F(s) - s f(0^-) - f'(0^-)$$ **(1 mark)**
>
> These properties allow the conversion of differential equations to algebraic equations while automatically including the effect of initial stored energy.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Write the s-domain voltage-current relations for an inductor and a capacitor, including initial conditions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an inductor of $L$ henries with initial current $i_L(0^-)$:
>
> $$V_L(s) = sL \cdot I_L(s) - L \cdot i_L(0^-)$$ **(1.5 marks)**
>
> This represents a series combination of an impedance $sL$ and a voltage source $L i_L(0^-)$ (polarity such that it represents the inductor's stored energy).
>
> For a capacitor of $C$ farads with initial voltage $v_C(0^-)$:
>
> $$V_C(s) = \dfrac{1}{sC} I_C(s) + \dfrac{v_C(0^-)}{s}$$ **(1.5 marks)**
>
> This represents a series combination of an impedance $1/(sC)$ and a voltage source $v_C(0^-)/s$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**In the network shown, an RL series circuit has $R = 4\,\Omega$, $L = 2\,\text{H}$, a DC step input $v_s(t) = 12\,u(t)$ V, and an initial inductor current $i_L(0^-) = 2\,\text{A}$.**

**(a)** Draw the s-domain equivalent circuit and write the loop equation in the s-domain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve for the loop current $I(s)$ using algebraic methods, perform partial fraction expansion, and find the time-domain current $i(t)$ for $t \geq 0$. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) Model Solution — s-Domain Circuit and Loop Equation

**Step 1:** Transform the source: $\mathcal{L}\{12 u(t)\} = \dfrac{12}{s}$ V. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** The s-domain inductor model: impedance $sL = 2s$ ohms in series with a voltage source $L I_0 = 2 \times 2 = 4$ V. The polarity of this source is such that it represents the inductor's attempt to maintain the initial current of 2 A in the original direction. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3:** The series $s$-domain circuit consists of the source $12/s$ in series with $R = 4\,\Omega$, impedance $2s$, and the source $4$ V (with appropriate polarity). **(2 marks)**

**Step 4:** Apply KVL around the loop (assuming all elements and current $I(s)$ are oriented in the same direction):

$$\dfrac{12}{s} = 4 I(s) + 2s \cdot I(s) - 4$$ **(2 marks)**

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Solving for $i(t)$

**Step 1:** Rearrange the KVL equation:

$$4 I(s) + 2s I(s) = \dfrac{12}{s} + 4$$ **(1 mark)**

$$I(s) \cdot (2s + 4) = \dfrac{12 + 4s}{s}$$ **(1 mark)**

$$I(s) = \dfrac{12 + 4s}{s \cdot 2(s + 2)} = \dfrac{2(3 + s)}{s(s+2)} = \dfrac{2(s+3)}{s(s+2)}$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Partial Fraction Expansion:

$$I(s) = \dfrac{A}{s} + \dfrac{B}{s+2}$$

Residue at $s = 0$: $A = \left. \dfrac{2(s+3)}{s+2} \right|_{s=0} = \dfrac{6}{2} = 3$ **(1 mark)**

Residue at $s = -2$: $B = \left. \dfrac{2(s+3)}{s} \right|_{s=-2} = \dfrac{2(1)}{-2} = -1$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Inverse Laplace Transform:

$$i(t) = 3 \cdot u(t) - e^{-2t} \cdot u(t) = 3 - e^{-2t} \text{ A}, \quad t \geq 0$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Verification:**
- At $t = 0^+$: $i(0^+) = 3 - 1 = 2$ A ✓ (matches initial inductor current).
- At $t \to \infty$: $i(\infty) = 3 - 0 = 3$ A = $V_s/R = 12/4$ ✓
- Time constant: $\tau = L/R = 2/4 = 0.5$ s ✓ (pole at $s = -2$).

**(Final 1 mark for proper verification and units.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**For the RLC series circuit with $R = 3\,\Omega$, $L = 1\,\text{H}$, $C = 1/2\,\text{F}$, an applied voltage $v_s(t) = 6 u(t)$ V, and initial conditions $i_L(0^-) = 0$ A, $v_C(0^-) = 1$ V:**

**(a)** Set up the s-domain circuit and derive the expression for the loop current $I(s)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Identify the poles of the response, perform partial fraction expansion, and find $i(t)$. State whether the response is overdamped, critically damped, or underdamped. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) Model Solution

**Step 1:** $V_s(s) = \dfrac{6}{s}$; $i_L(0^-) = 0 \implies L i_L(0^-) = 0$; $v_C(0^-) = 1 \implies v_C(0^-)/s = 1/s$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** s-domain impedances: $R = 3$, $sL = s$, $1/(sC) = 2/s$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** KVL around the loop:

$$\dfrac{6}{s} = 3 I(s) + s I(s) + \dfrac{2}{s} I(s) + \dfrac{1}{s}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 4:** Rearranging:

$$3 I(s) + s I(s) + \dfrac{2}{s} I(s) = \dfrac{6}{s} - \dfrac{1}{s} = \dfrac{5}{s}$$ **(1 mark)**

Multiplying both sides by $s$:

$$s \cdot 3 I(s) + s^2 I(s) + 2 I(s) = 5$$ **(1 mark)**

$$I(s) \left(s^2 + 3s + 2\right) = 5$$ **(0.5 marks)**

$$\boxed{I(s) = \dfrac{5}{s^2 + 3s + 2} = \dfrac{5}{(s+1)(s+2)}}$$ **(0.5 marks)**

#### Part (b) Model Solution

**Step 1:** Factor the denominator: $s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)$. **(1 mark)**

The poles are at $s = -1$ and $s = -2$, both real and negative. Since they are **real and distinct**, the response is **overdamped**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Partial Fraction Expansion:

$$I(s) = \dfrac{A}{s+1} + \dfrac{B}{s+2}$$

Residue at $s = -1$: $A = \left. \dfrac{5}{s+2} \right|_{s=-1} = \dfrac{5}{1} = 5$ **(1 mark)**

Residue at $s = -2$: $B = \left. \dfrac{5}{s+1} \right|_{s=-2} = \dfrac{5}{-1} = -5$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Inverse Laplace Transform:

$$i(t) = 5 e^{-t} - 5 e^{-2t} \text{ A}, \quad t \geq 0$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Verification and Damping Classification:**
- Initial value: $i(0^+) = 5 - 5 = 0$ A ✓ (matches $i_L(0^-) = 0$).
- Final value: $i(\infty) = 0$ ✓ (capacitor blocks DC).
- Discriminant of characteristic equation: $\Delta = 9 - 8 = 1 > 0$ → **Overdamped**, real distinct roots. ✓ **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Wrong sign on initial-condition source:** For an inductor carrying $i_L(0^-) = I_0$ in the assumed mesh current direction, the $s$-domain model must include the source $L I_0$ such that it *opposes* the applied source (a rise in the KVL sense). Drawing it the wrong way will flip the sign of the natural response and yield incorrect final values. **Examiner's deduction: up to 2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert sources to s-domain:** Students often leave the source as $V_s$ in the $s$-domain circuit instead of $V_s/s$ for a step input. This silently changes the entire problem. **Examiner's deduction: up to 3 marks.**
>
> 3. **Algebraic errors in partial fraction expansion:** A single sign error in residue calculation propagates to the final answer. Always verify by multiplying back: $A(s+2) + B(s+1) = 5$ should yield the numerator $5$. **Examiner's deduction: up to 2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Skipping the time-domain verification:** Even a one-line check of $i(0^+)$ and $i(\infty)$ is often rewarded with 1–2 marks. KTU examiners explicitly allocate marks for "verification of initial and final conditions" in step-response problems.
>
> 5. **Unit confusion:** The product $LC$ has units of $\text{second}^2$, $RC$ has units of seconds, and $L/R$ has units of seconds. A dimensional mismatch in your time-constant expression will be penalized.
>
> 6. **Misapplying Final Value Theorem:** Never use $\lim_{s \to 0} sF(s)$ unless you have verified that all poles of $sF(s)$ lie strictly in the left half-plane. For an oscillator, this gives a misleading result.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Solution Using Laplace Transforms**
>
> ☐ The **Laplace transform** converts linear ODEs to algebraic equations; it requires the system to be **linear and time-invariant (LTI)**.
>
> ☐ The integration is **unilateral**, from $0^-$ to $\infty$, ensuring initial conditions are automatically captured.
>
> ☐ The s-domain impedance of **R, L, C** are $R$, $sL$, $1/(sC)$ respectively — these are the cornerstones of s-domain circuit drawing.
>
> ☐ Initial conditions become **independent sources** in the s-domain: $L i_L(0^-)$ in series with inductor, and $v_C(0^-)/s$ in series with capacitor.
>
> ☐ Once the s-domain circuit is drawn, **every** DC-network analysis technique (KVL, KCL, mesh, nodal, Thevenin, Norton, Superposition, source transformation) applies directly.
>
> ☐ The **inverse Laplace transform** almost always requires **Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE)**. Master the cover-up method for distinct real poles; know how to handle repeated and complex-conjugate poles.
>
> ☐ **Initial Value Theorem (IVT):** $f(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s)$ — useful sanity check.
>
> ☐ **Final Value Theorem (FVT):** $f(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} sF(s)$ — only valid when poles of $sF(s)$ are in the open left half-plane.
>
> ☐ For a **step input** $V_s u(t)$, the transform is $V_s/s$. For an **impulse** $\delta(t)$, the transform is **1** (no $s$ in the denominator).
>
> ☐ Damped sinusoidal transforms (e.g., $e^{-at}\sin\omega t$) require recognizing the pattern $\dfrac{\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$ — this is essential for **underdamped RLC** responses.
>
> ☐ The **transfer function** $H(s) = Y(s)/X(s)$ characterizes a circuit completely (in zero-state). It is the Laplace transform of the impulse response $h(t)$.
>
> ☐ Poles of $H(s)$ in the **left half-plane** → stable system; on the **imaginary axis** → marginally stable (oscillator); in the **right half-plane** → unstable.
>
> ☐ In the KTU 14-mark question, the expected structure is: **s-domain circuit diagram (2 marks) + KVL/KCL equation (2 marks) + Algebraic solution for $Y(s)$ (3 marks) + Partial fraction expansion (3 marks) + Inverse transform (2 marks) + Verification (2 marks)**. Plan your answer accordingly.
>
> ☐ Always state the **time-domain function's domain of validity** explicitly as $t \geq 0$ or multiply by $u(t)$ to indicate the function is zero for $t < 0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Two Port Networks - Z
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEET302 - CIRCUITS & NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Two Port Networks - Z

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Two-Port Network?

A **Two-Port Network** is a black-box electrical network having **two pairs of accessible terminals** (ports). One pair serves as the **input port** (Port 1) and the other as the **output port** (Port 2). Each port has one terminal where current enters and another where it leaves.

For a two-port, four variables exist:
- $V_1, I_1$ at Port 1 (input)
- $V_2, I_2$ at Port 2 (output)

Two of these are independent (excitations) and the remaining two are dependent (responses). Choosing different pairs as independent gives different parameter sets: **Z, Y, h, g, ABCD, and Inverse-ABCD**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Convention:** Current $I_1$ flows **into** the +ve terminal of $V_1$ at Port 1, and $I_2$ flows **into** the +ve terminal of $V_2$ at Port 2. Both port currents are defined as entering the network. This is the standard IEEE/KTU convention.

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Z-Parameters

**Z-parameters** (also called **Open-Circuit Impedance Parameters**) express the port voltages $V_1$ and $V_2$ as linear functions of the port currents $I_1$ and $I_2$.

$$V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2$$

$$V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2$$

In matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = [Z] \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

The matrix $[Z]$ is called the **Z-matrix** or **Open-Circuit Impedance Matrix**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Black-Box with Knobs" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a sealed electronic "black-box" (like a transistor amplifier or a transmission line interface). You cannot see what is inside, but you can poke two wires in (Port 1) and two wires out (Port 2). By measuring how the voltages at each port respond to currents you push in, you create a "fingerprint" of the box. The Z-parameters are exactly this fingerprint — four numbers that uniquely describe how the box behaves electrically. The word "open-circuit" tells you *how* you measure them: you leave the opposite port **open** (no current flows out of it) and then simply apply Ohm's law to the port you're testing.

### 1.4 Physical Dimensions and Units

All four Z-parameters have the dimension of **impedance** and are measured in **Ohms (Ω)**.

- $Z_{11}$ — driving-point impedance at Port 1 (Ω)
- $Z_{12}$ — reverse transfer impedance (Ω)
- $Z_{21}$ — forward transfer impedance (Ω)
- $Z_{22}$ — driving-point impedance at Port 2 (Ω)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Symbol (Bold):** $\mathbf{[Z]}$ denotes the 2×2 impedance parameter matrix. Each element $\mathbf{Z_{ij}}$ is a complex number in the phasor domain, with real and imaginary parts representing resistance and reactance respectively: $Z_{ij} = R_{ij} + jX_{ij}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Determination of Each Z-Parameter

Each Z-parameter is obtained by setting **one port current to zero** (open-circuit) and exciting the other port. This is the direct consequence of the defining equations.

#### 2.1.1 Driving-Point Impedance at Port 1 ($Z_{11}$)

Set $I_2 = 0$ (Port 2 is open-circuited). Apply test current $I_1$ at Port 1 and measure $V_1$.

$$Z_{11} = \left. \frac{V_1}{I_1} \right|_{I_2 = 0}$$

This is the impedance "seen" looking into Port 1 with Port 2 open.

#### 2.1.2 Reverse Transfer Impedance ($Z_{12}$)

Set $I_1 = 0$ (Port 1 is open-circuited). Apply test current $I_2$ at Port 2 and measure $V_1$.

$$Z_{12} = \left. \frac{V_1}{I_2} \right|_{I_1 = 0}$$

It represents the voltage produced at Port 1 due to a current injected at Port 2.

#### 2.1.3 Forward Transfer Impedance ($Z_{21}$)

Set $I_2 = 0$ (Port 2 is open-circuited). Apply test current $I_1$ at Port 1 and measure $V_2$.

$$Z_{21} = \left. \frac{V_2}{I_1} \right|_{I_2 = 0}$$

It represents the voltage produced at Port 2 due to a current injected at Port 1.

#### 2.1.4 Driving-Point Impedance at Port 2 ($Z_{22}$)

Set $I_1 = 0$ (Port 1 is open-circuited). Apply test current $I_2$ at Port 2 and measure $V_2$.

$$Z_{22} = \left. \frac{V_2}{I_2} \right|_{I_1 = 0}$$

This is the impedance "seen" looking into Port 2 with Port 1 open.

### 2.2 Properties and Special Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reciprocal Network:** A network is **reciprocal** if $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$. This holds for any linear, passive, bilateral network containing only R, L, M (with $M$ properly signed) and C.
>
> **Symmetric Network:** A network is **symmetric** if $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ **AND** it is reciprocal. Symmetric networks are electrically and topologically interchangeable between the two ports.

### 2.3 Why "Open-Circuit"?

The term "open-circuit" parameter arises because the dependent variables ($V_1, V_2$) are measured under open-circuit conditions at the *opposite* port. This is in direct contrast to Y-parameters, which are measured under short-circuit conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Z-parameters are particularly useful for analyzing **transistor circuits at low/mid frequencies** where the input impedance is high (open-circuit approximation is valid). They are also used in **cascade analysis** of transmission line sections and **filter design**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Z-Parameters

| Parameter | Defining Equation | Open-Circuit Condition | Physical Meaning | Unit |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------------|------------------|------|
| $Z_{11}$ | $V_1 / I_1$ | $I_2 = 0$ | Input driving-point impedance | Ω |
| $Z_{12}$ | $V_1 / I_2$ | $I_1 = 0$ | Reverse transfer impedance | Ω |
| $Z_{21}$ | $V_2 / I_1$ | $I_2 = 0$ | Forward transfer impedance | Ω |
| $Z_{22}$ | $V_2 / I_2$ | $I_1 = 0$ | Output driving-point impedance | Ω |
| $V_1$ | $Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2$ | — | Port-1 voltage | V |
| $V_2$ | $Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2$ | — | Port-2 voltage | V |
| Reciprocity | $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ | — | Linear, passive, bilateral | — |
| Symmetry | $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ and reciprocal | — | Interchangeable ports | — |
| T-to-Z | $Z_{11} = Z_A + Z_C$, $Z_{22} = Z_B + Z_C$, $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = Z_C$ | — | Star / T-network conversion | Ω |

**Note on Notation:** In the T-network, $Z_A$ is the series arm at Port 1, $Z_B$ is the series arm at Port 2, and $Z_C$ is the common shunt arm.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Z-Parameters of a T-Network (General Three-Impedance Network)

Consider a **T-network** with three impedances $Z_A$, $Z_B$, $Z_C$, where $Z_A$ is in series with Port 1, $Z_B$ is in series with Port 2, and $Z_C$ is the shunt arm between them.

**Step 1: Define the network topology and apply KVL.**

The current $I_1$ enters Port 1 and current $I_2$ enters Port 2. The current through the common shunt arm $Z_C$ is $(I_1 + I_2)$ by KCL at the central node.

**Step 2: Apply KVL to Loop 1 (left mesh, $I_1$ direction).**

Going from the +ve terminal of $V_1$ through $Z_A$, then down through $Z_C$ back to the source:

$$V_1 = Z_A I_1 + Z_C (I_1 + I_2)$$

**Step 3: Group terms involving $I_1$ and $I_2$.**

$$V_1 = (Z_A + Z_C) I_1 + Z_C I_2$$

**Step 4: Apply KVL to Loop 2 (right mesh, $I_2$ direction).**

Going from the +ve terminal of $V_2$ through $Z_B$, then up through $Z_C$:

$$V_2 = Z_B I_2 + Z_C (I_1 + I_2)$$

**Step 5: Group terms involving $I_1$ and $I_2$.**

$$V_2 = Z_C I_1 + (Z_B + Z_C) I_2$$

**Step 6: Compare with standard Z-parameter equations.**

Comparing the derived equations with:

$$V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2$$

$$V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2$$

We identify:

$$\boxed{Z_{11} = Z_A + Z_C, \quad Z_{12} = Z_C, \quad Z_{21} = Z_C, \quad Z_{22} = Z_B + Z_C}$$

**Step 7: Write the Z-matrix.**

$$[Z]_T = \begin{bmatrix} Z_A + Z_C & Z_C \\ Z_C & Z_B + Z_C \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 8: Verify reciprocity.**

Since $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = Z_C$, the T-network of passive linear elements is always reciprocal. ✓

**Step 9: Verify symmetry condition.**

Symmetry requires $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$, i.e., $Z_A + Z_C = Z_B + Z_C$, which simplifies to $Z_A = Z_B$. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Z-Parameters of a π-Network

Consider a **π-network** with three impedances $Z_1$, $Z_2$, $Z_3$, where $Z_1$ is in shunt at Port 1, $Z_3$ is in shunt at Port 2, and $Z_2$ is the series arm between them.

**Step 1: Apply KCL at the top node (call it node A).**

Current entering from Port 1: $I_1$. Current leaving through $Z_1$: $V_1 / Z_1$. Current leaving through $Z_2$: $(V_1 - V_2) / Z_2$. Current entering from Port 2: $I_2$. Current leaving through $Z_3$: $V_2 / Z_3$.

KCL at node A:

$$I_1 + I_2 = \frac{V_1}{Z_1} + \frac{V_2}{Z_3} + \frac{V_1 - V_2}{Z_2}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange the equation to isolate $I_1$.**

$$I_1 = \frac{V_1}{Z_1} + \frac{V_2}{Z_3} + \frac{V_1 - V_2}{Z_2} - I_2$$

$$I_1 = V_1 \left( \frac{1}{Z_1} + \frac{1}{Z_2} \right) - V_2 \left( \frac{1}{Z_2} - \frac{1}{Z_3} \right) - I_2$$

**Step 3: Solve the KCL equations as a system.**

For a cleaner approach, write the KCL at the top node and the KCL at Port 2's top terminal:

**KCL at top node:**

$$I_1 + I_2 = V_1 Y_1 + (V_1 - V_2) Y_2 + V_2 Y_3$$

where $Y_i = 1/Z_i$.

$$I_1 + I_2 = V_1(Y_1 + Y_2) + V_2(Y_3 - Y_2)$$

**KCL at Port 2 input terminal:**

$$I_2 = V_2 Y_3 + (V_2 - V_1) Y_2$$

$$I_2 = V_2(Y_2 + Y_3) - V_1 Y_2$$

**Step 4: Solve for $V_1$ and $V_2$ in terms of $I_1$ and $I_2$.**

This requires inverting a 2×2 system. Let:

$$\Delta_Y = (Y_1 + Y_2)(Y_2 + Y_3) - (-Y_2)(-Y_2) \cdot \text{(rearranged)}$$

A cleaner method is to find the Z-parameters by **test-current injection**:

**Test 1: Set $I_2 = 0$, inject $I_1$.**

With $I_2 = 0$, no current enters Port 2. KCL at top node becomes:

$$I_1 = V_1 Y_1 + (V_1 - V_2) Y_2$$

KCL at Port 2 top (current leaving through $Z_3$ equals current entering through $Z_2$):

$$V_2 Y_3 = (V_1 - V_2) Y_2 \implies V_1 Y_2 = V_2(Y_2 + Y_3)$$

$$\implies V_2 = \frac{Y_2 V_1}{Y_2 + Y_3}$$

Substitute back:

$$I_1 = V_1 Y_1 + V_1 Y_2 - \frac{Y_2^2 V_1}{Y_2 + Y_3}$$

$$I_1 = V_1 \left[ Y_1 + Y_2 - \frac{Y_2^2}{Y_2 + Y_3} \right] = V_1 \left[ \frac{Y_1(Y_2 + Y_3) + Y_2 Y_3}{Y_2 + Y_3} \right]$$

Therefore:

$$Z_{11} = \frac{V_1}{I_1} = \frac{Y_2 + Y_3}{Y_1 Y_2 + Y_2 Y_3 + Y_1 Y_3} = \frac{Z_1 (Z_2 + Z_3)}{Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_3}$$

And:

$$V_2 = \frac{Y_2 V_1}{Y_2 + Y_3}$$

$$Z_{21} = \frac{V_2}{I_1} = \frac{Y_2 V_1}{(Y_2 + Y_3)} \cdot \frac{Y_2 + Y_3}{Y_1 Y_2 + Y_2 Y_3 + Y_1 Y_3} \cdot \frac{1}{V_1} = \frac{Y_2}{Y_1 Y_2 + Y_2 Y_3 + Y_1 Y_3}$$

$$Z_{21} = \frac{Z_1 Z_3}{Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_3}$$

**Step 5: By symmetry of the procedure (swap indices 1↔3):**

$$Z_{22} = \frac{Z_3 (Z_1 + Z_2)}{Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_3}, \quad Z_{12} = \frac{Z_1 Z_3}{Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_3}$$

**Step 6: Final Z-matrix for π-network.**

$$[Z]_\pi = \frac{1}{Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_3} \begin{bmatrix} Z_1(Z_2 + Z_3) & Z_1 Z_3 \\ Z_1 Z_3 & Z_3(Z_1 + Z_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ confirms reciprocity. Symmetry requires $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$, i.e., $Z_1(Z_2 + Z_3) = Z_3(Z_1 + Z_2)$, simplifying to $Z_1 Z_2 = Z_2 Z_3$, i.e., $Z_1 = Z_3$ (assuming $Z_2 \neq 0$).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Z-Parameters in Terms of T-Network Arms (Star-Delta Type Conversion)

Given a T-network with $Z_A, Z_B, Z_C$, you can also invert the [Z] matrix to obtain the equivalent Y-parameters:

$$[Y] = [Z]^{-1} = \frac{1}{(Z_A + Z_C)(Z_B + Z_C) - Z_C^2} \begin{bmatrix} Z_B + Z_C & -Z_C \\ -Z_C & Z_A + Z_C \end{bmatrix}$$

$$[Y] = \frac{1}{Z_A Z_B + Z_A Z_C + Z_B Z_C} \begin{bmatrix} Z_B + Z_C & -Z_C \\ -Z_C & Z_A + Z_C \end{bmatrix}$$

This shows the **T-network is reciprocal** since $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Numerical Z-Parameter Computation

**Problem:** Find the Z-parameters of the T-network with $Z_A = 2\,\Omega$, $Z_B = 4\,\Omega$, $Z_C = 3\,\Omega$.

**Solution:**

Using the formulas from Derivation 1:

$$Z_{11} = Z_A + Z_C = 2 + 3 = 5\,\Omega$$

$$Z_{22} = Z_B + Z_C = 4 + 3 = 7\,\Omega$$

$$Z_{12} = Z_{21} = Z_C = 3\,\Omega$$

$$[Z] = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 3 \\ 3 & 7 \end{bmatrix}\,\Omega$$

Since $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$, the network is reciprocal. Since $Z_{11} \neq Z_{22}$, it is **not** symmetric.

**Verification by direct test:** Set $I_2 = 0$, inject $I_1 = 1$ A at Port 1.

KVL: $V_1 = 2(1) + 3(1) = 5$ V ⇒ $Z_{11} = 5\,\Omega$ ✓

KVL at Port 2: $V_2 = 3(1) = 3$ V ⇒ $Z_{21} = 3\,\Omega$ ✓

**Verification by direct test:** Set $I_1 = 0$, inject $I_2 = 1$ A at Port 2.

$V_1 = 3(1) = 3$ V ⇒ $Z_{12} = 3\,\Omega$ ✓

$V_2 = 4(1) + 3(1) = 7$ V ⇒ $Z_{22} = 7\,\Omega$ ✓

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Two-Port Network

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **high-level signal-flow architecture** of a generic two-port network described by Z-parameters.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1["Source I1"] -->|I1 flows in| port1A["Port 1 Terminal A"]
    port1A --> netA["Network Internal Block Z11 Z12 Z21 Z22"]
    netA --> port2A["Port 2 Terminal A"]
    port2A --> load2["Load Side Returns I2"]
    load2 -->|I2 flows in| port2B["Port 2 Terminal B"]
    port2B --> netB["Network Internal Block Z12 Z21"]
    netB --> port1B["Port 1 Terminal B"]
    port1B --> sink1["Source Return"]
    netA -->|V1 measurement across| v1node["V1 Sensing Point"]
    v1node --> eq1["Equation V1 equals Z11 I1 plus Z12 I2"]
    netA -->|V2 measurement across| v2node["V2 Sensing Point"]
    v2node --> eq2["Equation V2 equals Z21 I1 plus Z22 I2"]
    eq1 --> result["Matrix Form V equals Z times I"]
    eq2 --> result
```

### 4.2 T-Network Schematic (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pa["Port 1 Top"] -- ZA Series Arm --> mid["Central Node"]
    mid -- ZC Shunt Arm --> gndA["Common Ground"]
    mid -- ZB Series Arm --> pb["Port 2 Top"]
    pa --> v1p["V1 Across Port 1"]
    v1p --> gndB["Common Ground"]
    pb --> v2p["V2 Across Port 2"]
    v2p --> gndC["Common Ground"]
```

**Interpretation of the T-Network Block Diagram:**

- The **central node** is the junction where $Z_A$, $Z_B$, and $Z_C$ meet.
- The current through $Z_C$ is $I_1 + I_2$ (by KCL).
- $V_1$ is measured from Port 1 top to the common ground.
- $V_2$ is measured from Port 2 top to the common ground.

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Computing Z-Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Given Two-Port Network"] --> identify{"Identify Topology"}
    identify -->|T-Network| tpath["Use Z11 equals ZA plus ZC etc"]
    identify -->|Pi-Network| ppath["Use Pi-Network Z formulas"]
    identify -->|Ladder or Lattice| gpath["Apply Test Current Method"]
    tpath --> zout["Output Z Matrix"]
    ppath --> zout
    gpath --> test1["Step 1 Open Port 2 Inject I1"]
    test1 --> meas1["Measure V1 V2 to get Z11 and Z21"]
    meas1 --> test2["Step 2 Open Port 1 Inject I2"]
    test2 --> meas2["Measure V1 V2 to get Z12 and Z22"]
    meas2 --> zout
    zout --> check{"Z12 equals Z21"}
    check -->|Yes| reci["Network is Reciprocal"]
    check -->|No| nonreci["Network is Non-Reciprocal e.g. Active"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each Mermaid block above is a functional block diagram — not a literal circuit schematic. It captures the logical flow of signals and measurement points, which is the convention adopted in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE answer sheets when full circuit drawings are not required.
